item.title: Pastor Brady Boyd partners with congregation to launch a new church, one year after forced resignation
item.author: Mark A. Kellner
item.dc:creator: Mark A. Kellner
item.description: Pastor Brady Boyd, who resigned under pressure from a Colorado Springs megachurch a year ago, is now partnering with a local congregation to launch a new church. Last June, elders at New Life Church asked for Boyd’s resignation, saying they no longer believed his claim that he had no prior knowledge of child sex abuse […]
item.content:encoded: Brady Boyd was forced out as pastor of New Life Church in Colorado Springs after elders said they no longer believed his account of what he knew about Robert Morris's abuse. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) Pastor Brady Boyd, who resigned under pressure from a Colorado Springs megachurch a year ago, is now partnering with a local congregation to launch a new church. Last June, elders at New Life Church asked for Boyd’s resignation, saying they no longer believed his claim that he had no prior knowledge of child sex abuse by former Gateway Pastor Robert Morris. On Wednesday, both Boyd and The Village Church in Colorado Springs, announced they would combine efforts to launch a new church. The merger follows several months of Wednesday evening gatherings organized through Boyd’s new Psalm 68 Ministries. Boyd is listed on The Village Church website as a “Legacy Pastor,” though that role is not defined. When contacted by phone, Joseph Rangel, a lead pastor at The Village Church, declined to discuss the relationship with Boyd, saying the church would have something to say “at the end of next week.” Boyd did not respond to a phone call from the Roys Report (RR). There was no information provided by either party as to what sparked the merger. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. A graphic posted by The Village Church announces the Aug. 23 launch of a new Colorado Springs congregation in partnership with Brady Boyd, who resigned from New Life Church last year. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) Boyd resigned from New Life Church in June 2025 after the church’s elders concluded he had made inaccurate statements regarding his prior knowledge of Morris’ abuse of Cindy Clemishire. Morris has acknowledged sexually abusing Clemishire beginning when she was 12 years old. He pleaded guilty in Oklahoma to a charge stemming from the abuse and served six months in jail. Before moving to Colorado Springs in 2007, Boyd served on the pastoral staff at Gateway Church in the Dallas-Fort Worth area under Morris. As RR previously reported, court filings, church records and emails indicated Boyd knew years earlier about allegations involving Morris. Boyd denied knowingly concealing the abuse. But 11 days after RR’s initial report, New Life elders said they no longer believed him. “Recently, Brady has insisted that he was unaware until 2024 that Miss Clemishire was 12 years old at the time that Morris had abused her in the 80s,” the elders stated at the time. “We believe this to be inaccurate. Brady also made statements in his public address to the congregation on June 8 that the Board of Elders know to be inaccurate.” Brady Boyd is listed as a “Legacy Pastor” on The Village Church’s website, though the role is not defined. (Photo courtesy of thevillagechurch.co) One month after his resignation, Boyd launched Psalm 68 Ministries. The organization initially focused on humanitarian work, ministry coaching and support for pastors. It also hosted weekly worship services in Colorado Springs. In 2025, Boyd wrote that he and his wife, Pam, intended to continue ministering in Colorado Springs despite his departure from New Life. “Pam and I are still called to pastor in Colorado Springs,” Boyd wrote in announcing the Wednesday gatherings. “We received this mandate 18 years ago, and the calling has only grown stronger.” The Wednesday services began in March at the Phil Long Music Hall in Colorado Springs. Boyd initially described the meetings as gatherings for prayer, Scripture, communion and fellowship, rather than a formal church plant. The newly announced merger with The Village Church — which on its website offered few details about the size or scope of the congregation — signals a transition from those meetings to something more permanent. Neither the merger announcement nor related social media posts revisited the controversy surrounding Boyd’s resignation from New Life. Both focused on the partnership between the two ministries and the anticipated launch of the new congregation. Mark A. Kellner is a reporter based in Mesquite, Nevada. He most recently covered statewide elections for the New York Post and was for three years the Faith & Family Reporter for The Washington Times. Mark is a graduate of the University of the Cumberlands and also attended Boston University’s College of Communication. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Resignation brings no answers at Texas megachurch
item.author: Mark A. Kellner
item.dc:creator: Mark A. Kellner
item.description: Senior Pastor Chris Lindberg has resigned from Life Fellowship Church in McKinney, Texas, ending more than two months of uncertainty after he and his wife, Tatum, were placed on leave without a public explanation. In an email sent Friday to church members, the Lindbergs and the church’s board of elders said the resignation followed “much […]
item.content:encoded: Chris Lindberg, former senior pastor of Life Fellowship Church in McKinney, Texas. (Photo courtesy of social media) Senior Pastor Chris Lindberg has resigned from Life Fellowship Church in McKinney, Texas, ending more than two months of uncertainty after he and his wife, Tatum, were placed on leave without a public explanation. In an email sent Friday to church members, the Lindbergs and the church’s board of elders said the resignation followed “much prayer, wise counsel, and careful consideration.” The statement said the decision was reached jointly by the Lindbergs, the elders and Assemblies of God leadership. The Lindbergs, who came to the church in 2010, each had leadership positions in the congregation. While Chris was senior pastor and a church elder, Tatum held the post of “Executive Pastor, Guest Experience & Pastoral Care.” The elders said the Lindbergs helped grow the church to become “a church that reaches thousands each week — in person, online, and through a prison ministry that now touches over 150,000 lives.” Before the current crisis, there were no apparent reports of problems at the church or with the Lindbergs. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Life Fellowship Church in McKinney, Texas, where Senior Pastor Chris Lindberg has resigned after more than two months on leave. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) The May church statement mentioned that Chris Lindberg had acknowledged unspecified “past personal struggles” but did not offer any further explanation. Church leaders did not disclose allegations against either Chris or Tatum Lindberg, whether any investigation occurred or whether they were given a severance package. A Roys Report (RR) email asking for more details was not immediately returned. The resignation follows nearly three months of questions. In May, the church announced that the Lindbergs had begun a season of “intentional rest and healing.” Elders later extended that leave but repeatedly declined to provide additional details, citing privacy. Friday’s message likewise omitted any explanation for the resignation. The elders said the Lindbergs would focus on “their next chapter together.” Chris and Tatum Lindberg, senior pastors of Life Fellowship Church in McKinney, Texas, were placed on leave in May while the church conducts a restoration process. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) Chris Lindberg thanked the congregation. “I love this church family more than words can say,” Lindberg wrote. “Serving as your pastor has been one of the greatest honors of my life. While this chapter has come to a close, I have complete confidence that Jesus will continue building His Church and that the greatest days of Life Fellowship are still ahead.” The elders praised the couple’s ministry, noting they had led the church for 16 years. “Life Fellowship wants our congregation to know how deeply grateful we are for Chris and Tatum’s years of faithful service, their vision, and their devotion to this church family,” the board statement said. “That legacy of service and commitment matters, and it will not be forgotten.” The email asked members to pray for the Lindbergs and outlined plans for new leadership. The elders said they have selected an interim pastor who will be introduced in August but did not name that individual. They also said a seven-member search committee composed of congregational leaders will assist the board in identifying a permanent senior pastor. Mark A. Kellner is a reporter based in Mesquite, Nevada. He most recently covered statewide elections for the New York Post and was for three years the Faith & Family Reporter for The Washington Times. Mark is a graduate of the University of the Cumberlands and also attended Boston University’s College of Communication. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Study: Pastors continue to struggle with burnout, mental health, finances
item.author: Diana Chandler
item.dc:creator: Diana Chandler
item.description: Vocational satisfaction, mental health and relational wellbeing continue to challenge pastors despite growing assurance in their calling, newly released State of the Church research found, with many pastors under 45 also citing financial concerns. While pastors are less exhausted and more confident in their calling, vocational satisfaction remains at its lowest point in more than […]
item.content:encoded: Pastors report feeling less burned out than in recent years, but only 52% now say they're "very satisfied" with their vocation. (RR Graphic) Vocational satisfaction, mental health and relational wellbeing continue to challenge pastors despite growing assurance in their calling, newly released State of the Church research found, with many pastors under 45 also citing financial concerns. While pastors are less exhausted and more confident in their calling, vocational satisfaction remains at its lowest point in more than a decade, Barna Group and Gloo said of the findings among Protestant senior pastors in the U.S. from a diversity of denominations. More than half of all pastors surveyed, 52 percent, said they need the most help to feel physically and mentally healthy, but that portion rose to 62 percent among pastors 45 and younger, researchers said. Many, 41 percent of all pastors and 38 percent of those under 45, said they also could use help establishing close and supportive relationships. Well over half of pastors, 58 percent, expressed confidence in their calling, rebounding from the 35 percent who cited such confidence in the COVID pandemic years spanning 2020-2022. “By several measures, pastors in 2026 are in a more sustainable season of overall wellbeing than they have been in years. Pastoral exhaustion is down. Confidence in one’s calling has largely rebounded from its pandemic low,” researchers wrote. “What has not recovered is deeper vocational satisfaction.” While 72 percent said they were “very satisfied” with their vocation in 2015, that portion has dwindled to 52 percent, researchers said. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Barna’s research, in cooperation with Gloo, was conducted Jan. 29 through Feb. 8 among 502 senior Protestant pastors. Gauging burnout, pastors said the resource that would help them most – sabbaticals or extended time off – was also the most difficult to attain. Pastors said it would also help to delegate some of their responsibilities to other staff, and to adjust their duties to better align with their strengths and limits. “Pastors deserve the time to step away and ask honestly whether their role is an expression of their actual gifts,” said Daniel Copeland, Barna’s vice president of research. “We need church leaders who are in touch with themselves, in touch with God, and living in alignment with how they’re made. If there’s any vocation we want liberated from the mundane, it’s this one.” “There’s a difference between recovering from burnout and actually resolving it,” Copeland said. “Consistent rest, boundaries and personal spiritual practices build the weekly rhythms that keep a pastor healthy,” he said. “But the deeper question – whether your role is genuinely an expression of your gifts and strengths – requires a different kind of time and attention altogether. That’s the work most pastors haven’t had space to do.” While most pastors aren’t able to take extended sabbaticals, researchers recommend smaller changes that can also impact wellbeing. Among them: Take care of physical and mental health by taking time away from the work desk, getting consistent sleep and participating in physical exercise not related to ministry obligations. “These register in the data as real contributors to restoration,” researchers wrote, “not merely nice additions to a busy schedule.” Nurture and consider building more relationships. Pastors said they primarily rely on spouses for relational support, followed by fellow pastors or ministerial leaders. “Close friends outside the congregation are named by 42 percent. Mentor or spiritual director rank lower, at 30 percent, and a counselor or therapist lower still, at 18 percent,” researchers said, describing these as “support structures that remain underutilized across the profession.” An overview of the findings is available here, with more extensive data also available. This article was originally published by Baptist Press and has been reprinted with permission. Diana Chandler is senior writer for Baptist Press. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Fulani Extremists Kill 25 Christians After Nigerian Army Ignores Early Warnings
item.author: ICC Staff
item.dc:creator: ICC Staff
item.description: At least 25 Christians, including a 2-month-old, were killed in separate attacks during the weekend in Nigeria’s Middle Belt region. The first attack occurred on Saturday night, July 11, in the Kum and Wereng-Comp communities of Ryom County in Plateau state. Residents told International Christian Concern (ICC) that the Fulani entered the community around 11:30 […]
item.content:encoded: Photo courtesy of International Christian Concern. At least 25 Christians, including a 2-month-old, were killed in separate attacks during the weekend in Nigeria’s Middle Belt region. The first attack occurred on Saturday night, July 11, in the Kum and Wereng-Comp communities of Ryom County in Plateau state. Residents told International Christian Concern (ICC) that the Fulani entered the community around 11:30 p.m. “We got a security alert on social media that Fulani are attacking us on Sunday,” said a local, who wished to remain anonymous. “We’re on alert but not prepared since we don’t have guns. The man said nine people were killed and buried on Sunday in a mass grave. The nine people killed belonged to the same Christian family, he added. “Why would the terrorist kill an infant?” the man asked. “This was to wipe out all the Christians.” Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Officials with the Nigeria Police Force in Niger State, Nigeria. (Photo: ICC) Among those killed was the village’s leader. The local said the Fulani planned to kill their leader, occupy the land for grazing, and establish an Islamic caliphate in the region. “Our village head is critically injured; the Fulani want to kill him just because he is saying the truth during meetings. He emphasized that he doesn’t want Fulani grazing and destroying our crops on the farm.” The National Publicity Secretary of the Berom Youth Molders Association confirmed the attack in a press release. He said the attack occurred near a Nigerian army checkpoint and that the army failed to respond, despite the arrival of the Fulani in large numbers. Another Attack in Benue Hours later, armed assailants attacked the Nobi community in Otukpo Local Government Area in Benue state. Residents said the raid began between 3:30 a.m. and 4:30 a.m. on Sunday, July 12, when gunmen opened fire and set some property ablaze. Comfort Inalegwu, a resident who joined demonstrations after the attack, said at least 16 people were killed. She said her sister and two of her sister’s children were shot inside their home and later taken to the General Hospital mortuary in Otukpo. Several people were injured, while families continued searching for residents whose whereabouts were unknown. Casualty figures from Nobi differed. Benue police reported eight deaths and five injuries, while Amnesty International Nigeria said at least 10 people had been killed. Local witnesses continued to place the toll at 16. Police said additional officers were deployed and that an investigation had begun. A new study identifies Fulani militias as the deadliest persecutors of Christians in Nigeria. (Photo courtesy of ICC) Women and youth blocked sections of the Makurdi-Otukpo and Enugu-Otukpo highways, as well as roads near the palace of the Och’Idoma. Demonstrators demanded improved protection, arrests, and prosecution of those responsible. Some women said they would continue protesting and could boycott election activities if attacks persisted. Benue Governor Hyacinth Alia condemned the killings in a statement issued through his chief press secretary, Tersoo Kula. He directed security agencies to increase surveillance in vulnerable communities, coordinate with neighboring states, and pursue those responsible for the attack. No group had publicly claimed responsibility for either attack at the time of reporting. Residents and community representatives attributed the assaults to suspected armed Fulani militants, but authorities had not publicly established the identity, motive, or organizational affiliation of the attackers. Benue and Plateau lie within Nigeria’s Middle Belt, where predominantly Christian farming communities have experienced repeated raids, displacement, destruction of homes, and loss of farmland. Violence in the region has been linked to several overlapping factors, including land disputes, criminal activity, ethnic tensions, religious hostility, and the movement of armed groups. In June 2025, about 150 people were killed in an attack on the Yelwata community in Benue. Nigerian prosecutors later filed terrorism-related charges against nine men accused of planning and supporting that assault. Families affected by the latest attacks are seeking security, medical assistance for survivors, help locating missing relatives, and support for displaced residents. Community leaders are also asking federal and state authorities to publish the findings of investigations, maintain a sustained security presence in exposed villages, and ensure that suspects are brought before a court. This article originally appeared at persecution.org and has been reprinted with permission. Founded in 1995, International Christian Concern (ICC) is a Christian organization. It’s an ecumenical, non-governmental, and non-partisan group focused on protecting the human rights of Christians and religious minorities. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Appeal renews legal fight over Harlem’s Abyssinian Baptist Church pastor
item.author: Adelle Banks
item.dc:creator: Adelle Banks
item.description: Current and former members of New York City’s Abyssinian Baptist Church have filed an appeal in state Supreme Court arguing that a 2025 court ruling was in error when it dismissed their case challenging the election of the congregation’s current senior pastor. The four plaintiffs filed a brief July 9 with the New York Supreme […]
item.content:encoded: Congregants attend Abyssinian Baptist Church, Sunday, July 12, 2026, in Harlem, New York. (Photo courtesy of Abyssinian Baptist Church) Current and former members of New York City’s Abyssinian Baptist Church have filed an appeal in state Supreme Court arguing that a 2025 court ruling was in error when it dismissed their case challenging the election of the congregation’s current senior pastor. The four plaintiffs filed a brief July 9 with the New York Supreme Court’s Appellate Division, 1st Department, requesting that the election of the Rev. Kevin R. Johnson be set aside so a new election can be held. The plaintiffs argue that the court “misread” the church’s bylaws when it denied their petition, according to the filing. Johnson’s election as the Harlem church’s new senior pastor was announced in June 2024, and he was installed in September of that year. He succeeded the Rev. Calvin O. Butts III, who served as a minister at the historically Black church for 50 years and died in 2022. However, the plaintiffs say the church bylaws require a pastoral election by a majority of all members who are eligible to vote and are in good standing, rather than a majority of just the members who vote at a meeting. In total, 44% of registered church members voted in Johnson’s election. “Nobody disputes that the term ‘majority’ means more than half,” the plaintiffs note. “The question is a majority of what.” Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. The Rev. Kevin R. Johnson at Abyssinian Baptist Church, Sunday, July 12, 2026, in Harlem, New York. (Photo courtesy of Abyssinian Baptist Church) In a statement announcing the appeal, the petitioners said their case “has never been about the court or the petitioners dictating congregational choice of a pastor” but rather about whether the church abided by the governance procedures stated in its bylaws. Abyssinian responded to the development with confidence that a new pastoral election would be determined not necessary. “As the church has continued to heal, grow and thrive under the leadership of Rev. Dr. Kevin R. Johnson over the past two years, it’s clear the congregation is pleased with their properly and duly elected pastor and have moved on from this chapter,” said LaToya Evans, the church’s spokesperson. “We’re confident the appellate court will reach the same conclusion as the trial court and find the lawsuit to be without merit.” Three of the plaintiffs are church members, including Kevin McGruder, an associate professor at Antioch College in Yellow Springs, Ohio, who told media he watches Abyssinian’s worship services virtually but attends annual meetings in person. The fourth plaintiff, the Rev. C. Vernon Mason, is now a member and minister in residence at another Harlem church. They note that the bylaws use language such as “who are present in a Meeting duly assembled” when determining who would vote for changing the church’s location, and a “majority vote at any duly called Meeting” is required for the expulsion of members. But the lack of such modifiers about the election of the pastor is an intentional omission, the plaintiffs say. The plaintiffs cite a past New York Court of Appeals case related to an interpretation of a corporation’s shareholders during a vote. The 2012 ruling found that a dissolution vote required passage by a “supermajority” of the shareholders rather than a mere two-thirds of voters present at a shareholder meeting. “Consistent with its status as a congregational church and with its Baptist tradition, Abyssinian requires a candidate for pastor to earn the support of a majority of members in good standing who are eligible to vote,” the July 9 brief reads. Members of Harlem’s Abyssinian Baptist Church attend a court hearing in lower Manhattan, Thursday, July 17, 2025, in New York City. (RNS photo/Fiona André) The plaintiffs also state that the vice chair of the pastoral search committee told the congregation on two separate occasions that the vote required a majority of the membership and stressed that standard was higher than the church’s voting standard for other matters. “As a result, a significant portion of members could have sat out because they believed that Dr. Johnson could prevail only with enough affirmative votes for him,” they argued. “The only fair outcome here is to set aside the election to ensure that the church has a full opportunity to reach a new, clear, and adequate expression of the will of the congregation.” Abyssinian was the subject of other litigation about the selection of its newest pastor. The Rev. Eboni Marshall Turman, a former assistant minister at the church and a candidate for the senior pastor position, filed a gender discrimination lawsuit in 2023 after she did not get the job. The church asked a judge to dismiss the case on the grounds of the “ministerial exception,” under which religious institutions are given more latitude in personnel and other matters. In March 2025, a federal district judge sided with the church, and records show that a federal appeals court granted Marshall Turman’s request to voluntarily dismiss her appeal months later. Johnson first came to Abyssinian in the 1990s, serving as an assistant pastor to Butts. In 2014, he resigned from his position as senior pastor of Bright Hope Baptist Church in North Philadelphia after the congregation disapproved of his handling of the church’s financial affairs and his plan to run for mayor of Philadelphia, which he eventually abandoned. He subsequently founded Dare to Imagine Church in Philadelphia, an interdenominational church affiliated with the American Baptist Churches USA. Johnson and Butts were preceded by other prominent Abyssinian ministers, including the Rev. Adam Clayton Powell Sr., Rep. Adam Clayton Powell Jr. and the Rev. Samuel DeWitt Proctor. And the plaintiffs’ brief cited the Rev. Raphael Warnock, now a Georgia senator and the pastor of Atlanta’s Ebenezer Baptist Church, who served for a decade as a youth pastor and assistant minister under Butts’ leadership at Abyssinian. “Baptist pastors are recommended by a search committee of the church’s members and elected by a vote of the full congregation, unlike denominations where a presiding bishop appoints the head of the church,” the senator wrote in his 2022 book “A Way Out of No Way.” Warnock preached at Abyssinian on Sunday (July 12) at the worship service that marked Johnson’s second pastoral anniversary there, during his tour for his newest book “The Crooked Places Made Straight: Reflections on the Moral Meaning of America.” Adelle Banks is production editor and a national correspondent at Religion News Service. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Georgia evangelical group fined for dozens of campaign finance violations
item.author: Daniel Silliman
item.dc:creator: Daniel Silliman
item.description: The Georgia State Ethics Commission fined a conservative evangelical organization twice last month for violating the state’s campaign finance law 25 times. The government is now considering increasing penalties for repeat offenders. “It’s one of the highest lobbying fines we’ve ever doled out,” Executive Director David Emadi told the Georgia Recorder last week. “It’s not […]
item.content:encoded: rontline Policy Action, a conservative evangelical advocacy organization based in Georgia, was fined $17,500 after admitting to 25 violations of the state's campaign finance law. (RR Graphic) The Georgia State Ethics Commission fined a conservative evangelical organization twice last month for violating the state’s campaign finance law 25 times. The government is now considering increasing penalties for repeat offenders. “It’s one of the highest lobbying fines we’ve ever doled out,” Executive Director David Emadi told the Georgia Recorder last week. “It’s not about inflicting pain …. It’s just about making sure that the law is properly enforced so the public has the information that it’s supposed to have.” Frontline Policy Action agreed in late June to pay $7,500 and $10,000, a little more than 12% of its annual revenue. Frontline also formally accepted the commission’s allegations as fact. The charges include failing to report campaign contributions and unregistered lobbying. On its website, Frontline touts its “first-class lobbying tactics” that get conservative legislation passed in Georgia. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Cole Muzio (right), President of Frontline Policy Council, leads a conversation with Thomas Hammond (Georgia Baptist Mission Board), Matt Sharp (Alliance Defending Freedom), and Joseph D’Angelo (Church Ambassador Network) on what the Church is called to be in November 2025. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) “We go toe-to-toe with the behemoth organizations of the Left,” Frontline says, “and win!” According to public tax filings, however, Frontline has spent no money on lobbying. And in 2023 and 2024, the group had no registered lobbyists. Frontline President Cole Muzio described the violations as “essentially a clerical error on our part.” “We rectified that,” Muzio said. “Our goal has and remains to always abide by the letter and spirit of the law.” Political victories Muzio founded Frontline in 2021, after working more than four years with Focus on the Family’s state lobbying arm. The group describes itself as a “Gospel-centered organization” with a “legislative agenda … firmly rooted in scriptural principles.” A 501(c)(3) nonprofit, Frontline Policy Council works to “glorify God, make Him known and bless our neighbors” through political education. The lobbying arm, registered with the IRS as a 501(c)(4) entity, endorses candidates and promotes legislation. Both sides of the organization are a “Christian ministry,” according to the group’s website. “Politics is never the end goal,” Frontline says. “It is the means through which we seek to glorify King Jesus.” Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp has worked closely with Frontline Policy Action, signing dozens of bills the conservative evangelical advocacy group supported. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) Muzio and his team have racked up an impressive number of political wins in Georgia, a traditionally conservative state with a growing number of liberal voters. Frontline takes credit for pushing more than 70 items of legislation onto the desk of Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp. Kemp signed 45 Frontline-backed bills into law in 2024 and 2025. The group has a close, working relationship with the governor. Until recently, Brian Hughes, the governor’s director of field services, sat on the lobbying organization’s board. Muzio has also stood with prominent statehouse Republicans as they promote conservative legislation. Observers credit the group with the passage of laws prohibiting transgender athletes from playing on women’s sports teams and increased protections for religious liberty. Frontline also advocated for an expanded school vouchers bill that narrowly passed in 2024. Other priorities for the evangelical organization include “opposing comprehensive sexual education” in public schools, restricting access to abortion drugs and fighting the expansion of legalized gambling. Political enemies Success has won the group ample enemies on the left. Democratic legislator Karen Lupton, for example, who represents a suburban district north of Atlanta, called Frontline an extreme far-right organization that misuses Scripture to mask hate. “I don’t think the general public has a real grasp of just how deeply embedded this Christian nationalist organization is in making policy for the state of Georgia,” Lupton told the Georgia Recorder. “It’s just kind of accepted as, ‘Well this is the way we do business in Georgia,’ and that’s not acceptable to me.” The Southern Poverty Law Center (SPLC) — a controversial, internally troubled civil rights group that was recently indicted on federal charges that it defrauded donors — has been tracking Frontline for several years. In 2024, the SPLC “Hatewatch” newsletter reported that Muzio had apparently let his lobbying registration lapse in 2022, despite continued political activity. “If you spend money to influence a decision, you have to tell people, you have to disclose that,” the SPLC said. “That’s part of transparency in the democratic process.” Eight months later, Seth Levi, the SPLC’s chief strategy officer, filed a complaint with the Georgia Ethics Commission, prompting an investigation. A second complaint was filed in May 2025 by civil rights attorney Brian Sells, who previously worked as a litigator in the Justice Department’s Civil Rights Division during the Barack Obama administration. Sells accused Frontline of nine violations, including running campaign ads on social media without proper funding disclosures. Muzio has acknowledged the mistakes and agreed to pay the fines. But the evangelical organization also celebrates facing opposition. “Frontline is flattered by the attention it’s received from those who hate us,” a statement on the website says. “The Southern Poverty Law Center, one of the most evil, progressive, and well-funded organizations in the world, recognizes that going up against Frontline is like ‘fighting a forest fire with a bucket of water.’ “This fire won’t be extinguished anytime soon.” The group has agreed to pay $17,500 in fines by the end of August. Daniel Silliman is senior reporter/editor at The Roys Report. He began his two decades in journalism covering crime in Atlanta and has since led major investigations into abuse and misconduct in Christian contexts. Daniel and his wife live in Johnson City, Tennessee. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Fount Church NYC pastor resigns following months of financial turmoil
item.author: Rebecca Hopkins
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Hopkins
item.description: New York City’s Fount Church Pastor Josh Kelsey, who has been accused of $1.4 million in questionable spending, has resigned. The megachurch’s board, which recently said it cleared Kelsey of deliberate wrongdoing, toldRoys Report (RR) about his resignation today. The statement, which the board sent to RR through an external public relations firm, praised Kelsey’s […]
item.content:encoded: Josh Kelsey, founder of New York City's Fount Church, has resigned after months of scrutiny over questionable church spending. (Photo courtesy of social media) New York City’s Fount Church Pastor Josh Kelsey, who has been accused of $1.4 million in questionable spending, has resigned. The megachurch’s board, which recently said it cleared Kelsey of deliberate wrongdoing, toldRoys Report (RR) about his resignation today. The statement, which the board sent to RR through an external public relations firm, praised Kelsey’s leadership. “The Board wishes to express its sincere gratitude to Josh for his thirteen years of faithful leadership, vision and service in founding and building Fount Church,” the statement said. One board member resigned and left the church in March. Flavia Mendonca, who also worked as a manager overseeing audits, told RR she felt church leaders cared more about optics than accountability. “One of the reasons (my family) left was we were not fully comfortable anymore with what we were seeing behind the scenes,” Mendonca told RR. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Worship service at Fount NYC-Manhattan campus in New York. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) In its statement, the Fount board didn’t explain about a process to find a replacement pastor. RR emailed the PR firm for further clarification on this but the firm didn’t immediately respond. The New York City church is facing another transition. Kelsey founded Fount in 2013. At the time he was pastoring in a C3 Church Global congregation in Sydney. But according to Fount’s website, he believed God was calling him to start a similar church in New York. He planted Fount — also a part of the Australian-based C3 Church Global church planting movement — by initially hosting dinner parties. It soon grew to church services throughout New York, then to Berlin and Paris, which are now independent churches. Fount left C3 last year, RR previously reported. Now Fount is joining Kingdomcity, a self-described “global Christian church movement,” founded by Australian attorney-turned-pastor Mark Varughese. Board member and Fount Pastor Olusegun Olujide made the announcement at Fount’s July 5 church service. Fount Church Pastor Olusegun Olujide addresses the congregation on July 5, 2026, announcing Josh Kelsey’s resignation and the church’s plans to join the global church movement Kingdomcity. (Screen grab) “We are just so thankful for Pastor Josh and Georgie for sowing their hearts out into New York City, but … what they have done is just a seed,” Olujide said. “Now Kingdom City (is) going to come in with their covering and leadership, and God’s going to take that seed and He’s going to increase it to the whole of New York City.” The board’s statement to RR gave few details on this arrangement. “Fount also confirms that it is working to bring the church under the oversight of a larger, established global church movement to strengthen its long-term leadership and sustainability,” the board stated. “Further details will be announced once those arrangements are finalised (sic).” RR asked Fount’s public relations firm for information on new oversight and accountability. RRalso emailed Kingdomcity to ask about whether it requires annual audits to be made public, and whether it has safeguarding and whistleblowing policies. Kingdomcity didn’t immediately respond. Josh Kelsey could not be reached for comment either. Board member resigned In January, a leaked preliminary review by the auditing firm CapinCrouse flagged unapproved expenses on the Kelseys’ church credit card, RR previously reported. The expenses included almost $30,000 spent on personal flights, $3,500 on alcohol, and more than $9,000 on surfing. Josh and Georgie Kelsey, founders of New York City’s Fount Church. (Photo courtesy of social media) Mendonca was one of Fount’s newest board members, elected by the congregation on Jan. 11 to the office of treasurer. One of the first orders of business for the board was to hire an auditor, the board president previously stated. Finding fault with the CapinCrouse report, the newly elected board hired Dentons, a multinational law firm. On April 6, board president Frank Wheatley sent RR a six-page summary of findings, written by the board, that cleared the Kelseys of wrongdoing. This summary referenced but didn’t link to a 455-page investigative report from Dentons. According to the board, the report says the pastoral couple didn’t misspend anywhere near the $1.4 million attributed to them; rather a range of church employees and volunteers incurred the expenses. What the board didn’t disclose to RR at that time was that Mendonca had tried to resign in February, citing accountability concerns. Her Feb. 2 resignation letter states she couldn’t fully provide financial oversight. “Due to current circumstances and limitations that prevent me from exercising the full financial oversight and independent governance required by my role, I can no longer fulfill my fiduciary duties,” she wrote. She told RR that other board members seemed more focused on clearing Kelsey’s name and“moving forward,” than holding anyone accountable for possible misspending. “I was not comfortable with the framing of the communications,” she told RR. “That’s not the right way to do it. You have to hold people accountable for what they did.” Branding image for Fount NYC (Screengrab) She said she was often the only board member raising concerns about the wisdom of some expenses. “You think I hate Pastor Josh, but I don’t,” Mendonca said. In her resignation letter, Mendonca gave the board a parting word. “I also advise that the board focus the effort in implementing bylaws, policies and procedures considering internal controls,” she wrote. The board asked Mendonca to stay longer to continue working on policies and procedures, and she delayed her resignation to March 24. Mendonca told RR she never saw a copy of the full audit because by the time it was ready, she was on her way out. Though the church promptly removed her email access after her March 24 resignation, she said, it kept her name on the website as a board member until April 9. This date was three days after the board had sent RR a summary clearing the Kelseys of wrongdoing. The church removed her name, Mendonca told RR, only after her husband sent the entire board a request. RR emailed Wheatley, the board president, for comment. He didn’t immediately respond. The official board statement mentioned no concerns about Kelsey. It said he will remain in Australia full-time. He was living mostly there for the past several months, citing health concerns in his extended family, RR previously reported. Sometimes he would fly to New York to preach. “Through his commitment, countless lives have been impacted,” the board stated. “(The board) fully supports Josh and his family as they begin this new season and thanks him for the lasting legacy he leaves.” Mendonca told RR she left Fount wishing it would build a foundation of transparency moving forward. She added, “How can you build trust without being clear and transparent?” Rebecca Hopkins is a journalist based in Colorado. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Nathan Peternel, pastor whose private sex tape surfaced in son’s criminal investigation, returns to pulpit
item.author: Mark A. Kellner
item.dc:creator: Mark A. Kellner
item.description: Indiana megachurch pastor Nathan Peternel has returned to the pulpit after stepping away following his son’s conviction for child exploitation and possession of child sex abuse material. The elder Peternel was ordered to step away from his ministry at Life Church, a multi-campus Assemblies of God congregation in central Indiana, after Jonathan Peternel, then 24, […]
item.content:encoded: Nathan Peternel returned to the pulpit at Life Church in Fishers, Indiana, on July 5, eight months after stepping away following his son's arrest on child sex abuse material charges. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) Indiana megachurch pastor Nathan Peternel has returned to the pulpit after stepping away following his son’s conviction for child exploitation and possession of child sex abuse material. The elder Peternel was ordered to step away from his ministry at Life Church, a multi-campus Assemblies of God congregation in central Indiana, after Jonathan Peternel, then 24, was arrested in October. His denomination ordered him to undergo a “structured restorative process,” after determining the sex tapes were not in keeping with ministerial standards. The younger Peternel pleaded guilty in February and is serving a six-year prison term. His arrest sent shockwaves through Life Church, not least because police indicated the son had videos of his parents engaging in sex acts. At sentencing, Jonathan directly addressed his parents: “I’m sorry for putting you in this humiliating situation. You’ve been dishonored, your wonderful reputations have been ruined, and your names have been dragged through the mud all because I cared about myself more than you.” Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Nathan Peternel addresses the Life Church congregation in Fishers, Indiana, on July 5, his first Sunday back after an eight-month absence. (Screen grab) On July 5, the elder Peternel spoke to the Life Church congregation in the Indianapolis suburb of Fishers, saying he was happy to be back. “It is a joy to be with you this morning; I can’t even tell you, and I’ve been looking forward to this for a long time,” Peternel began. “I want to say for our family how much we’ve missed you and how much we love our church!” In an online statement before Peternel’s return, the church’s leadership described the absence as “a season of rest and renewal.” It was, they said, a “restorative season” in which “the Lord has been strengthening them physically, spiritually, and emotionally.” Peternel did not describe what he did during the eight months he was away. But he spoke about the strains presented by his son’s arrest and the disclosure that intimate videos of Nathan Peternel and his wife were in his son’s possession. Jonathan Peternel, son of Nathan Peternel, pleaded guilty to possession of child sexual abuse material. (Photo courtesy of Madison County Sheriff) “Our family has experienced, you know, really, the hardest season we’ve ever gone through, as you know,” Peternel said July 5. “So, we have been very appreciative for the rest that we’ve been able to get, and we’ve been very appreciative for the counseling and the people who’ve poured into us during this season, it’s meant a lot.” The following week, Peternel spoke to the Life Church congregation at the Eagle Creek campus and said he appreciated the support of church members during the period. “It’s not just my family that’s been going through it. Our whole church family has been going through it,” Peternel said. “So many people have been carrying the effects of the brokenness that has happened, and it’s been heavy. It’s been weighty, and I want to thank you for continuing to pray the way you are and continuing to trust God in the midst of the storm.” Peternel’s pulpit comeback apparently turns a page in the saga that roiled a congregation well-connected in Indiana political circles. The Life Church lead pastor is a longtime associate of the state’s Republican Lt. Gov. Micah Beckwith, who is campus pastor at the church’s Noblesville campus. The two co-hosted a podcast titled “Jesus, Sex & Politics” that frequently discussed sexual morality, along with the role of faith in public life and government. No new episodes of the podcast have appeared since police searched Peternel’s home, which happened before Jonathan’s arrest. Two episodes, including one on child abuse and sex trafficking, were pulled from distribution. Roys Report (RR) has contacted the Indiana District of the Assemblies of God and Life Church for comment. Mark A. Kellner is a reporter based in Mesquite, Nevada. He most recently covered statewide elections for the New York Post and was for three years the Faith & Family Reporter for The Washington Times. Mark is a graduate of the University of the Cumberlands and also attended Boston University’s College of Communication. SHARE THIS:

item.title: EXCLUSIVE: PCA censured Wheaton President Phil Ryken for ‘aiding the strong in preying on the weak’
item.author: Daniel Silliman
item.dc:creator: Daniel Silliman
item.description: Wheaton College President Phil Ryken, who has been under fire for covering up an abuse scandal at the prominent, Chicago-area evangelical school, appears to have a history of protecting sexual predators. And his silence has reportedly twice enabled sexual predators to abuse again. In 2001, while Ryken was senior minister at the historic Tenth Presbyterian […]
item.content:encoded: Wheaton College President Phil Ryken, who led Tenth Presbyterian Church in Philadelphia before becoming Wheaton's president, was censured by a PCA court in 2017 over his handling of abuse allegations against former music director Paul Jones. (Photo courtesy of Wheaton.edu) Wheaton College President Phil Ryken, who has been under fire for covering up an abuse scandal at the prominent, Chicago-area evangelical school, appears to have a history of protecting sexual predators. And his silence has reportedly twice enabled sexual predators to abuse again. In 2001, while Ryken was senior minister at the historic Tenth Presbyterian Church in Philadelphia, the music director there, Paul Jones, confessed to beating a naked, male college student with a stick. Ryken kept Jones on staff and his misconduct confidential. In 2017, Ryken’s denomination officially censured him for limiting the investigation into Jones’ abuse and withholding information about the abuse. The Philadelphia region of the Presbyterian Church in America (PCA) condemned Ryken and other leaders of Tenth for prioritizing “institutional reputation” over “the cries of the victims.” A later investigation by GRACE concluded that Ryken’s silence enabled Jones to abuse more victims. Similarly, Ryken’s silence about a former administrator’s abuse at Wheaton reportedly enabled him to abuse more victims decades after being quietly dismissed. The PCA court document, exclusively obtained by Roys Report (RR), said Ryken and the church elders were guilty of “aiding the strong in preying on the weak.” Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Former Wheaton College presidents Duane Litfin and Philip Ryken, under whose administrations the school is accused of keeping allegations against longtime ministry leader Dennis Massaro confidential. (Photo courtesy of social media) “The victims … were pushed aside,” the censure said. “You have damaged the Church; you have damaged Christ’s reputation before the world.” PCA admonitions, excommunications, suspensions and depositions are not typically published online. But church members involved in the proceedings receive copies and may share them and discuss them informally. Ryken’s censure was not reported at the time, nor since. According to a statement Wheaton’s spokesman emailed to RR, the PCA censure was shared privately with the college’s board of trustees. Ryken, who led Tenth for a decade before becoming president of Wheaton in 2010, kept the board “fully informed at every step of the process,” the statement said. The trustees described Ryken’s response to the PCA court ruling as “God honoring.” “Dr. Ryken accepted (the) admonition,” the college statement said. “He stood with the elders and the victims before the Tenth congregation to ask for and receive forgiveness by apologizing publicly for failing to take additional necessary steps of pastoral oversight when he first learned of the abuse.” However, one of Jones’ seven known victims told RR that the college’s statement is “lies.” “I asked him to stand with us,” the man told RR. “He didn’t do a damned thing.” Pattern of silence Ryken appears to have a decades-long pattern of keeping abuse allegations quiet. As previously reported by RR, the college president refused to expose former administrator Dennis Massaro, who was dismissed quietly after allegedly molesting students on college-sponsored retreats. Family portrait of former Wheaton administrator Dennis Massaro, his then-wife Kathy, and their daughters. (Courtesy photo) Massaro’s ex-wife, Kathy Gallagher, told RR she pleaded with Ryken in 2014 to publicly disclose the real reason Massaro was dismissed from the college 16 years earlier. Wheaton had Massaro sign a non-disclosure agreement and allowed the campus newspaper to claim in 1998 that the former head of the Office of Christian Outreach had resigned for “personal reasons.” Massaro moved on to new ministry positions, where he reportedly abused more young men. In a 2014 email to Gallagher and her husband, Ryken communicated a policy of remaining silent about abuse allegations. “For legal and other reasons, the College generally does not provide confidential personnel information to persons outside the College,” he wrote. “I have concluded that I am not able to share any information the College may have about allegations of misconduct.” Following RR’s reporting, an alumnus posted a petition online, calling for Wheaton to repent and asking the board to investigate Ryken’s handling of Massaro’s abuse. The petition now has more than 2,500 signatures. Ryken has not responded to the petition or any of RR’s reporting. The PCA censure, never before made public, indicates that Ryken has a history of maintaining silence to protect the reputation of Christian institutions. The 59-year-old theologian, who has written about the temptations of power and the biblical response to suffering, previously described his time in pastoral ministry as critical preparation for his leadership at Wheaton. “I’d actually be hard pressed to think of better preparation,” Ryken told The Gospel Coalition, where he serves on the board. “Because most of the difficult problems that you have are always going to be people problems.” Ryken has, in fact, dealt with some of the difficult “people problems” at the church and the school in the same way. Documents obtained by RR show that when Jones, an esteemed composer at the prominent PCA congregation, was accused of abuse in 2001, Ryken worked to keep it quiet. The future college president sharply limited the investigation and withheld information from both the elders and the congregation, according to his own testimony. As Ryken told one of the victims in a failed reconciliation meeting, he “did not think the details … needed to be disclosed.” Abuse report minimized According to a statement Ryken submitted to the PCA in 2017, he learned of the abuse at Tenth during his first week as senior minister. In 2001, he was promoted to replace the recently deceased James Boice, a popular Reformed theologian and prominent defender of the doctrine of biblical inerrancy. Ryken said that before he could even settle in, Jones asked to see him. Jones sought Ryken’s counsel because he had been asked to resign from his teaching position at Philadelphia Bible University (later renamed Cairn), Ryken said. The Christian school had received a report that Jones had mistreated a student. Former Tenth Presbyterian Music Director Paul Jones Jones, who trained in Toronto’s Royal Conservatory and composed hundreds of pieces of sacred music, including 15 hymns he co-wrote with Boice, said there was only one complaint. He called it a misunderstanding, according to Ryken. Jones claimed that the young man in question had asked to be spanked because he was homeschooled, and that’s how he learned from his parents. Jones told Ryken he had simply gone along with the request and insisted it wasn’t sexual. Jones, who signed a non-disclosure agreement when he left Tenth in 2014, did not respond to RR requests for comment. Ryken, according to minutes of a 2014 meeting, said he met with the Philadelphia Bible president, who could not give him many details about the allegations against Jones. Ryken said he learned, however, that the school did not see the incident as sexual abuse and “the circumstances regarding why (Jones) was being asked to resign would not be disclosed” to the public. In that case, Ryken said, “Tenth would likely not be firing Paul Jones.” The president of the school at the time, W. Sherrill Babb, said that Ryken’s decision shocked him, according to another document obtained by RR. He told PCA leaders he thought he had communicated that the offense was serious enough that students needed to be protected from Jones. “I used strong words,” Babb reportedly said. “I told (Ryken) that it was so severe (Jones) could no longer be on campus.” ‘Trusted an incomplete and inaccurate account’ According to his testimony to the PCA court, Ryken decided the situation could be handled with some counseling and “pastoral oversight.” He tapped two elders — university law professor David Skeel and music educator Tom Elliot — to help him. The three men, according to Ryken, listened to Jones’ account of events and asked him a few questions. They did not do any further investigation. They did not talk to the known victim, who had left school by that time. They did not talk to students who worked closely with Jones and could have known about the abuse — or been abused themselves. “We trusted Paul Jones,” Ryken told the ecclesiastical court in 2017. “We wrongly trusted an incomplete and inaccurate account.” Ryken also said he recommended Jones seek counseling but did not require it as a condition of continued employment. He recalled that he specifically recommended Diane Langberg, a Christian psychologist who specializes in religious abuse and trauma. Jones reportedly ignored Ryken’s suggestion. Philip Ryken, president of Wheaton College. (Photo courtesy of Wheaton.edu) Ryken’s small committee then reported back to the full body of elders, according to Ryken, but only to tell them no details would be forthcoming. The three men said they knew the reason Jones had resigned from the college but “they did not think that the details of his sin needed to be shared.” According to evidence submitted to the church court, the elders at Tenth did not preserve minutes of the meeting with the committee. The information was considered “off the record.” Ryken said he gave a similar statement to members of the choir. He assured them the allegations had been investigated, and Jones had the church’s support. The music minister was under pastoral care, and they did not need to know more than that. The allegations were not made public. One of Jones’ victims who later came forward said that at the time, he accepted Ryken’s interpretation of his abuse. In a letter he wrote to PCA authorities, he said he figured that what happened to him must be “no big deal.” He believed that if Jones had sinned, church leaders would have told him to repent. They didn’t. So, the music director must not have sinned. It would take another 13 years before Jones’ behavior — reportedly including throwing parties where shirtless young men reportedly gave each other massages — would push Tenth’s leaders to ask him to leave. Jones separated from the church in 2014. The leadership at the church told no one the real reason. There were several Sunday morning tributes to Jones’ great work, however. A bulletin announcing his departure reassured the congregation that Tenth had no plans to change its style of music and praised Jones for “grooming” his replacements. Seven years after that, in 2022, the church asked Godly Response to Abuse in the Christian Environment (GRACE) to investigate an elder and nursery worker who was arrested for sexual crimes against minors. The investigation uncovered several additional scandals. The final, 144-page report spent 22 pages on Jones. Ryken did not cooperate with GRACE’s investigation, according to the report. The final document identifies him only as a “former leader.” GRACE concluded that decisions Ryken and his committee made in 2001 “warranted ownership, apology, and a change in behavior.” Keeping the allegations quiet and letting Jones continue to work at Tenth “enabled Jones to perpetrate additional misconduct,” according to the investigation. The investigation found at least six additional victims that Ryken missed in 2001. ‘No questions asked’ The last thing Ryken did in 2001 was go to Jones’ house for dinner. He brought along several church leaders, and they sat down for a meal with the music director and the young men who lived, worked and studied with Jones. Two of them would later come forward as victims. Ryken asked them no questions. Phil Ryken, in front of the pulpit at Tenth Presbyterian Church in 2010. (Photo courtesy of social media) One of the victims who was present that evening told RR that he can’t forget that meal. “It was so weird,” he said. “There were no questions asked. I had to assume that Ryken was okay with what happened to me.” The man spoke to RR on the condition of anonymity. It is RR’s policy to protect the identity of abuse victims. Key details of the man’s story and biography have been confirmed with independent reporting. He told RR that he originally went to Philadelphia Bible to study with Jones. The music director was his teacher and mentor, he said, and beatings were part of the instruction. As a 19-year-old student, the man told RR, he struggled to learn a particularly complicated piece of music in the allotted time. Jones told him he’d have to be “held accountable,” he said. Then he was instructed to write down everything he was struggling with, including the “sin” of making out with his girlfriend and getting distracted from music practice. After the man did as he was told, he said, Jones punished him. “It wasn’t paddling,” the man told RR. “I took down my shorts, naked, knelt down beside the bed, and after reading every single sin, he beat me with a stick, like a stick that you’d use to hold a sliding door closed.” Jones beat him “to a pulp,” the man recalled. After the beating, he said, Jones hugged him. Then the student pulled up his underwear and shorts, cleaned himself up in the bathroom, and the two went to lunch, he said. “I couldn’t walk right for weeks,” he told RR. The man said Jones told him multiple times that he couldn’t tell anyone about the beating. They would not understand, he said, “how much I love you and how much I want to help you.” But sitting at that meal with the senior minister of Tenth, it seemed to the young man that Ryken, at least, did understand. And it seemed like he approved. “I went to a pretty, pretty bad spot after that,” the man said. “By God’s grace, I did the best I could.” Would not go public After years of therapy, the man told RR he still has trouble distinguishing between the impact of the sadistic sexual abuse, spiritual abuse, “the loyalty part” and the way Ryken helped cover it up. In 2014, with the support of PCA minister and Reformed Theological Seminary professor Jay Harvey, the man confronted Ryken. He asked Ryken, now the president of Wheaton, to explain what happened and if there had been some kind of misunderstanding. He wanted to know if Ryken would try to make things right. They met in a Marriott hotel near the Philadelphia airport. Ryken apologized for the weird dinner and seemed to express support for Jones. He even had tears in his eyes, according to a memo of the meeting that everyone present agreed was accurate. The man asked Ryken to go public and make a statement about Jones, who had continued to work in churches, write church music and perform around the country. “I stand ready to forgive you,” the man wrote in a follow-up email. “But, I do need you to acknowledge … that this was a gross lapse of judgment on your part as the Senior Pastor of Tenth Presbyterian Church.” Ryken said he would agree to talk to the current elders at the PCA church in Philadelphia and revisit the question of how the situation was handled. Phil Ryken preaches at Tenth Presbyterian Church in Philadelphia in 2024. Ryken served as the church’s senior minister from 2001 until becoming president of Wheaton College in 2010. (Screen grab) The elders at Tenth reviewed the facts in 2015 and decided to release a vague statement to the congregation. Ryken did not say anything publicly at the time and did not address the congregation. Jones’ victim said he was deeply disappointed. It felt, to him, like another cover-up. He went to the regional PCA authorities the following year, asking them to get involved. The Philadelphia-area church leaders concluded an appeal was legitimate. The silence and secrecy around the abuse had created additional problems for the congregation and the victims. “It was necessary to … set the record straight,” PCA moderator Marc Davis wrote in a statement at the time. “There has been much confusion, hearsay, and difference of opinion regarding these events, and sadly that has resulted in a compounding of the suffering of those initially abused by the music director.” Ryken read his testimony to the Philadelphia-area leaders. He said he was deceived in 2001 and should not have been so trusting. He concluded by saying, “I humbly ask forgiveness.” In 2017, the ecclesiastical court found Ryken and the elders negligent in their pastoral duties. “There is no excuse for the wounding of those who are under your care,” the court said. “Neither legal nor financial concerns should come before the care for Christ’s church.” Tenth Presbyterian Church in Philadelphia, Pa. (Courtesy Photo) The PCA issued its censure. The document went silently into a file. Ryken’s ‘trouble’ At the same time the Philadelphia-area church leaders were meeting, Ryken published a book about suffering. In the introduction to “When Trouble Comes,” he talked about a period in his own life when he was overcome by despair. He faced so much trouble, he said, he wondered whether he had the “will to live.” Ryken wrote that he couldn’t talk about all the things that weighed him down in the spring semester of 2014, but they included “attacks on my character” and “painful experiences from the past.” The abuse survivor who tried to confront Ryken that same semester was startled. Ryken, the man felt, was describing him and his plea for help as “trouble” that was so onerous it drove Ryken to contemplate suicide. “I just couldn’t believe it, you know?” the man told RR. “It was, like, stolen trauma.” The victims’ stories, including his own, had been completely erased, the man said. If Ryken learned anything about handling abuse, the 2016 book did not mention it. The censure was handed down the following year. There was no coverage in Christian media. No mention at the PCA’s annual meeting. No discussion online. According to Wheaton’s statement to RR, Ryken “stood with the elders and the victims before the Tenth congregation to ask for and receive forgiveness by apologizing publicly.” The victim who spoke to RR said this is not true. The only formal statement he is aware of is the one read before the PCA censure. It wasn’t public. Only a few members of the congregation were present. And the college president was not “with” the victims, the man said. After, it seemed to the victim like the censure never even happened. No one appeared to ask any hard questions about Ryken’s judgment, he said. No one asked what other allegations he might have helped keep quiet. No one, from what the man could tell, was calling for repentance. “I had thought, ‘If I do this right, if I bring it privately, if I handle it Christianly, if I do it in a good spirit, my fathers in the church will do what’s right,’” he said. “We don’t expect Christian leaders to be perfect. We just expect them to be repentant.” Daniel Silliman is senior reporter/editor at The Roys Report. He began his two decades in journalism covering crime in Atlanta and has since led major investigations into abuse and misconduct in Christian contexts. Daniel and his wife live in Johnson City, Tennessee. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Louisiana pastor ordered to stay away from home of man he attacked
item.author: Rebecca Hopkins
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Hopkins
item.description: A Louisiana pastor who assaulted his church’s neighbor last month has been ordered to stay at least 50 yards away from the young man’s home. Mark Anthony “Tony” Spell, 48, pastor of Life Tabernacle Church near Baton Rouge, was arrested for second-degree battery in June, Roys Report (RR) previously reported. A video of the attack […]
item.content:encoded: Video showing pastor Tony Spell beating a 20-year-old neighbor. (Screen grab) A Louisiana pastor who assaulted his church’s neighbor last month has been ordered to stay at least 50 yards away from the young man’s home. Mark Anthony “Tony” Spell, 48, pastor of Life Tabernacle Church near Baton Rouge, was arrested for second-degree battery in June, Roys Report (RR) previously reported. A video of the attack shows Spell punching his 20-year-old neighbor more than 30 times. Spell also threw the man to the ground, sat on top of him, jerked the man’s neck and twisted it, then stood up and kicked him. A protection order against Spell was issued Friday in Commissioner Kory Tauzin’s Courtroom, reported the Baton Rouge-based Advocate. Tauzin works in the 19th Judicial District Court as a quasi-judge, a person who exercises court-like powers but is not a judge. The order states that Spell must stay more than 50 yards away from the home of the family that lives across the road from the church, unless he is checking the mail. Spell must also not contact the family in person or electronically, including through social media, the Advocate reported. RR requested a copy of the protection order from the district clerk but didn’t immediately receive the document. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Pastor Tony Spell addresses his Life Tabernacle congregation following his arrest, telling members he had a “red-hot sermon ready” and casting himself as a victim of “domestic terrorism.” (Screen grab) RR also emailed Spell at his church for comment, but he didn’t immediately respond. Spell previously said the 20-year-old neighbor threatened to rape his wife and grandchildren, which the neighbor denied. The two families have been feuding since 2020, when Spell’s church defied COVID-19 lockdown orders. Scott Sherwin, father of the man Spell punched, had allowed federal authorities to install cameras on his property to surveil the church, RR previously reported. Life Tabernacle is a Oneness Pentecostal church located 14 miles northeast of Baton Rouge. The elder Sherwin called police on June 25 — two days after Spell’s arrest, according to a police bodycam video obtained by WBRZ-TV Ch. 2, an ABC affiliate in Baton Rouge. A police bodycam video shows officers confronting Tony Spell, who was mowing the church lawn near his neighbor’s home. (Screen grab) In the police video, Sherwin told police Spell was mowing the church’s lawn at 4 a.m. within 50 yards of his residence. Sherwin told police about a protection order that was in process. Sherwin also said he believed Spell was disturbing the peace and trying to “intimidate” his family by mowing his lawn so early. “He’s got a protective order and he’s doing this on purpose to keep me and my family awake and drive us insane,” Sherwin told police in the video. The video next shows police confronting Spell, who was sitting on his mower in the church’s yard. Spell then called Sherwin’s 20-year-old son an anti-gay slur, according to the TV station, which redacted it from the video. “He’s just sore because I beat the crap out of his f—-t boy,” WBRZ reported Spell saying in the video. “And he’s next if he comes over here and harasses these boys.” Some teenagers were in the video with Spell holding weed trimmers. The June incident was at least the second time Spell was arrested for assault, RR previously reported. Spell allegedly backed a bus toward a man who was protesting the church’s decision to stay open during the pandemic. This was also caught on video. Those charges were later dismissed, RR previously reported. Rebecca Hopkins is a journalist based in Colorado. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Iranian Christian refugee wins custody battle against Pastor Saeed Abedini, former political prisoner
item.author: Bob Smietana
item.dc:creator: Bob Smietana
item.description: A federal judge ruled Friday (July 10) that a 5-year-old girl must be returned to her mother, an Iranian refugee living in Turkey, ending an international custody battle with the girl’s father, Saeed Abedini, an evangelical pastor and once a religious freedom celebrity.  The child’s mother, Niloofar Aragh, filed a complaint in federal court last […]
item.content:encoded: Saeed Abedini, left, outside a courthouse in Lynchburg, Va., Friday, July 10, 2026. (Screen grab) A federal judge ruled Friday (July 10) that a 5-year-old girl must be returned to her mother, an Iranian refugee living in Turkey, ending an international custody battle with the girl’s father, Saeed Abedini, an evangelical pastor and once a religious freedom celebrity. The child’s mother, Niloofar Aragh, filed a complaint in federal court last month alleging the girl’s father had abducted her while she was on holiday with her grandparents in December 2024, and took her to the U.S. without permission. Abedini, a one-time political prisoner in Iran, told media earlier this year he had fled Turkey and taken his daughter with him because he feared for his life and thought the child was in danger. While Abedini, who lives in Virginia, admitted in court filings he had taken his daughter without permission, he argued the girl was now settled in the U.S. and that returning her to Turkey would be harmful. “Removing the child from the only home, family, community, and language she meaningfully knows, and delivering her to a country she does not remember and to a caregiver with whom she now communicates only through a translating parent, would present a grave risk of serious psychological harm to a 5-year-old whose entire conscious life has been established in the United States,” Abedini, who represented himself for part of the legal process, wrote in a court filing. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Pastor Saeed Abedini in a 2022 ministry video. (Video screen grab) Senior Judge Norman Moon of the U.S. District Court for the Western District of Virginia ruled that the 5-year-old had been removed without her mother’s permission and that Aragh had custody rights. He rejected the allegation that returning the girl to her mother would be harmful. Moon also ordered Aragh’s daughter be placed in the custody of her aunt, who also lives in the U.S., immediately. “Based on the substantial evidence introduced during today’s hearing, including respondent’s history of failure to comply with court orders and respondent’s correspondence insisting he would defy an order transferring custody, the court finds it necessary to order this custody transfer immediately,” the judge wrote. Moon’s ruling came after an all-day hearing at a courthouse in Lynchburg, where Aragh testified by video. Devon Slovensky, Aragh’s attorney, said the U.S. travel ban made it impossible for her to come in person. A native of Iran, Aragh is currently living in Turkey, where she met Abedini. The two lived together for several years but were not legally married. Instead, Abedini said he performed a religious ceremony that married them in God’s eyes, a claim he repeated in court filings. Slovensky said the ruling showed the American legal system, while flawed at times, still works. She said the odds were stacked against her client, who did not speak English, did not have much money and is not in the U.S. Niloofar Ilbaki Aragh. (Photo courtesy of Slovensky Law) “What we see here is an Iranian Christian refugee fighting to get her child back through a court system where she can’t even speak the language, and being successful,” she said. “And I think that is, like, a really awesome story about righteousness and justice.” Slovensky also said Abedini’s own social media posts undermined his arguments. While he claimed that Iran still was threatening his life, he also posted several photos of his daughter in recent weeks, trying to show her as settled in the U.S. — one of the key disputes in the custody case, which was based on the Hague Convention on the Civil Aspects of International Child Abduction. “Then the question became, well, why do you need your daughter close to you if you’re being stalked and these people are persecuting you wherever you go?” Slovensky said. Abedini told media he had trouble finding a lawyer and that terrorists had threatened him in Turkey, but his dogs had frightened the terrorists away. He has also been accused of sexual abuse, a charge he denied. He said God told him to flee the country, but the court in Virginia did not listen to his concerns. “I believe I have been set up by the court,” he said. He also fears he will never see his daughter again. During an interview, he alleged that Iran and Turkey were conspiring against him and that he wanted to save his daughter. He also claimed that Turkish officials were after him because he supported President Donald Trump and the nation of Israel. “I was the first pastor who made Trump president with prophecies,” he said. “If you don’tbelieve it, go look at my name, the prophecies.” The ruling is the latest twist in the story of Abedini, a pastor who was jailed in Iran from 2012 to 2016 for his work with Christians there. His case gained international attention through a “Save Saeed” social media campaign. A few months before he was released, Adedini’s then-wife, Naghmeh Panahi, who had been a leader in the campaign to free her husband, alleged Abedini had been abusive during their marriage. The two eventually divorced, and Panahi filed for an order of protection against him. In 2018, Abedini, who had pled guilty to domestic abuse in 2007, was arrested for violating that order. Panahi told media that her marriage had been troubled before Abedini was jailed in Iran but that his experience in jail harmed him. She also said becoming a celebrity for religious freedom had changed him. “Fame poisoned our family,” she told media. Panahi helped raise about $14,000 to help Aragh with her legal expenses and with the cost of returning her daughter to Turkey. Panahi said she had become concerned about the girl’s welfare about a year ago, and that caused her to reach out to Aragh. She was criticized a decade ago for talking about the troubles in her marriage and said it was hard to get people to believe her. Though it was hard, she said it was the right thing to do. And she felt God called her to help Aragh. “It is just such a miracle,” she said. Bob Smietana is a national reporter for Religion News Service. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Pastor Greg Locke says federal authorities are investigating his finances, following raid of home
item.author: Mark A. Kellner
item.dc:creator: Mark A. Kellner
