item.description: Pastor Greg Locke told his Tennessee congregation Sunday that federal authorities are investigating his financial records, after conducting an early morning search of his home and media business several weeks ago. The pastor noted for his provocative messages and public actions defying COVID-19 lockdowns did not say what day the raid occurred. But Locke did […]
item.content:encoded: Pastor Greg Locke tells his Global Vision Bible Church congregation that federal authorities raided his home and are investigating his church's finances. (Screen grab) Pastor Greg Locke told his Tennessee congregation Sunday that federal authorities are investigating his financial records, after conducting an early morning search of his home and media business several weeks ago. The pastor noted for his provocative messages and public actions defying COVID-19 lockdowns did not say what day the raid occurred. But Locke did say it took place while he had stepped back from preaching following the May 8 drug overdose death of his son Evan Roberts Locke. Speaking during a church service following a six-week sabbatical, Locke choked up while describing how 50 to 60 federal agents raided his home around 6 a.m. They forced entry into his home with a battering ram and executed search warrants for his residence and Locke Media, he said. Locke added that investigators also served a subpoena for Global Vision Bible Church, his congregation. In an email to the Roys Report (RR), Capt. Sam Moore of the Wilson County Sheriff’s Office confirmed his officers assisted the Federal Bureau of Investigation and the Internal Revenue Service with the raid. They did so only because federal officials asked “due to the residence being located in our jurisdiction,” he said. Locke said no arrest warrant was issued. Instead, he said, investigators told him they were examining allegations that church funds had been misappropriated. “They pulled me aside and said, ‘We’re here because of an overabundance — the last couple of years — of allegations online that you have misappropriated church funds,’” Locke said. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Pastor Greg Locke (center) ordained Justin and Kasey Greenwell as pastors at Global Vision Bible Church in Mt. Juliet, Tennessee in 2023. (RR Graphic/Facebook) The allusion to “allegations online” appears to refer to accusations made by former Global Vision Bible Church online pastors Justin and Kasey Greenwell, who claim Locke has mismanaged church finances. They also published a book on the alleged mismanagement, “Pulpit of Leaves: Deception in Plain Sight.” Locke claims the book has been withdrawn, but the title is still listed at Amazon.com, with the notation “Out of Print — Limited Availability.” As reported previously by RR, some, in addition to the Greenwells, have claimed Locke used money from his church’s building fund to buy a $1.6 million home in 2024. According to Wilson County property records, the home is not owned by Locke but by Global Vision Bible Church. Locke has vehemently denied these allegations. Kasey Greenwell also alleged that the church has received loans from individuals, but that Locke handled the loans and refused to reveal details to staff. On Sunday, Locke said investigators searched his property for three hours. They seized phones, computers and other electronic devices, and collected only “a few bank statements.” “They went through the pages of our library books, every drawer, every cabinet, every bed, every attic, every space, every chicken coop, every shed on our property,” he said. Locke attributed the investigation to “whistleblowers” and online critics, including a Facebook page and a book that he said had promoted allegations against him. He said he would not grant interviews or comment further on social media. “This is the only time that I will publicly talk about it,” Locke said. “I will not give any interviews. I will not respond to any news media. I will not post anything on social media because I’m not in fight mode, nor am I in flight mode. I’m in faith mode.” RR reached the FBI’s National Press Office via email for comment. It replied, “The FBI has no comment.” The IRS did not immediately respond to a comment request from RR. Locke told the congregation that he and his attorneys have cooperated with requests for records dating to 2020. “To God be the glory, the attorney general has given us extra time to be able to solidify all the paperwork that we need,” he said. “We’re talking about tens of thousands of documents.” Locke said investigators subpoenaed donors, missionaries and other individuals connected with the church. “We have nothing to hide,” he said. “The more they dig, the more they begin to see things.” The investigation produced financial consequences beyond the searches, Locke said. He said banks closed Global Vision Bible Church accounts and his personal accounts after receiving subpoenas, leaving the ministry temporarily unable to process donations or pay expenses through normal channels. “We have operated this church for two weeks with no income and no outflow,” he said. Worship service at Global Vision Bible Church in Juliet, Tennessee. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) Church leaders had continued serving without pay while the ministry sought a new banking relationship, Locke told the congregation. Online donations were being temporarily held by the church’s payment processor until replacement accounts could be established, he added. Despite the investigation, Locke told congregants he expected the matter to end favorably. “We’re praying for a swift restoration,” he said. “The books will just be closed, exoneration.” Locke said he believed the investigation would ultimately demonstrate that church finances had been handled properly. He urged members to remain unified, continue worshipping and support the church financially while the inquiry continues. Mark A. Kellner is a reporter based in Mesquite, Nevada. He most recently covered statewide elections for the New York Post and was for three years the Faith & Family Reporter for The Washington Times. Mark is a graduate of the University of the Cumberlands and also attended Boston University’s College of Communication. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Texas pastor targeted church members in schemes worth $3.2 million, FBI alleges
item.author: Rebecca Hopkins
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Hopkins
item.description: A Fort Worth, Texas, pastor has been charged with allegedly defrauding more than 50 investors of $3.2 million with schemes to renovate churches, host concerts and fund trips to Africa. Richard Reinaldo Garcia, 53, senior pastor of Gracia Church, targeted church members and their family and friends, the FBI said in a June 23 press […]
item.content:encoded: Richard Reinaldo Garcia, senior pastor of Gracia Church in Fort Worth, Texas, is charged with defrauding more than 50 investors of $3.2 million. A Fort Worth, Texas, pastor has been charged with allegedly defrauding more than 50 investors of $3.2 million with schemes to renovate churches, host concerts and fund trips to Africa. Richard Reinaldo Garcia, 53, senior pastor of Gracia Church, targeted church members and their family and friends, the FBI said in a June 23 press release. Garcia used the money for personal gain, according to an indictment filed in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Texas. “The defendant allegedly took advantage of his victims’ faith by promising significant profits through investment opportunities,” said U.S. Attorney Ryan Raybould in a statement published by NBC-TV Ch. 5 in Fort Worth. Instead, Raybould said Garcia “used their funds to selfishly enrich himself at their expense.” A federal grand jury indicted Garcia on one count each of conspiracy to commit wire fraud and aggravated identity theft. Garcia was also indicted for two counts of wire fraud, according to court records. Garcia allegedly committed the crimes from 2021 to 2026 with victims throughout the United States and in other countries, the indictment states. He used multiple ministry names for his schemes, including Ministerio Gracia, Iglesia Gracia de Texas, Gracia Church of Texas, A Pesar de Todo LLC, and MG Kingdom, Inc., the indictment states. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Richard Garcia preaches at Gracia Church in Fort Worth, Texas. (Screen grab) In some cases, he worked with an unnamed co-conspirator, which the indictment refers to as “Co-Conspirator A.” “Specifically, defendant Garcia and Co-Conspirator A would induce others to enter into contracts through false pretenses promising significant returns,” the indictment states. An FBI agent arrested Garcia on May 2 in the Dallas/Fort Worth International Airport, his arrest record shows. The grand jury issued the indictment on June 2, and Garcia entered a not guilty plea June 9. His trial is set for Oct. 13, court records show. Roys Report (RR) emailed Garcia’s attorneys, William Biggs and Matthew Smid, seeking comment, but they didn’t immediately respond. The schemes One of the schemes Garcia and his unnamed co-conspirator allegedly engaged in was to convince investors they would renovate church buildings and then re-sell them at a profit, the indictment states. But instead of undertaking a church-flipping operation, Garcia and his co-conspirator reportedly used the money for business operating costs, unrelated business expenses and their own personal expenses. One investor gave Garcia and his co-conspirator $50,000 to renovate a church in Arlington, Texas, the indictment said. Garcia told the investor the project had produced a 55% profit, which he said he used to invest in a new project. This wasn’t true, the indictment states. Another investor transferred $125,000 to Garcia and his co-conspirator to renovate a church in Texarkana, Texas. In another example, an investor gave $168,000 for a church renovation in Lubbock, Texas. In both cases, no church renovation happened, the indictment states. In another scheme, Garcia and his co-conspirator convinced investors to invest in joint ventures to put on Christian concerts in foreign countries. They promised to share profits from tickets sales with investors. But only one concert took place, and Garcia never paid back investors, according to a criminal complaint filed on April 9 by the FBI agent investigating the case. In one example, an investor gave $40,000 for a concert in the Dominican Republic but never received a return on the investment, the complaint states. In another example, an investor gave $100,000 to secure a stadium in Colombia for a concert that never happened. And Garcia didn’t return the funds. Garcia and the co-conspirator “instead intended to use the funds provided by victims to pay for Relevant Entity operating expenses, unrelated business expenses, and their own personal expenses,” the indictment states. Garcia also directed investors to send their investments as donations to a church he was associated with, the indictment states. He disguised “funds intended to fund a business venture and to earn a profit as funds intended as a charitable donation,” the indictment explains. Investors told investigators they believe Garcia also wanted the donations to bolster the church’s revenue to help secure future loans, according to the complaint. Richard Garcia preaches at Gracia Church in Fort Worth, Texas. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) Garcia also sold church-related trips to Kenya for $2,000 per person, which Garcia either canceled or never booked, the indictment said. At times, Garcia made “lulling payments” to investors, the indictment states, which were small payments made to ease investors’ minds. In his schemes, Garcia used wire transfers across state lines, leading to federal charges of wire fraud. Garcia also used someone else’s name and address to create false documentation for the Texas secretary of state. This led to the identity theft charge. Each wire fraud charge carries a potential sentence of 20 years in prison. The aggravated identity theft charge carries a two-year prison sentence upon conviction. Rebecca Hopkins is a journalist based in Colorado. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Louisiana youth pastor charged with 90 counts of molesting children
item.author: Ann Marie Shambaugh
item.dc:creator: Ann Marie Shambaugh
item.description: A Louisiana youth pastor faces 90 counts of child molestation, stemming from allegations he abused four children more than 30 years ago. Detectives from the Bossier Parish Sheriff’s Office drove more than 300 miles across Louisiana to arrest David Mercer, 72, without incident July 10 in Folsom, where he resides. Folsom lies approximately 50 miles […]
item.content:encoded: David Mercer, a Louisiana youth pastor, faces 90 counts of molestation over allegations he abused four children while working at a church in the 1990s. (Photo courtesy of Bossier Parish Sheriff's Office) A Louisiana youth pastor faces 90 counts of child molestation, stemming from allegations he abused four children more than 30 years ago. Detectives from the Bossier Parish Sheriff’s Office drove more than 300 miles across Louisiana to arrest David Mercer, 72, without incident July 10 in Folsom, where he resides. Folsom lies approximately 50 miles north of New Orleans. Investigators, who conducted a search of Mercer’s residence, expect other victims will come forward and additional charges will be filed. Mercer was booked July 11 into the Bossier Parish Maximum Security Facility, with bond set at $2.5 million. Prosecutors charged Mercer with 90 counts of “forcible fondling molestation of a juvenile.” The crime is defined as someone 17 or older using force, violence, duress or other means to extract sexual pleasure from someone at least two years younger. Detectives began investigating Mercer on June 29, after learning of allegations that he had sexually abused four victims between 1989 and 1995. At the time, Mercer worked as a youth minister at Cypress Baptist Church in Benton, which is in Bossier Parish. He resigned in 1995. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Worship service at Cypress Baptist Church in Benton, Louisiana, where David Mercer served as a youth minister until 1995 — the period during which he is now accused of abusing four children. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) “Although nearly 40 years have passed, the passage of time does not lessen the seriousness of these crimes or the impact they have had on those affected,” Bossier Parish Sheriff Julian Whittington stated. “I want to encourage anyone who may have been a victim or witnessed these actions to come forward and contact the Bossier Parish Sheriff’s Office. We are committed to thoroughly investigating these allegations and seeking justice for those who may have been impacted.” At the time of his arrest, Mercer worked as a youth pastor at First Baptist Church of Folsom. Will Robbins, pastor of First Baptist Church of Folsom, posted a statement about the incident July 12 on Facebook, calling Mercer’s arrest “a total surprise.” “We care about kids, youth, adults, and families,” the statement reads. “Our hearts are broken by those affected by this situation. While we have background checks and child safety training for all our workers, we are currently reviewing our hiring and child protection policies.” Robbins stated that he is not aware of any allegations that Mercer committed a crime while employed at the Folsom church. Mercer began working there in 2023. First Baptist Church of Folsom, Louisiana, where David Mercer worked as a youth pastor until his July arrest. (Photo courtesy of Google Maps) Roys Report (RR) contacted Cypress Baptist Church in Bossier Parish for comment but did not receive a response before time of publication. In a statement reported by KTBS Ch. 3, an ABC affiliate in Shreveport, church leaders said they are cooperating with law enforcement. “Cypress is committed to helping with the justice process however we can,” the statement reads. “Our deepest prayer is for the victims of this terrible crime.” Mercer also worked as a youth pastor at 5th Street Baptist Church in Hannibal, Missouri, between 1998 and 2000, according to Muddy River News, a website based in Quincy, Illinois. Pastor Tom Carpenter of 5th Street Baptist Church told the news outlet that he isn’t aware of any allegations against Mercer during his time there. “We’re going to be looking at our church records to see if there were any issues,” Carpenter said. “What we do know is that there were no accusations, according to our church clerk.” First Baptist Church of Folsom is offering to connect those harmed by Mercer with a licensed professional counselor. The church also urges anyone with information about Mercer to contact the St. Tammany Parish Sheriff’s Office at 985-726-7854. Ann Marie Shambaugh has reported as a print journalist in multiple states, including currently in Carmel, Indiana. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Australian Baptist School under scrutiny for taking public money, while not paying staff
item.author: Jessica Morris
item.dc:creator: Jessica Morris
item.description: Last year, leaders of Redeemer Baptist School in Sydney, Australia, received nearly $6 million ($8.6 million AUD) in government grants to educate students. Now, they face allegations of defrauding volunteer staff by appealing to their religious devotion. In addition to recent investigations by the media, state and federal authorities are investigating possible abuses by the […]
item.content:encoded: Redeemer Baptist School in Sydney, which faces allegations of exploiting unpaid staff and students as laborers. (Photo courtesy of redeemer.nsw.edu.au) Last year, leaders of Redeemer Baptist School in Sydney, Australia, received nearly $6 million ($8.6 million AUD) in government grants to educate students. Now, they face allegations of defrauding volunteer staff by appealing to their religious devotion. In addition to recent investigations by the media, state and federal authorities are investigating possible abuses by the school. A joint investigation released by the Sydney Morning Herald (SMH), The Age and 60 Minutes Australia revealed that workers at Redeemer Baptist School are barely receiving enough money to survive. Meanwhile, the church is profiting and building a $60-million-dollar, authoritarian empire built on the name of Jesus Christ. At the top of the system are Jonathan Cannon and Russell Bailey, leaders of the school and elders at Redeemer Baptist Church. Redeemer Baptist Church controls the elite private school, set in Sydney’s western suburbs. The school earned $74 million between 2015 and 2024, according to Clinton Free, an accounting professor at the University of Sydney. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Russell Bailey (left) and Jonathan Cannon (right), leaders of Redeemer Baptist School and elders at Redeemer Baptist Church, outside Reid Home. (Photo courtesy of LinkedIn) Forty-eight percent of the school’s revenue in the most recent reporting period came from government payments, according to the Australian entity that regulates charities. In Australia, the government partially funds public and private schools through needs-based allocations, based on a universal right to education. The rest of the school’s revenue came primarily from parents of students. But the government is also doling out welfare funds to the school’s staff, who receive no wages for their work. According to the Australian Charities and Not-for-Profits Commission, Redeemer Baptist School declared zero employee expenses on its latest report. One hundred individuals who work at the school receive a stipend of approximately $1,390 a month from the church. But the NSW Supreme Court ruled in 2006 that the stipend was not a wage or salary and was unrelated to volunteer activities. The workers’ stipend places them below the poverty line for a single income household in Australia. By contrast, the average pay for a first-year teacher in Australia is approximately $4,570 per month before tax, according to Open Universities Australia. School workers live on properties surrounding the school and church. They share accommodation with members of the church community, with arrangements often dictated by elders. Some of their expenses, such as gas and rent, are covered in return for their service. These costs, listed under “other” on the school’s 2025 financial report, total just over $10.1 million. In a statement, the school insists its workers are volunteers who work “in response to a calling from Jesus Christ.” Some of Redeemer’s workers receive payments from the public welfare system, according to various media reports. The school says it does not rely on welfare benefits, but individuals may apply “of their own volition.” Staff members have no school-sponsored retirement plan, and “there’s no personal investment for your future,” a former elder told The Age. “You are held captive by their financial system and the older you get, the worse it becomes. The members can’t survive without the church.” Follow the money Approximately 600 students pay the school more than $7,000 a year in tuition. That money, along with government grants and donations, funds an expansive internal network, encompassing both school and church, worth $60 million after expenses. It includes 37 properties worth more than $46.5 million. There is an asset and property management business, a community aid business, and a commercial and operational arm. The latter covers 10 operations, including a bookstore, a press house, Castle Hill House — the location of a vocational educational program — maintenance and excursions. In many cases, former members and staff said, students did maintenance or construction work to reduce the church’s expenses. Castle Hill House, the site of a vocational education program run by Redeemer Baptist School in Sydney’s western suburbs. (Photo courtesy of redeemer.nsw.edu.au) A student’s unpaid labor may continue beyond graduation. New alumni are encouraged to stay for “Year 13” of additional work. “It’s non-stop work, and honestly, this is my opinion, it’s so that you don’t have time to think,” Michael Jones, a former student, told SMH. “You don’t have time to realize what’s going on, why they’re controlling all this sort of psychological, social pressure to follow.” Females complete tasks like cleaning the bathroom and kitchen, former students told the publication. Males spent time raking leaves. Over some weekends, students take part in “work parties” involving building and ground maintenance. They begin in the morning and finish in the early afternoon. Once they finish their work on the school grounds, they allegedly work on the elders’ houses. “We had no cleaners as such. We were the cleaners,” a Redeemer graduate of 2022 told SMH. Redeemer Baptist School’s 2025 financial report says that almost 34% of its annual revenue was spent on repairs and maintenance. Redeemer Baptist School controls a network worth about $60 million, including 37 properties, yet pays its workers a stipend of roughly $1,390 a month, well below Australia’s poverty line of $2,530 a month. (RR Graphic) One student, who asked to remain anonymous, told SMH he felt like an “indentured servant.” He said he was made to get up at 6 a.m. for devotions and did manual labor until 4 p.m. His tasks included carpentry and cleaning the trash cans before he left the school in 2015. “You get home at the end of the day with nothing. It was very soul-destroying,” he told SMH. Redeemer Baptist School has denied all claims against it. A long statement from the school reads, in part, “Just because our practices are unfamiliar it does not mean they are unlawful, coercive or abusive and we reject the attempt to portray us as anything other than a law-abiding community with strong Christian faith and practices.” The work students do, school leaders said, is part of their learning responsibility and practical skills. The conservative school also said it meets all federal and state requirements for schools and charities. History repeats itself The recent media investigation is not the first time Redeemer Baptist School has come under scrutiny for its financial practices. For decades, people have spoken up internally, to the media and to state and federal authorities. In 2004, a group of teachers unsuccessfully tried to claim that Redeemer Baptist School owed them $6 million in unpaid wages. Their effort was led by accountant Graeme Glossop, whose daughter attended the school. It culminated in protests outside the school. School leaders attempted to place a restraining order against Glossop to keep him off their property. They did not succeed, and they sued Glossop for defamation, arguing its staffers were volunteers. Glossop claimed, “The teachers and staff had been working for reduced wages, in most circumstances, 20% of the award (wage).” However, the court ruled for the school, finding that the teachers were not employees and did not have any contractual rights. This ruling is the legal foundation of the school’s financial structure. The Rev. Fred Nile, a longtime member of the New South Wales Parliament, defended Redeemer Baptist School in a 2004 speech, calling it “one of the finest schools in the country.” (Photo courtesy of Facebook) Some government officials have been powerful allies for the school and church. The Rev. Fred Nile and David Clarke, two such defenders, served a combined 60 years in the New South Wales Parliament, the state legislature governing Sydney. When the teachers sued in 2004, Nile responded with a speech to the state Parliament, labeling the teachers “disgruntled former members.” He also referred to Redeemer Baptist as “one of the finest schools in the country.” In 2007, John Kaye, a member of the liberal leaning Greens party, invoked parliamentary privilege to ask for a state and federal investigation into tax and welfare fraud by Redeemer Baptist School. This never happened after the court ruled staffers were volunteers. In a statement to court the next year, Bailey, who was then a bursar at the school, called Kaye’s claims “rubbish.” “The school has undergone extensive investigations from a number of government bodies as a result of false allegations made by various people, including Mr. Kaye,” he said. “Those investigations have found that none of the allegations can be substantiated, and that is simply because they are not true.” The latest allegations have drawn the attention of state officials. A spokesperson for the New South Wales Education Standards Authority said the office is “investigating matters raised relating to teacher accreditation, school registration compliance and funding obligations.” Federal authorities have stepped in too. Assistant Minister for Charities Andrew Leigh has referred the investigation to the Australian Charities and Not-for-profits Commission (ACNC). Federal Education Minister Jason Clare called the allegations “very serious” and declared that the Department of Education is investigating the school’s finances. Jessica Morris is a music journalist, podcaster and author based in Melbourne, Australia. SHARE THIS:

item.title: ‘Extra layer of betrayal’: Buffalo megachurch pastor who advocated for victims pleads guilty to rape and abuse of girls
item.author: Rebecca Hopkins
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Hopkins
item.description: A megachurch pastor from Buffalo, New York — once a trusted advocate for sexual abuse victims — pleaded guilty today to two felony charges of sexually abusing two girls. John A. Camardo III, 47, executive pastor of The Chapel, a multi-site megachurch, pleaded guilty to one count of rape in the second degree, the Erie […]
item.content:encoded: John Camardo III, executive pastor of The Chapel near Buffalo, New York, pleaded guilty to two felonies for sexually abusing two girls. (Photo courtesy of Eric County District Attorney) A megachurch pastor from Buffalo, New York — once a trusted advocate for sexual abuse victims — pleaded guilty today to two felony charges of sexually abusing two girls. John A. Camardo III, 47, executive pastor of The Chapel, a multi-site megachurch, pleaded guilty to one count of rape in the second degree, the Erie County District Attorney’s Office announced online. He also pleaded guilty to one count of course of sexual conduct against a child in the second degree, District Attorney Michael J. Keane stated. Camardo admitted to raping a girl under 15 in 2024 and sexually abusing a child younger than 13 years old in 2025 in Amherst, New York, Keane said. Camardo knew both victims, the district attorney added. Camardo was also a girls’ travel soccer coach for the Amherst Soccer Association, MSN reported. The Chapel, which has four locations near Buffalo, also announced today that Camardo has resigned from his position at the church. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. The Chapel’s Crosspoint campus near Buffalo, New York. (Photo courtesy of Google Maps) “Of first priority, our hearts are burdened and grieved for the pain and devastation endured by those abused and the reporting party,” Jerry Gillis, The Chapel’s lead pastor, said in a statement issued on behalf of the church’s leadership team. “May God grant grace to heal.” Roys Report (RR) called Barry Covert, Camardo’s defense attorney, seeking comment, but he did not immediately respond. The Chapel also said it confirmed with the Erie County district attorney that the allegations are not connected to its operations. “(T)hey confirmed to us that we have no basis for concern regarding his conduct with minors through any ministry or venue of The Chapel at this time,” The Chapel said. ‘An extra layer of betrayal’ Victims of a separate sex abuse case connected to The Chapel are grieving for Camardo’s victims, said Elle Campbell, former Chapel youth pastor and victim advocate. “This is chiefly a betrayal of, and disregard for, the kids he abused and whatever trust they placed in him as an authority figure and trusted adult,” Campbell told the Roys Report (RR). Elle Campbell, a former youth pastor at The Chapel and a victim advocate, called Camardo’s crimes “an extra layer of betrayal” given his prior role supporting abuse survivors. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) Camardo was an advocate for alleged sex abuse victims of former Chapel youth pastor Wayne Aarum, RR previously reported. Aarum’s victims are now reeling from compounded trauma. “Because (John) served as an advocate, it is like an extra layer of betrayal,” Campbell told RR. “In abuse cases, there are always ripples.” Campbell, who was also an advocate for Aarum’s victims, also worked for Camardo for four years. She said she saw no warning signs with him. But Campbell, who grew up in The Chapel, has been concerned about how the church, and especially evangelical ministries, handle abuse. “When I heard what happened, I was surprised, but not disbelieving or shocked,” she said. “I’ve seen enough that it doesn’t shock me anymore when abuse is revealed.” Campbell said she worries that predators find churches an easy place to abuse and get away with it. “It’s an easy place to get access to kids,” she said. “Often times there is theology in place that, I would say, makes certain groups of people, especially women and children, more vulnerable to abuse and makes it easier for an abusive man to feel like he can get away with it.” Wayne Aarum, a former youth pastor at The Chapel, was accused by 16 women of sexually abusing them as teens. (Screen grab) Gillis said in The Chapel’s statement today that when the church first learned of the allegations, it placed Camardo on administrative leave and contacted Amherst police. This morning, Camardo entered his guilty plea before State Supreme Court Justice Deborah Haendiges. Camardo faces between six and eight years in prison, depending on the outcome of the sentencing hearing on Sept. 10, according to the district attorney. Camardo is being held without bail. Authorities issued temporary orders of protection for the victims, the district attorney stated. The Chapel will continue to help Camardo family members “materially and spiritually,” including setting up a meal chain, the church said in its statement. ‘John acted as a safe place’ In 2021, RR reported that 16 women claimed Wayne Aarum sexually abused them as teens. USA Today published an investigation about these allegations, which dated back to 1997. USA Today also reported that people made 20 attempts to confront Aarum and leaders at The Chapel, as well as Circle C Ranch, where Aarum worked. In 2020, The Chapel hired MinistrySafe to investigate the allegations against Aarum. The safeguarding ministry found a pattern of Aarum manipulating teenage girls, USA Today reported. Aarum denied the allegations. Aarum still seems to be associated with a Christian youth camp in western New York. His own ministry website says Aarum serves on the board of Living Waters Ministries, the parent organization of Circle C Ranch. Circle C Ranch, a western New York youth camp where former Chapel youth pastor Wayne Aarum worked and where he now serves on the board of its parent ministry. (Photo courtesy of Google Maps) Circle C Ranch doesn’t list names of staff members on its website. But Living Waters Ministries lists Aarum as a board president on 2024 tax form 990, the most recently available filing. Aarum’s continued work in ministry, despite the allegations, has been frustrating, victim Jennifer Adema told RR today. Today’s news of Camardo’s abuses adds to the pain, she said. “This stuff is absolute church hurt,” she added. “It just sucks. It really sucks.” In its statement, The Chapel recognized the pain that Aarum’s victims must feel now knowing Camardo’s crimes. “We are sorry to all those who had John as a point of contact for sensitive or painful issues that we had to address in the past as a church,” wrote Gillis in The Chapel’s statement. “We are angry and disappointed you are in that position. We can only hope you might eventually see beyond it to a church that was committed to standing with you, but we are so sorry for the hurt this brings you.” Adema told RR that she was likely one of Aarum’s first victims. Aarum groomed her in the 1990s, she said, when she was 14 to 18 years old. In 2021, she said, The Chapel reached out to her to ask her to participate in its investigation. Camardo, specifically, asked her to share her story. She did. “John, to me, acted as a safe space and he really seemed to act as an advocate for me and the other victims,” Adema told RR. “He said … he was proud of the words I shared and the story I shared.” Camardo was even sued for his advocacy of victims. In 2021, Aarum sued The Chapel and Camardo for defamation. Aarum cited Camardo’s “intensive campaign against his reputation as he sought testimonies from women.” A judge dismissed the suit the following year. The hope in exposure Campbell, the former youth pastor, said she believes abuse is “rampant” in Christian ministries. News of abuse is more common, she believes, because victims are gaining courage to come forward. “The fact that abuse is being exposed is a good thing,” she said. “And the fact that (Camardo) was arrested is a great thing.” “When people discover a trusted spiritual leader abused someone, it can be shocking and devastating, she said. But victims are relieved when the news comes out”, she said. “To the victim, this is a very good day,” she said. In Camardo’s case, the process worked, she said. She added, “It means … that people are being brought to justice in ways that maybe they weren’t before.” Rebecca Hopkins is a journalist based in Colorado. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Calvary Spokane pastor resigns amid abuse allegations
item.author: Rebecca Hopkins
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Hopkins
item.description: Pastor Ben Ortize, who has faced allegations of abusive leadership while heading up ministries in three states, has resigned his position from Calvary Spokane, where his father Ken Ortize is the senior pastor. The elder Ortize announced his son’s resignation during yesterday’s worship service at Calvary Spokane. Calvary Spokane is still listed as a member […]
item.content:encoded: Ben Ortize preached and led human resources at Calvary Spokane after his father brought him on last summer. He stepped down Sunday amid sexual harassment allegations. (RR Graphic) Pastor Ben Ortize, who has faced allegations of abusive leadership while heading up ministries in three states, has resigned his position from Calvary Spokane, where his father Ken Ortize is the senior pastor. The elder Ortize announced his son’s resignation during yesterday’s worship service at Calvary Spokane. Calvary Spokane is still listed as a member of the Calvary Chapel Association with 1,800 churches around the world. But its statement of faith says it is not legally associated with any denomination or fellowship of churches. “Over the last few months, obviously, we have gone through a lot of difficult trauma,” Ken Ortize said. “As we’ve really prayed about it, last week, Ben decided he would resign from the staff in order to take some of the pressure off.” Ken Ortize has been accused of covering up decades of Ben’s alleged abuse at Calvary Spokane, Grace Sandpoint in Idaho and a Bible school in Montana, Roys Report (RR) previously reported. Last summer, Ken hired Ben to return to Calvary Spokane to direct its human resources department and to preach. By December 2025, however, Ben was accused of sexual harassment, as RR previously reported. Ben also recently lost tax-exempt status on his $1.2 million Idaho parsonage, RR reported two weeks ago. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Ken Ortize, senior pastor of Calvary Spokane, announces the resignation of his son, Pastor Ben Ortize, during a Sunday worship service. (Screen grab) Ken, when referencing his handling of allegations against his son, has compared himself and his staff to biblical figures who were falsely accused, RR previously reported. On Sunday, Ken didn’t name any wrongdoing Ben may have committed. “(Ben) decided that it would be in the best interest of the church for him to just go do his calling out of elsewhere,” Ken said. Ken said that he and his church have been grieving. “We’ve all been, in quite honestly, in a deep sense of loss and grief and sadness, as I know many of you have as well,” he said. “So, I just ask you to pray for (Ben) and his family, his kids, that God would just take him on the next chapter.” The elder Ortize then encouraged people to follow Ben’s online ministry. Ben didn’t appear on stage during his father’s announcement. RR emailed Ben, seeking his comment on his resignation, but he didn’t immediately respond. Ben still preaches at Grace Sandpoint, an online ministry named for the brick-and-mortar Grace Sandpoint church in Sandpoint, Idaho, which closed last year. Ben Ortize delivers an online sermon for Grace Sandpoint, the internet ministry he continues to lead. (Screen grab) Ben’s latest online sermon, posted last night, did not mention his resignation. While speaking on 2 Timothy 4:9-16, he said God redeems the acts of “wicked people” for righteous purposes. Ben referenced people who “strongly opposed” him several years ago. He also told a story of a friend who confronted those people for their “evil” behavior. Ben also mentioned that some friends have offered to assault others on his behalf. “I have literally had people approach me and said, ‘If you want me to, just tell me which body part you want me to hurt on them and they’ll never track it to you,’” Ben said Sunday. “But I told them, ‘No, don’t do it.’” Rebecca Hopkins is a journalist based in Colorado. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Report Confirms Fulani Militias Responsible for Majority of Deaths in Nigeria
item.author: ICC Staff
item.dc:creator: ICC Staff
item.description: According to a six-year study conducted by the Observatory for Religious Freedom in Africa (ORFA), Islamic Fulani militias are by far the deadliest persecutors of Christians in Nigeria. The network of militias is responsible for four times more civilian deaths than the two prominent Islamic terror organizations, Boko Haram and Islamic State West Africa Province (ISWAP), combined.  The study’s […]
item.content:encoded: A new study identifies Fulani militias as the deadliest persecutors of Christians in Nigeria. (Photo courtesy of ICC) According to a six-year study conducted by the Observatory for Religious Freedom in Africa (ORFA), Islamic Fulani militias are by far the deadliest persecutors of Christians in Nigeria. The network of militias is responsible for four times more civilian deaths than the two prominent Islamic terror organizations, Boko Haram and Islamic State West Africa Province (ISWAP), combined. The study’s findings challenge the commonly held belief that the two organized terror groups kill the larger number of Christians, highlighting the Fulani network’s expanded efforts to target Christians. “Violence linked to Fulani militia is the dominant force behind Nigeria’s death toll. The Western preoccupation with Boko Haram is, at best, misleading,” Frans Vierhout, a senior research analyst at ORFA, told TruthNigeria. The report shows that terror-related violence killed 79,323 people from 2019 to September 2025. This equates to 36 people a day. More than 42,000 of those killed were civilians. Islamic Fulani accounted for 44% of the deaths — far more than the 12% that Boko Haram and ISWAP killed together. While nominal Muslims are often targeted and killed in these attacks as well, the study found that Christians were killed at a rate 4.4 times that of Muslims. The study also examined the abduction of Christians in Nigeria. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Security personnel in Plateau State, Nigeria, took the bodies of murdered Christians away for their investigations under protest from community leaders. (Photo: CSW) Further Action Needed Judd Saul, founder and president of Equipping the Persecuted, which focuses on helping persecuted Christians in Nigeria, applauded the report in a recent email to supporters, saying it validates what their workers have witnessed for years. His organization recently issued a terror alert after at least 150 Christians were massacred throughout the Middle Belt region. “While President Trump has rightly condemned terrorism in Nigeria and supported military operations against ISIS-linked terrorists, those actions have not addressed what we believe is now the primary threat facing Christian communities throughout Nigeria’s Middle Belt,” Saul said. “As of today, the Trump Administration has taken no direct action to stop the Islamist Fulani Ethnic Militia, the group responsible for the overwhelming majority of attacks against Christian communities in Benue, Plateau, and Kaduna states.” International Christian Concern (ICC)’s Nigeria-based reporter has documented Fulani extremist attacks for years. In November 2025, ICC cited ORFA’s previous research on Fulani extremism in an article calling for the U.S. to designate the Fulani militias as an Entity of Particular Concern (EPC), following the designation of Nigeria as a Country of Particular Concern (CPC). ICC’s recently released Global Persecution Index (GPI) offers an in-depth overview of Christian persecution in Nigeria and 20 other countries. It can be downloaded for free. This article originally appeared at persecution.org and has been reprinted with permission. Founded in 1995, International Christian Concern (ICC) is a Christian organization. It’s an ecumenical, non-governmental, and non-partisan group focused on protecting the human rights of Christians and religious minorities. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Probe ends of Methodist pastor who worked for Epstein
item.author: Heather Hahn
item.dc:creator: Heather Hahn
item.description: An ordained United Methodist elder who briefly worked for Jeffrey Epstein is no longer under suspension but will not be able to serve as pastor of a United Methodist congregation for the time being. The Rev. Stephanie Remington is not accused of any crime. She faced a complaint under church law that she had not […]
item.content:encoded: Stephanie Remington, a former United Methodist pastor, was suspended after it was revealed she worked as an assistant and property manager for Jeffrey Epstein. (Photo courtesy of social media) An ordained United Methodist elder who briefly worked for Jeffrey Epstein is no longer under suspension but will not be able to serve as pastor of a United Methodist congregation for the time being. The Rev. Stephanie Remington is not accused of any crime. She faced a complaint under church law that she had not kept her home Missouri Conference adequately informed of where she was serving, including when she worked for the convicted sex offender. The Missouri Conference announced the conclusion of the complaint process in an update late July 9. Bishop Robert Farr, the conference’s bishop, determined that “under the current provisions of The Book of Discipline and based on the available information, the matter would not proceed to disciplinary action.” The update said the complaint against Remington “has been dismissed, and her suspension from appointment has been lifted.” As things stand, Remington retains her clergy credentials but can be appointed to a United Methodist church again only after following a review process and receiving approval under church law. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. A view of Little St. James, the private island once owned by Jeffrey Epstein. (Photo courtesy of oversight.house.gov) “This is not a punitive block but an approved exit with good standing following findings of no misconduct and no evidence linking me to Mr. Epstein’s criminal conduct,” Remington said. At her request and with the conference’s approval, Remington is on what church law calls “Honorable Location.” That means she is no longer part of the United Methodist itinerant appointment system, where the bishop appoints her to a ministry setting each year. While she keeps “the Rev.” title, she also is no longer a member of the Missouri Conference — meaning she loses guaranteed appointment, voting privileges and eligibility to be elected as a clergy delegate to jurisdictional or General Conference. Bishop Robert Farr of the Missouri Conference oversaw the complaint process against the Rev. Stephanie Remington. (Photo courtesy of unitedmethodistbishops.org) Farr suspended Remington and launched an investigation in March after learning that she had worked for Epstein without first informing the conference. “This matter raised serious concerns about judgment and public witness,” Farr said in the July 9 announcement. Farr is also board president of United Methodist Communications, which includes United Methodist News. “Clergy are entrusted with a visible and accountable ministry, and employment with a company connected to Jeffrey Epstein is deeply troubling given what is now known about the scope of his crimes and the harm done to his victims.” Remington first worked for Epstein as an administrative assistant from August to December 2018 and then as a temporary property manager of his private island from January until May 2019. That was when he was already a convicted sex offender, but before his second arrest in July 2019 on charges of sex trafficking minors. In August of that year, Epstein was found dead in his jail cell in what authorities ruled a suicide. In March, Remington told UM News that she never saw Epstein or anyone else on the island abuse anyone. During her time working with him, many of the island’s facilities were closed for repairs after damage by Hurricane Irma in 2017. In the Epstein files released by the U.S. Department of Justice, her name appears in more than 1,800 records. Many of her emails deal with the day-to-day running of facilities on the island, including travel arrangements for guests and the kitchen renovations she was overseeing. “I never saw anything,” she told UM News in March. “I knew him for the last nine months of his life, well after he served time for the things that he was accused of doing.” Still, she acknowledged that she knew Epstein was a registered sex offender when she accepted a job in his employ. At the time, Epstein had already completed an 18-month sentence after pleading guilty a decade earlier to one count of soliciting prostitution and one count of soliciting prostitution from someone under the age of 18. In a July 2025 memo, the Department of Justice said it has identified more than 1,000 victims of Epstein’s sex trafficking. Remington said she did not know about these crimes. Before coming to the U.S. Virgin Islands, Remington had previously served as a pastor at a series of United Methodist churches in Missouri for more than 15 years. She took leave in 2016 from pastoral ministry after she and her husband, a fellow United Methodist pastor, divorced. In 2017 and 2018, during part of the time she lived in the U.S. Virgin Islands, she worked remotely as a research manager for the Lewis Center for Church Leadership — part of United Methodist-related Wesley Theological Seminary in Washington, D.C. In subsequent years, she continued to do some contract work with the center. But she explained that her work with the center was a part-time position, which is why she needed to apply for another job on the Virgin Islands. She left her job with Epstein, months before his second arrest, to care for her father after he had received a cancer diagnosis. The Book of Discipline, The United Methodist Church’s policy book, details the rules guiding ministry beyond church walls — what the denomination calls extension ministries. Under The United Methodist Church’s connectional system, conference leaders and clergy in extension ministries are supposed to stay in regular contact. Clergy, no matter where they are serving, are supposed to file reports at least annually about their activities. That did not happen in Remington’s case. She told UM News in March that for years, she did not file any reports, and conference leaders did no follow-up. Remington said she did file a report in 2025 and later told her district superintendent through Zoom about her time in the Virgin Islands, including her work with Epstein. But she said she was unsure if the district superintendent fully understood her mention of Epstein. “Bottom line is that I did not misrepresent nor did I fail to disclose all the facts about my vocational and nonvocational work,” she said on June 10. In its announcement, the conference acknowledged that it needs to improve its supervision of clergy in extension-ministry appointments. The conference said it is reviewing its internal procedures to ensure clearer expectations and accountability, including for bivocational clergy who hold jobs in both church and secular settings as Remington did. “The Missouri Conference recognizes the seriousness of the concerns raised and the pain associated with the exploitation and abuse connected to Epstein’s criminal conduct,” the conference said in its announcement. “Nothing in this decision should be understood as minimizing that harm or weakening the Church’s commitment to the protection of vulnerable persons.” This article originally appeared at UM News. Heather Hahn is assistant news editor for UM News. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Texas church’s elders offer few details as uncertainty over senior pastors stretches into third month
item.author: Mark A. Kellner
item.dc:creator: Mark A. Kellner
item.description: Nearly two months ago, elders of Life Fellowship Church in McKinney, Texas, placed pastors Chris and Tatum Lindberg on leave for a season of “intentional reflection and renewal.” But they still have not explained why they acted. Church elders acknowledged the growing frustration in a July 7 email to the congregation while again declining to […]
item.content:encoded: Chris and Tatum Lindberg have remained on leave from Life Fellowship Church in McKinney, Texas, since May while undergoing what church elders describe as a restoration process. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) Nearly two months ago, elders of Life Fellowship Church in McKinney, Texas, placed pastors Chris and Tatum Lindberg on leave for a season of “intentional reflection and renewal.” But they still have not explained why they acted. Church elders acknowledged the growing frustration in a July 7 email to the congregation while again declining to explain the underlying issues. “We know it has been far too long since you’ve heard from us directly about where things stand with Pastors Chris and Tatum, and we want to begin simply by saying: we hear you, and we are sorry,” they wrote. “We recognize that the silence has been hard,” the board added. “It has left space for worry, speculation, and frustration — and those feelings are valid.” The latest message follows a May 15 announcement informing members that Chris Lindberg had acknowledged unspecified “past personal struggles” and would step away from public ministry while undergoing a restoration process. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Life Fellowship Church in McKinney, Texas. (Photo courtesy of Google Maps) “Late last year and early this year, Pastor Chris acknowledged past personal struggles,” the elders wrote in that earlier email. Both messages were provided to the Roys Report (RR) by a concerned member. The board said it had sought “the Lord’s wisdom” after learning of those struggles. It worked with the couple, “desiring their full strengthening and restoration physically, spiritually and emotionally.” Elders coordinated with the North Texas District of the Assemblies of God (AG), they said in May of the process that includes “comprehensive physical, mental, emotional, and spiritual care.” “Chris and Tatum remain our Senior Pastors,” the elders wrote. “We are hopeful Pastor Chris will be able to return to the pulpit when this process is completed.” RR has contacted both Life Fellowship Church and Gaylan Claunch, superintendent of the North Texas AG District, seeking comment. The church is located roughly 30 miles north of Dallas. Neither of the elders’ email identifies the nature of Mr. Lindberg’s acknowledged struggles. They are silent about whether misconduct, health concerns, family matters or another issue is involved. The board has not announced an investigation, disciplinary proceeding or permanent leadership transition. Instead, the July update describes months of meetings involving church elders, Assemblies of God leaders and the Lindbergs. “Since we last spoke, the Board of Elders has been in extensive and ongoing conversation — with each other, with Assemblies of God leadership, and directly with Pastors Chris and Tatum,” the board wrote. Chris and Tatum Lindberg are the senior pastors of Life Fellowship Church, an Assemblies of God congregation in McKinney, Texas. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) This process has included “many meetings, honest and transparent discussions, counseling sessions, deliverance sessions, and a great deal of prayer,” the elders said. The reference to “deliverance sessions” marked the first new detail disclosed since May, although the board did not explain what those sessions involved or why they were considered necessary. Church leaders said it has been hard to describe the restoration process because it “does not move on a fixed timeline.” “We haven’t withheld information to be secretive; we simply haven’t always had firm answers to give, and we didn’t want to share half-formed updates that might create more confusion or false expectations,” the elders wrote. The board said it believes it is “close to being able to share next steps and concrete details,” but did not provide a timetable for when members should expect more information. Instead, the email urged congregants to extend grace to the Lindbergs, church leadership and one another. It cited passages from Romans, Colossians and Galatians encouraging patience, unity and the fruit of the Spirit. The prolonged lack of specifics has left many questions unanswered. Neither communication addresses whether Mr. Lindberg remains actively involved in church governance, whether Ms. Lindberg is participating in ministry, or who is making long-term leadership decisions. The May email said trusted staff members and guest ministers would preach throughout the summer while encouraging members to continue attending services. The board has publicly acknowledged personal struggles, extensive counseling, deliverance sessions and ongoing discussions with denominational leaders. Yet it continues to withhold the facts that prompted the restoration process, saying only that it is attempting to balance transparency with sensitivity while preparing a more complete update. Mark A. Kellner is a reporter based in Mesquite, Nevada. He most recently covered statewide elections for the New York Post and was for three years the Faith & Family Reporter for The Washington Times. Mark is a graduate of the University of the Cumberlands and also attended Boston University’s College of Communication. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Faith-based AI company Gloo faces moment of truth after $438M in losses
item.author: Bob Smietana
item.dc:creator: Bob Smietana
item.description: Scott Beck has long hoped the faith-based tech company he founded in 2013 would help churches and other Christian groups harness technology to spread God’s word and help save the world. More than $400 million in losses and 13 years later, that dream faces a critical juncture. Leaders at Gloo Holdings Inc. hope a stock […]
item.content:encoded: The Gloo booth at the National Religious Broadcasters convention in February 2026, in Nashville, Tenn. (RNS photo/Bob Smietana) Scott Beck has long hoped the faith-based tech company he founded in 2013 would help churches and other Christian groups harness technology to spread God’s word and help save the world. More than $400 million in losses and 13 years later, that dream faces a critical juncture. Leaders at Gloo Holdings Inc. hope a stock sale on Friday (July 10) will bring in more than $20 million to the faith-based company to help keep it going and make it a kind of one-stop shop for outsourcing church services. But a filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission ahead of a proposed stock offering for Gloo Holdings Inc. shows the company, headquartered in Boulder, Colorado, faces significant challenges, having lost more than $240 million in fiscal years 2024 and 2025. The company has also reported a total deficit of $438 million since its founding in 2013, as of last summer. Gloo’s “recurring operating losses, negative cash flows, limited liquid resources and dependence on external financing,” have left its future in doubt, according to the filing. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. The Gloo website. (Screen grab) “Because it is not possible at this time to predict the outcome of future equity placements or additional borrowings, substantial doubt remains regarding our ability to continue as a going concern during the following year,” according to the SEC filing. Gloo’s leaders, including CEO Beck, a former Blockbuster and Boston Market executive, and Pat Gelsinger, former Intel CEO and Gloo’s executive chair and head of technology, hope to sell seven million shares in the upcoming stock offering. Company leaders have offered to buy $6 million in stock, according to a news release. As of April, the company had $33 million in cash, while continuing to run deficits. But Beck told media in an interview earlier this year that the company has finally reached critical mass. About 140,000 churches and ministry leaders are signed up for its services, which include technology, marketing and fundraising products. It has also signed contracts to provide tech services and access to what he called “network capability providers” for major Christian nonprofits like Wycliffe Bible Translators and the American Bible Society. “We have one side paying us millions of dollars a year,” he said. “We have the other side paying us, you know, thousands a year. And those two sides have now really exploded in terms of their growth.” The company’s leaders predict the company will turn a profit by the end of 2026. During the first quarter, Gloo brought in $41.5 million — more than twice the revenue in the first quarter of 2025 — and the company’s leaders have predicted revenue will top $195 million this year. While the company still lost $17 million in the first quarter of 2026, that is an improvement from the first quarter of 2025, when the company lost $27 million. The question is whether other investors still have faith in Gloo’s vision for the future. It’s not clear if Wall Street believes in Gloo. The company’s stock dropped to under $4 a share in early July, down from a high of $9.50 in November 2025, when the company went public. The new shares in Friday’s offering will sell for $3.25. Paul Hawkinson, associate professor of strategy and finance at North Park University in Chicago and a former investment banker, said it’s not unusual for early-stage companies to lose money for years. And the financial disclosures Gloo made in its recent filing are also common in stock offerings so that potential investors understand the risk of investing, he said. The challenge facing Gloo is whether or not investors still believe the company can become profitable. “It is indeed a real risk — and if investors lose confidence, then they may well run out of capital,” Hawkinson told media in an email. Hawkinson said Gloo is relatively small for a public company, which brings challenges. The company, he said, seems to believe that its investments in technology and in buying other companies that serve churches will lead to profitability. “But as a small public company whose investor base is highly concentrated, they’ve had to rely on consistently raising external capital (equity and debt) to fund this vision,” Hawkinson said in an email. “To date, they’ve not been able to translate that strategy into core operating cash flow, and until they do, the stock will likely remain depressed.” He wondered if Gloo might be better as a private company. Scott Beck. (Courtesy photo) “It is an interesting business model, but also complex as they seem to be integrating many platforms and services in the faith-based space,” he said. “They also face enormous competition from many startup/existing AI-enabled platforms.” Gloo is the latest venture for Beck, an investor with a knack for helping build iconic franchises — Blockbuster, Boston Market, Einstein Bros. Bagels and Angi — and a history of involvement with Christian nonprofits. He’s long hoped to give churches and other faith-based groups access to the technology and economies of scale that corporations have long used. That way, they could spend more time on their ministries and less time on the mundane back-office support needed to run a nonprofit, he said. Beck said churches are often disconnected from each other. While they serve the same God, they don’t have the same software or back-office tech from which large corporations take advantage. But making that dream a reality has been difficult for Beck, who has invested about $150 million in the project. “People would say, ‘Well, you can’t get churches on a common platform. You can’t get them to cooperate on anything,’” Beck told media in an interview during the National Religious Broadcasters convention earlier this year in Nashville. Beck started working as a teenager at Waste Management Inc., the garbage hauling company his father co-founded, and later was an early investor in major franchises. He said he’s seen the power of what he called the “collective might” of organizations that work together. Along with providing back-office information technology services, Gloo has also bought up a host of church service providers in recent years, including the church marketing company Masterworks, research outfit Barna Group, Outreach Inc., which publishes a magazine and provides marketing services, and a podcast run by Canadian pastor and leadership guru Carey Nieuwhof. It also acquired Westfall Group Inc., a church donor support company, and has partnerships with the popular Bible app YouVersion, the Human Flourishing Program at Harvard University, and InterVarsity, an evangelical campus ministry. The company also runs Gloo Media Network to provide marketing for churches and has developed its own Gloo AI Studio. Its strategy has started to pay off over the past year. The company has signed contracts with 25 nonprofits that pay at least a million dollars a year for IT services, according to the SEC filing. And more than 140,000 churches and ministry leaders use at least some of Gloo’s other products, such as a texting service to follow up with visitors, an artificial intelligence product that creates shareable video clips of sermons, Barna research and Gloo Insights demographic data. Churches can also sign up for both free and subscription Gloo plans, with access to resources for sermons, media clips from the hit Jesus show “The Chosen” and data from Barna. Gloo also offers churches help with fundraising and marketing. Gloo leaders believe that the more churches get used to using Gloo’s products, the more services they will buy. “These flywheels are turning,” Beck said. Though Gloo has been around for more than a decade, its efforts have shifted over time, and for some stretches, it was unclear what exactly the company did. As reported previously by Roys Report (RR), the company runs the billion-dollar “He Gets Us As” campaign, which claimed its purpose was to get people better see and understand “the Jesus of the Bible.” But the campaign has also collected personal information of tens of thousands of people, raising questions about whether the real purpose of the campaign was to mine data. Website for He Gets Us (Screenshot) Recently, company leaders have often spoken about making AI a “force for good.” Gloo has created AI models shaped by Christian values and has recruited former NASA staffers and other experts to build its AI platform. The shift to providing IT services to churches — and buying companies that provide products and services to churches — appears to have finally gotten Gloo closer toward a sustainable business model. Signs of Gloo were everywhere at the annual gathering of the National Religious Broadcasters at the Gaylord Opryland Resort and Convention Center in Nashville in mid-February. The opening session of the convention kicked off with a video ad for the company — just ahead of a video greeting from President Donald Trump — and a display for the company occupied prime real estate in the mammoth exhibit hall. “Powering your reach,” read a stand-up Gloo ad in one of the convention center’s lobbies. “Scale your reach with targeted marketing and donor engagement solutions.” That message of scaling reach with tech was also part of the key pitch for Rebecca Kelly, who was brought on last September as Gloo’s chief growth officer after a career at Western Union financial services. The company, she told media, has two audiences. The first is churches and pastors, and the second is large Christian ministries and nonprofits. “How do we serve those two audiences? By powering their tech and by powering their reach,” she said. Kelly’s pitch, which was repeated by company executives during a series of interviews at the NRB convention, can be boiled down like this: Leaders of churches are called to focus on ministry, not technology and back-office operations like bookkeeping or other infrastructure. Using AI and its expertise with new technology, Gloo can take on those logistics so that churches can focus on ministry. For Kelly, coming to Gloo felt as much a calling as a job opportunity. She said she took a year off to have a baby and was planning to shift into the ministry rather than return to the corporate world. Then Gloo came calling. Like other Gloo staffers, Kelly said she knows that technologies like AI are reshaping the world around us and believes Christians should have an active role in determining how those technologies are used. She hopes Gloo can help churches and other Christian groups do God’s work in the world. “I’ve never in my career felt the Lord so near to the work I’m doing every day,” she said. “It’s one of the most special moments of my career ever.” Scott Evans, longtime CEO of Outreach Inc., the church marketing and media company, said joining forces with Gloo was a way to expand the mission of his company, which Gloo bought in 2024. Evans got his start in church marketing when he moved to San Diego to help start a church in the early 1990s. Evans, who had worked in marketing, was the church’s associate pastor. “The senior pastor said, ‘Scott, you’re the guy with a marketing degree. It’s your job to make sure somebody shows up on grand opening Sunday,’” said Evans. “It’s like, no pressure. And so I got to help do the church marketing for our little church, and it grew.” That marketing eventually turned into Outreach Inc. Evans said he had been thinking and praying about the future when Beck called him about buying the company. It was a way to expand what Outreach was already doing and to help more churches. For example, he said, Outreach has a site called Sermon Central, where an AI tool can help churches create video clips of a sermon, post them online and then create a small group study guide — tasks most pastors don’t have time to do. “In that case, AI is making their sermon more efficient and more effective,” Evans said. Brad Hill, the chief partner success officer at Gloo, said the organization hopes to identify other companies like Outreach that are already helping churches and expand their reach. The hope is to set up what he called a “permanent, redemptive engine.” “Once we invest in or acquire somebody, my role is to make sure we’re helping them grow,” he said. “And we’ve done that now more than a dozen times.” According to Gloo’s SEC filings, those acquisitions brought in 56% of the company’s revenue in 2025. For Beck, starting the company has been an act of faith. He said about 13 years ago, he began worrying that while technology was racing ahead, churches were being left behind. He and his wife, Theresa, decided to come up with a solution and launched Gloo. “Theresa and I just put all of our eggs in one basket and put all of our cards on the table and put our relationships and our network and our capital (in) to be able to help this ecosystem get connected,” he said. He told media that after years of start-up struggles, the company is on the right track. The road has not been easy. Earlier this year, Gloo laid off staff, while Beck and Gelsinger cut their salaries to $1, in hopes of helping the company become profitable by the end of the year. Beck said the company will continue to cut costs and that God has guided them along the way. “God gave me a conviction,” he said. “That was, in order for the church to get the benefit of the next generation of technologies, it’s got to be connected. Go, connect the church. Now, that’s a really big thought, and that’s the journey that we’ve been on.” Roys Report contributed to this article. Bob Smietana is a national reporter for Religion News Service. SHARE THIS:

item.title: The $50 million business of child sponsorship in Christian music
item.author: Jessica Morris
item.dc:creator: Jessica Morris
item.description: How much are overseas children worth? A cool $50 million, according to one of the top names in the Christian music industry. This is why sponsoring vulnerable children has become a mainstay of the contemporary Christian music (CCM) scene — as much as the prayer and praise music. Scarcely a concert goes by where the […]
item.content:encoded: Child-sponsorship appeals have become a fixture of Christian concerts, and a new lawsuit reveals the multimillion-dollar business built on them. (RR Graphic) How much are overseas children worth? A cool $50 million, according to one of the top names in the Christian music industry. This is why sponsoring vulnerable children has become a mainstay of the contemporary Christian music (CCM) scene — as much as the prayer and praise music. Scarcely a concert goes by where the audience doesn’t see a heart-wrenching video, then hears a plea to pledge money to children in the Global South to transform their lives. Such a request, usually delivered by the artists themselves amid smoke machines and lights, is hard to refuse. Many people raise their hands for a piece of cardboard with a child’s face on it. Later, in a tent filled with branded merchandise and music, concertgoers sign up to sponsor that child monthly until he or she turns 18. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. MercyMe concert in Spokane, Washington, on April 26, 2026. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) What the donors don’t know is that the initial downpayment goes to the event organizer for administration fees. It may take up to a year for any of their funds to directly reach the child they signed up to help. They also don’t know the band makes money from these sponsorships. Nonprofits like World Vision, ChildFund, Feed The Hungry and Compassion rely on CCM concerts to net many of their donors. For that, they need the artist to bring their charity onto the stage. Some of this backstage bargaining got revealed in a recent lawsuit filed by Wes Campbell, owner of the band Newsboys Inc., a leader in recruiting fans to sponsor children. The band was so successful that Campbell estimated child sponsorship is a $50 million business. Newsboys owner Wes Campbell pictured with Michael Tait in a Nashville recording studio. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) In his April 20 lawsuit, he said charities are so integral to the chaotic economics of CCM that “most agree that without nonprofit participation, there would be no live CCM touring market.” Campbell alleges that a host of defendants, including the band MercyMe, promotion agency LiveCo/TPR and nonprofit World Vision Inc., conspired to deprive him of a $50 million sale to Dutch holding company Waterland. Campbell also named Roys Report (RR) as a defendant. He claims an RR article destroyed Campbell’s brokerage business, Thriving Children’s Advocates (TCA), which acts as a liaison between artists and nonprofits such as World Vision. Waterland then stole TCA’s business model, the suit alleges. For the public, what was most shocking wasn’t the amount of money that almost changed hands. It was the idea that child sponsorship is a business opportunity. “I’m not at all surprised by the lawsuit,” said Threads user @joshuarcrocker. “What’s worse, though, IMO is the details of those kickbacks from World Vision for every sponsor that signs up during a bands tour. Y’all really out here profiting off the back of starving children in Jesus name?” Touring artists receive a payment of $200 to $500 for each sponsor who signs up, the lawsuit states. This gives an artist who completes at least 60 shows per year the chance to earn up to $7.2 million. Many CCM artists struggle with this. Mike Donehey, lead singer of Tenth Avenue North, has spoken openly about the moral tension of profiting from child sponsorship, saying his band felt “very uncomfortable taking money from a nonprofit.” (Photo courtesy of social media) “We always felt very uncomfortable taking money from a nonprofit,” said Mike Donehey, lead singer of Tenth Avenue North (TAN). “Here’s the problem: they’re a nonprofit and I’m a for-profit.” Donehey’s group partners with Compassion International, a nonprofit that serves more than 2 million children in 29 countries through educational, nutritional and other programs. In TAN’s early days, Donehey would promote the cause voluntarily with no remuneration. Compassion later offered them a fee structure, which gave them tour support and payment in return for advertising Compassion during their concerts. Naturally, the group had mixed feelings about accepting money on behalf of the poor. “I think as a Christian, anything I do is ministry,” Donehey said to RR. “So, one of the ways we came to terms with our conscience feeling conflicted is we started a charity golf tournament once a year. I hear that through that event, year after year, we’re able to raise more money for Compassion than we ever received in tour sponsorships. To me as an artist, that’s the goal. I’m in partnership.” The common thread among CCM artists is they want to use their platform for good. Early in their careers, many bands lack the resources or means to do so substantially. When a charity or a go-between like Campbell’s TCA reaches out with a lucrative offer, it seems like a perfect opportunity to make a difference.
