Successful and philanthropically minded artists such as Donehey face questions about the moral structure of this system. “The reason I would give up stage time to an organization is because I personally believe in what they are doing,” he said. “I think the saddest part — it isn’t that anyone didn’t do anything necessarily (wrong). I think from a business perspective, I don’t know that I could say anyone did anything that wasn’t above board, right? But philosophically . . . as an artist, am I cool with just whomever made the biggest bid?” The business of child sponsorship “Partnering with CCM artists gives Compassion access to audiences we may not otherwise be able to reach,” Tim Glenn, head of Compassion’s global public relations, told RR in an email. “We have found that this model is one of the most cost-effective, financially efficient ways to reach Christian audiences and connect them to our mission of releasing children from poverty in Jesus’ name.” Compassion has partnered with more than 100 artists to date, resulting in more than 400,000 children being sponsored. It’s fair to say that many of those sponsorships wouldn’t have occurred unless a Christian music artist had spoken about the organization at a concert. The staggering figures in the Campbell lawsuit left many shocked. But Glenn says that people can rest assured that the largest part of every donation to Compassion goes toward the child and family that sponsors designate. He added that any child shown as available for sponsorship, either on a packet at a concert or on Compassion’s website, is already receiving some benefits. This occurs through a church-based, or occasionally home-based, development center. According to Glenn, “Seventy-five to 82 cents of every dollar goes directly to support our programs, which provide educational, health, spiritual and socio-emotional support for each child. The remaining 18-25 cents goes toward administrative and fundraising costs, which includes artist partnerships.” Often, the nonprofit sees its relationship with the artist as a way for that artist to give back. “Compassion’s focus in every partnership is to work alongside those who share our commitment to Jesus and a desire to release children from poverty around the world,” Glenn said. “The Christian music industry can be volatile. By partnering with Christian artists, we are also able to help them build their ministry through support of their gifts and talents.” In other words, a rising tide floats all boats. And now, they are tied to each other. Sponsorship deals distinguish the CCM touring industry from its secular counterpart. At a Christian concert, Campbell said in his lawsuit, fans and artists “share a mutual sense of engagement, goodwill and trust.” Fans expect to be moved and empowered to change the world — whether it comes through buying an album, purchasing a Christian-themed T-shirt or sponsoring a child. If just 6% of an audience of 4,000 sponsor a child, the band will receive at least $48,000. This could make up to half of the artist’s entire budget for a performance. In this environment, artists and child sponsorship organizations have become linked to each other and incorporate each other in their budgets. With funds from sponsorships, Christian artists can afford bigger stages, more lights and bigger trucks. A glance at the Newsboys 2016-2017 rider — a contract given to the event promoter prior to the performance — reveals how a typical band used these funds. In addition to what the band earned through sponsorships, the Newsboys were paid 100% of the performance fee: what a promoter pays an artist for a show. The more prominent the artist, the bigger the fee. Given the Newsboys’ stature and their vaunted ability to sell out events prior to front man Michael Tait’s resignation in January 2025, their fee ranged from $40,000 to $74,999, according to Celebrity Talent International. Throw in $48,000 from sponsorships, and that’s a 64% jump to $122,999 per show, not including extras like corporate partnerships, which Campbell often negotiated. Such a substantial budget helped the band with perks like a 45-foot bus, a 20-foot trailer and one 65-foot semi-truck for equipment and instruments, plus ample space for 12 people to sleep, work and live on the road. And it doesn’t include costs that Campbell passed on, such as having the promoter or venue pay for hotel rooms, some production and lighting equipment, security and catering. When the Newsboys required an opening band, that was either subsidized by the promoter, or the band covered its own expenses. Thus, sponsorships made life much easier for the Newsboys, and luckily for its members, TCA took care of all the business details. All the band had to do was deliver the message during the concert. Thriving Children’s Advocates Campbell opened TCA in 2016 and quickly became a lucrative middleman between nonprofits and CCM artists or promoters. Along with the Newsboys, artists on the brokerage’s line-up included MercyMe, Tauren Wells, Danny Gokey and Micah Tyler. With TCA’s help, the musicians worked with child sponsorship charities, including World Vision, a defendant in Campbell’s lawsuit. The relationship between TCA and World Vision ended acrimoniously in October 2019, Campbell alleges, when World Vision set up its own department to manage artist relationships. As of 2025, TCA was publicly working with only one nonprofit, Children International. The charity-artist-middleman model isn’t exclusive to TCA. But it was one of the first intermediaries between artists and charities, so it’s fair to say that Campbell changed the face of the Christian touring market. The model is straightforward. TCA approaches the charity on behalf of the artist, offering to give the nonprofit roughly 15 minutes of airtime during a show. This mainly consists of a video and the artist encouraging fans to sponsor a child. In return, the charity gets a new sponsor, and the artist gets a commission on each new child sponsored. TCA also receives a percentage of the total deal for its services. Just how much remained an industry secret prior to the lawsuit, but filings by a former nonprofit partner suggests the arrangement was highly lucrative. For instance, when TCA partnered with ChildFund International in 2019, TCA raised $11.3 million in sponsorships and donations. However, ChildFund retained only $2.06 million (18%) and TCA got the remaining $9.2 million or 82%, as first reported by Ministry Watch. The next year, when TCA raised $16.5 million for the charity, TCA pocketed $3.2 million with ChildFund getting the rest ($13.2 million). However, the same IRS 990 form shows ChildFund paying TCA $7.9 million that year as a contractor, which combined with the earlier amount, equals $11.2 million. Thus, ChildFund ended up with more money — $5.3 million or 32 percent — but TCA still retained the bulk (68%) of the proceeds. Some of that money gets passed onto the artists. Donehey says TCA approached him early in his solo career with an attractive offer. Despite being promised a “very lucrative” return, he turned them down. “The opening pitch was, ‘You already love this, you’re already great at this, and how would you like to make far more than you’re currently making doing it?’” Donehey said. Donehey soon saw TCA’s involvement drive up the costs for child sponsorship nonprofits. Brokerages such as TCA started pitting nonprofits against each other, and they started seeing each other as competitors, he told RR. With TCA’s brokerage business flourishing in August 2024, Dutch hedge fund Waterland offered Campbell $50 million for his company, including the exclusive model and artist roster. Waterland Private Equity Investments logo. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) Campbell’s lawsuit alleges that Waterland broke a confidentiality contract it signed during negotiations. Waterland, Campbell says, then went on to replicate TCA’s model, using its recently acquired tour promotion agency LiveCo/TPR as a broker and World Vision as the charity of choice. TPR soon came to represent about 80% of all Christian music tours and shows, giving it a monopoly in the Christian concert business, Campbell said. Artists were promised a $500 kickback per sponsorship, allegedly boosting Waterland’s top line by $60 million. Then in June 2025, RR began publishing multiple stories alleging that Michael Tait had sexually assaulted nine people. In addition, RR published a story alleging Tait watched while a woman was reportedly raped by a Newsboys contracted crew member in Fargo, North Dakota, in December 2014. Soon afterwards, Newsboys, owned by Campbell, lost radio airplay, tour dates and a record contract. Campbell believes the June 2025 article was “formed, facilitated and fomented, to destroy Campbell and his companies as a competitor, seize his business for themselves and thereby consolidate and maintain their monopoly power.” As a result, he says, TCA was worthless by the end of June. MercyMe allegedly broke their contract a month later, Campbell said. Children International followed suit in September when its contract ended. Along with MercyMe, Children International moved to LiveCo/TPR as their broker of choice. Consequently, Campbell says, he and his businesses became pariahs. He put a five-bedroom villa — Bellavista — on St. Thomas, U.S. Virgin Islands, on the market to pay bills. Previously advertised as a bed and breakfast, it’s worth almost $1.7 million. A new way forward Brokers such as Campbell’s TCA aren’t the only entities that profit from child sponsorships. Major conferences, book tours, popular podcasts and YouTubers also partner with charities to finance their work. On a recent episode of the podcast Faith Adjacent, social commentator Jamie Golden explained how this works. Podcaster Jamie Golden has spoken about turning down a per-child sponsorship deal because it felt “icky,” (Photo courtesy of LinkedIn) Her employer, Popcast Media Group, had also been offered a lucrative sponsorship deal in the past, she said, by an unspecified nonprofit that offered $200 per child sponsorship for a tour. But the media company negotiated the contract for a pre-established fee per show, rather than per child, because it felt “icky.” “It’s pretty easy to justify and to be like, well, we’re really changing people’s lives. But when you’re not disclosing it and saying I’m a tentmaker (to the audience)?” Golden added. “So we negotiated the contract where we were like, ‘No, we’re not going to make money (per child).’ They’re like, ‘We’ve never done a contract like that.’” Golden and other staff members would later travel overseas to see the nonprofit’s work. She said they refused to publish photos from their trip, believing it commodified the people they were visiting. “We were the only people on that trip that did not take photos of children and post them publicly,” she said. “We are not white saviors.” Some artists are rejecting the sponsorship model and giving 100% of funds donated at concerts to nonprofits they’ve started themselves. One of those is David Zach, lead singer of Christian rock band Remedy Drive. After partnering with a small nonprofit for 13 years, Remedy Drive started its own charity last year called Daylight United. All funds raised go toward a network of organizations that fight human trafficking, with a focus on raids and investigation. David Zach, lead singer of the Christian rock band Remedy Drive, rejects the sponsorship model, calling it “morally ambiguous at best.” His band now runs its own anti-trafficking charity, Daylight United. (Photo courtesy of Facebook/SEU Photography) “I love that we know exactly where our impact is,” Zach said. “I’m allergic to the customer acquisition model. Instead, we have introduced this cause to people and helped raise millions of dollars and millions of in-kind donations.” Zach told RR he believes the sponsorship model is “morally ambiguous at best.” He added, “Ninety-nine percent of audiences would be shocked and disillusioned to hear how this works and what is being kept from them.” For Zach, this conviction led him to leave a Christian record label in 2013. At the height of the band’s success, he began volunteering with a counter-trafficking organization. Volunteering undercover in sting operations in Thailand, Zach helped gather intelligence and evidence against traffickers. He wanted to make music about the survivors he met, but his record label disagreed, and Remedy Drive became independent. This made the band less commercially successful. But it also prompted more fans to participate in anti-trafficking efforts like band members were doing. “Why ask our fans to risk their lives if we are not also risking our lives?” Zach asked. Zach isn’t the only artist to find a new way forward in the Christian music/nonprofit model. Chris Tomlin and his wife Lauren founded the charity For Others in 2019 to help end the child welfare crisis in America. They work with churches and volunteers to support struggling families, provide for children who have aged out of foster care, and reunite families. In 2025, they served 152,940 children and raised $14,503,449, 7.42% of which was invested in fundraising and 6.9% covered the salaries of 13 staff. Likewise, Jars of Clay founded Blood:Water in 2004 with activist Jena Lee Nardella to help end the water and HIV/AIDS crises in Africa. The band doesn’t currently perform, yet fans have helped raise more than $40 million to help communities in 10 African countries. Jars of Clay, co-founded the nonprofit Blood:Water with activist Jena Lee Nardella in 2004 to address the HIV/AIDS and water crises in Africa.(Photo courtesy of bloodwater.org Once the charity commits to a community, it stays for eight years. In 2025 it earned $2,020,361. With a staff of 13, including a Kenya office, 56.67% went toward salaries and 17.46% went towards fundraising. Other artists stay close to traditional charities but work closely with artist liaisons who cultivate an ethical relationship between the band and a nonprofit partner. Donehey’s former bandmate, Jason Jamison, left Tenth Avenue North in 2018 to become an artist liaison with Compassion. This relationship gives artists space to ask questions about financial transparency, ensuring profits are reaching the promised children. Other artists sponsor children and travel overseas before promoting a sponsorship nonprofit. In the CCM world, it is difficult to cover the costs of touring without a nonprofit partnership, especially if fans or record labels expect a show filled with bells and whistles. For Donehey, the tension of profiting from child sponsorship will remain until he can platform them for free, no strings attached. He hopes his fellow CCM artists will follow suit. “My goal is to keep advocating for Compassion and just stop taking money, which I’m scared to do because it means I will probably be back in a van (and) I’ll make less money on a tour. I’ll have to be gone from my family longer.” he said. “(But) a pure heart is worth fighting for. We’re gonna get it wrong often. We’re gonna fail and fall. Keep striving to go back to your first love. When you make a career out of something, it’s easy to make concessions in the name of good things.” *The author of this article sponsors a child through Compassion International. *This article has been amended to reflect new information from Compassion international. Jessica Morris is a music journalist, podcaster and author based in Melbourne, Australia. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Nashville Presbyterian church staffer arrested on child sex charge
item.author: Rebecca Hopkins
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Hopkins
item.description: A Nashville Presbyterian church staffer was arrested Tuesday after police found he’d allegedly shared a sexually explicit video of a child. Jason Douglas Ford, 34, the facilities coordinator at Nashville’s Midtown Fellowship, was charged with aggravated sexual exploitation of a minor on July 7. Metropolitan Nashville police arrested Ford in his home, according to a […]
item.content:encoded: Jason Ford, facilities coordinator at Midtown Fellowship (PCA) in Nashville, was arrested and charged with sexual exploitation of a minor. (Photo courtesy of Nashville Police Department) A Nashville Presbyterian church staffer was arrested Tuesday after police found he’d allegedly shared a sexually explicit video of a child. Jason Douglas Ford, 34, the facilities coordinator at Nashville’s Midtown Fellowship, was charged with aggravated sexual exploitation of a minor on July 7. Metropolitan Nashville police arrested Ford in his home, according to a departmental press release. Police said Ford shared the video, displaying a naked girl performing a lewd dance, on Kik, a messaging app. Kik allows people to join topical groups or chat one-on-one anonymously. Midtown Fellowship’s website shows an employee named “Jay Ford” who works for the church’s central ministry team. The church is a member of the Presbyterian Church in America (PCA). The PCA is the second largest Presbyterian denomination in the United States with more than 1,600 churches. Roys Report (RR) emailed Midtown, seeking comment on Ford’s arrest and to ask how long Ford has worked there. The church didn’t immediately respond. Ford could not be reached at his church email address. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Jason Ford, pictured on Midtown Fellowship’s website, where he worked as facilities coordinator. (Photo courtesy of Waybackmachine.org) RR also emailed Savannah Edmonds, who is listed as Ford’s attorney in records of the Metropolitan Nashville and Davidson County’s criminal court. The police stated that their investigation started with a cybertip, which came through the National Center for Missing & Exploited Children. Nashville police’s special unit for investigating internet crimes against children spent seven months investigating. When subpoenaed, internet providers traced the Kik account used to share the video back to Ford’s email address. Nashville police determined that the IP address provided in the cybertip is registered to Ford’s home. The charge is a felony in Tennessee. Inmate records show Ford is in Davidson County’s Correctional Development Center with bond set for $75,000. Ford has a court appearance today (July 9). Rebecca Hopkins is a journalist based in Colorado. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Former Rhema Bible Church pastor pleads no contest in animal cruelty case
item.author: Ann Marie Shambaugh
item.dc:creator: Ann Marie Shambaugh
item.description: After a high-profile arrest involving a lurid animal cruelty situation at a home he owns in Oklahoma, former Rhema Bible Church Pastor Craig Hagin got off this week with two misdemeanor counts of disturbing the peace. The ruling, issued July 7 in Tulsa County District Court, requires Hagin to pay a $100 fine for each […]
item.content:encoded: Craig Hagin, former pastor of Rhema Bible Church in Broken Arrow, Oklahoma, received two misdemeanor counts in his animal cruelty case after originally being charged with a felony. (Photo courtesy of Tulsa Police Department/ Google Maps) After a high-profile arrest involving a lurid animal cruelty situation at a home he owns in Oklahoma, former Rhema Bible Church Pastor Craig Hagin got off this week with two misdemeanor counts of disturbing the peace. The ruling, issued July 7 in Tulsa County District Court, requires Hagin to pay a $100 fine for each count and take an online course on preventing animal cruelty; a total of $200, plus court costs. Police arrested Hagin and his now-estranged wife, Mia Hagin, in March after discovering two dozen cats and dogs living in unsanitary conditions in a home they owned. Investigators also found a dead cat in the house. Both Hagins were charged with cruelty to animals, a felony. After she pleaded no contest on April 8, that charge was downgraded to two misdemeanor counts of disturbing the peace. Court documents show her total costs were also $200 plus court fees. Craig Hagin apparently employed the same strategy in getting his conviction lessened. He is the grandson of Kenneth E. Hagin, leader of the Word of Faith movement. He is the son of Kenneth W. Hagin, founding pastor at Rhema Bible Church in Broken Arrow, Oklahoma. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Kenneth W. Hagin, alongside his wife, Lynette, addresses the Rhema Bible Church congregation on June 21, 2026 about their son, Craig Hagin. (Screengrab) Roys Report (RR) reached out to Craig Hagin and his attorney, Ronald Fraley, for comment but did not receive a response before publication. After his arrest, Craig Hagin stepped down from his roles as pastor at Rhema Bible Church, instructor at Rhema Bible Training College and chief operating officer for Kenneth Hagin Ministries. In May, Mia Hagin announced on social media that she and her husband were divorcing after 20 years of marriage. In a written statement sent in June to RR, Mia Hagin said she discovered her husband’s infidelity in 2007 and reported it to her mother-in-law. Eight years ago, she said, she discovered her husband had been dallying with prostitutes. “So I went to my mother-in-law the second time again in person and told her that ‘her son needs help’ this is not just a man having an affair,” Mia stated. “This is your son paying for sex over and over again for years.” Eventually, she confronted her husband. “I asked him, ‘How many?’” Mia stated. “He replied ‘more wom(e)n than he can count.’ He could not give me a number. This was heart-shattering for me, and that comment has replayed in my head for years.” Mia Hagin said her in-laws failed to address the situation and labeled her a liar. In June, Craig Hagin’s parents spoke during a service at Rhema Bible Church. They said they received “documented evidence” of their son’s misconduct and “acted immediately” to fire him. “This evidence made it clear that Craig had been living a life we did not see,” the elder Hagins stated. “It was conduct we could not reconcile with the son and the leader we believed him to be, and frankly, conduct we could not imagine he was capable of. He hid this from us. He was living a double life.” The elder Hagins said this was not the first time they had questions about their son’s conduct. They did not, however, mention those other occasions. The divorce case is ongoing in Tulsa County court. Ann Marie Shambaugh has reported as a print journalist in multiple states, including currently in Carmel, Indiana. SHARE THIS:

item.title: San Jose pastor convicted in domestic violence attack
item.author: Mark A. Kellner
item.dc:creator: Mark A. Kellner
item.description: A Santa Clara, California, jury has convicted a San Jose pastor and former community activist of misdemeanor domestic violence, after prosecutors said he assaulted and strangled his female partner. After she reported the incident to police, he then sued her for defamation. The jury deliberated about three hours on July 2 before finding the Rev. […]
item.content:encoded: The Rev. Ray Fitzgerald Montgomery, a San Jose pastor and former community activist, was convicted of misdemeanor domestic violence for assaulting and strangling his partner. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) A Santa Clara, California, jury has convicted a San Jose pastor and former community activist of misdemeanor domestic violence, after prosecutors said he assaulted and strangled his female partner. After she reported the incident to police, he then sued her for defamation. The jury deliberated about three hours on July 2 before finding the Rev. Ray Fitzgerald Montgomery, 59, guilty of misdemeanor domestic violence. The charges stemmed from a June 6, 2024, attack in his San Jose home, according to the Santa Clara County District Attorney’s Office. Montgomery is scheduled to be sentenced Sept. 8 and faces possible jail time. Prosecutors said Montgomery violently assaulted the then-42-year-old woman by picking her up, dragging her down a hallway and strangling her. The woman escaped, hid in a bathroom and sought help. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Ray Fitzgerald Montgomery, pictured at Second Baptist Church in Martinez in 2017. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) Prosecutors said the woman told a friend about the assault, photographed her injuries, sought treatment at Stanford Hospital and reported the incident to police several months later. She formally identified Montgomery as her alleged attacker to San Jose police on Nov. 26, 2024, and provided additional statements to investigators. District Attorney Jeff Rosen said the verdict underscored prosecutors’ commitment to pursuing domestic violence cases despite efforts to discredit victims. “We believe domestic violence victims,” Rosen said. “Lawsuits and lying will never stop us from seeking justice for those who are hurt by their partners.” Before criminal charges were filed, Montgomery sued the woman in 2025, alleging she had falsely accused him of abuse and defamed him. The civil lawsuit became an unusual backdrop to the criminal case that followed. The woman, who leads a South Bay nonprofit organization, was not identified in court filings or some media reports because she is the victim of an alleged domestic violence crime. At the time of the reported assault, Montgomery served as executive director of People Acting in Community Together, or PACT, a San Jose faith-based community organizing group. He stepped down from that position after the allegations surfaced. Montgomery also has served as a former senior pastor of Second Baptist Church in Martinez. The case attracted attention because of Montgomery’s prominence in South Bay civic and faith communities and because of the competing civil and criminal proceedings involving the same allegations. The Mercury News reported that Montgomery and the woman dated between 2020 and June 6, 2024, the day prosecutors say the assault occurred. The newspaper also reported that Montgomery led PACT when the domestic violence allegations arose. During the criminal case, prosecutors relied on the victim’s testimony, photographs documenting her injuries, medical treatment records and subsequent police investigation. According to the district attorney’s office, the evidence persuaded jurors beyond a reasonable doubt that Montgomery committed misdemeanor domestic violence. Santa Clara County prosecutors noted strangulation is a significant indicator in domestic violence cases because it can produce serious injury even when visible injuries appear limited. Montgomery remains free pending sentencing, according to media reports. Mark A. Kellner is a reporter based in Mesquite, Nevada. He most recently covered statewide elections for the New York Post and was for three years the Faith & Family Reporter for The Washington Times. Mark is a graduate of the University of the Cumberlands and also attended Boston University’s College of Communication. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Are megachurches making a comeback? New study shows they’re growing, thriving and here to stay
item.author: Rebecca Hopkins
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Hopkins
item.description: Scandals. Politics. A pandemic. Many things could’ve killed megachurches in the last five years. But a recent study shows they are growing, thriving and likely here to stay. Most megachurches are bigger than they were before the pandemic temporarily closed them, according to a June study from the Hartford Institute for Religion Research. Additionally, most […]
item.content:encoded: Multi-site megachurch Eastview Christian Church has campuses in Normal and Blomington, Illinois. (Photo via social media) Scandals. Politics. A pandemic. Many things could’ve killed megachurches in the last five years. But a recent study shows they are growing, thriving and likely here to stay. Most megachurches are bigger than they were before the pandemic temporarily closed them, according to a June study from the Hartford Institute for Religion Research. Additionally, most megachurch leaders are optimistic about the future. The study found that megachurches — categorized as those attended by 1,800 or more — are involved in their communities. Many of the 10 million Americans who attend megachurches participate in small groups. They’re inviting their friends. Their giving is up. This is true despite the internal conflicts that 95% of megachurches endured during the COVID-19 pandemic. “While many churches continue to struggle, a substantial percentage of very large churches and megachurches demonstrate that growth, innovation, and vitality remain quite possible in the contemporary religious environment,” the study states. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Lakewood Church is a nondenominational Christian megachurch in Houston. It is one of the largest congregations in the U.S., occupying a former sports arena. (Photo courtesy of Creative Commons) From Aug. 20 to Nov. 13, 2025, the Hartford Institute gathered responses from 331 of America’s 1,850 megachurches. A key church leader — often an executive or senior pastor — filled out the survey and provided attendance figures on behalf of the church. (In 2024, University of Chicago researchers questioned self-reported attendance, saying cellphone geolocation data shows lower attendance levels.) Other recent studies on religious institutions show mixed results. Lifeway Research reported last year that roughly half of all churches are growing post-pandemic. In 2025, Pew research showed that after steadily dropping over 15 years, the Christian share of the population is leveling off. Gallup polls have shown that engagement in all religions has been steadily declining in the U.S. For 2025, Gallup reported that 47% of Americans consider religion to be “very important,” the second lowest figure in 20 years, beat only by 2022’s and 2023’s 46%. A 2023 survey by the Christian researchers of Barna Group showed that most Americans, including Christians, don’t like megachurches and celebrity pastors. Recent news headlines may offer explanations. In 2020, Carl Lentz, pastor of megachurch Hillsong NYC, was fired for having an affair and later was accused of sexually abusing his kids’ nanny. Following this and other scandals, Hillsong has shrunk considerably, Roys Report (RR) previously reported. In 2024, Gateway Church founder Robert Morris resigned after former parishioner Cindy Clemishire accused him of sexually abusing her, starting when she was 12. He later served jail time. Gateway, based near Fort Worth, Texas, is declining in size and giving. Gateway Church’s main campus location in Southlake, Texas. (Courtesy Photo) The Association of Related Churches (ARC), one of the largest church growth networks in North America, trains churches to “launch large.” It has been involved in numerous abuse scandals. By 2025, these scandals had slowed the association’s growth, RR previously reported. Its model has been called “unbiblical.” Some Christians unhappy with megachurches are finding their way to micro-churches, said Rob Wegner, a former megachurch pastor who founded the Kansas City Underground. The Underground is a decentralized network of small, volunteer-led groups of Christians. “All forms of the church can be healthy, and all forms of the church can be sick,” Rob Wegner previously told RR. “Personally, I have a set of convictions that the smaller, simpler expression of the church is a better environment for disciple-making. I think it’s better in terms of incarnational mission.” But the megachurch has a future, according to the Hartford Institute report. “If the past five years are any indication, the story of megachurches and other very large churches in America is not primarily one of survival or even rebound,” the study stated. “It is a story of adaptation, resilience, and the continued desire to influence the countryʼs spiritual landscape.” How megachurches are growing The Hartford Institute, connected to Hartford International University, has studied churches for 20 years, looking at five-year increments. It conducted its survey with Faith Communities Today, a research coalition that studies religion in America. The research team Warren Bird, a Christian and Missionary Alliance pastor, and Scott Thumma, a professor at Hartford International University, asks similar questions year to year. Research conducted from 2000 to 2020 showed a consistent downward trend in attendance, regardless of church size. During the pandemic, 88% of megachurches closed temporarily. In the two years that followed, many people switched churches. Things started to change in 2025, with researchers finding a comeback. In fact, 43% of American churches of all sizes are now growing at a rate of at least 5%. Megachurches are showing the most growth, with 45% of them growing at least 25%. They are, researchers say, resilient and have focused on rebuilding. The “attractional model” could be one example. Churches that encourage members to invite friends grow, according to the study. Specifically, 80% of megachurches that reported recruiting people “a lot” grew by at least 5%. “It shows that churches with a high invitation culture tend to be growing,” the study states. “In short, the news is not surprising: growing churches have greater recruiting by their participants.” Small group participation has become increasingly important to megachurches. In 2000, only 50% of megachurches were highlighting small groups for spiritual formation. By 2025, 94% of megachurches did so. As a result, 40% of megachurch attendees are part of a small group. Congregational commitment to attend weekly worship services has waned in megachurches, the study found. Most people involved in a megachurch — 68% — attend just once a month, the study found. The share of attendees showing up weekly has gone down during most years over the last two decades. In 2000, 91% of an average megachurch’s population attended weekly. By 2020, that percentage was down to 68%. Now it’s at 60%. Most megachurches report vitality The study shows that 84% of megachurches report an “overall vitality and strength” that is better than their pre-COVID-19 status. Similarly, 86% say they are “thriving and will continue to.” By contrast, just 21% of Protestant churches of all sizes believe they are thriving. By 2025, megachurches have focused on young adults, community impact, higher commitment, disciple-making, and digital ministry, the study found. Congregants sing at The Summit Church, an SBC-affiliated megachurch based in Durham, N.C.(Photo via Facebook) “These top-named qualities, taken together, challenge any stereotypes that megachurches are an inch deep and mile wide — or if they are, they donʼt want to stay that way!” the study states. Other qualities characterize megachurches, according to the study: Diversity: 66% of megachurches have a population that is at least 20% minority, up from 21% in 2000 and 58% in 2020. Megachurches have become more intentional in modeling diversity from the stage. Some 5% of pastors are first-generation immigrants. Involvement in local community: 88% of megachurches are involved in local service projects. Almost 60% report sharing their space with other organizations, such as health clinics and food pantries. Community service increases a church’s spiritual vitality and willingness to change, the study states. Strong finances: 86% of megachurches reported their financial health was excellent or good, up from 80% in 2020. Changes in giving rates have outpaced both changes in the cost of living and growth in attendance. People are more likely to give more if they can see the church’s financials themselves, the report said. Strong board structure: 82% have a governance structure employing mostly nonfamily, non-staff voting members to promote accountability. How megachurches are here to stay The megachurch phenomenon is a creature of the Baby Boom generation, but it will outlive Boomers, the study found. The median year when current megachurches were founded is 1982. But 19% of megachurches were founded after 2000, suggesting that successive generations have embraced the trend. Gen X pastors (43%) now nearly equal Boomer pastors (47%). Taken together, Gen X and millennial pastors (51%) outnumber their Boomer counterparts. The study provides a more optimistic view of megachurches than most headlines show, the authors say. “Rather than retreating into self-centered postures, most megachurches report substantial engagement with their surrounding communities,” the study states. “Rather than representing a passing generation of religious participants, they continue to raise up younger leaders, launch new campuses, plant new churches, and attract newer cohorts.” But Wegner, the micro-church advocate, cautions about numbers. “There’s certain fragility to the large, predominant model. It looks big and strong,” Wegner previously told RR. “It’s really about the operating system underneath and less about the surface size.” He added, “That is ultimately what we stand for in Jesus and give an account for. That’s what going to get tested for wood, hay, stubble or gold.” Rebecca Hopkins is a journalist based in Colorado. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Louisiana pastor resentenced to 130 years for sexually abusing teen boys
item.author: Mark A. Kellner
item.dc:creator: Mark A. Kellner
item.description: Former Terrytown, Louisiana, pastor Terry Reed was resentenced to 130 years in prison on June 30 for multiple sex crimes, following a request by prosecutors for a harsher penalty. Judge Ray Steib of the 24th Judicial District Court initially sentenced Reed, 66, to 80 years in prison. But prosecutors filed a multiple bill June 30, […]
item.content:encoded: A former Louisiana pastor has been resentenced to 130 years in prison for sexually abusing two teenage boys he mentored and took into his home. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) Former Terrytown, Louisiana, pastor Terry Reed was resentenced to 130 years in prison on June 30 for multiple sex crimes, following a request by prosecutors for a harsher penalty. Judge Ray Steib of the 24th Judicial District Court initially sentenced Reed, 66, to 80 years in prison. But prosecutors filed a multiple bill June 30, seeking an enhanced sentence. The judge, finding Reed to be a habitual sex offender, agreed and extended the sentence. Prosecutors, in making their request, cited Reed’s 2017 conviction of indecent behavior with a juvenile and molestation of a juvenile. Reed pleaded guilty as charged in that case and received five years of probation. A Jefferson Parish jury deliberated less than an hour on May 6 before finding Reed guilty of two counts of third-degree rape and two counts of molestation of a juvenile. On June 18, Steib sentenced Reed to 25 years on each rape count, to be served at hard labor without parole or probation. Reed also received 15 years on each molestation count, with all four sentences running consecutively for a total of 80 years. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Reed ministered from his North Marlin Court home in Terrytown, where he mentored the victims, both of whom had troubled home lives. He allowed them to move in with him and used biblical passages to manipulate, normalize and justify his sexual behavior with them, prosecutors said. One victim began visiting Reed’s home in April or May of 2021, when he was 16. Reed sexually abused him, and the youth moved into Reed’s home in August, prosecutors said. The abuse continued for about three years, and the victim disclosed it to authorities at age 19, leading to Reed’s arrest in 2023. Those crimes formed the basis of the third-degree rape convictions. After Reed’s 2023 arrest became public, a 29-year-old man reported that Reed had also sexually abused him, starting in May 2011. He, too, had moved into Reed’s house, doing so at age 16. His abuse continued until he was 21, prosecutors said. Reed was charged with two counts of molestation of a juvenile for abuse that occurred before the victim turned 17. More than two decades earlier, however, Reed pleaded guilty to indecent behavior with a juvenile. Then, in 2017, he pleaded guilty to indecent behavior with a juvenile and molestation of a juvenile, receiving probation both times. In the 2017 case, he told the juvenile he had to submit to certain acts “to fight off demons.” “He has preyed on these very, very troubled boys who don’t have a father figure in their lives,” Assistant District Attorney James Wascom told jurors in closing arguments. Assistant District Attorney Eric Cusimano told jurors during the May 6, 2026, trial that Reed “held himself out to be a saint” to the victims and his congregation. Some members of the congregation testified on Reed’s behalf. “He is the worst kind of predator in this community that we can have,” Cusimano said. When Jefferson Parish Sheriff’s Office detectives arrested Reed in 2023, he admitted to being a sex offender. He said diabetes and prostate cancer left him unable to perform sexually and that he had not had sex in 10 years. He also said his sexual relations with the teens occurred when they were 17, the age of consent, meaning no crime occurred. Mark A. Kellner is a reporter based in Mesquite, Nevada. He most recently covered statewide elections for the New York Post and was for three years the Faith & Family Reporter for The Washington Times. Mark is a graduate of the University of the Cumberlands and also attended Boston University’s College of Communication. SHARE THIS:

item.title: HCU President Robert B. Sloan Jr., 77, influential leader in Christian higher education, dies
item.author: Mark A. Kellner
item.dc:creator: Mark A. Kellner
item.description: Robert B. Sloan Jr., 77, president of Houston Christian University (HCU) and former president of Baylor University, died July 4 after a stroke, the university announced. Describing the news as a profound loss for the institution and for Christian higher education, Houston Christian University board chairman Ramiro Peña said Sloan died Saturday morning in Houston.  […]
item.content:encoded: Robert Sloan Jr., who led Houston Christian University, died July 4 at age 77. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) Robert B. Sloan Jr., 77, president of Houston Christian University (HCU) and former president of Baylor University, died July 4 after a stroke, the university announced. Describing the news as a profound loss for the institution and for Christian higher education, Houston Christian University board chairman Ramiro Peña said Sloan died Saturday morning in Houston. In a statement, Peña said, “The fingerprints of Dr. Sloan’s leadership will continue to be upon the success of HCU for generations to come, and the impact of his presidency will yield outcomes both measurable and eternal.” Sloan had led Houston Christian University — known as Houston Baptist University until 2022 — since 2006. His tenure was marked by expanded academic programs, campus growth and a renewed emphasis on Christian mission. Under his leadership, the university launched its first doctoral program in executive educational leadership. Sloan and his trustees said the institution’s name change reflected a broader Christian identity while maintaining historic Baptist roots. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Robert Sloan Jr. with Houston Christian University students. (Photo courtesy of robertsloan.com) The university also launched Houston Theological Seminary, extending HCU’s reach in graduate and ministry training. Sloan oversaw development of the school’s College of Science and Engineering and guided its return to National Collegiate Athletic Association competition in 2011. They also adopted “The Ten Pillars: Faith and Reason in a Great City,” a long‑range strategic vision later updated as Ten Pillars 2030, which framed the university’s academic expansion and spiritual priorities. Before arriving at HCU, Sloan served as Baylor University’s president from 1995 to 2005 and its chancellor until 2006. During that time, Baylor — located in Waco, Texas — sharpened its research ambitions and articulated a distinctive Christian identity. Baylor president Linda A. Livingstone praised Sloan’s leadership at the institution she now leads. “His greatest contribution was the adoption of Baylor 2012, a 10‑year vision that placed Baylor on the path to become a top‑tier university while remaining committed to our historic Christian mission. And that is where Baylor stands today,” Livingstone said. Sloan, a native of Coleman, Texas, grew up in Abilene and graduated from Baylor in 1970. He later completed degrees at Princeton Theological Seminary and the University of Basel in Switzerland. He taught New Testament at Southwestern Baptist Theological Seminary and at Baylor, where he became founding dean of George W. Truett Theological Seminary. In that role, he helped establish the seminary’s curriculum and faculty. Tributes to Sloan from university officials, faculty and Christian leaders emphasized his commitment to integrating rigorous academics with evangelical faith. “Dr. Sloan was one of the most remarkable men I ever had the privilege of knowing, and most importantly he truly led the university with Christ at the center of every decision,” Houston Christian baseball coach Clay VanderLaan wrote on X, formerly Twitter. Author and speaker Lee Strobel, known for his work in Christian apologetics, also praised Sloan’s life. “Stunned to hear that President Robert Sloan of Houston Christian University passed into eternity this morning,” Strobel wrote. “He was always a great encourager when I was professor of evangelism there. Prayers for his wife, Sue, and family.” Beyond working in administrative roles, Sloan spent decades in ministry, serving as pastor and interim pastor at churches in Texas and Oklahoma while teaching and writing. From 2022 to 2026, he was co‑pastor of Heights Church in Houston alongside his son, Paul, continuing a long pattern of local church service alongside academic leadership. Sloan wrote numerous works of New Testament scholarship and contributed to the New Living Translation of the Bible in First and Second Corinthians. He also wrote the Hamelin Stoop series of young‑adult fantasy novels. Houston Christian University’s memorial tribute described him as a “faithful and humble servant of the Lord,” noting his “deep love of the scriptures,” quick wit, family traditions and habit of daily Bible reading and theological study. Sloan and his wife, Sue Collier Sloan, were married for 56 years and have seven children and 26 grandchildren. “He will be greatly missed, but we grieve with hope because of the promise of Christ’s return and resurrection,” the HCU memorial said. A public memorial service for Sloan will be held at 11 a.m. July 10 in the Linda and Archie Dunham Theater at the Morris Cultural Arts Center on the HCU campus. Mark A. Kellner is a reporter based in Mesquite, Nevada. He most recently covered statewide elections for the New York Post and was for three years the Faith & Family Reporter for The Washington Times. Mark is a graduate of the University of the Cumberlands and also attended Boston University’s College of Communication. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Family celebrates release of pastor imprisoned 9 months in China
item.author: Ann Marie Shambaugh
item.dc:creator: Ann Marie Shambaugh
item.description: The family of a pastor imprisoned in China for nine months is celebrating his release and publicly thanking everyone from the attorneys to U.S. President Donald Trump who made it happen. Ezra Jin Mingri, 57, founder and pastor of Zion Church, was released July 3 and reunited with family members in California. He spent nine […]
item.content:encoded: Ezra Jin Mingri, founder and pastor of Zion Church, was released from a Chinese prison after nine months in detention. (Courtesy photo) The family of a pastor imprisoned in China for nine months is celebrating his release and publicly thanking everyone from the attorneys to U.S. President Donald Trump who made it happen. Ezra Jin Mingri, 57, founder and pastor of Zion Church, was released July 3 and reunited with family members in California. He spent nine months in prison after Chinese officials conducted a multi-day raid of Zion Church locations, leading to 30 arrests. Zion Church is an unregistered Protestant congregation with an estimated 5,000 members worshiping at 100 underground sites in China, including apartments, restaurants and bars, across 40 cities. A source with close ties to the family told Roys Report (RR) that Jin, his wife, Anna Liu, and daughter, Grace Jin Drexel, are recuperating from the ordeal at a home the family owns near Chicago. They aren’t granting interviews at this time. But in a lengthy post on X July 5, originally posted in Chinese, Anna Liu expressed her gratitude for her husband’s release. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Pastor Ezra Jin Mingri with his daughter, Grace Jin Drexel. (Photo courtesy of X) “Thank God, who heard our countless urgent prayers,” she posted. “Just when my family and I were nearly overwhelmed by this immense pressure, He prepared this miraculous path for us. I know full well that this grace would not have been possible without everyone’s advocacy, so I also earnestly ask that we all remember together those who fought tirelessly to make this happen.” She also asked for “a little time to heal our wounds and recover our health.” “I promise it won’t be too long; everyone will see him again soon—the Pastor Jin who has lost 30 pounds suddenly, with hair even more gray, shoulders slightly stooped, but always carrying a warm smile, always willing to build others up,” she said. Chinese officials arrested Jin Oct. 11 after raiding his home in Beihai, Guangxi Province, the previous evening and conducting an all-night search. Jin was charged with “illegally using information networks,” according to The New York Times. U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio quickly condemned the raids, saying they demonstrated “how the (Chinese Communist Party) exercises hostility towards Christians who reject Party interference in their faith.” During diplomatic talks in May, Trump urged Chinese leader Xi Jinping to release Jin and Jimmy Lai, a pro-democracy entrepreneur and British citizen imprisoned since 2020. Lai is serving a 20 year prison sentence for colluding with foreign forces. ChinaAid, a nonprofit that promotes religious freedom, reported that the discussions led to Jin’s release as a “goodwill gesture coinciding with America’s Independence Day.” “We rejoice with Pastor Ezra Jin, his family, and the global Body of Christ on this remarkable Independence Day miracle,” said the Rev. Dr. Bob Fu, founder and president of ChinaAid. “We thank Almighty God for hearing the prayers of His people. We are deeply grateful to President Donald Trump, Secretary of State Marco Rubio, and all U.S. officials who worked tirelessly behind the scenes to secure Pastor Jin’s freedom. Their unwavering commitment demonstrates that principled leadership and persistent diplomacy can make a life-saving difference.” Jin’s release led former U.S. Vice President Mike Pence, who has often been publicly critical of Trump since the two men left office in January 2021, to praise the president’s role in the matter. “President Trump should be commended for securing Ezra Jin’s Release by raising the case with Xi Jinping in Beijing this year,” Pence posted July 6 on X. “Truly Treasure in Heaven to see this godly man of faith set free.” After Chinese officials shut down Zion Church’s Beijing headquarters in 2018, Jin broadcast his sermons online. Grace Jin Drexel told The New York Times that an estimated 10,000 people throughout China tuned in to hear his messages. She issued a statement on behalf of the family thanking Trump and his administration for their “tremendous leadership.” “We know that this could not have happened without the direct intervention from Chairman Xi Jinping,” the statement said as relayed by the Times. “We hope this is a signal of a positive turn for people of faith in China and relations between our two nations.” Jin’s release is worth celebrating, Fu of ChinaAid said in a press release. He added that eight other people with ties to Zion Church, as well as many practitioners of other religions, remain in Chinese prisons. “We respectfully call on President Trump and his administration to continue making religious freedom and the release of all prisoners of faith a top priority in every engagement with Beijing,” Fu said. “True progress in U.S.-China relations must include freedom for those imprisoned simply because they choose to believe.” In her post on X, Liu likewise named those from Zion Church still imprisoned and urged prayer and fasting for their release. “In this moment of reflecting on the past and looking toward the present, my heart remains heavy,” Liu posted. “I ask everyone to continue to fervently advocate for the 8 pastors and coworkers still in detention! They have never given up proving their innocence.” Grace Jin Drexel, daughter of Pastor Ezra Jin Mingri, testified before the U.S. Commission on International Religious Freedom in a hearing on Christian persecution Jan. 13. (Screen grab) Although China’s government claims it protects religious freedoms, the U.S. Commission on International Religious Freedom stated in its 2026 Annual Report that the nation “perpetuated particularly severe violations of religious freedom” in 2025. “Chinese authorities sought to exert complete control over religion through an extensive web of laws, regulations, and policies that do not conform to international human rights standards,” the report states. The U.S. State Department has listed China as a Country of Particular Concern, meaning it has engaged in or tolerated “particularly severe violations of religious freedom,” under the International Religious Freedom Act of 1998. Ann Marie Shambaugh has reported as a print journalist in multiple states, including currently in Carmel, Indiana. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Former California apologetics teacher pleads guilty to sexual abuse of girl
item.author: Ann Marie Shambaugh
item.dc:creator: Ann Marie Shambaugh
item.description: A former teacher at a California Christian school accused of sexually abusing a girl in his family over several years has pleaded guilty. Kevin Conover taught apologetics at Christian High School in El Cajon, California, a suburb of San Diego, for nearly 15 years, according to his LinkedIn profile. He began serving as vice principal […]
item.content:encoded: Kevin Conover taught apologetics at Christian High School in El Cajon, California, for nearly 15 years before pleading guilty to sexually abusing a family member. (Photo courtesy of Google Maps/LinkedIn) A former teacher at a California Christian school accused of sexually abusing a girl in his family over several years has pleaded guilty. Kevin Conover taught apologetics at Christian High School in El Cajon, California, a suburb of San Diego, for nearly 15 years, according to his LinkedIn profile. He began serving as vice principal of Christian Unified Schools in March 2020, though his LinkedIn profile does not contain an ending date for that position. In October 2025, the San Diego County Sheriff’s Office began investigating Conover after receiving a call that an underage girl was sexually abused by a family member. Police arrested Conover at the school on Dec. 16, 2025. He was charged with aggravated sexual abuse of a child under the age of 14, lewd and lascivious acts with a child and continuous sexual abuse of a child. Conover, 50, initially pleaded not guilty. He changed his plea June 24, the day before a scheduled preliminary hearing, to guilty on five felony sex crime counts, according to KGTV Ch. 10, an ABC affiliate in San Diego. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Shadow Mountain Community Church in El Cajon, California, which is affiliated with Christian High School, where Kevin Conover taught for nearly 15 years. (Photo courtesy of Shadowmountain.org) A judge is set to sentence Conover July 31 to 15 years to life in state prison, plus five years to run concurrently, according to a spokesperson for the San Diego County district attorney’s office. If Conover had been convicted on all original counts, he could have faced 111 years to life in state prison, KGTV reported. The reported abuse began when the victim was 7 and occurred between 2015 and 2019, according to KGTV. Christian Unified Schools Superintendent Bob Ruhlman told Roys Report (RR) in an email that the charges against Conover do not involve any students in the school system. “While we give thanks to God that justice is being served, the primary focus of our prayers is for the victim, whose bravery led to this outcome, as well as for all who have been harmed by these crimes,” Ruhlman said. Ruhlman directed all further questions to the San Diego County district attorney’s office. The school system suspended Conover’s employment after his arrest, according to a statement provided in December 2025 to KFMB Ch. 8, a CBS affiliate in San Diego. Author Tim LaHaye and his wife, Beverly, founded Christian Unified Schools of San Diego in 1965 “as an alternative to the secular curriculum that was being introduced in California’s public schools,” according to the school system’s website. Tim and Beverly LaHaye founded Christian Unified Schools of San Diego in 1965 as an alternative to secular public education. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) In addition to working at the school, Conover founded and taught apologetics for Educate for Life, a nonprofit that offers online and in-person classes. He also hosted a podcast for the nonprofit. Its latest episode posted on Dec. 8, 2025, the week before Conover’s arrest. Educate for Life did not respond to a request for comment. According to his LinkedIn profile, Conover previously worked as a teacher at Horizon Christian Academy in San Diego and as a youth pastor at Scott Memorial Community Church, now known as Grace Church San Diego. Anyone with information about the case is asked to contact the San Diego County Sheriff’s Child Abuse Unit at 858-285-6112 or anonymously by calling San Diego County Crime Stoppers at 888-580-8477. Ann Marie Shambaugh has reported as a print journalist in multiple states, including currently in Carmel, Indiana. SHARE THIS:

item.title: East Texas pastor arrested on three felony child sex charges, released on $150,000 bond
item.author: Mark A. Kellner
item.dc:creator: Mark A. Kellner
item.description: A Texas pastor accused of multiple sex offenses involving a child has been released on $150,000 bond after being arrested last week on three felony charges. Authorities said George G. Finney, 52, pastor of New Life Church in Kenefick — about an hour northeast of Houston — was arrested June 29 at his home in […]
item.content:encoded: George Finney, senior pastor of New Life Church in Kenefick, Texas, was arrested on felony charges including continuous sexual abuse of a child. (Photo courtesy of Google Maps/Liberty County Sheriff's Office) A Texas pastor accused of multiple sex offenses involving a child has been released on $150,000 bond after being arrested last week on three felony charges. Authorities said George G. Finney, 52, pastor of New Life Church in Kenefick — about an hour northeast of Houston — was arrested June 29 at his home in Dayton. He is charged with continuous sexual abuse of a child under 14 years of age, indecency with a child by exposure and assault on a family member, impeding breath. Investigators say the counts stem from alleged conduct on Jan. 23, 2024, the same day his first wife, Susan, died of undisclosed causes. Jail and court records show bond was set at $50,000 on each charge, for a total of $150,000. Records indicate Finney was released on bond July 1. According to Liberty County Sheriff’s Office Chief Deputy Billy Knox, Finney’s arrest followed an investigation that began after a minor disclosed the alleged abuse to an adult. The sheriff’s office told KTRK‑TV 13, an ABC affiliate in Houston, that a report against Finney was made on June 19. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. George Finney speaks at a National Day of Prayer observance in Liberty, Texas, in May 2026. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) As of last Friday, investigators had not publicly released further details about the allegations. Officials have said they are unaware of any additional victims but acknowledged there may be more. Authorities have not disclosed the circumstances of the investigation nor the age of the alleged victim. Bluebonnet News reported that “details surrounding the allegations, including the circumstances of the investigation and the ages of the alleged victim or victims, had not been released by investigators.” Finney has served as senior pastor of New Life Church in Kenefick since 2016, according to the church website. In a June 21 guest appearance, founding pastor James Holt told congregants Finney had been placed on “administrative leave.” ChurchLeaders.com reported the following as Holt’s comments: “Your pastor (Finney) is going to be on a sabbatical. For those of you that may be new and don’t know the term ‘sabbatical,’ let me use a term you might know—it’s called administrative leave.” As of Wednesday, Finney’s name remained displayed on a sign outside the church building, KTRK reported. But Finney was apparently immediately relieved of his pastoral duties once they learned of the allegations. “We, the Leadership of New Life Kenefick are aware of the serious allegations and criminal charges involving our Pastor George Finney,” the church said in a statement released to area media on Thursday. “From the moment we were made aware of these allegations, he was relieved of his duties here at our Church. “We are deeply saddened and our hearts go out to all who are being affected by this, especially the victim and her family,” the statement continued. The church added that the congregation “takes the safety and wellbeing of children and vulnerable individuals very seriously” and is “cooperating fully with law enforcement and the legal process.” Finney’s current wife, holding their newborn child, told KTRK she was “shocked” by the charges and said she does not “believe [Finney] would do something like this.” Bluebonnet News reported it attempted to reach Finney by telephone for comment but had not received a response. Initially, court documentation indicated Finney sought a court‑appointed attorney based on indigency, according to the local Vindicator newspaper. Subsequent records show he has retained Houston lawyer Chad Davis. Roys Report (RR) reached out to the church and attorney Davis for comment but did not receive a reply. Mark A. Kellner is a reporter based in Mesquite, Nevada. He most recently covered statewide elections for the New York Post and was for three years the Faith & Family Reporter for The Washington Times. Mark is a graduate of the University of the Cumberlands and also attended Boston University’s College of Communication. SHARE THIS:

item.title: South Carolina SBC pastor arrested for child sex crime
item.author: Rebecca Hopkins
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Hopkins
item.description: A Southern Baptist pastor from South Carolina has been arrested for the sexual exploitation of a minor after police found more than 25 sexually explicit images of children on his cellphone. Daniel Keith Rabon, 62, was arrested July 1 by the Lexington County sheriff’s office, court records show. He is the pastor of Taylor Street […]
item.content:encoded: Daniel Rabon, pastor of Taylor Street Baptist Church in Cayce, South Carolina, was arrested July 1 on a charge of sexual exploitation of a minor. (Photo courtesy of Google Maps/South Carolina Attorney General's Office) A Southern Baptist pastor from South Carolina has been arrested for the sexual exploitation of a minor after police found more than 25 sexually explicit images of children on his cellphone. Daniel Keith Rabon, 62, was arrested July 1 by the Lexington County sheriff’s office, court records show. He is the pastor of Taylor Street Baptist Church in Cayce, according to an archived site for the Lexington Baptist Association. Cayce is five miles southwest of Columbia, the centrally located state capital. The Southern Baptist Convention (SBC), which lists Taylor Street Baptist Church as a member church, is still trying to recover from a decades-long sex abuse scandal. The SBC’s newly elected president, Willy Rice, has said the sex abuse crisis was overblown. But survivors have expressed frustration at stalled reforms, the Houston Chronicle recently reported. The images on Rabon’s phone were of children either engaged in sex acts or nude, according to the arrest warrant issued by the Lexington County sheriff’s office on June 29. Police also have evidence from social media and statements from Rabon and his alleged victims. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Rabon faces a third-degree felony charge that could carry up to 10 years in prison. Authorities released Rabon on July 1 after he posted a $125,000 bond, according to court records for the Lexington County 11th Judicial District. He’ll be under GPS monitoring, reported WIS-TV Ch. 10, an NBC station based in Columbia. Roys Report (RR) unsuccessfully tried contacting Taylor Street Baptist Church for comment. Its Facebook page also appears to have been removed. The church did, however, give a statement to WIS-TV. “We at Taylor Street Baptist Church are shocked and in disbelief at the current situation we have found our church family in,” the statement said. “We are fully cooperating with the investigation and helping law enforcement in any way we can. At this time the only knowledge or information we have is what is already known to the public.” The church continued, “We were advised by law enforcement to remove our social media page to prevent further families from being targeted. We ask that all those that do to please pray for the victims and their families as well as the members of our congregation.” The South Carolina attorney general’s office will handle the case going forward, according to Capt. Adam Myrick, public information officer for the Lexington County sheriff’s office. Rebecca Hopkins is a journalist based in Colorado. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Thoughts on Ryan Stollar’s Suicide from a Fellow Male Survivor of CSA
item.author: David Bumgardner
item.dc:creator: David Bumgardner
item.description: Note: This article discusses child sexual abuse and suicide. (OPINION) There is no such thing as a perfect survivor. This is one of the primary takeaways I have gleaned from the recent tragic suicide of childhood sexual abuse survivor, advocate, and self-described child liberation theologian Ryan Stollar. In a heartbreaking note posted to his blog […]
item.content:encoded: Ryan Stollar, childhood sexual abuse survivor, advocate, and child liberation theologian. (Photo courtesy of Ivan Vranić/Unsplash and Facebook) Note: This article discusses child sexual abuse and suicide. (OPINION) There is no such thing as a perfect survivor. This is one of the primary takeaways I have gleaned from the recent tragic suicide of childhood sexual abuse survivor, advocate, and self-described child liberation theologian Ryan Stollar. In a heartbreaking note posted to his blog that was then emailed to his subscribers, Ryan shared that he no longer felt loved, worthy, or capable of receiving love and care. Particularly heart-wrenching were his repeated assertions that he was a burden to everyone he had ever known, including his family, his friends, and most tragically, his wife, who had recently filed for divorce. As various evangelical denominations continue to wrestle with the fallout of their various abuse crises, one of the most tragic and neglected subjects has been the steep challenges faced by male survivors of childhood sexual abuse (CSA). I am one such survivor. And I offer my reflections here as both survivor and theologian. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Ryan Stollar. (Photo courtesy of rlstollar.com) It is estimated that anywhere between 8 million and 26 million adult men in the United States are victims of childhood sexual assault. Contrary to popular slurs coming from a certain side of the political aisle, perpetrators of male childhood sexual abuse are not exclusively gay men, debunking the long-standing right-wing myth of the “gay predator.” Male perpetrators are often attracted not to features of manhood—as in adult homosexual relationships—but to properties of childhood, including hairlessness, innocence, and immature physical features. Furthermore, while male victims are predominantly abused by male perpetrators, the proportion of female perpetrators who target boys is substantially higher than those who target girls. For men who experienced abuse in childhood at the hands of a female perpetrator, the shame runs counter. A male victim with a male perpetrator will often wonder if the biological reactivity he experienced during the assault—due to the autonomic nature of the male reproductive system—means that he is secretly gay or effeminate. However, male survivors of female abuse suffer devastating consequences that are just as long-standing, volatile, and lethal. These include a profound distrust of women, romantic struggles, and high self-reported rates of marital, relationship, and sexual dissatisfaction. Male survivors who cope by exhibiting signs of post-traumatic anger are often misdiagnosed as having personality disorders, defiance disorders, or, in popular political parlance, are accused of “toxic masculinity.” Violence is never okay. But the stigmatization of male anger is undoubtedly a leading comorbidity in the tragic epidemic of suicide among male childhood sexual assault survivors. Tragically, Stollar exhibited such post-traumatic anger. Clinical frameworks, such as the Interpersonal Theory of Suicide, show that the acquired capability for suicide in heavily traumatized males frequently translates into violent, lethal, and highly decisive final actions. While men and women who have experienced childhood sexual abuse report comparable absolute rates of suicidality, men are much more likely to actually kill themselves due to access to weapons and substances. In Stollar’s case, this decisiveness extended also to his narrative of events. The anger in his note was, in a certain sense, deployed as a final shield against his abusers. In the exhausting psychology of severe trauma that accompanies male survivors for their lifespan, rage and anger often feel safer than the crushing powerlessness of vulnerability and the cultural stigma of emasculation. Clinicians often misinterpret survivor hostility or emotional detachment as a personality, anger, or defiance disorder rather than a symptom of deep trauma. Similarly, the public—and especially critics on social media—evaluate the actions of men in the middle of terminal psychological breakdowns through the lenses of ordinary, rational relationship dynamics. But this is a tragic misreading of Stollar’s case. Adult life stressors, particularly marital instability, divorce, and profound loneliness, do not merely cause standard emotional distress for the male survivor. They are profound psychological triggers that actively reactivate the neurobiology of the original childhood trauma. When a breakup or divorce occurs, it is experienced not just as the loss of a partner, but as a catastrophic reinforcement of the survivor’s core negative schema: that they are fundamentally unworthy, dirty, effeminate, weak, vulnerable, unlovable, and destined to be abandoned. Stollar was facing an impending divorce and severe isolation at the end of his life. To an observer without knowledge of clinical realities, his lashing out at family seems like calculated cruelty. One commentator even speculated in a nasty and violent tone that Stollar’s family was lucky he did not take them with him. But to those of us with eyes to see, including fellow male survivors, we understand Stollar’s anger and reactivity on a fundamentally deeper level. Stollar, like so many other men, collapsed entirely under the weight of a fractured attachment and a lifelong sense of unworthiness because of the horrendous evil wrought on him by perpetrators who violated natural, moral, and divine law. Dismissing Stollar’s suicide note as merely abusive is not only incredibly reductive, but harmful. It strips away the immense, agonizing context of the disease that caused it: the disease of male childhood sexual abuse, and the disease of lifelong loneliness and stigma. To accuse Stollar of abuse is to treat the symptom of a total psychiatric collapse as a simple, intentional, and malicious choice designed to harm his wife and family. At the same time, this does not grant Stollar absolution. A person can be entirely a victim of horrific abuse, a failed support system, and severe psychiatric morbidity, and simultaneously inflict deep emotional pain and trauma on living people left in their wake. Validating that the criticism toward Stollar is reductive does not mean ignoring the harm his suicide caused to his family or to the survivor community. A truly trauma-informed perspective allows for both realities to exist at once. As a theologian, I am particularly worried for the future of young men. As our culture and the church at large continues to effeminize—which is not necessarily a bad thing, given the church’s horrific history of patriarchal abuses—men are increasingly left behind. Visions of Christ-like manliness preserved in the New Testament, and in the lives and witnesses of the fathers and reformers, are now being supplanted by patriarchalism and other unbiblical, even satanic forms of masculinity. These come from the sewers of Doug Wilson, Nick Fuentes, and other manosphere influencers. These forms of manhood further inflict pain on male CSA survivors, reinforcing the stigma that they either desired the abuse, or that because they were not powerful enough to stop it, they are weak, effeminate “beta males.” The sneers of certain voices from the ideological left are not all that different from the sneers of manosphere influencers. For manosphere influencers, male CSA survivors are a threat to other young men. But to the left, these men appear threats to the women and children they attempt to care for, as they seek to undo the demonic power and generational strongholds of CSA. I, like millions of other men, am a victim of sexual assault twice over. Once by a male family member, and once by a female family member. I live with immense pain. But in the words of the Apostle Paul, I do not grieve as one who has no hope. Though I experienced a kind of death of innocence in my childhood, and though those who touched me did so in evil ways, I am reminded that Jesus Christ said: “Let the little children come to me, and do not hinder them, for to such belongs the kingdom of God.” In the Gospels, Jesus laid his hands upon children to bless them. The sad reality is that not everyone who lays their hands on children intends to bless them. Through tools like therapy, deep friendships, romantic relationships, studying the Scriptures, involvement in a local church, medication, seeing a psychiatrist, and other interventions, I have come to find healing, though I am not perfectly whole. I wait for the day when the Lord Jesus will take me to himself and heal me of every trauma and every pain that was ever inflicted upon me by those who claimed to love me. I believe this is only possible because our Lord Jesus, too, was abused. He was abused sexually as Roman soldiers stripped him naked. He was abused physically as government officials struck him. He experienced the immense pain of emotional abuse and betrayal as those who claimed to love him—the people of God—sneered at him, mocked him, and ultimately cried out for his execution on Good Friday. I have hope that because of Jesus’s abuse, one day the wounds I carry from my own experiences of abuse will be healed. As Isaiah says, by his stripes, we are healed. I grieve for Mr. Stollar’s family, though I take comfort in the hope that he may now be in the presence of his Savior. Ryan did not pretend to know his eternal destiny and neither do I. Those decisions are God’s alone. But I pray that despite his anger at other people, and his very public anger at God, Ryan Stollar is now in the gentle and merciful arms of Jesus, the Good Shepherd, the mighty Savior, and the guardian of children who said, “Let the little children come to me.” May Ryan Stollar rest in peace. And may the church learn to love and show Christlike compassion toward abuse victims like him, so fewer find such a tragic end. David Bumgardner is a writer and evangelical minister located in Northwest Ohio. He is a survivor of childhood abuse and journalist whose writings of clergy abuse and ministerial misconduct have been widely read.
