The document said the unlawful sexual conduct happened in Shelton’s truck as well as the woods near Cowan Lake, just north of Blanchester. According to WLWT-TV Ch. 5, Cincinnati’s NBC affiliate, Debbie was a member of Shelton’s church at the time of the abuse, just like Janet. She felt pressured to do what Shelton wanted during the time of her abuse because of her age and the relationship that he had with her family, Clinton County Prosecuting Attorney Brian Shidaker said. Shelton had gotten her a job working with him, and he was her pastor. Shelton was a local activist, advocating that the graphic novel “Heartstopper,” which featured a gay boy, not be sold at a book fair for the Little Miami School District. “It’s more than foul language,” he told WXIX-TV Ch. 19, the local Fox affiliate. “There’s so much sexual innuendo especially in ‘Heartstopper.’” Weeks before his arrest, Silas Shelton posted a video to Facebook reflecting on the biblical figure of Job and suffering, telling viewers “we still need humbled” even when life seems to be going right. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) Just weeks before his arrest, Shelton posted a video to Facebook about the biblical character of Job and how God allowed Satan to take everything from him. “Could it be that in a time in your life when you feel like everything is going right … that we still need humbled,” he said. In another post, he said, “If you’re anointed to do something big for God, you’ll often become isolated.” During his sentencing, Shelton asserted his innocence, saying, “I can tell you that I did not do these things that I’ve been accused of.” He also noted responsibilities he has to his son and his mother. “I want to take care of my mother,” he also said. “I want to be there for my son.” Clinton County Courts Judge Andrew McCoy chided Shelton, saying he never apologized for the crimes he committed. “When you take absolutely no accountability for your actions, you demonstrate no remorse,” McCoy told him. McCoy sentenced Shelton to 15 years in prison, according to WKRC-TV Ch. 12, a CBS affiliate in Cincinnati. That’s five years, the state maximum, for each count of unlawful sexual conduct. Shelton must also register as a Tier II sex offender for the next 25 years. Ohio has a three-tier registration system, with Tier III imposing the most stringent requirements on an offender. Peter Johnson is a student at Boston University in Boston, Massachusetts. He is studying his master’s in journalism. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Baptist Network created to oppose nationalism, foster Black church collaboration
item.author: Adelle Banks
item.dc:creator: Adelle Banks
item.description: Baptist leaders are creating a new network focused on Black churches and communities in light of what some see as growing nationalism among evangelicals and wider political decisions they feel are detrimental. “The conflating of faith with this rightward drift and the infringement not only on the autonomy of the church but all issues related […]
item.content:encoded: President Clint Pressley, on video screen, calls a vote during the Southern Baptist Convention annual meeting, Wednesday, June 10, 2026, at the Orange County Convention Center in Orlando, Fla. (RNS photo/Bob Smietana) Baptist leaders are creating a new network focused on Black churches and communities in light of what some see as growing nationalism among evangelicals and wider political decisions they feel are detrimental. “The conflating of faith with this rightward drift and the infringement not only on the autonomy of the church but all issues related to justice, the Voting Rights Act, the deification of (conservative activist) Charlie Kirk and all of these things have given us great concern,” Pastor Gregory Perkins, spokesperson for The Baptist Network, told media. Perkins, who is the immediate past president of the Southern Baptist Convention’s National African American Fellowship, said a meeting was scheduled for Monday (June 29) at the Arlington, Texas, church of Pastor Dwight McKissic. McKissic is one of the network’s conveners and a leader who successfully encouraged Southern Baptists to adopt resolutions criticizing the Confederate flag and condemning white supremacy in 2016 and 2017. Leaders expected to attend or join the network are affiliated with groups such as the American Baptist Churches USA, Cooperative Baptist Fellowship, Full Gospel Baptist Church Fellowship, Southern Baptist Convention and historic Black Baptist denominations such as the Progressive National Baptist Convention, National Baptist Convention of America and National Baptist Convention, USA. Their focus is not to become another denomination, but “a connector and a catalyzer,” Perkins said. Pastor Gregory Perkins, president of the National African American Fellowship of the SBC. (Courtesy photo) The network will comprise representatives of predominantly Black churches or denominations in North America but also aims to be racially inclusive, he added. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. The group, which plans to have its official launch in the fall at a meeting of dozens of leaders, said in a news release that they have “grown weary of being criticized or marginalized for addressing issues of race, immigration, poverty, and justice from a biblical perspective.” Perkins said organizers had been in conversation about developing a network long before the Southern Baptists’ recent vote that could lead to a ban on women pastors but “the current climate within the SBC helped inform our needs, perhaps in a more expedited fashion.” He said “like-minded Baptists” who are forming the new network also oppose the recent Supreme Court ruling “allowing the administration to take away the protected status of Haitian citizens, while inviting South African citizens to come.” The new network, which will include mentoring, training and missions partnerships, is one of several recent attempts to address issues of concern by faith leaders of color in a collaborative way. Perkins inaugurated the Black Collective of the Southern Baptist Convention at an event featuring representatives of various Caribbean and African fellowships just before the June annual meeting of the nation’s largest Protestant denomination. “I wanted to send a message of unity amongst the diaspora, but also amongst the broader SBC family, because there had been this notion that we somehow were working in cross purposes,” he said. A broader gathering featuring Black church leaders, The Black People’s Convention, is tentatively planned for February 2027 in Maryland, said Bishop Erika Crawford of the African Methodist Episcopal Church. It would be a revival of an 1830 event in which African Methodist Episcopal Church founder Richard Allen brought together Black leaders of different faiths and organizations at his Philadelphia church. “Today, we face a different moment — but the same urgency,” reads a description on a website about the upcoming convention. “Across this nation, we are witnessing the erosion of civil rights, widening economic gaps, threats to education, and ongoing challenges to our communities’ health, safety, and stability.” Perkins said he thinks the various initiatives are sparked by a period where there are “coordinated actions that are intentionally disadvantaging communities of color, specifically Black and brown communities.” “I do believe that there’s a coming together,” he said. Adelle Banks is production editor and a national correspondent at Religion News Service. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Child liberation theologian R.L. Stollar dies
item.author: Mark Wingfield
item.dc:creator: Mark Wingfield
item.description: Child liberation theologian and abuse survivor advocate Ryan L. Stollar died June 28 at age 42. The author, speaker and counselor left a detailed note titled “The End” on his website. According to that note, his death was not an impulsive decision but one he had been contemplating for years. The note indicates a pending divorce from […]
item.content:encoded: Ryan L. Stollar, author of "The Kingdom of Children" and co-founder of Homeschoolers Anonymous. (Photo courtesy of rlstollar.com) Child liberation theologian and abuse survivor advocate Ryan L. Stollar died June 28 at age 42. The author, speaker and counselor left a detailed note titled “The End” on his website. According to that note, his death was not an impulsive decision but one he had been contemplating for years. The note indicates a pending divorce from his wife may have been a factor in his timing but was not the reason for his death. Multiple close friends have confirmed in online posts that Stollar has passed, although there has been no official announcement. In his final blog post, he wrote of his struggles with depression, anxiety, bipolar disorder, substance abuse, PTSD and suicidal ideation. He also cited the “fascist hellscape” he saw in America today and his despair over that. “I have spent my whole life making excuses for a god who never raised a finger to spare me from being molested as a child or the lifelong consequences of that abuse,” he wrote. “Those consequences have slowly but surely consumed everything in my life, including now my marriage and soon here me as well.” Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Ryan L. Stollar’s 2023 book, “The Kingdom of Children,” which centers children in theology and ecclesial life. (Photo courtesy of rlstollar.com) Homeschooled from kindergarten through high school graduation, he worked eight years as a public communications educator for high school students in homeschooling communities. He authored the first and only comprehensive curricula on child abuse awareness and suicide prevention specifically tailored to homeschooling families and communities. He also was author of the 2023 book The Kingdom of Children: A Liberation Theology, published by Eerdmans. Publicity for the book described it as “a liberation theology of the child. Stollar begins with a theoretical framework that centers children in our theology and ecclesial life. Reframing biblical stories to center children, we can see how the binding of Isaac reflects the spiritual effects of child abuse, or how children like Miriam can serve as leaders in their communities. Using scriptural examples as well as real studies of children’s spiritual lives, Stollar asserts that children can be priests, prophets, and theologians in our communities.” Stollar co-founded the website Homeschoolers Anonymous, and his work was cited in other bestselling books, including Jesus and John Wayne by Kristin Kobes Du Mez. Stollar earned a master of health science degree in child protection from Nova Southeastern University and a master of arts degree in Eastern classics from St. John’s College. He lived in the California Bay Area. Stollar’s rejection of evangelical theology reflected his own personal wounds and a reaction to authoritarian structures and a theology that equated submission with holiness. His last known writing aside from the death note was a blog post dated June 27, which argued that the notion taught in some evangelical circles that “you are not your own” enabled abuse. Evangelicals fear the exvangelical movement because those who leave evangelicalism understand what they have experienced was, in fact, traumatic, he wrote. “The more evangelicals avoid looking at how their theology itself is actively damaging to many people, the stronger the #exvangelical movement will become.” Other notable anti-abuse advocates, who share Stollar’s concern for child protection, argue that the harms Stoller exposed are not core to evangelicalism but a distortion of Jesus’ teaching by sinful and misguided leaders. Leading trauma psychologist and author, Diane Langberg, writes, “Jesus’s strongest rebukes were for those religious leaders who used the words of God to crush and control humanity.” Last year, BNG columnist Rick Pidcock interviewed Stollar for his “Highest Power: Church + State” podcast. “Ryan Stollar was a rare beacon of rest for those of us who felt traumatized by the abusive theology of conservative evangelical patriarchy,” Pidcock said. “After John MacArthur, Voddie Baucham and James Dobson passed away in three successive months, I interviewed Stollar to help those of us who survived their teaching process the wounds they caused. “Stollar told me: ‘Child liberation theology is a call to us as adults that we need to change our ways. We have to create communities in our churches, in our schools, even in our government, where there’s space for children to be seen and heard as fully human.’ I’ll always remember him as someone who, more than anything else, loved the white evangelical empire’s ‘least of these.’” The Roys Report contributed to this article, which was first published by Baptist News Global. Mark Wingfield, who is based in Dallas, Texas, serves as executive director and publisher of Baptist News Global. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Blogger, former pastor J.D. Hall spreads antisemitic views on Tucker Carlson’s podcast
item.author: Sarah Einselen
item.dc:creator: Sarah Einselen
item.description: New Christian Right blogger and former pastor Jordan “J.D.” Hall appeared on Tucker Carlson’s podcast last week as he rebuilds his media platform, weeks after completing court-ordered restitution to his former church. Hall took aim at dispensationalist theology and corruption within American evangelical theology, mixing in antisemitic remarks throughout. At one point he blamed the […]
item.content:encoded: Former pastor Jordan "J.D." Hall appears alongside Tucker Carlson on Carlson's podcast to discuss Christian Zionism. (RR Graphic) New Christian Right blogger and former pastor Jordan “J.D.” Hall appeared on Tucker Carlson’s podcast last week as he rebuilds his media platform, weeks after completing court-ordered restitution to his former church. Hall took aim at dispensationalist theology and corruption within American evangelical theology, mixing in antisemitic remarks throughout. At one point he blamed the American church’s support for Israel on what he described as “Jewish talking points that you see on the television and the news constantly.” Hall also recommended a publishing house that hosted a neo-Nazi vendor at its conference earlier this month. “Bigger picture, it feels like the story of Christianity is a universal promise to all people,” Carlson said on the podcast. “And dispensationalism seems like it reduces Christianity back to its pre-Jesus state where, no, this is a promise just to one group, one genetic line.” Hall agreed. “Yeah, I would say at its heart, dispensationalism is a Jewish interpretive model,” he responded. “As a matter of fact, they will actually take that as a point of bragging that we interpret the Scripture the way the Jews interpret the Scripture. Well, the Jews missed Jesus. … So maybe that’s not who we should be taking pointers from in terms of understanding biblical prophecy.” Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. J.D. Hall discusses Christian Zionism during an appearance on Tucker Carlson’s podcast. (Screen grab) Hall also described Israel’s participation in the current conflict in Iran as buying “bombs to blow up more children over there, whether it’s Lebanon or Gaza or in Iran.” “Here we have this whole Jewish/Christian cottage industry where we are collecting millions of dollars every Sunday into the offering plate to go directly to the Israeli state, not to share the gospel, not to tell anyone about Jesus and the good news of what Jesus did on the cross to save sinners from the wrath of God, but to give money essentially to a genocide fund,” he told Carlson. “And so it’s like, if you love Jesus first and foremost, if you’re a Christian, tell the Jews about Jesus because God wants to bring them to a saving faith in the king that they’ve rejected. And then just like anyone else that you love, just because you love them doesn’t mean you necessarily give them what they want, because what Jews want from Christians is political clout.” Tucker Carlson interviews pastor Doug Wilson. (Screengrab) Carlson, a former Fox News personality, promoted the 102-minute podcast episode as an interview with a “theologian” on “the corrupting lie of Christian Zionism.” Hall pastored Fellowship Baptist Church in Sidney, Montana, until he was fired in 2022 over his failure to repent of prescription drug dependency, Roys Report previously reported. Hall faced embezzlement charges regarding church funds in 2024 and was ordered to pay back more than $15,000 to Fellowship Baptist. He did so as part of a deferred sentencing agreement, The Christian Post reported earlier this month, and in March, the charges were dismissed and court records sealed. But having been disqualified from ministry by Fellowship Baptist, Hall has said he has no wish to return to the pastorate. Jordan “J.D.” Hall speaks about God and politics at the home of Montana State Senator Theresa Manzella (R) in October 2021. (Screengrab) He has, however, joined far-right pastors Joel Webbon and Calvin Robinson, among others, who bill themselves as the “New Christian Right.” Webbon is a Texas pastor known for racist and antisemitic views, as well as his opinion that women should not vote. Robinson was defrocked last year over a Nazi-like salute he displayed while speaking at a conference. That trio and others run NXR Studios, where they publish podcasts and other media. NXR Studios recently hosted white supremacist Nick Fuentes on its podcast and features the interview prominently on its website. Webbon promoted Hall’s appearance with Carlson as well. Carlson was openly condemned earlier this year at an event sponsored by the Jewish Republican Coalition, and the group StopAntisemitism has dubbed him “Antisemite of the Year.” One speaker Hall targeted in Carlson’s podcast was Ron Cantor, president of Shelanu TV, which broadcasts Christian content in Israel. “Ron Cantor is a Christian celebrity in Israel, and he was on X one day talking about how loving and kind Israel is to him as a Jew who believes in Jesus,” Hall told Carlson. “And they will phrase it that way on purpose so as to not imply that they’re a Christian, because if they say they’re a Christian, they might have to leave. So they’ll say ‘Jew who believes in Jesus.’ They’re trying to work the system.” Ron Cantor (Courtesy Photo) Cantor struck back on Facebook Thursday that Hall lied about Cantor’s ministry when Hall alleged that Cantor “had to promise that he would not give the gospel to Jewish people.” “I made no such promise. NEVER EVER,” Cantor wrote (emphasis original). “Sharing the Good News with the Jewish people of Israel is the entire purpose of our channel.” Cantor also took issue with other statements Hall made, writing at length on Facebook and appearing in a video posted Thursday to explain his positions and how his ministry operates in Israel. He wrote he would be willing to appear on Carlson’s podcast to correct the record, though he doubted that opportunity would come. Cantor has been a guest on the Roys Report podcast. This wouldn’t be the first time Hall has been accused of lying. Hall was best known for founding the polemics blog Protestia, formerly named Pulpit & Pen. He used it to criticize what he viewed as liberal or worldly influences within evangelicalism and the Southern Baptist Convention, as previously reported by Religion News Service. But Hall also admitted to fabricating a story published in 2021 in one of his outlets. Sarah Einselen is an award-winning writer and editor based in Saskatchewan, Canada. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Son of Australian pastor says he raped a minor to ‘feel something’
item.author: Jessica Morris
item.dc:creator: Jessica Morris
item.description: A 24-year-old childcare worker and pastor’s son from Melbourne has narrowly avoided years in prison, after pleading guilty to a child sex crime. At a hearing in the County Court of Victoria on Tuesday, Judge Kellie Blair ruled that former Encourage Church youth leader Joshua Emonson will spend 12 months in prison. The judge knew […]
item.content:encoded: Joshua Emonson, a former youth leader at Encourage Church in Romsey, Australia. (RR Graphic) A 24-year-old childcare worker and pastor’s son from Melbourne has narrowly avoided years in prison, after pleading guilty to a child sex crime. At a hearing in the County Court of Victoria on Tuesday, Judge Kellie Blair ruled that former Encourage Church youth leader Joshua Emonson will spend 12 months in prison. The judge knew the ruling was light. But Blair stated that Emonson’s actions against a 13-year-old girl were “impulsive rather than predatory,” and therefore made under “exceptional circumstances.” After his release, Emonson will be listed as a registered sexual offender for 15 years and complete 2,000 hours of community service. Encourage Church, formerly led by Emonson’s father Tim Emonson and located in Romsey, 38 miles north of Melbourne, has split over allegations of cover up. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Joshua Emonson, a childcare worker and pastor’s son, was sentenced to 12 months for a child sex crime. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) The victim, who was unrelated to the church or the associated daycare center where Emonson worked, has moved to another state. Emonson wanted to “feel something” “Obviously I knew it was wrong. I just couldn’t stop myself,” Emonson told police, according to the Australian Broadcasting Corporation. “I needed to feel something. I knew she must have been underage,” he said. Emonson used Wizz and Snapchat, two apps popular with Australian teens, to connect with his victim before the two met Feb. 20, 2025. He used a burner phone and arranged to meet her at the Sunbury train station, in Melbourne’s northern suburbs. He raped her in his sports car for approximately 20 minutes before driving off. She was left alone at the station. It happened just hours after Emonson left his mother-in-law’s funeral. The court learned that Emonson knew the girl was 13 and told her he “wanted to be her first.” After the assault, Emonson told her not to tell anyone. The victim later recounted the event to an adult who filed a report with state police, and Emonson was arrested in March 2025. Encourage Church, a small congregation of approximately 70, split in two in the aftermath. Claims of cover-up On hearing about the allegations against Emonson, the board of Encourage Church, led by Emonson’s father, did not reveal details to the church or daycare center. Named Kidzone, the daycare centre is run as a separate entity to the church, but it is advertised as a Christian service run from the same location. Church members later criticized this decision, arguing the church had a duty of care to share the allegations publicly due to Emonson’s proximity to children. Members of the board, however, insisted they had shared only limited information because “the police asked us not to identify the alleged offender.” The board told the Australian Broadcasting Corporation “prompt action” was taken and that there was “no indication” Emonson had harmed any children in the youth group or daycare. The senior Emonson denied claims from those inside the church that its leaders were hiding information about his son’s criminal behavior. As a result, multiple church members left, including Pastors Tino and Mia Fichera. “The fact that he was a senior pastor’s son, that gave him a level of trust without saying anything,” Pastor Fichera said in an interview with the Australian Broadcasting Association. “The community is so small and so tight knit. The church has become part of the fabric of the community and unfortunately many people have been affected by it, and it has caused a divide.” Emonson’s arrest triggered several investigations. The Victoria Police, Australian Christian Churches (ACC) and the Victoria Education Department each investigated the church and daycare’s compliance with child safety standards and looked into any potential cover-up. In September, Pastor Tim Emonson was suspended by the ACC, while the denominational governing body investigated the claims. Tim Emonson, former senior pastor of Encourage Church in Romsey, Australia. (Courtesy photo) “During this process, information emerged that Pastor Tim had been made aware of concerns about his son’s behavior with underage females on several occasions over the preceding three years,” the ACC told ABC News. “As a result, Pastor Tim’s credential was suspended, and the church board was advised to place him on paid leave, which they did.” Joshua Emonson denied the ACC’s implication that there were additional allegations made against him, calling them “untrue.” Facing the music In February, Emonson pleaded guilty to sexually penetrating a child under the age of 16 years old. He was put on bail before being sentenced last week. Emonson appeared in court Tuesday from the Melbourne Assessment Prison through a video link. He was supported by his family members and people from Encourage Church. Other members of the congregation were there in protest, jeering and giving him the finger as the ruling was announced. Emonson’s lawyer, Samuel Tovey, told the court his client’s actions were a result of a “cascading series of events and decisions,” stemming from a pornography addiction. Elise Heerde, mental health practitioner and co-founder of The Religious Trauma Collective AUS/NZ. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) He said Emonson had since gone through rehabilitation and accepted responsibility for his behavior. Tovey also described Emonson’s experience since arrest as “torrid.” He has since divorced, experienced abuse from the community and has lost his place on a local soccer team. Emonson received a sentence of 12 months in prison. The judge told the court her sentence considered Emonson’s character references, his remorse and his commitment to change. She also mentioned the well-being of the young victim. However, Mental Health Practitioner Elise Heerde, co-founder of The Religious Trauma Collective AUS/NZ, said the sentence reflects a systemic lack of compassion toward victims of sexual assault. “What the legal process communicates to survivors, over and over, is that your experience of harm is one data point among many,” Heerde wrote on Medium. “He got 12 months, with the acknowledgment that without a guilty plea he would have received two years and eight months. The message to every survivor watching this case is clear: The system will hear you. It just won’t prioritize you.” Emonson is currently facing separate charges of grooming and committing sexual acts against a 16-year-old in 2024. He has not entered a plea for these charges. RR reached out to Encourage Church for comment but did not hear back before publication. Jessica Morris is a music journalist, podcaster and author based in Melbourne, Australia. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Texas church rejects minister who covered up son’s sexual abuse
item.author: Daniel Silliman
item.dc:creator: Daniel Silliman
item.description: A midsized Texas church voted Sunday evening not to rehire a pastor who covered up his son’s sexual abuse. Members of Heritage Baptist Church, in Haslet, about 15 miles north of Fort Worth, cast paper ballots to decide whether they wanted to welcome Eric Crawford back to the pulpit. A majority of the approximately 180 […]
item.content:encoded: Eric Crawford, founder and former pastor of Heritage Baptist Church in Haslet, Texas. (RR Graphic) A midsized Texas church voted Sunday evening not to rehire a pastor who covered up his son’s sexual abuse. Members of Heritage Baptist Church, in Haslet, about 15 miles north of Fort Worth, cast paper ballots to decide whether they wanted to welcome Eric Crawford back to the pulpit. A majority of the approximately 180 voters — 56% — said yes. But the congregation’s bylaws require pastors to receive three-quarters of the vote to get hired. Crawford, who founded the Independent Fellowship Baptist church in 2000 and Heritage Christian Academy in 2014, sped away in an SUV after the vote, according to Jennifer Wedel, a local mother protesting outside. “He got more votes than I would have liked,” Wedel told Roys Report (RR). “But they didn’t let him weasel back in.” Multiple sources confirmed the vote total on Monday morning. Heritage did not respond to an email from RR. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Former Heritage Baptist pastor Eric Crawford with his son Caleb Crawford, a registered sex offender who worked at the church’s school. (Screen grab) Crawford was placed on leave in February and stepped down in March after several parents discovered his son Caleb Crawford is a registered sex offender. The younger Crawford, 31, led music at the church and worked as a janitor and maintenance man at the school, which is on church property. Parents said they were not informed of his criminal past. And when they asked about the decision to employ Caleb Crawford, they said that school officials told them he wasn’t technically employed, since he was an independent contractor. “I don’t care if he’s (an) independent contractor,” Ryan Gunderson, a father of three students at Heritage, told KXAS-TV Ch. 5, an NBC affiliate in North Texas. “He’s here with my kids every single day, and he’s in contact with them and a lot of the kids.” Videos of school and church events show Caleb Crawford, who was convicted in 2016 of sexually touching a 12-year-old girl, was frequently around children and on stage at school and church events. Pastor called controversy ‘unnecessary distraction’ Eric Crawford said in a statement in February that he had thought it was fine to hire his son since the legal proceedings “concluded several years ago.” “My intent was to allow his gifts to serve the ministry while ensuring that he held no role of responsibility or authority over minors entrusted to our care,” the pastor said. The elder Crawford expressed regret that his son’s employment had become an “unnecessary distraction.” Several parents did not feel that was adequate. Some started a petition asking Eric Crawford to resign and calling on the church to review the school’s hiring practices. The independent congregation is part of an association of Baptist churches started by fundamentalist J. Frank Norris, frequently called “God’s Rascal.” The elder Crawford has served in several leadership positions in the Baptist association. The fellowship does not credential ministers, however, and each congregation is autonomous. Heritage, which was built on a 17.5-acre ranch and expanded with a new auditorium in 2012, advertises itself as a church “committed to the faithful preaching of God’s word” and “gospel outreach.” Heritage Christian Academy is a ministry of the church. It has about 140 students and promises parents that “Biblical integration into all classes is a priority.” Wedel, the local protestor, said she’s not religious but liked the idea of sending her daughter to a school that takes faith and the Bible seriously. And she appreciated how much emphasis the school put on safety. “They went to great lengths to talk about how safe they were going to keep our children,” she told RR. “They were going on and on about how safe they were. But the predator we needed to worry about was inside.” The state of Texas says there is a moderate risk that Caleb Crawford will reoffend. He is required to register as a sex offender every year for the rest of his life. Victim came forward Crawford’s cousin Taylor Hamilton came forward to identify herself as his victim earlier this year. “This isn’t just an accusation,” Hamilton told KDFW-TV Ch. 4, the Dallas-Fort Worth Fox affiliate. “This is something that he was convicted of and served time for. No matter what anyone says, he was found guilty.” Hamilton said she went to live with the Crawfords when she was 9 because her mother died of cancer. The sexual abuse started when she was 12 and Caleb Crawford was 17 and continued for two years until he left for college. “I couldn’t sleep at night,” Hamilton said. She reported the abuse after she moved out at 16. When Caleb Crawford was convicted of “indecency with a child by contact” and sent to prison in 2016, Hamilton never expected that a decade later he’d be working at a Christian school. The thought he might hurt another child terrified her, she said. “I ran to the bathroom, tears behind my eyes, ready to drown in tears thinking that something happened to another kid,” Hamilton told KDFW. “I have a responsibility.” To date, there are no allegations that Caleb Crawford hurt anyone else at the church or school. The church’s last statement, released in March, reiterated the importance of children’s safety. “We recognize the seriousness of these concerns,” the statement posted on Facebook said. “While we cannot address every question publicly, we remain committed to ongoing communication. … Heritage Baptist, along with its academy and daycare ministries, remain focused on providing a safe, stable, and Christ-centered environment.” Demands for accountability Eric Crawford resigned on March 1. Three months later, however, he sought to return to leadership. According to Katie Thompson, a former member of Heritage Baptist, a search committee led by the interim minister did not interview any other candidates. The committee recommended him for the job, and the hiring decision went to the congregation for a vote. Wedel and four other people protested outside on Sunday, holding signs that said, “This church is not safe,” “Honk if you believe in protecting children” and “Accountability before leadership.” Some attending the services on Sunday said the protestors would be punished by God, Wedel told RR. But others expressed support and told them accountability is important at Heritage. Wedel said she did not know how the vote would go. But she is grateful that 44% of the congregation believe that someone who covered up sexual abuse should not be a pastor. “You got to ask yourself, ‘Why do predators get away with it?’” Wedel said. “It’s because there’s no accountability and there’s no one forcing them to have accountability.” Daniel Silliman is senior reporter/editor at The Roys Report. He began his two decades in journalism covering crime in Atlanta and has since led major investigations into abuse and misconduct in Christian contexts. Daniel and his wife live in Johnson City, Tennessee. SHARE THIS:

item.title: FCC fines NYC church $20,000 for alleged pirate radio
item.author: Mark A. Kellner
item.dc:creator: Mark A. Kellner
item.description: A church in the Bronx, along with two pastors, stands accused of operating a pirate radio station. The station illegally uses an FM frequency that is licensed to a Long Island station, according to the Federal Communications Commission (FCC). The FCC, in a Notice of Apparent Liability for Forfeiture released June 25, said Sweet Hour […]
item.content:encoded: The FCC has proposed a $20,000 fine against a Bronx church and two of its pastors for allegedly operating a pirate radio station. (RR Graphic) A church in the Bronx, along with two pastors, stands accused of operating a pirate radio station. The station illegally uses an FM frequency that is licensed to a Long Island station, according to the Federal Communications Commission (FCC). The FCC, in a Notice of Apparent Liability for Forfeiture released June 25, said Sweet Hour of Prayer church, Pastor Neville Morgan and Pastor Lilleth Morgan apparently operated a radio station on 103.5 FM in the Bronx. But, the agency, said, 103.5 FM is assigned in the New York market to WKTU-FM, a rhythmic adult contemporary station licensed to Lake Success, New York, and owned by iHeartMedia. The government agency proposes a $20,000 fine. Federal officials say they started investigating when they received complaints that an unlicensed signal was interfering with WKTU-FM’s broadcasts. FCC agents traced the unauthorized broadcasts to the church’s location and to equipment under the pastors’ control, according to an FCC statement. The Morgans allegedly violated Section 301 of the Communications Act. Section 301 prohibits anyone from operating a radio station without a federal license. The agency said the operation also appeared to violate rules protecting licensed FM signals from interference. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Pastors Neville and Lilleth Morgan, who the FCC alleges operated an unlicensed radio station out of their Bronx church. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) The FCC’s Enforcement Bureau proposed the statutory maximum penalty, citing recent amendments that sharply increased fines. The notice also said the alleged violations continued after agents warned the church and pastors to cease the radio operations. Under the FCC’s rules, only noncommercial educational licensees may lawfully operate low-power FM, or LPFM, stations. They must apply during designated filing windows, obtain construction permits and meet interference-protection standards. LPFM stations are limited to 100 watts effective radiated power at 30 meters height above the average terrain and typically cover only a three-to-five-mile radius. They must protect full-power facilities like WKTU-FM through minimum distance separations and other technical safeguards designed to prevent disruption of licensed service. The FCC said Sweet Hour of Prayer and the Morgans did not hold any LPFM or full-power license authorizing use of any FM radio frequency. According to the notice, interference with a full-power station in New York’s densely populated radio market can affect large audiences and commercial operations. The agency said this was another reason for the proposed maximum penalty. Under Section 503(b) of the Communications Act, a Notice of Apparent Liability for Forfeiture is not a final penalty. The commission said the church and the Morgans have an opportunity to respond and argue that no violation occurred. They could also ask that the proposed penalty be canceled or reduced. If the pastors decline to pay and the FCC issues a forfeiture order, the government must seek enforcement in federal court, where the respondents can contest both the facts and application of the law. The FCC has warned religious organizations and other community groups that they must obtain proper authorization before going on the air, even at low power. Advocates of LPFM broadcasts say legal community radio remains available through licensed noncommercial channels, but enforcement actions underscore that pirate operations face substantial financial risk. The Roys Report (RR) has reached out to Sweet Hour of Prayer for comment. Mark A. Kellner is a reporter based in Mesquite, Nevada. He most recently covered statewide elections for the New York Post and was for three years the Faith & Family Reporter for The Washington Times. Mark is a graduate of the University of the Cumberlands and also attended Boston University’s College of Communication. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Asbury Theological Seminary cut by United Methodist Church over same-sex marriage issue
item.author: Adelle Banks
item.dc:creator: Adelle Banks
item.description: Asbury Theological Seminary, a 103-year-old Kentucky evangelical seminary, is no longer approved as a school for United Methodist candidates for ordained ministry, the school announced June 25. It attributed its removal by the United Methodist Church’s university accrediting body to the school’s objections to a new version of the denomination’s social principles that no longer […]
item.content:encoded: The Henry Clay Morrison Administration Building at Asbury Theological Seminary in Wilmore, Ky. (Photo courtesy of Asbury Theological Seminary/Wikimedia/Creative Commons) Asbury Theological Seminary, a 103-year-old Kentucky evangelical seminary, is no longer approved as a school for United Methodist candidates for ordained ministry, the school announced June 25. It attributed its removal by the United Methodist Church’s university accrediting body to the school’s objections to a new version of the denomination’s social principles that no longer solely affirm heterosexual marriage. In its acknowledgment of the decision, the seminary based in Wilmore, Kentucky, said “this outcome reflects a determination made by the UMC rather than a shared resolution.” A statement from the UMC General Board of Higher Education and Ministry said that the June meeting of the denomination’s University Senate included the removal of both Asbury and Northeastern Seminary in Rochester, New York, from its “list of approved non-United Methodist schools.” The organization, which determines which schools may be attended by United Methodist ministerial students, also said it was notified during its most recent review period that Palmer Theological Seminary in St. Davids, Pennsylvania, and Luther Seminary in St. Paul, Minnesota, requested removal from the same list. But the new juncture with Asbury was notable due to recent UMC developments. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Northeastern Seminary in Rochester, New York, was removed alongside Asbury from the UMC’s list of approved non-United Methodist schools. (Photo courtesy of nes.edu) Asbury President David Watson said in a statement, “The Senate’s requirements, particularly those related to the UMC’s 2024 Social Principles concerning ‘Human Sexuality’ and ‘Marriage,’ are not aligned with Asbury Theological Seminary’s institutional ethos and the historic witness of the Christian faith.” The statement continued, “We affirm marriage as sanctioned by God, which joins one man and one woman in a single, exclusive union for life, as delineated in Scripture, and provides the sole context for sexual intimacy, helping to ensure the blessings of that relationship as God intended.” The denomination’s social principles were revised during its quadrennial 2024 General Conference, when delegates voted to remove from the UMC’s rule book a statement added in 1972 that called homosexuality “incompatible with Christian teaching.” “Within the church, we affirm marriage as a sacred, lifelong covenant that brings two people of faith, an adult man and woman of consenting age, or two adult persons of consenting age into union with one another,” reads a portion of the section on marriage in the revised principles. The UMC statement said its Commission on Theological Education decided “Asbury’s published ethos statement is not compatible with the Social Principles of The United Methodist Church. In addition, Asbury does not have a full-time United Methodist faculty member who consistently teaches required United Methodist history, doctrine, and polity.” A lack of a United Methodist faculty member was also among the reasons cited for the delisting of Northeastern Seminary, as well as its primarily offering online programs. United Methodist students currently enrolled or matriculating at the four schools in fall 2026 will be allowed to complete their programs and pursue ordination in the denomination. But after this fall, students pursuing United Methodist ordination must attend schools that remain approved by the University Senate. The United Methodist bishop of the region where Asbury is located addressed the mixed emotions the decision about the seminary is expected to prompt. Bishop David Graves, leader of the UMC Kentucky-Tennessee Episcopal Area. (Photo courtesy of sgaumc.org) “Many in our conference will receive this news with disappointment and sadness,” wrote Bishop David Graves, leader of the UMC Kentucky-Tennessee Episcopal Area. “I share those feelings. For many clergy and laity across Kentucky and Tennessee, Asbury is more than an institution. It is a place where faith was deepened, callings were clarified, friendships were formed, and lives were changed.” Mark Tooley, president of the conservative Institute on Religion and Democracy, posted on X: “Asbury Seminary is the largest theologically Wesleyan seminary in the world. It will easily outlast the shrinking United Methodist Church.” In its statement, Asbury noted that the decision by the UMC will not affect its accreditation. It is still an approved theological school of the Global Methodist Church — a breakaway denomination that started in 2022 by departing United Methodist congregations — among other denominations. Asbury, founded in 1923, has additional sites in Tulsa, Oklahoma; Memphis, Tennessee; and the Florida cities of Orlando and Tampa, in addition to fully online degrees. About 9% of its student body identifies with the UMC, according to the school. It was approved in 1946 by The Methodist Church, one of the predecessors of the UMC, for educating students seeking ordination. In 1981, it was approved by the UMC for educating candidates for ordination in the denomination. The United Methodist accrediting body now approves of 13 United Methodist schools of theology and 25 non-United Methodist schools of theology. Adelle Banks is production editor and a national correspondent at Religion News Service. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Institute in Basic Life Principles founder Bill Gothard reportedly hospitalized after heart attack
item.author: Daniel Silliman
item.dc:creator: Daniel Silliman
item.description: Bill Gothard, the 91-year-old disgraced minister who taught his “basic life principles” to millions of evangelical Christians, reportedly has had a heart attack and is in a coma. Ryan Stollar, a victims’ advocate and the author of a book on liberation theology, shared screenshots Thursday from a private Facebook group for Gothard supporters. One of […]
item.content:encoded: Bill Gothard, founder of Institute in Basic Life Principles. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) Bill Gothard, the 91-year-old disgraced minister who taught his “basic life principles” to millions of evangelical Christians, reportedly has had a heart attack and is in a coma. Ryan Stollar, a victims’ advocate and the author of a book on liberation theology, shared screenshots Thursday from a private Facebook group for Gothard supporters. One of the 809 members of “Friends of Mr. G” posted a request for urgent prayer. He said news of hospitalization came from Angie Levendusky, wife of Tim Levendusky, Gothard’s successor at Institute in Basic Life Principles (IBLP). Alfred Corduan, a Chicago-area man who frequently defends Gothard on social media, confirmed the report. “It is true,” he commented to the group. “Pray.” Holly McLean, who has recorded more than 80 YouTube videos defending Gothard from allegations of abuse, described the news as devastating. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Headquarters of Institute in Basic Life Principles in Oak Brook, Illinois (Photo: IBLP / Facebook) “I pray he will recover to be able to see the exoneration of his good name,” she wrote in the Facebook group. Gothard resigned from IBLP in 2014 after confessing to “crossing the boundaries of discretion” with young women in the organization. He acknowledged this included holding hands, hugging and touching feet and hair but insisted that none of it was illegal, immoral or done “with sexual intent.” A group of former employees sued in 2016, accusing Gothard of sexual abuse, sexual harassment and cover-up. Multiple young women recounted how he would insist they sit on a couch with him and tell him intimate details about their lives. When eating meals, they said, he would touch them under the table with his foot. “You couldn’t get away from this,” a woman who worked for Gothard in 1999 and 2000 recalled, “because (his foot) kept coming up your legs. And that got really uncomfortable.” ‘What kind of legacy do you want to leave?’ Allegations against Gothard first emerged in the 1980s. Gothard, who never married, built a compound near Chicago for his ministry. The staff, including his unmarried brother and the many young women from Christian families who were occasionally referred to as Gothard’s “harem,” all lived on the same property In 1980, Christianity Today reported that Gothard’s brother Steve had confessed to “affairs” with women who worked for him. He was forced to resign. Crowds fill an IBLP seminar in Atlanta, where attendees received Bill Gothard’s signature red binders outlining his “basic life principles.” (Photo courtesy of billgothard.com) Ministry leaders also said that Gothard knew of the sexual abuse and covered it up for at least five years. Gothard stepped down but resumed control of the ministry three weeks later, rejecting calls for serious reform. Then in 1982, the Los Angeles Times reported that staff members had also seen Gothard “patting and fondling female employees.” Nearly two decades later, the man who was Gothard’s top lieutenant in 1982 said he was one of those witnesses. Christian counselor Gary Smalley, writing to Gothard in 2009, said he personally saw Gothard holding a woman, who worked for the ministry, on his lap. She was ready for bed, wearing a nightgown, Smalley said. “That alone was so inappropriate that a case could be made from just that one act I saw for you to take time away from ministry in order to be healed by God’s Spirit and renewed to purity,” Smalley wrote to Gothard. “I am wondering what kind of legacy do you want to leave?” Seminars attended by millions Gothard had a massive influence on American evangelical culture through his seminars on what he called “basic life principles.” There were seven: design, authority, responsibility, suffering, ownership, freedom and success. Gothard described these as universal, non-optional natural laws for ensuring “harmonious relationships in all areas of life.” He discovered the principles, he said, while meditating on Scripture and developed them in graduate school at Wheaton College. He put a special emphasis on authority, teaching that hierarchical order was essential to God’s plan. Much of human suffering, he said, could be traced to lack of submission to authority. Gothard taught that children need to submit completely and without question to their parents, wives to their husbands and men to their superiors in church, the workplace and the government. A diagram illustrating Gothard’s “umbrella of protection,” which depicted hierarchical authority as a covering that guarded against satanic “storms.” (Photo courtesy of Facebook) He described authority as an umbrella protecting people from satanic storms and warned that bad things would happen to those “out from under their authority.” One seminar handout said that people who had been sexually abused as children should be instructed to ask themselves if God let it happen because they were “out from protection.” Gothard tested out his principles on youth in the Chicago area starting in 1961 with an effort known as Campus Teams. Within seven years, he had systematized his teaching into a seminar called Basic Youth Conflicts, which he decided to take on the road. The first lecture series, held in Seattle in 1968, drew 42 people. But within a few years, thousands were flocking to the six-day, 32-hour seminars, receiving thick red binders outlining his teachings. Observers at the time were amazed that crowds of thousands would pay $45 each and sit in rapt attention as Gothard lectured. “He’s sort of an evangelical guru,” Christianity Today reported in 1973. “There is no singing or special music; only Gothard and an overhead projector.” In 1973, about 200,000 people attended a Gothard seminar. The ministry was renamed Institute in Basic Life Principles in 1989, to communicate an appeal across generations. IBLP reached its peak in the late 1990s and continued to draw crowds into the 21st century. Many felt that Gothard was offering the biblical answers they needed to navigate the massive social upheaval of the era. Provided order but cultivated fear “In a time of massive social and cultural instability,” Wheaton theology professor Emily Hunter McGowin wrote in 2023, “parents were overwhelmed by the enormity of the child-rearing task. In Gothard, they found a person with straightforward answers—which came, it seemed, directly from God. And that was the big selling point: certainty amid uncertainty; stability amid instability; assurance amid chaos.” Wheaton College theology professor Emily Hunter McGowin. (Photo courtesy of ethics.nd.edu) The sense of order and belonging could appeal to young people, not just their parents. Alissa Wilkinson, a movie critic for The New York Times, remembers begging her parents to enroll her in Gothard’s homeschool program, Advanced Training Institute (ATI). “I wanted to belong to something where the decisions were made for me already and where everyone seemed so happy about that fact,” Wilkinson wrote in 2014. “I desperately wanted a calf-length corduroy navy blue jumper dress with a V-neck, and a white blouse with a Peter Pan collar, so that I could look like my friends in ATI. … In ATI, each person knew exactly his or her role, based on age, gender and marital status.” Many, however, found that order and hierarchy caused long-term damage. Jinger Duggar Vuolo, part of the reality TV family that was made famous on “19 Kids and Counting” in the early 2000s, wrote a book about escaping from that form of hyper-fundamentalism. Vuolo said Gothard’s teachings were traumatic and taught her to live in constant fear she’d violated God’s plan. “His teachings were so harmful, and I’m seeing more of the effects of that in the lives of my friends and people who grew up in that community with me,” she told People magazine. “The fear kept me crippled with anxiety. I was terrified of the outside world.” Others raised in families that subscribed to IBLP teachings have gone even further in their criticisms. Two women in Texas sued the ministry last year, calling the ministry a “sex abuse conspiracy.” The seminars and curriculums taught women and girls to be “readily available, compliant victims,” their lawsuit said. And “IBLP’s doctrines, particularly those on authority, sexuality, and suffering, became a blueprint for fathers and older brothers to sexually abuse their daughters and younger sisters.” The lawsuit is still in court. Daniel Silliman is senior reporter/editor at The Roys Report. He began his two decades in journalism covering crime in Atlanta and has since led major investigations into abuse and misconduct in Christian contexts. Daniel and his wife live in Johnson City, Tennessee. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Michigan pastor charged with six felonies in hidden camera case
item.author: Mark A. Kellner
item.dc:creator: Mark A. Kellner
item.description: A former co-pastor of Resurrection Life Church in Holland, Michigan, faces six felony charges after authorities alleged he secretly recorded an adult family member with a hidden camera. His attorney said the allegations involve only his wife and have no connection to the western Michigan church or its members. Joshua Peter VanderKlok, 48, faces three […]
item.content:encoded: Joshua Peter VanderKlok, a former pastor at Resurrection Life Church in Holland, Michigan, faces six felony charges after authorities allege he used a hidden camera to secretly record an adult family member inside their home. (Source: Google Earth/Hudsonville District Court) A former co-pastor of Resurrection Life Church in Holland, Michigan, faces six felony charges after authorities alleged he secretly recorded an adult family member with a hidden camera. His attorney said the allegations involve only his wife and have no connection to the western Michigan church or its members. Joshua Peter VanderKlok, 48, faces three counts of surveilling an unclothed person and three counts of using a computer to commit a crime. Court records show a family member contacted local authorities, with charges subsequently authorized June 10 in Michigan’s 58th district court in Hudsonville. VanderKlok was arraigned June 12 and remains free on a $1,000 cash bond pending further proceedings. According to reports by WZZM-TV 13, an ABC affiliate in Grand Rapids, and news website MLive.com, investigators with the Ottawa County sheriff’s office began investigating after an adult family member reported finding a small black camera hidden beneath a bed. Authorities later obtained a search warrant and alleged the camera had been positioned toward the doorway of a private bathroom. Court testimony cited by investigators says that the camera’s memory card contained images of the alleged victim unclothed on multiple occasions. During a June 10 probable cause hearing, an investigator testified VanderKlok admitted placing the camera because it gave him a “connection” to the alleged victim. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Joshua Peter VanderKlok is no longer a pastor at Resurrection Life Church in Holland, Michigan, but the congregation’s YouTube page still has his videos online. (Screen grab) The alleged offenses occurred between June 1, 2025, and May 2, 2026, according to MLive, citing court records. Defense attorney Bonnie Prevette urged the public not to draw conclusions while the case remains pending. “Our firm is committed to ensuring that the facts are presented accurately and fairly. Out of respect for the judicial process and all parties involved, we will not discuss specific details or evidence at this time,” the attorney said in a statement emailed to the Roys Report (RR) and other media outlets. She added: “However, we can confirm that the alleged offenses do not involve activity at any ResLife Church location. The allegations do not involve a child or any other member of the church.” VanderKlok takes a selfie at a church conference in March 2026. (Source: Facebook) The attorney said the charges stem from activity alleged by VanderKlok’s wife at their marital residence. Resurrection Life Church does not list a current pastor on its website, although its YouTube channel still contains videos featuring VanderKlok. A statement posted on the church’s website says the congregation’s board of elders is “prayfully” seeking a new pastor. It says the board is overseeing church operations during the transition. The church did not immediately respond to an RR request for comment. Authorities have not alleged that any church member, youth participant or church activity was involved in the investigation. District Court Judge Judith K. Mulder on Thursday granted VanderKlok’s request to make one escorted visit to his former Georgetown Township residence to retrieve personal belongings. The judge also scheduled a preliminary examination for July 29 to determine whether sufficient evidence exists to send the case to Ottawa County circuit court for trial. Mark A. Kellner is a reporter based in Mesquite, Nevada. He most recently covered statewide elections for the New York Post and was for three years the Faith & Family Reporter for The Washington Times. Mark is a graduate of the University of the Cumberlands and also attended Boston University’s College of Communication. SHARE THIS:

item.title: United Methodist church in Uganda uses soccer to ‘kick out HIV’
item.author: Kudzai Chingwe
item.dc:creator: Kudzai Chingwe
item.description: As World Cup excitement reaches a fever pitch, The United Methodist Church is scoring health goals on soccer fields in Eastern Africa. The Kick Out HIV campaign uses soccer tournaments to attract refugees and members of their host communities to voluntary HIV testing. The goal is to break barriers, reduce stigma and build healthier communities. […]
item.content:encoded: Lab technician Arike Joel (right) conducts HIV testing during a soccer tournament hosted by The United Methodist Church at Imvepi Settlement in Arua, Uganda. The medical testing is part of the church’s Kick Out HIV campaign, which focuses on HIV prevention and self-care in refugee camps. (Photo courtesy of Juma Denis Daniel/United Methodist News) As World Cup excitement reaches a fever pitch, The United Methodist Church is scoring health goals on soccer fields in Eastern Africa. The Kick Out HIV campaign uses soccer tournaments to attract refugees and members of their host communities to voluntary HIV testing. The goal is to break barriers, reduce stigma and build healthier communities. The campaign, supported by a $60,000 grant from the United Methodist Board of Global Ministries, is part of the denomination’s HIV and AIDS efforts focused on prevention and self-care at refugee settlements. Uganda is home to 1.7 million refugees, the majority of whom are from South Sudan, Sudan and the Democratic Republic of Congo, according to the United Nations Refugee Agency. Within these settlements, South Sudanese United Methodists have set up churches that are implementing projects like the HIV campaign. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Organizers of the church’s Kick Out HIV campaign arrange uniforms to be distributed to players participating in a soccer tournament at Imvepi Settlement in Arua, Uganda. (Photo courtesy of Juma Denis Daniel, UM News.) “The reason why we have chosen soccer is that the rate of HIV among youths is high,” said the Rev. Faustino Duku Stephen, dean of superintendents for the Uganda-South Sudan Conference, at a recent tournament. “Youth are the most vulnerable, and we have to mobilize them.” Eastern and Southern Africa carries the largest share of the global HIV burden, according to UNICEF’s 2024 Snapshot on HIV and AIDS. Of the 1 million adolescents ages 15-19 living with HIV in 2023, 670,000 were from this region. “While steady progress has been made, children and adolescents (0-19 years) fare worse on HIV testing, treatment initiation, treatment adherence, and viral suppression than all other age groups,” the report said. The recent tournament at the Imvepi Settlement in the church’s Terogo District drew 24 teams with members from both refugee and host communities, transforming the soccer field into a hub of health awareness and fellowship. While players battled for victory on the pitch, health workers offered confidential HIV testing and counseling on the sidelines. The excitement of the matches encouraged participation from groups who might otherwise avoid testing, Stephen said. “The United Methodist Church is holistic,” he said. “We do not only look at the spiritual part, but also health. When there is a pandemic, we must raise our voice and say ‘No.’ We balance health and spirituality so that we bring all together.” Malish Kennedy, 29, who works for the Imvepi Youth and Sports Network, said she is grateful for the support from The United Methodist Church. “Through this tournament, the community will understand their HIV status,” she said. “If they are negative, they must continue abstaining or use protection. If they are positive, counseling will continue, and they will be introduced to medication and regular checkups.” Children compete in a United Methodist Church-hosted soccer tournament at the Imvepi refugee camp in Uganda. (Photo courtesy of Juma Denis Daniel, UM News.) Juma Jack, who plays for Imvepi Central Football Club, said the initiative allows soccer, which is his passion, to be a bridge to health. “I joined because of my talent. Soccer can save my life, bring friends, jobs and scholarships. Above all, it removes the fear of HIV testing because once we are gathered with friends, it becomes easier.” Lokugi Samuel Rombe, a player for veteran team Salam FC, said the soccer tournaments build community. “The main objective of this tournament is to kick HIV out. What motivates me is the concept itself. It exposes talent, creates friendships and even brings opportunities like sponsorships and jobs. Sports bring peace because people from different cultural backgrounds join together,” he said. “Before these tournaments, if there was anything to be disseminated to the community, the refugees would come, but the host community would not. When tournaments are organized, both would come, and that peaceful coexistence between refugees and hosts is strengthened.” Teams line up at Imvepi Settlement’s central grounds during the launch of The United Methodist Church’s Kick Out HIV tournament in Uganda. (Photo courtesy of Juma Denis Daniel, UM News.) Asunta Moriba, 18, plays for the Imvepi Women Central Team. She said she wanted to play soccer with friends to exercise and to promote peace and unity. “Soccer also educates me and encourages me for free testing about HIV and AIDS.” Stephen said reaching those not interested in soccer can be a challenge. He noted several resettlement areas in the vast West Nile region, where reaching people requires moving from house to house. “Once we reach them all, we will have won.” Another hurdle is meeting the other needs of those living in the settlements. “The problem is that once we bring them together, they have other requests, which the church cannot address. Sometimes the group is too large to contain,” he said. “In addition, as much as we try to maintain confidentiality, they fear stigmatization. They think that if they test positive, others will look at them as already dead.” Despite these obstacles, the vision remains clear. “As we preach, we must make sure that the health of the person is also good,” Stephen said. “My wish is to extend this program to South Sudan, to places like Yei River County and Juba County of Central Equatoria State, which is currently the capital city of South Sudan, where our churches are. If resources permit, we will not give up.” This article originally appeared at UM News. Juma Denis Daniel is the district superintendent for the Koboko District of The United Methodist Church in the Uganda South-Sudan Conference. Kudzai Chingwe is a communicator for the Zimbabwe East Conference. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Attack thy neighbor? Louisiana pastor arrested for beating up 20-year-old
item.author: Rebecca Hopkins
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Hopkins
item.description: A Louisiana pastor known for defying governmental COVID-19 lockdowns was arrested for assaulting a young man near his church Tuesday — an assault caught on video. Mark Anthony “Tony” Spell, 48, pastor of Life Tabernacle Church, was arrested Tuesday on a felony second-degree battery charge.  Life Tabernacle is a Oneness Pentecostal church in Central, 14 […]
item.content:encoded: Newly released video shows pastor Tony Spell beating a 20-year-old neighbor. (Screen grab) A Louisiana pastor known for defying governmental COVID-19 lockdowns was arrested for assaulting a young man near his church Tuesday — an assault caught on video. Mark Anthony “Tony” Spell, 48, pastor of Life Tabernacle Church, was arrested Tuesday on a felony second-degree battery charge. Life Tabernacle is a Oneness Pentecostal church in Central, 14 miles northeast of Baton Rouge. Videos released by WAFB-TV Ch. 9, a Baton Rouge CBS affiliate, show Spell crossing the street and into the yard of the 20-year-old neighbor. A video shows the neighbor swung at Spell first, about three times. Then Spell punched the man six times, threw him to the ground, and punched him another 26 times, while the man tried to protect his head. Spell sat on top of him, jerked his neck up and twisted it, then stood up and kicked him. Scott Sherwin, the father of the victim, told WAFB that he and the Spells have been feuding. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Life Tabernacle Church in Central, Louisiana, where pastor Tony Spell drew national attention for defying COVID-19 lockdown orders. Spell was arrested this week on a felony battery charge. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) According to a probable cause affidavit from the East Baton Rouge Sheriff’s Office, the victim likely has a broken orbital bone above his eye and needed stitches on his chin. Although Spell didn’t respond to a request for comment from Roys Report (RR), he later said at a press conference that he was trying to protect his family and congregation from a threatening neighbor. ‘I’m going to rape your wife’ Spell was cited by Christianity Today as the first pastor in the country to publicly defy pandemic lockdown orders by holding services. In March 2020, Central police arrested him on six misdemeanor counts of violating governor’s orders, WAFB reported at the time. But he and Life Tabernacle sued the Louisiana governor, mayors of Baton Rouge and Central, the East Baton Rouge Parish sheriff and the Central police chief for violating their religious liberties. Spell won in May 2022 when the Louisiana Supreme Court ruled against the governor. This week’s incident was not the first time Spell was arrested for assault. Spell allegedly backed a bus toward a man who was protesting the church’s decision to stay open during the pandemic, WAFB reported in April 2020. The incident was caught on video. Charges in that case were dismissed in 2022, WAFB reported. Tony Spell, pastor of Life Tabernacle Church near Baton Rouge, was arrested on a felony second-degree battery charge. (Photo courtesy of East Baton Rouge Sheriff’s Office) Spell posted a $25,000 bond on Tuesday, according to WVLA-TV Ch. 33, a Baton Rouge station affiliated with NBC. On Wednesday, Spell held a press conference with his attorney. Spell said he was working on a bus in the church parking lot when the neighbor from across the street threatened to harm his family. “He said, ‘Tony, I’m gonna rape your wife. I’m gonna rape all your grandchildren,'” Spell said at the press conference. “He said, ‘The next time you go out of town, I’m going to kill them. And what the F are you going to do about it?'” The affidavit stated the victim yelled a profanity at Spell but didn’t mention the alleged threats. The victim screamed, “F— you!” and “that was it,” the police affidavit states. RR was unable to find accurate contact information for the victim for comment. But the victim’s father, Scott Sherwin, told Baton Rouge investigative journalist Kiran Chawla of Unfiltered with Kiran (UWK) that Spell has threatened him. Sherwin said he plans to file a protective order. “We have had threats from Pastor Spell to physically hurt me and my wife,” Sherwin told Chawla, a former WAFB reporter. Spell, surrounded by church members at his press conference Wednesday, said he was just trying to protect his family. “I have an obligation and a duty to protect life, liberty, threats of bodily harm,” Spell said. “So, to the people that think this was a bridge too far … this will be determined in a court of law.” Sherwin told UWK that the accusations that his son started the altercation are false. “Pastor Spell has a habit of not telling the truth in an attempt to shift the narrative into his direction,” Sherwin said. “He’s a professional at playing the victim.” WAFB filmed Spell as he walked out of the East Baton Rouge parish jail on Tuesday, then hugged his wife. “The truth will come out in court,” Spell told WAFB. Then Spell added, “I’m heading to the pulpit now. I’ve got a red-hot sermon ready.” In the church service that night, he said, “Domestic terrorism is tolerated in our day.” He also said Mark 16:18 had been fulfilled. Pastor Tony Spell preaches a sermon titled “What It Means to Be a Man” at Life Tabernacle Church following his arrest, telling members he had a “red-hot sermon ready” and casting himself as a victim of “domestic terrorism.” (Screen grab) “In my name, they shall lay hands on the sick and they shall recover,” Spell said, quoting the verse. “I laid hands on the sick. I don’t know how much recovery they’re going to have, but I laid hands on the sick.” A history of tensions In April 2020, Scott Sherwin gave an on-camera interview to French media, criticizing the church’s services during COVID. Sherwin’s interview with the unnamed French outlet was dubbed into French, making Sherwin’s comments difficult to hear. But Central City News (CCN), a newspaper based in Central, linked to the interview and translated it for its June 2022 edition. In the interview, Sherwin can be heard complaining about Life Tabernacle members going from church to the grocery store, contaminating stores with coronavirus germs. Spell said during the pandemic that the virus was a scam that would help Joe Biden steal the presidential election, Christianity Today reported in May 2022. He also instructed church members not to get COVID-19 vaccines. Sherwin, whose property is across the street from the church, told the French outlet that federal agents set up video cameras there to surveil Life Tabernacle. The CCN’s translation from the dubbed French says that Sherwin made what seemed like a physical threat against the church. “You know what I really think?” states the translation. “If I had a Kalashnikov—.” “Scott, stop!” Sherwin’s wife interrupts him, according to the translation. “Be quiet!” A Kalashnikov is an assault rifle. RR couldn’t hear this part of the interview in English to confirm this because of the French dubbing. CCN accused Sherwin June 25 of “domestic terrorism and hate crimes.” It also posted a link to a legal defense fund to benefit Spell. Woody Jenkins, the CCN editor, also posted in an introduction to a video of Life Tabernacle’s Tuesday service that police haven’t taken action when the Spells reported past threats. Central Police Chief Roger Corcoran says that Jenkins is not credible. “Woody Jenkins is a coward that hides behind a keyboard,” Corcoran told WBRZ-TV Ch. 2, an ABC affiliate in Baton Rouge. At his press conference Wednesday, Spell said church members in his ethnically diverse church have made “numerous complaints” to the police and sheriff’s office because of threats the Sherwins made. “They are constantly intimidated with threats of bodily harm,” Spell said. “It’s the job of the shepherd to protect his sheep.” At the press conference, Spell’s attorney, Jeff Wittenbrink, also blamed the police and sheriff for allowing tensions to go unresolved for years. “It’s sad that we’ve had a failure in law enforcement and it may be that there’s a gap in the law,” Wittenbrink said. Wittenbrink also accused the police department of cooperating with the Sherwins to surveil the Spells. “We also know that the Central city police are friends with these folks across the street,” Wittenbrink said. “Every man has his limits … All these folks want to go to church in peace.” But local law enforcement denies any failure on their part. Casey Rayborn Hicks, public information director of the East Baton Rouge sheriff’s office, told RR that the sheriff has received no reports of threats. The Central Police Department used its Facebook page to defend its own work. Corcoran wrote that Central police received only one complaint from the Spells against their neighbors in the last five years. Through a public information request, RR obtained a copy of the report. Shaye Spell, Tony’s wife, filed a complaint on March 8 about two road rage incidents alleging Scott Sherwin’s son showed his middle finger to Shaye. No one was ticketed or summoned due to no probable cause, the report stated. Corcoran said he welcomes an audit to prove the department’s proper conduct. “If dates and times complaints were allegedly made are provided to me, I will order an audit of our systems to investigate any claim of a failure to investigate,” he said. Rebecca Hopkins is a journalist based in Colorado. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Former youth minister facing murder charges dies in Las Vegas police custody
item.author: Daniel Silliman
item.dc:creator: Daniel Silliman
item.description: A 49-year-old former youth pastor died of self-inflicted wounds in Las Vegas Thursday. David Vander Meer was facing charges he murdered his wife 20 years ago and made it look like an accident. Officials previously believed Bernadette Vander Meer, 28, slipped and fell in 2006 while hiking to the top of one of Utah’s famous […]
item.content:encoded: David Vander Meer, a former youth pastor charged with murdering his wife, Bernadette Vander Meer, who fell to her death at Angels Landing in Zion National Park, where authorities say she was pushed. (RR Graphic) A 49-year-old former youth pastor died of self-inflicted wounds in Las Vegas Thursday. David Vander Meer was facing charges he murdered his wife 20 years ago and made it look like an accident. Officials previously believed Bernadette Vander Meer, 28, slipped and fell in 2006 while hiking to the top of one of Utah’s famous rock formations. The 4.8-mile climb up Angels Landing in Zion National Park is strenuous, with a half-mile segment considered one of the most dangerous hikes in America, according to the Salt Lake Tribune. More than a dozen people have fallen to their deaths, and it seemed like that’s what happened to the young worship minister and casino worker from Nevada. A rescue team found Vander Meer at the bottom of a 1,200-foot cliff on Aug. 22, 2006. Her husband, David, said they were taking pictures of the sunrise for their 10th wedding anniversary when she somehow slipped. He claimed he didn’t see it happen; he only heard her scream. The death was ruled accidental after a three-month investigation. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church.
