Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. David Vander Meer was arrested by U.S. marshals outside a Las Vegas studio where he taught yoga, nearly 20 years after his wife’s death at Angels Landing. He died by suicide days later. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) Her family took some comfort in the thought. “I wanted to think it was an accident,” Vander Meer’s mother, Laura Gudenkauf, said recently. “I miss everything about her.” But people close to the situation — people who knew the Vander Meers, people who went to church with them, and the kids in David Vander Meer’s youth group — always had doubts. Two decades later, authorities in St. George, Utah, say they don’t think the woman’s death was caused by a tragic misstep after all. They say she was pushed. “David caused Bernadette to fall,” Washington County district attorney’s office investigator Jessica Bate wrote in a June 16 application for an arrest warrant. “David Vander Meer, acting with clear intent, committed the crime of murder … intentionally pushing his wife, Bernadette Vander Meer, off the top of Angels Landing.” According to Bate’s investigation, David Vander Meer told a woman he had been sexually abusing since she was 16 that they could only be together if his wife was somehow not alive. Shortly after that, he purchased $150,000 in life insurance for himself and his wife and then increased the payouts to $600,000 each. At the time, he was making $38,000 per year. Bate believes David Vander Meer was “acting with intent to commit a crime for the purposes of financial gain and in efforts to preserve his image.” He was charged in Utah Tuesday with first-degree murder and insurance fraud. Allegedly raped girl in youth group The Vander Meers were part of a Las Vegas church plant in 2001. David Vander Meer worked with the youth, and Bernadette, described by her family as a joyous spirit who loved worship, led the music at New Song Christian Church. The following year, according to the district attorney’s office investigation, David Vander Meer started grooming a high school freshman in the youth group. Court documents identify her only as “SH.” She was 14. SH told the investigator that David Vander Meer showed her special attention, buying her gifts, touching her knee and giving her hugs. According to the arrest warrant affidavit, this continued for years. SH said that when she turned 16, the legal age of consent in Nevada, David Vander Meer took her to a family-owned cabin in Brian Head, Utah, a ski resort area about 200 miles northeast of Las Vegas. At the cabin, she claims, her youth minister took her virginity. Utah state law defines this as rape. SH, a teenager at the time, said she thought of it as a “relationship” that “became sexual.” The woman told the investigator that after the Brian Head trip, they had sex frequently. This was legal in Nevada, but considered immoral by the church and called clergy sexual abuse by many experts. Sometimes, SH said, David Vander Meer wanted to have sex at the church after hours. Sometimes they went to pay-by-the-hour hotels. He bought her a phone, she claims, and told her to keep it secret from her mother, so he could contact and tell her where to meet him. The district attorney’s office investigator said she found phone records from the early 2000s corroborating SH’s account. “SH recalls David never talked about their future together,” Bates wrote in her affidavit, “but did make one statement that has always stuck with her. … SH recalls David telling her the only way they could be together is if Bernadette was not alive.” SH said that when she turned 20, she tried to end her abuse. The “relationship,” she said, was always secret and she decided it “felt wrong.” She remembers telling Vander Meer on Aug. 20, 2006, that it was over and she “wanted to move on.” He left with his wife for an anniversary hiking trip in Utah the following day, the investigator found. An unhappy marriage Bernadette Vander Meer was not happy in her marriage, according to the investigation. She reportedly suspected her husband was cheating on her. They were also tight on money. Bernadette Vander Meer got a job at New York-New York Hotel and Casino, an MGM resort on the Las Vegas strip, to supplement her work at the church. She wrote her mother several letters, describing the problems in her marriage and how lonely she felt, according to the investigation. Bernadette Vander Meer told her mom her husband would take trips with the youth group but never take her anywhere alone. She said he would give other people attention and show them affection, but never her. The hiking trip, a month before their 10th anniversary, was apparently the first time in years that just the two of them had gotten away. She was excited to go, the investigator found. Bernadette Vander Meer “did a lot of hiking,” according to her father, Richard Gudenkauf. “She was a mountain goat.” But then she ended up dead at the bottom of a cliff the next morning. People who knew her wondered if it was really an accident. Investigation raises suspicions The initial investigation found that some of David Vander Meer’s account of what happened on that hike didn’t make sense. He said he was taking pictures as the sun came up and turned his back on his wife, according to the investigation. But he called 911 more than half an hour before sunrise. An officer involved in the initial investigation remembered later that Vander Meer’s statement seemed “contrived.” Park rangers also told law enforcement that Bernadette Vander Meer didn’t fall in one of the places where people normally fell. She wasn’t in one of the more dangerous spots. Angels Landing in Zion National Park where Bernadette Vander Meers died. Photo by Joshua Gresham on Unsplash Investigators asked David Vander Meer if he’d ever been unfaithful to his wife, or she to him. He said no, according to the arrest warrant affidavit. The 2006 case was closed after three months. David Vander Meer allegedly applied for benefits from his wife’s life insurance policy and received approximately $567,000. Property records show Vander Meer bought a new home nearly three times the size and three times the cost of the property he’d shared with his late wife. He also bought SH a car with some of the money, according to the district attorney’s office, and took the whole youth group on a trip, all expenses paid. Vander Meer was fired from the church in 2008 for allegedly providing alcohol to children at his home. SH said she and Vander Meer married secretly a short time later. They got divorced in 2014. New investigation Pastor Barry Diamond, at the time the senior leader of New Song, told investigators he started to hear more allegations a few years later. Starting around 2015, kids who had grown up in the youth group began to tell him about the former youth minister’s inappropriate behavior. Some of the stories involved deception. People remembered multiple times when Vander Meer reported things stolen, including a drum kit and a car, then collected insurance money, and then, suspiciously, recovered the stolen items. It seemed, they said, like he was defrauding insurance companies. The former youth group members also started piecing together stories of sexual abuse, according to the district attorney’s investigation. They went to local law enforcement in 2022. Authorities opened a case in April 2022, but couldn’t find much information and the investigation stalled. It was relaunched in 2025 when investigators heard new allegations that David Vander Meer’s “relationship” with SH was going on at the time his wife died. Jessica Bate, at the Washington County district attorney’s office, was assigned the case in October 2025. She investigated for eight months and found enough evidence, she believes, to prove that Bernadette Vander Meer was murdered. Bate applied for an arrest warrant 19 years, 9 months and 25 days after Bernadette Vander Meer fell to her death and her husband claimed it was an accident. “Thank you Jesus!” her father, Richard, told the Las Vegas Review-Journal. U.S. marshals arrested David Vander Meer on Monday outside a studio where he taught yoga in Las Vegas. He was booked into the Clark County Detention Center until he could be extradited to face prosecution and a potential life sentence in Utah. He turned 49 on Wednesday. Las Vegas police said they discovered he had seriously harmed himself that evening. He was declared dead at a local hospital early Thursday morning. Daniel Silliman is senior reporter/editor at The Roys Report. He began his two decades in journalism covering crime in Atlanta and has since led major investigations into abuse and misconduct in Christian contexts. Daniel and his wife live in Johnson City, Tennessee. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Retired North Carolina pastor awaits trial for nine sex acts against minor
item.author: Jessica Morris
item.dc:creator: Jessica Morris
item.description: A retired pastor from Wilkesboro, North Carolina, could face life behind bars after he was arrested for three counts of first-degree sex crimes against a child. Timothy Clinton Pruitt, 72, a former pastor at Peace Haven Baptist Church in North Wilkesboro, was arrested June 18 in his hometown, 80 miles north of Charlotte. He faces […]
item.content:encoded: Timothy Pruitt, a former pastor at Peace Haven Baptist Church in North Wilkesboro, North Carolina, was arrested for sex crimes against a child. (Photo courtesy of Wilkesboro Sheriff’s Office) A retired pastor from Wilkesboro, North Carolina, could face life behind bars after he was arrested for three counts of first-degree sex crimes against a child. Timothy Clinton Pruitt, 72, a former pastor at Peace Haven Baptist Church in North Wilkesboro, was arrested June 18 in his hometown, 80 miles north of Charlotte. He faces nine charges: three for a sex act against a child under 13; three for indecent liberties with a child under the age of 16; three for sex acts as a parent or custodian against a child under 18. Court records state that the incidents started in 2020. The victim lived with Pruitt, who “unlawfully, willfully and feloniously” committed the sex acts. Pruitt will make his plea July 1 at the Wilkes County courthouse. He is currently in the Wilkes County jail while a district court judge sets bail. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Once bail is set and paid, he will be released on the condition he stays 250 feet from the victim and does not threaten, assault, harass or communicate with that person. Fox8 News of High Point reported that Pruitt left Peace Haven Baptist Church leadership in 2012. “The charges that have been reported are extremely serious, and our hearts are with anyone who may have been harmed,” Peace Haven elders told the media. “While we respect the legal process and recognize that these matters will proceed through the courts, Peace Haven Baptist Church unequivocally condemns any form of abuse, exploitation, or misconduct.” Court records say Pruitt is a lifelong resident of Wilkes County. He has no criminal record or underlying mental health conditions. If Pruitt is found guilty of even one of the counts against him, he will face between 12 years to life in prison. Jessica Morris is a music journalist, podcaster and author based in Melbourne, Australia. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Faith groups answer Cuba’s crisis with aid and advocacy
item.author: Aleja Hertzler-McCain
item.dc:creator: Aleja Hertzler-McCain
item.description: In the face of an accelerating U.S. pressure campaign, deteriorating public utilities and economic inefficiency, Cuba’s communist government on Thursday (June 18) announced sweeping economic reforms, the largest privatization since before Fidel Castro’s revolution in 1959. Since January, the Trump administration has made repeated threats of military action, and its sanctions and an oil blockade […]
item.content:encoded: A pile of trash burns in Havana, Cuba, Wednesday, June 17, 2026. (AP Photo/Jorge Luis Banos) In the face of an accelerating U.S. pressure campaign, deteriorating public utilities and economic inefficiency, Cuba’s communist government on Thursday (June 18) announced sweeping economic reforms, the largest privatization since before Fidel Castro’s revolution in 1959. Since January, the Trump administration has made repeated threats of military action, and its sanctions and an oil blockade have been compounding Cuba’s existing fuel shortages, power outages and scarcity of food and medicines. Amid the mounting internal and external pressure, faith communities have been speaking up and meeting with both the U.S. and Cuban governments. U.S. Republican administrations have long seen faith groups as a cornerstone for humanitarian aid and community trust as they push for regime change in Cuba. In the last few months, the top U.S. diplomat in Cuba, Mike Hammer, has met with top Catholic bishops, a Catholic priest known for being critical of the Cuban government, a Methodist bishop and members of the Alliance of Evangelical Churches in Cuba, which includes several groups more often critical of the government, including the Assemblies of God. Despair has become intense, said Rita María García Morris, the executive director of the Centro Cristiano de Reflexión y Diálogo (Christian Center for Reflection and Dialogue) based in the Cuban province of Matanzas, who with her team has helped meet the daily needs of vulnerable people and to advocate for peace, including several meetings with Hammer and U.S.-based pastors. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Youths carry freshly caught fish from the sea in Havana, Cuba, Monday, June 1, 2026. (AP Photo/Ramon Espinosa) “Suicide, mental illnesses and hopelessness are extreme, extreme,” said García Morris in Spanish. “Our psychologists cannot keep up. We have a team of psychologists working even at night with phone calls, and they cannot keep up.” García Morris, a Presbyterian ruling elder, said that suffering due to days-long blackouts and spoiled food is widespread. In December, she had to travel to the Dominican Republic because she had developed diabetic ketoacidosis, a condition that can become life-threatening, because she was not able to keep her insulin refrigerated. She told media she is waiting to see how the economic reforms will affect the population. “Where does that leave the poor people and the humble people?” she asked. Cuban state media has said the survival rate for children with cancer has fallen from 85% to 65% since the oil blockade began and that more than 75% of essential medications produced on the island can’t be made right now because of unavailable components. The power outages and lack of flour are also limiting the Catholic church’s ability to produce unconsecrated bread for Communion. Puerto Rican parishes and Dominican religious sisters worked to send nearly 300,000 hosts to Cuba this month. Outreach Aid to the Americas (OAA) distributes humanitarian aid to Cuba through largely evangelical churches independent from the government. Teo Babun, OAA’s Cuban-American president and CEO, was quoted on the important role of faith communities in a 2004 Bush administration report issued by the Commission for Assistance to a Free Cuba. The evangelical businessman told RNS that “most of those reports interestingly enough are applicable today.” He expressed confidence that the U.S. government and evangelical organizations are ready to provide a greater surge of humanitarian aid, even before a regime change. “ We are aware of a lot of conversation taking place regarding Cuba and the aid that needs to be put together,” he said. “ They are becoming more and more familiar with the fact that the evangelical church has tremendous, broad resources and experience working in Cuba to be able to assist in providing humanitarian assistance at the right time.” Since the beginning of the year, the U.S. State Department has funneled humanitarian aid to Cuba through the Catholic church, citing concerns about government corruption. The first batch of $3 million was designated after last fall’s Hurricane Melissa and took over four months to distribute. The State Department announced in February another $6 million in aid to be distributed through the Catholic church, but Secretary of State Marco Rubio said in May that half of that amount was still being held up by Cuban government permitting. The department made a third offer of $100 million in assistance distributed through both the Catholic church and “other reliable independent humanitarian organizations” last month. The Catholic Church has also been continuing their long history of diplomacy promoting dialogue, rather than military conflict, in the U.S.-Cuba relationship. In February, several high-level meetings at the Vatican involving U.S. and Cuban leaders speaking about the Cuban crisis were publicized. Carmen Casado, 84, is served a free meal of ground meat, rice, red beans and crackers through a program run by the Church of the Holy Spirit at a dining hall adjacent to the Catholic church in Old Havana, Cuba, Thursday, April 21, 2026. (AP Photo/Ramon Espinosa) “This is the oldest diplomatic corps in the world and is certainly one of the most effective,” said Peter Martin, a former U.S. diplomat to the Holy See who now teaches at Boston College. Those diplomats have played key roles in Cuba’s recent history. On Pope Francis’ 78th birthday, President Barack Obama announced that he would begin normalizing relations with Cuba, a sharp break in over 50 years of policy — and he personally thanked Francis for his “moral example” and role in brokering key prisoner releases that allowed for the agreement. That announcement came after decades of work by the Vatican and U.S. bishops. U.S. bishops began to back Cuban bishops’ calls for an end to the U.S. embargo on Cuba in 1972, and Vatican leaders had played a key role in easing government repression of religious groups in the once-atheist country, starting with a 1989 trip by Cardinal Roger Etchegaray. “Vatican officials always urge leaders to consider the human cost of war, aggression and economic sanctions,” Martin said. “In my experience, when Holy See officials raised these issues, they weren’t asking us to turn a blind eye to the Cuban government’s abuses; they were simply pressing us to engage in dialogue and consider policies that would not harm the most vulnerable.” Anna Lee Stangl, head of advocacy for CSW, an ecumenical Christian religious freedom organization, told RNS that it was possible to both believe that U.S. sanctions are “unjust” and that the Cuban government is engaging in “the systematic and serious violation of individual people’s basic human rights.” Her organization’s research with sources on the ground in Cuba informs the U.S. government’s assessment of religious freedom in Cuba, and she said they continue to collect credible reports about religious leaders experiencing harassment, fines, surveillance and unjust incarceration. Catholics weren’t the only religious groups to contribute to the Obama administration policy. “They wanted to hear from us and valued the expertise because they knew that our partners on the ground had the knowledge of exactly what was happening on the ground unfiltered,” said Catherine Gordon, Presbyterian Church (USA)’s representative for international issues, citing high-level meetings between denominational faith offices and the National Security Council. Though the Vatican has continued to play a role in U.S.-Cuba diplomacy, faith groups opposing punitive U.S. economic policy on Cuba had not seen much success since the Obama years. In his first term, President Donald Trump reimposed restrictions on the island country, and despite some faith groups expecting the Biden administration would echo Obama’s Cuba policy, he continued Trump administration policies and added new sanctions. Gordon said her coalition, largely left-leaning Protestant faith groups who had helped shape Obama’s Cuba policies, have been shut out ever since. Under Biden, “ we were seen as another network to promote their agenda with,” said Gordon, but despite assurances the administration was reviewing Cuba’s state sponsor of terrorism designation, “ they were never working on it,” she said, saying the Biden administration’s engagement with those groups was in “bad faith.” Under Trump, those organizations, which work together under the Interfaith Working Group on Cuba, have tried alternate strategies to keep their ideas in conversation. In February, they delivered a letter to the White House and Congress calling on policymakers to end sanctions, enable humanitarian assistance and engage in diplomacy. At the end of March, a group including leaders from the World Council of Churches, World Communion of Reformed Churches, the Anglican Communion, the World Methodist Council, Presbyterian Church (USA) and the United Church of Canada made a solidarity visit to Cuba, designed to highlight Cuban suffering and condemn U.S. sanctions, and met with Cuban government leaders. And in April, Gordon and the working group also organized a webinar with Obama administration alumnus Ben Rhodes, who highlighted the importance and potential power of faith communities in shaping policy on Cuba. “There are faith-based arguments that can be made about the human suffering,” Rhodes said. But they say they’ve largely been hitting walls in their efforts. Carol Blythe, the advocacy coordinator for the Alliance of Baptists, described feeling unheard in a January Zoom meeting with a State Department staffer when she asserted that their counterparts in Cuba are able to worship freely, counter to the department’s assessment of Cuba as a Country of Particular Concern for religious liberty. At the end of 2024, Blythe, along with Stan Hastey, a retired denominational leader for the Alliance of Baptists, spearheaded a report about religious liberty based on a survey of pastors in Cuba and the testimonies of U.S. partners, which they delivered to the State Department. “We contend that there is no systematic, ongoing, egregious violations of religious freedom that specifically include (1) torture; (2) prolonged detention without charges; (3) forced disappearances; or (4) other flagrant denial of life, liberty, or security of persons,” several denominations wrote in a letter introducing the report to then-Secretary of State Antony Blinken. In the report, which emphasizes the improvements in religious liberty in Cuba, one U.S. pastor who served in Cuba argued that policing seditious political dissidence and controlling religious expression are significantly different. The State Department and the U.S. embassy in Cuba did not respond to requests for comment. “I believe that the church, international Christian leaders, can really call for peace and insist that the governments have a dialogue,” García Morris said. “The church in Cuba and the United States has not been indifferent. I believe that it has worked and continues working to prevent a much bigger and worse catastrophe.” Aleja Hertzler-McCain is a national reporter for Religion News Service based in Prince George’s County, Maryland. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Craig Hagin’s parents say their son ‘hid’ his ‘double life’; his wife says they knew
item.author: Rebecca Hopkins
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Hopkins
item.description: Former Oklahoma pastor Craig Hagin, heir to the Word of Faith movement founded by the late Kenneth E. Hagin, has been “living a double life,” his parents announced on Sunday. But Hagin’s estranged wife, Mia, says the elder Hagins knew of their son’s alleged dealings with prostitutes and pornography for far longer than they’ve let […]
item.content:encoded: Craig Hagin, former co-pastor of Rhema Bible Church and grandson of Word of Faith founder Kenneth E. Hagin, was fired in June over undisclosed misconduct. His estranged wife alleges years of infidelity. (RR Graphic) Former Oklahoma pastor Craig Hagin, heir to the Word of Faith movement founded by the late Kenneth E. Hagin, has been “living a double life,” his parents announced on Sunday. But Hagin’s estranged wife, Mia, says the elder Hagins knew of their son’s alleged dealings with prostitutes and pornography for far longer than they’ve let on, yet did nothing to stop it. During services at Tulsa’s Rhema Bible Church, founder Kenneth W. Hagin told his congregation that “what we saw was both shocking and devastating, not only as the leaders of this ministry, but as his parents.” In a statement posted on Rhema’s website, Kenneth W. Hagin and his wife, Lynette, said they’ve known “serious questions about Craig’s personal conduct” for some time. They didn’t offer details. But the divorce petition that Mia Hagin filed June 4 includes a key detail the parents left out. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Kenneth W. Hagin, alongside his wife, Lynette, addresses the Rhema Bible Church congregation on June 21, 2026, revealing that his son Craig had been “living a double life.” (Screengrab) “The Respondent has committed the act of adultery, and the Court should dissolve the marriage of the parties for that reason,” Jessika Tate, Mia’s attorney, wrote in the petition. Mia Hagin told Roys Report (RR) she disclosed Craig’s infidelity and hiring of prostitutes to Lynette years ago. But Mia said that Lynette blamed her instead. “She made sure I knew that ‘happy husbands don’t stray,’” Mia wrote to RR. “I could feel their blame.” RR emailed Rhema Bible Church’s media contact and messaged Kenneth Hagin’s Facebook page about these allegations with no immediate response. RR also reached out to Craig Hagin through social media about his response to Mia’s accusations and his parents’ statement, but he didn’t immediately respond. Hagin, the grandson of Kenneth E. Hagin, has made headlines this year for multiple scandals. In March, he was arrested for animal cruelty. In late May, Mia Hagin announced their divorce on social media, accusing her husband of betrayal. Craig Hagin was arrested on animal cruelty charges in March 2026. (Photo courtesy of Tulsa Police Department) And on June 3, Rhema Bible Church, based in the Tulsa suburb of Broken Arrow, fired him from all roles for unspecified misconduct. Hagin had been its co-pastor and chief operating officer. On June 7, after Craig Hagin was fired, he posted on Facebook about a “difficult season” for his family and thanked his followers for their support and prayers. “During this time, I will be focusing on my family and seeking God’s wisdom and direction for the future,” he wrote. “I would appreciate your continued prayers for my family and me, for wisdom, discernment, peace, strength, and God’s direction.” Hagin’s name surfaced in national news June 14 in reporting by the Daily Mail on fellow Tulsa pastor Jackson Lahmeyer. Lahmeyer, in a text to his fundraiser, Caitlin Simmons, offhandedly mentioned dropping by a strip club with Hagin and other friends after a visit to President Donald Trump’s Florida residence in Mar-a-lago. Lahmeyer ended his candidacy for U.S. House three days later, as RR previously reported. Hagin served on the board of Pastors for Trump, an organization Lahmeyer founded in 2022. ‘Not the first time’ Hagin’s parents admitted in their June 21 statement that, “This was not the first time that serious questions about Craig’s personal conduct had been raised.” But they didn’t disclose a timeline of their knowledge. Kenneth Hagin said both Craig and Mia previously spoke to them about “issues.” The elder Hagins said they counseled the couple, and their son convinced them that their problems were resolved. Then the elder Hagins said they learned that wasn’t the case. “We heard those concerns,” the elder Hagins wrote. “We took them seriously.” They said they confronted their son. “He denied them,” the Hagins stated. “We believed our son and we now know that in doing so, we were deceived by him.” After the elder Hagins returned from a recent international trip, the elder Hagins received “documented evidence” of Craig’s misconduct. The Hagins said they “acted immediately” to fire him. “This evidence made it clear that Craig had been living a life we did not see,” the Hagins stated. “It was conduct we could not reconcile with the son and the leader we believed him to be, and frankly, conduct we could not imagine he was capable of. He hid this from us. He was living a double life.” The Hagins said Craig’s deception was widespread. “Craig deceived not only his wife and his family, but also Lynette and me; his sister and brother-in-law, Denise and Don Burns; his ministry; and the people who trusted him,” the Hagins stated. “We are Craig’s parents. We love him. But love does not require us to be silent about what happened, and our responsibility to this ministry, to its integrity, and to every one of you, demands that we speak plainly.” ‘Not at all the man I thought I married’ But in a bombshell written statement, Mia told RR that she tried reporting Hagin’s infidelity and misconduct to Lynette Hagin as early as 17 years ago. In 2007, one year into their 20-year marriage, she told RR, she first discovered her husband’s infidelity after reading his emails. She eventually told Lynette. Craig and Mia Hagin made news when they announced they were divorcing after 20 years of marriage in May 2026 (Photo courtesy of social media) “When I went to my mother-in-law the first time, I felt like she heard me and that she would help and confront her son,” Mia wrote. “No changes came after I met with her.” At that time, Mia took her mother-in-law’s advice to keep her husband happy so he wouldn’t stray. She stated she tried harder “with our intimate life together.” Then eight years ago, Mia stated she woke up in the middle of the night from a dream. “I went to get a drink of water, and I felt a prompting to go check my husband’s iPad messages,” Mia stated. “What I found was dark, deceptive and not at all the man I thought I had married.” Her husband was dallying with prostitutes, Mia explained to RR. “So I went to my mother-in-law the second time again in person and told her that ‘her son needs help’ this is not just a man having an affair,” Mia stated. “This is your son paying for sex over and over again for years.” She said Lynette promised she’d handle the situation. But “after that conversation, nothing happened!” Mia stated. Mia then confronted her husband. “I asked him, ‘How many?’” Mia stated. “He replied ‘more wom(e)n than he can count.’ He could not give me a number. This was heart-shattering for me, and that comment has replayed in my head for years.” Craig refused to go to counseling with her, she said, and lied to his parents about his infidelity. “It was easier for my in-laws to blame me,” she said. “To call me a liar than to see the truth and actually hold their son accountable and work to get him the help he needs.” She felt like a pariah at family functions. “Suddenly I was being pushed away from family dinners, family gatherings,” Mia stated. “I was pushed to sit away from family; nobody talked to me and nobody was interested in anything I had to say. “I felt isolated and I knew I was completely alone in trying to get my husband help. I have no one to talk to.” Two years ago, she said, Craig informed her that his parents requested she stop attending family and church functions altogether. “Did I feel like my in-laws handled this situation correctly?” she asked. “Absolutely not. They knew!” The divorce filings Mia Hagin’s divorce filing, made in the district court of Tulsa County, doesn’t offer these details. But another filing that her attorney made on June 4 blames the couple’s financial problems on Craig. The couple faced foreclosure, filed in January, on a half-million-dollar house they owned. The property was also subject in December to a tax lien of nearly $36,000, RR previously reported. Craig W. Hagin preaches at Rhema Bible Church in Tulsa, Oklahoma on April 14, 2024. (Screen grab) In a motion for a temporary order, Mia is requesting the temporary possession of the home and its immediate sale. Her filing asks the court to order Craig’s cooperation selling the house, and for him to pay costs associated with the sale. “Given the debts of the parties and the financial crisis Respondent (Craig) has placed Petitioner (Mia) in, the marital residence needs to be sold immediately to address the debts and provide financial resources for the Petitioner,” the motion states. The divorce filing requests alimony and a fair division of joint property. Court records show Craig Hagin hasn’t responded to the divorce filing. Rebecca Hopkins is a journalist based in Colorado. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Passion Bible scholar says there was no ‘rigorous review’
item.author: Daniel Silliman
item.dc:creator: Daniel Silliman
item.description: Broadstreet Publishing claims the Passion Bible, a much-criticized version that former missionary Brian Simmons says he translated from the original languages, went through a “rigorous review process” with “reputed scholars.” One of the top scholars involved in that process describes it differently. “My job was simply to consult,” Rick Wadholm Jr., former professor of Old […]
item.content:encoded: Bethel Church in Redding, California, has quietly stopped selling its special edition of the Passion Bible, the much-criticized translation by Brian Simmons now facing allegations of extensive plagiarism. (RR Graphic) Broadstreet Publishing claims the Passion Bible, a much-criticized version that former missionary Brian Simmons says he translated from the original languages, went through a “rigorous review process” with “reputed scholars.” One of the top scholars involved in that process describes it differently. “My job was simply to consult,” Rick Wadholm Jr., former professor of Old Testament at Assemblies of God Theological Seminary and Evangel University, told Roys Report (RR) in an email. “I have never checked to see what of my very many comments/edits/revisions/etc., were accepted or followed in any fashion.” Wadholm said he had no oversight or editorial control. He does not know how Simmons reached any of the decisions he made about the final version of the text that Broadstreet published. “The translation always remained Brian’s own,” Wadholm said. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Pastor Brian Simmons is lead translator for The Passion Translation—a controversial Bible version, which has been pulled from Bible Gateway. (Screengrab via YouTube / BroadStreet Publishing) Simmons was accused this month of extensive plagiarism. A nondenominational pastor in Texas said he found 500 instances of theft in the Passion version of Luke alone. David Fish, formation minister at New River Fellowship, 25 miles west of Fort Worth, told RR that Simmons steals from popular paraphrases, such as The Message. Many of the footnotes, which are often praised by readers, appear to be taken word-for-word from an obscure filmmaker who claimed Christians had suppressed the true text of the Bible. Fish published two spreadsheets online with more than 100 examples of borrowed language. “Smoking gun after smoking gun after smoking gun,” he told RR. “How many smoking-gun instances of plagiarism do you need before you pull something and recognize there is a serious problem?” Passion Bible faces plagiarism allegations after a Texas pastor claims extensive copying from other sources in its translation and footnotes, sparking controversy over its authenticity. (Source: BroadStreet Publishing) The 2020 edition of the Passion New Testament, published with Psalms, Proverbs, and Song of Songs, sold a half million copies in one year and 1 million in five years. Broadstreet also publishes individual books of the Passion version of the Bible and makes the complete text available free on YouVersion, where 18 million people reportedly read Scripture regularly. More than 17,000 people have asked YouVersion to remove the Passion Bible since Fish first made his allegations on a 4.5-hour episode of the Minor Prophets podcast earlier this month. Simmons has not responded publicly to the exposé. Broadstreet, a small, Minnesota-based publisher, has not made a public statement and did not respond to multiple emails from RR. Trust in scholarship Supporters of the Passion Bible have, in the past, accused critics of being too picky and pointed to the scholars who, they said, ensured the translation is reliable. “I’m not a Hebrew or Greek (guy),” Bill Johnson, pastor of Bethel Church in Redding, California, said in a video posted on the church website. “I have to believe whoever is.” The Passion Bible’s online FAQ page lists seven “professionals” involved in the editing process. Three of them have doctorates. Jacqueline Grey, professor of Biblical studies at Alphacrucis University College in Australia, could not be reached for comment. Gary S. Greig, who briefly taught at Regent University, in Virginia Beach, Virginia, died in 2023. Rick Wadholm Jr., former Old Testament professor at Assemblies of God Theological Seminary, served as a consultant on the Passion Bible but says he had no editorial control over Brian Simmons’ final translation. (Photo courtesy of evangel.edu) Waldholm taught biblical languages and introduction to Old Testament at the graduate and undergraduate level in Springfield, Missouri, until he was laid off in budget cuts this spring. He said he only looked at Simmons versions of Genesis, Joshua, Judges and Ruth. His role, as he understood it, was to make suggestions. “(Simmons) would send portions as he completed them and then I would offer all manner of proposed revisions, edits, etc., in light of my own translation done independently,” he said in an email. “I cannot speak … to how Brian Simmons came to his proposed iterations of translations.” Waldholm, who has published a commentary on 1 Kings and teaches biblical languages, does not list consultation on the Passion Bible as one of his accomplishments on his 16-page curriculum vita. Simmons compared to Martin Luther It is unclear if the other people consulted had any more oversight over Simmons. Only one has spoken publicly about his involvement. David Householder, a now-retired pastor who spent a year in Germany after seminary on a Fulbright scholarship, said in 2023 he went through Simmons’’version of Matthew “with a fine-tooth comb.” Householder said he found “picky” things to change but was profoundly impressed with the Passion Bible, according to comments Simmons posted to a Facebook account he shares with his wife. He compared Simmons to Martin Luther, who translated Scripture into German, and Thomas Cranmer, who composed the Anglican Book of Common Prayer. Householder also said that many critics of the Passion Bible misunderstand what it means for a translation to be accurate. “You want the reader to feel the same thing that the original writer of this book, whatever book it was, wanted the original reader to feel,” he said in a recorded conversation with Simmons. “That’s a faithful translation.” Householder did not respond to multiple emails from RR asking about the allegations of plagiarism. Quietly removed from Bethel website Some who have praised the Passion Bible appeared to be quietly backing away from the publication. Bethel, a prominent independent charismatic congregation, seemed to stop selling its special edition of the Passion New Testament on the church site. The webpage for the Bethel edition was still online but the “shop now” button was disabled last week. The product was subsequently restored, however, and a church receptionist told RR the Bible is still for sale. The special edition came with a 16-page foreward from Johnson, senior leader of the California megachurch. The New Testament sold for $50 with a faux leather cover or $150 with real leather. The special edition of the Passion New Testament once sold on Bethel Church’s website. The “shop now” button stopped working last week, though the page remains online. (Screen grab) When Johnson first introduced the special edition in October 2023, it sold out after the first service. More were made available that December. Johnson has regularly quoted from Simmons’ version of Scripture during sermons, telling his 11,000-member congregation he was reading from the Passion Bible, “which I so love.” He also officially endorsed Simmons version, calling it “One of the greatest things to happen with Bible translation in my lifetime.” The church has not made a statement responding to allegations of plagiarism and did not respond to emails from RR. The Passion Bible is also for sale on Amazon and in brick-and-mortar stores, including Barnes & Noble, Target and Walmart. Correction: A previous version of this story said that Bethel was no longer selling the Passion Bible. The story has been updated with a comment from the church. Daniel Silliman is senior reporter/editor at The Roys Report. He began his two decades in journalism covering crime in Atlanta and has since led major investigations into abuse and misconduct in Christian contexts. Daniel and his wife live in Johnson City, Tennessee. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Gateway Church wins tithing lawsuit dismissal as federal judge cites First Amendment
item.author: Mark A. Kellner
item.dc:creator: Mark A. Kellner
item.description: A federal judge dismissed a class-action lawsuit accusing Texas megachurch Gateway Church and former senior pastor Robert Morris of misrepresenting tithing expenditures, marking a significant legal victory for the scandal-plagued congregation. On June 23, U.S. District Judge Amos Mazzant of the Eastern District of Texas ruled the First Amendment’s ecclesiastical abstention doctrine bars courts from […]
item.content:encoded: Gateway Church Founding Pastor Robert Morris pictured with the megachurch's main campus in Southlake, Texas. (RR Graphic) A federal judge dismissed a class-action lawsuit accusing Texas megachurch Gateway Church and former senior pastor Robert Morris of misrepresenting tithing expenditures, marking a significant legal victory for the scandal-plagued congregation. On June 23, U.S. District Judge Amos Mazzant of the Eastern District of Texas ruled the First Amendment’s ecclesiastical abstention doctrine bars courts from adjudicating internal church disputes. The ruling dismissed all claims brought by plaintiffs Katherine and Garry Leach and Mark and Terri Browder. They alleged Gateway leaders promised that portions of church tithes would support missions and Jewish ministries but diverted funds elsewhere. Mazzant granted Gateway’s motion to dismiss and granted a similar motion that Morris made. The court dismissed plaintiffs’ various claims — involving fraud, breach-of-contract and the Racketeer Influenced and Corrupt Organizations Act (RICO) — then ordered the case closed. The lawsuit had alleged that Gateway leaders had, through sermons, conferences, church materials and the church website, declared that 15% of tithe dollars would support global missions and “Jewish ministry” partners. Plaintiffs contended those representations induced them to donate. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Gateway Church’s main campus location in Southlake, Texas. (Courtesy Photo) According to the complaint, other church members also claimed Gateway promised dissatisfied donors could receive refunds of their tithes. The plaintiffs said the church later refused those requests. Gateway argued the claims would require judicial review of religious decisions concerning tithing and church governance, matters protected from government involvement by the First Amendment. Mazzant agreed. “The First Amendment prohibits such an assault on Gateway’s religious autonomy and freedom,” said the church’s attorney Ron Breaux, a partner at Haynes Boone, a Dallas-based law firm. “We are grateful that the United States District Court has dismissed all claims against Gateway,” Breaux said. “Plaintiffs sought to second-guess Gateway’s beliefs and decisions on the fundamental religious question of tithing. The First Amendment prohibits such an assault on Gateway’s religious autonomy and freedom. The District Court rightly recognized that resolving the plaintiffs’ claims would require the court to conduct an inquiry into matters that are essential to Gateway’s central mission, and it properly dismissed the plaintiffs’ claims.” The judge relied heavily on the Fifth U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals’ 2025 decision in McRaney v. North American Mission Board of the Southern Baptist Convention. The court broadly reaffirmed protections for religious institutions against judicial interference in ecclesiastical matters. Mazzant noted he had previously denied an earlier request for dismissal because the original complaint appeared to focus on nonreligious conduct, including alleged financial irregularities and lack of transparency. He concluded, however, that a revised complaint centered on disputes over religiously motivated decisions regarding the allocation of church funds. “At its core,” the judge wrote, the lawsuit amounted to an “internal church controversy.” The court found that resolving the claims would require determining whether church expenditures properly qualified as “global missions,” “Jewish ministry partners,” “local, national, and international outreach efforts” or other charitable purposes. Such inquiries would necessarily involve questions of faith, doctrine and church governance, Mazzant said. The opinion also emphasized that plaintiffs did not allege Morris, church officers or other leaders personally pocketed donated funds. Instead, the complaint alleged donations were redirected to unspecified “secret organizations” or other charitable purposes. Interior of Gateway Church. (Courtesy photo.) “Determining whether Gateway diverted money from ‘Global Missions and Jewish ministry partners’ and ‘local, national, and international outreach efforts,’ to unspecified ‘secret organizations’ or other ‘charitable purposes,’ would require the Court” to decide matters protected from judicial review, Mazzant wrote. The ruling marks a rare positive development for Gateway, one of the nation’s largest nondenominational churches. Located in the Dallas-Fort Worth metroplex, Gateway has faced sustained scrutiny since June 2024. That’s when Morris resigned after allegations became public that decades before he sexually abused Cindy Clemishire when she was 12 years old. Morris later pleaded guilty in Oklahoma to abusing her and served six months in jail. Since then, Gateway has confronted multiple lawsuits, leadership changes and declining attendance. The church has worked to stabilize operations under new leadership while addressing legal and financial challenges. Mark A. Kellner is a reporter based in Mesquite, Nevada. He most recently covered statewide elections for the New York Post and was for three years the Faith & Family Reporter for The Washington Times. Mark is a graduate of the University of the Cumberlands and also attended Boston University’s College of Communication. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Connecticut pastor charged with submitting forged signatures
item.author: Peter Johnson
item.dc:creator: Peter Johnson
item.description: A Middletown, Connecticut, pastor has been arrested and charged with submitting forged signatures so he could run for state office. Robert Hoggard, 33, was arrested June 11 and charged with 14 counts of second-degree forgery and six counts of perjury, a press release from the state attorney’s office said. Hoggard was the associate pastor of […]
item.content:encoded: Robert Hoggard, associate pastor of New Jerusalem Christian Center, faces 14 counts of forgery and six counts of perjury tied to his 2024 primary challenge for a Connecticut House seat. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) A Middletown, Connecticut, pastor has been arrested and charged with submitting forged signatures so he could run for state office. Robert Hoggard, 33, was arrested June 11 and charged with 14 counts of second-degree forgery and six counts of perjury, a press release from the state attorney’s office said. Hoggard was the associate pastor of New Jerusalem Christian Center in Middletown. According to the court records, Hoggard’s charges come from a June 2024 primary challenge to Rep. Brandon Chafee in the Connecticut House of Representatives. Hoggard did not receive the party endorsement that year, but according to Connecticut law, a person seeking office can “circulate a petition to challenge the endorsed candidate in a primary.” Earning a spot on a local ballot through the petition process requires a candidate to get signatures from at least 5% of the party’s enrolled voters in the town. In Hoggard’s case, that was about 293 people. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. After Hoggard presented what he said were legitimate signatures, Chafee flagged them, according to Hoggard’s arrest warrant. In an interview with CTInsider, Chafee said that while reviewing the signatures Hoggard submitted, he noticed names of his own supporters. He called many of the signers, hoping to understand why they were now backing his challenger. Everyone he called claimed not to have signed the petition, Chafee said. According to the warrant, Chafee flagged the names in June 2024 and sent them to the Democratic Registrar of Voters Patricia Alston. In January 2025, the State Elections Enforcement Commission referred the matter to the Division of Criminal Justice, alleging that Hoggard had “forged signatures and falsified circulator certifications.” On June 17, Chief State’s Attorney Patrick J. Griffin announced that Hoggard had been arrested June 11 and was released on promise to appear in Superior Court in Middletown. John Kennelly, Hoggard’s attorney, released a statement, defending his client’s innocence. “A casual and quick signature on a petition is going to be very hard for most of us to recall accurately and completely years later,” he said. “The evidence will show that Robert is an innocent man.” Roys Report reached out to the church for comment but did not receive a response. Connecticut Democrats are having other voting issues. Earlier this month, officials revealed that hundreds of voters had been assigned to the wrong state house district in New Haven, according to CTInsider. In Hamden, police are investigating one candidate’s ballot petitions that disappeared. Hoggard has a court date of Aug. 5. Peter Johnson is a student at Boston University in Boston, Massachusetts. He is studying his master’s in journalism. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Pastor Saeed Abedini, former political prisoner in Iran, accused of abducting daughter
item.author: Bob Smietana
item.dc:creator: Bob Smietana
item.description: Evangelical pastor Saeed Abedini was once an international symbol of a Christian being persecuted for his faith. Now, he’s been accused in a lawsuit of abducting his 5-year-old daughter from her mother in Turkey and fleeing the country. A federal judge in the U.S. District Court for the Western District of Virginia issued a restraining […]
item.content:encoded: Pastor Saeed Abedini in a 2022 ministry video. (Video screen grab) Evangelical pastor Saeed Abedini was once an international symbol of a Christian being persecuted for his faith. Now, he’s been accused in a lawsuit of abducting his 5-year-old daughter from her mother in Turkey and fleeing the country. A federal judge in the U.S. District Court for the Western District of Virginia issued a restraining order last week barring Abedini from taking his daughter out of the country or out of Virginia, where he resides. The judge also ordered him to surrender his passport and barred him from concealing his daughter’s whereabouts from the court. Abedini, who was born Muslim in Iran and converted to Christianity, told media that he fled Turkey out of fear for his life and to protect his daughter. “I’m concerned for my daughter’s safety more than anything,” he told media in a phone interview on June 24. According to a recent complaint filed in U.S. federal court, Niloofar Ilbaki Aragh, the girl’s mother and an Iranian refugee living in Turkey, said she met Abedini while he was serving as a pastor and the two began a romantic relationship in 2019. Their daughter was born two years later. The two are not legally married but lived together from 2019 to 2023, according to the complaint. Instead, Aragh alleged that Abedini performed a religious ceremony, which he said counted as a marriage. She also alleged he was verbally and physically abusive. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Saeed Abedini, far right, the Iranian-American pastor released last weekend from Iran, is greeted by his parents, sister and Franklin Graham after arriving Thursday in the United States. Abedini will spend time resting at the Billy Graham Training Center in North Carolina. (Photo courtesy of David K. Morrison) According to the complaint, the child was picked up by Abedini to visit her paternal grandmother in December 2024 and was to return to her home in Turkey after the New Year. Instead, the girl was taken to the United States. “The child’s habitual residence at the time of removal was Turkey, where she was born and lived with the Mother from birth,” according to the complaint, which cites the Hague Convention on the Civil Aspects of International Child Abduction. “The Father removed the child from Turkey to the United States without the Mother’s consent and in circumvention of a Turkish court order that barred Respondent from leaving Turkey.” The complaint also alleges Abedini is considered a fugitive by the Turkish government and is under investigation for abuse. In a text message cited in the complaint, Abedini told Aragh he was fleeing legal troubles. “Don’t worry, I will take your daughter to a good country. The state here is already corrupt, nothing is being done for me,” he allegedly texted her. “They will put me in prison, and your child will be left without a father.” The lawsuit is the latest legal trouble for Abedini, an evangelical pastor and U.S. citizen who was jailed by Iranian officials from 2012 to 2016. At the time of his arrest, he had been working with Christian churches in Iran. His plight led to a massive “Save Saeed” social media campaign that caught the attention of the White House. In 2016, he was freed as part of a prisoner exchange in Iran. Naghmeh Panahi (Source: Tahrir Alnisa Foundation) However, a few months before he was released, Abedini’s then-wife, Naghmeh Panahi, told supporters that Abedini had long been abusive. In an exclusive interview with Roys Report (RR) in 2021, Panahi said she was bullied by Samaritan’s Purse CEO Franklin Graham and others to stay with Abedini to protect the #SaveSaeed campaign. She says Graham asked her if she was cheating on her husband. “I felt like this bleeding woman on the side of the road,” Panahi told RR at the time. “And this is the big stone being thrown—the first stone of adulteress.” Despite the pressure, Panahi wouldn’t budge on her requirement that Abedini get abuse counseling before she agreed to any effort at reconciliation. The couple later divorced, and in 2018, Abedini was arrested for violating a no-contact order. He had also been previously arrested in 2007 for domestic abuse, had pleaded guilty and was sentenced to probation. Abedini has long denied any wrongdoing. In 2021, he told media he was a victim of false allegations. “I never saw anyone be a used and abused person like myself,” he said at the time. He told media he faces death threats from Iran because he is a Christian Zionist. He plans to tell his story in court next week and in the second volume of his book, which will be out later this year. Abedini, who lives in Lynchburg, Virginia, is representing himself in court. “I’m the first Iranian Zionist pastor, they hate it, and so they are doing exactly the same way that the Iranian regime did,” he told media. “So, for the safety of myself and my daughter, I chose this direction.” In granting the restraining order, U.S. District Court Judge Norman Moon wrote that Aragh is likely to prevail in her legal claims. The next hearing in the case is set for Tuesday. Bob Smietana is a national reporter for Religion News Service. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Fired TMU president Caner sues school, alleges contract breach, wrongful termination
item.author: Mark A. Kellner
item.dc:creator: Mark A. Kellner
item.description: Truett McConnell University president Emir Caner says the Baptist-affiliated school improperly sacked him last year, thus breaching his contract without establishing a “for cause” reason to dismiss him. Attorneys representing Caner sued the school June 5. The lawsuit, filed in White County Superior Court in Cleveland, Georgia, says Caner’s 2021 contract required any early termination […]
item.content:encoded: Truett McConnell University President Emir Caner speaks at the commencement ceremony on the TMU campus in Cleveland, Georgia. (Photo: Facebook) Truett McConnell University president Emir Caner says the Baptist-affiliated school improperly sacked him last year, thus breaching his contract without establishing a “for cause” reason to dismiss him. Attorneys representing Caner sued the school June 5. The lawsuit, filed in White County Superior Court in Cleveland, Georgia, says Caner’s 2021 contract required any early termination to be made “for cause.” The university’s board of trustees never established that he breached the agreement, the suit argues, making his September 2025 termination improper. Caner led the school, known as TMU, for more than 17 years. Trustees hired him as the school’s eighth president on Aug. 8, 2008, and he was inaugurated as TMU’s youngest president on March 26, 2009. The firing dispute traces to the case of Bradley Reynolds, TMU’s former vice president of academic services. Caner fired Reynolds in 2024 immediately after Reynolds disclosed that he was under investigation for sexual misconduct, the complaint says. Caner consulted with TMU’s outside counsel before terminating Reynolds, offering him a standard severance, then notified the board within days, according to the filing. The board took no action against Caner at that point, the lawsuit states. It also took no action after attorney Marcia Shein wrote the board on Feb. 6, 2025, accusing Caner of mismanaging 2021 misconduct allegations against Reynolds and a former professor, Jeremy Lyons. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. TMU president Emir Caner (far left) and former VP Bradley Reynolds at a TMU graduation. (Source TMU Facebook) But the board sprang into action a week after a May 29, 2025, Roys Report (RR) news article and podcast renewed scrutiny of the matter. Trustees acted only after public pressure, not new information, according to the filing. It says the board knew for months about sexual misconduct allegations against a former employee before suspending Caner. But the RR article documented claims that Caner had long been aware of allegations that Reynolds had groomed Hayle Swinson when she was a student and then an employee at TMU. She also alleged that Reynolds raped her. As previously reported by RR, a former TMU employee said Caner was informed of allegations against Reynolds as early as 2016, but did nothing. Caner also allegedly ignored a 2019 petition from 50 female TMU students complaining about Reynolds. TMU VP Bradley Reynolds (left) and President Emir Caner (right). (Source: TMU Facebook) One week after the RR podcast, TMU trustees placed Caner on administrative leave and opened a third-party investigation into the Reynolds matter, the lawsuit says. On Sept. 25, 2025, the board met to decide whether to fire Caner and whether the termination should be deemed “with cause.” The board voted 19-10 to terminate him, citing his handling of the Reynolds investigation and termination as the reason, according to the complaint. Caner’s attorneys argue that his actions did not violate his contract. Terminating and offering severance to a subordinate fell within his authority as president, they say. The lawsuit details Caner’s record at TMU, crediting him with transforming the school from a small college into a university. Under his leadership, TMU achieved university status May 14, 2016, after graduating its first master’s degree recipient. The Southern Association of Colleges and Schools Commission on Colleges reaffirmed TMU’s accreditation in December 2020 for 10 years, the maximum term available. In recognition of that record, the board extended Caner’s contract in 2021 for 10 years, running from July 1, 2021, through June 30, 2031, the complaint says. Emir Caner (Courtesy Photo) Under the contract, TMU agreed to pay Caner a base salary of $272,639.69 annually, with increases tied to inflation and to milestones such as enrollment and graduation-rate growth. The lawsuit says TMU hit several of those milestones under Caner’s leadership between 2021 and 2025, including completing two campus projects under budget and growing its graduation rate by 6%. The contract also entitled Caner and his family to free housing in the university’s “President’s house,” a university-issued vehicle, cell phones, an unlimited meal plan and coverage for health care, life insurance and retirement benefits. The complaint estimates the annual value of those benefits at more than $133,000. Caner also earned about $30,000 a year in honoraria from speaking engagements, which the contract allowed him to keep, according to the filing. Caner is seeking actual, compensatory and remote damages, along with pre- and post-judgment interest. He is also seeking attorneys’ fees and litigation costs under Georgia law, arguing TMU acted in bad faith and was stubbornly litigious. TMU is a private Baptist university in White County in northeast Georgia. The school did not immediately respond to an RR request for comment, nor has it filed a legal response with the court. Caner’s attorneys, Andrew Y. Coffman and James D. Dean of the Atlanta firm Parks, Chesin & Walbert, P.C., also did not respond to a comment request nor make their client available for an interview. Mark A. Kellner is a reporter based in Mesquite, Nevada. He most recently covered statewide elections for the New York Post and was for three years the Faith & Family Reporter for The Washington Times. Mark is a graduate of the University of the Cumberlands and also attended Boston University’s College of Communication. SHARE THIS:

item.title: How women pastors became public enemy No. 1 in the SBC
item.author: Bob Smietana
item.dc:creator: Bob Smietana
item.description: In the first summer of this century, more than 11,000 Southern Baptists gathered in Florida to update their convention’s statement of faith by limiting the office of pastor to men only. But that 2000 limit was not binding on local churches. Instead, it applied only to the denomination’s seminaries and mission boards. “We don’t have […]
item.content:encoded: Attendees hold up their ballots while voting on a motion during the annual meeting of the Southern Baptist Convention, June 9, 2026, in Orlando, Fla. (AP Photo/Phelan M. Ebenhack) In the first summer of this century, more than 11,000 Southern Baptists gathered in Florida to update their convention’s statement of faith by limiting the office of pastor to men only. But that 2000 limit was not binding on local churches. Instead, it applied only to the denomination’s seminaries and mission boards. “We don’t have the right, the authority or the power to limit anybody,” the Rev. Adrian Rogers, a famed Baptist preacher who chaired the committee that revamped the Baptist Faith and Message, told reporters at the time. “We would resist that.” Al Mohler, head of Southern Baptist Theological Seminary, agreed. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. The Rev. Al Mohler addresses the Southern Baptist Convention annual meeting, Tuesday, June 9, 2026, in Orlando. (RNS photo/Marty Jean-Louis) “We would never presume to tell another church whom they may call as a pastor or tell another person whether or not they may serve as pastor,” Mohler, who also had served on the committee, told Baptist Press, an official SBC publication, in 2000. “We’re not trying to force our beliefs on someone else.” Some churches left the SBC and joined groups such as the Cooperative Baptist Fellowship, which allows women to serve as pastors. A few churches were expelled by their local association — including Glendale Baptist Church in Nashville, Tennessee, which was kicked out not because its pastor was a woman, but because she was gay. But there was no national action to expel churches with women pastors. For more than two decades, Mohler’s view was the status quo. While his seminary and other Baptist institutions taught that only men should be pastors, some churches believed the rule on male-only clergy applied just to the senior pastor. So they gave women in staff roles the title of pastor. Then Mohler changed his mind and decided all women pastors had to go. How and why that happened reveals a larger trend among conservative evangelicals and MAGA advocates, in which women are seen as threats to both the church and the country. It also reflects the way social media has transformed every local debate into a national controversy — and the way well-intentioned reforms can have unintended consequences. During the recent SBC meeting in Orlando, Mohler claimed the Bible demanded that Southern Baptists make it clear women pastors were no longer welcome. “There’s a great line that divides liberal and biblical evangelicalism, and you can see it on this very issue,” Mohler told thousands of local church representatives, known as messengers, gathered in a cavernous meeting hall at the Orange County Convention Center. “The trajectory of liberal denominations is clear.” Thousands of messengers attend the Southern Baptist Convention annual meeting, Tuesday, June 9, 2026, in Orlando. (RNS photo/Marty Jean-Louis) Mohler’s “Truth and Unity Amendment,” which would bar churches with women pastors — or churches that allow women to preach — was approved by three-quarters of the messengers at the SBC’s annual meeting in mid-June. If approved again next year at the SBC’s 2027 meeting in Indianapolis, the amendment would become part of the SBC’s constitution. In an interview with reporters after the vote on the amendment, Mohler said he was unaware until a few years ago that there were still churches in the SBC that had women pastors. He’d assumed most had left during the SBC’s long-running civil war, known as the conservative resurgence. Then, a social media post from Saddleback, a prominent megachurch, about ordaining some women staffers as pastors sparked an online controversy over the issue. Those posts led to repeated attempts to expel churches like Saddleback, which was deemed not in “friendly cooperation” with the SBC in 2023. “Social media and the digital revolution, where all of a sudden someone in one part of the SBC can find out what’s going on, or be presented with a social media feed, of something on the other side of the country, that’s a game changer,” Mohler told reporters. Mohler is partly right. Heidi Campbell, a professor of communication at Texas A&M University, said social media has made the internal workings of churches everybody’s business — not just the members of that church. And more people can weigh in on what should be done about it, said Campbell, who studies how digital media affects religious groups. That explains some of what happened in the SBC. Messengers vote during the Southern Baptist Convention annual meeting, Wednesday, June 10, 2026, at the Orange County Convention Center in Orlando. (RNS photo/Bob Smietana) “It makes something that would have been just behind closed doors into a huge media event,” Campbell said. Still, it’s unclear exactly how many SBC churches have women pastors. In 2000, when the male-only limit was added to the SBC statement of faith, less than “one-tenth of one percent” of the denomination’s then 41,000 churches had a woman serving as senior pastor, according to a report in SBC Life, an official denominational publication. Even in the Baptist denominations that allow female pastors, relatively few women serve as senior or co-pastors, ranging from about 10% of churches in the Cooperative Baptist Fellowship to about 1% of Texas Baptist congregations and 4% of churches in the Baptist General Conference of Virginia. Mike Law, a Virginia pastor who has advocated for a ban on churches with women pastors, claimed in 2023 to have found 170 SBC churches where women hold the title of pastor. The SBC has more than 40,000 congregations. But there’s more to the story. The debate over whether any woman could hold the title of pastor, no matter what her responsibilities, came at a time when the SBC was trying to gain more control over local congregations. In 2019, the SBC set up a permanent credentials committee to help determine if churches were in “friendly cooperation” with the denomination. That change made it easier for the SBC to vote out churches for issues like racism or sexual abuse. But soon, Southern Baptists who disapprove of women pastors began to report churches like Saddleback to the committee. In 2023, Saddleback, whose pastor, Rick Warren, had been an evangelical celebrity for decades, was one of several churches expelled for having women pastors on staff. The controversy over Saddleback also started during a time when Southern Baptists were reeling over a sex abuse scandal. The scandal threatened the power of male SBC leaders, said Meredith Stone, executive director of Baptist Women in Ministry. “They became the face of the enemy to many people,” she said. “So, to get that off themselves, they needed to create a new enemy.” Many of the advocates for reforms to prevent sexual abuse were women. Those women and women pastors became a convenient target, said Stone. And a tool meant to deal with racism and abuse was used to paint women pastors as a threat to the denomination. Meredith Stone. (Photo courtesy of Baptist Women in Ministry) “It is unfathomable to think that women who are simply sharing the love and grace of Jesus Christ are an equal kind of threat to the ministry of the Southern Baptist Convention as racism and the perpetuation of sexual abuse,” said Stone. Stone also worries that social media is being used to harass women who serve as pastors, rather than just as a tool for debate. Critics of women pastors have posted videos and photos of women preaching, calling for the pastors and their churches to be removed from the SBC, or in some cases contacting the pastor and their churches. “It’s not just calling attention to things,” she said. “It has enabled harassment, which is harmful.” The controversy over women pastors is coming at a time when the SBC is rethinking how to engage the outside world post-COVID and during the age of President Donald Trump. Calvin University professor Kristin Kobes Du Mez, author of “Live, Laugh, Love,” a forthcoming book about Christian women, said that during the Obama era, Southern Baptists had tried showing a friendlier face to the outside world, more concerned about loving their neighbor than winning at politics. Southern Baptist leaders also championed diversity and lifted up women as important to the church, even if they could not be pastors. That more winsome approach is now out of style. Churches or leaders who are less than enthusiastic MAGA supporters are now viewed with suspicion. Diversity is seen as a liberal plot. Outspoken women are accused of being more feminist than Christian. Critics of sex abuse reforms, including Willy Rice, who was recently elected SBC president, began to claim that concerns about abuse in the SBC have been overblown and that feminists and activists had a political agenda to harm the SBC and its Nashville-based Executive Committee. Rice has ties to a faction of MAGA activists and pastors who believe the SBC has become too “woke” or liberal in recent years and wants the denomination to become more aggressive in fighting the culture wars. That faction, which also opposes women pastors, is part of a larger conservative Christian movement that views women with suspicion, said Du Mez. Some in the movement say women should have no authority in the church. Others say women should not be allowed to vote. “What you’ll hear in these circles, not just in the SBC, but in the Christian right generally, and in the right more broadly, is that women are the problem here,” she said. “There’s a sense that if women had less power, then our country and our church would be more on track and more faithful.” Ironically, the push to expel churches with women pastors comes at a time when women, who have long served as the backbone of congregations of all kinds, are losing faith in religion. That’s especially true of young women, according to national polling. Baylor historian Beth Allison Barr, author of “The Making of Biblical Womanhood,” said she suspects that leaders in the SBC like Mohler fear their hold on power is slipping away, and so are doubling down on their theology to preserve their legacy. “The whole Southern Baptist world has staked its entire identity on this issue of male-only leadership with the conservative resurgence,” she said. “For that to be questioned would undo everything that the conservative resurgence has been trying to accomplish.” Bob Smietana is a national reporter for Religion News Service. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Former residents sue Indiana Teen Challenge, alleging ‘bizarre, random and cruel’ punishments
item.author: Daniel Silliman
item.dc:creator: Daniel Silliman
item.description: Brooke Pietrouski recalls losing the “privilege” of wearing clothes. In March 2020, according to a recent lawsuit filed in federal court, Pietrouski was a troubled teen. Her parents sent her to The Refuge Girls Academy in Lebanon, Indiana, to go through a faith-based program that promised to teach her to “live well and flourish.” According […]
item.content:encoded: The Refuge Girls Academy in Lebanon, Indiana, a faith-based Teen Challenge program now facing a lawsuit from 35 women alleging abuse, forced labor, and cruel punishments. (Photo courtesy of refugegirlsacademy.com) Brooke Pietrouski recalls losing the “privilege” of wearing clothes. In March 2020, according to a recent lawsuit filed in federal court, Pietrouski was a troubled teen. Her parents sent her to The Refuge Girls Academy in Lebanon, Indiana, to go through a faith-based program that promised to teach her to “live well and flourish.” According to the Refuge Girls website, the Teen Challenge organization would provide personal therapy, tutoring, Bible study and “character-building activities” in “a structured, reliable setting” that would help heal her “mind, body and spirit.” There was even an opportunity to work with horses. No one said anything about being deprived of basic necessities. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. The worship center at The Refuge Girls Academy in Lebanon, Indiana. Thirty-five women are suing the faith-based Teen Challenge program. (Photo courtesy of refugeacademy.com) But the Michigan woman says that when she was a teen at the school, the staff took away her clothes and forced her to wear a trash bag to teach her submission. More than two dozen other women have similar stories of spirit-crushing punishments that they call “bizarre, random and cruel.” Thirty-four women and the mother of one girl are suing Indiana Teen Challenge in U.S. District Court, asking the court for $150,000 per violation of their rights and “punitive damages as applicable.” An amended version of the complaint, updated to include all 35 plaintiffs, was filed June 16. The Christian group, which began with the ministry of the late David Wilkerson, author of “The Cross and the Switchblade,” has more than 200 residential programs across the country. The programs offer faith-based solutions to drug addiction, criminal behavior and destructive choices. Some of the former students in the Indiana program allege the solutions were extreme and abusive. They claim they were not allowed to sleep, shower or use a toilet except under tightly controlled conditions. Some say they were not permitted to take prescribed medicine. They recall “talking fasts” and a “safe room” where they were deprived of human contact for up to 30 days. They claim they were forced to share their testimonies — including intimate, humiliating details — in churches around the state. “Plaintiffs … were abused, neglected, humiliated,” the lawsuit says, “pitted against one another to earn privileges and avoid punishment, physically assaulted, spiritually coerced, given simplistic and largely meaningless ‘workbooks’ in the guise of education, and forced to perform a variety of physically hard, dangerous, and even bizarre and cruel unpaid labor.” Previous suits dismissed Indiana Teen Challenge’s lawyers are expected to ask the court to dismiss the case.
