Attorney Jeffrey Roberts, founding partner of the firm representing the Christian organization, described the allegations as “deliberately outrageous” and “opportunistic.” “We have not had the opportunity to review these claims in detail,” Roberts told Fox59, an affiliate in Indianapolis. “Once we do, we will make every effort not to litigate this case in the media. Our client will file a response to these allegations with the court as the rules allow and intends to aggressively defend itself against the claims.” A number of previous lawsuits against Teen Challenge have been dismissed. A 2021 New Yorker investigation described it as a “shadow penal system for struggling kids.” A girl at the Lebanon program told the magazine she witnessed 13 suicide attempts in one week, not including her own. The teen said that when she asked a Teen Challenge staff member about a treatment plan, she was told, “That’s not how we work here, you cooperate with the program.” But according to the New Yorker, the girl’s parents, like other legal guardians who sent people to Teen Challenge programs, signed a contract waiving legal liability and the right to sue. It is unclear how attorneys bringing the current lawsuit, filed in the Southern District of Indiana, intend to overcome that objection. The lawsuit does not name the national organization and only includes allegations about the program in Indiana. Attorney Gregory L. Laker, with the Indianapolis firm Cohen Malad, argues in the complaint that the Christian organization violated the Trafficking Victims Protection Reauthorization Act, the federal law prohibiting what legislators called “modern forms of slavery.” “Defendants received financial benefits from running a business that recruited, exploited, and manipulated children from desperate families and held them in involuntary servitude,” Laker wrote. “This action arises from the systematic abuse, neglect, trafficking, exploitation and forced labor of the Plaintiffs while they were minors and residents at Indiana Teen Challenge.” Victims want to tell their stories One plaintiff, a minor, is not using her name in court documents. The other 34 are publicly identifying as victims and asking the court to let them tell their stories to a jury. The 35 plaintiffs were at Refuge between 2012 and 2026. Some were there for as little as one month, according to the lawsuit. Some more than a year. The women say that Teen Challenge sold parents and guardians on the promise of a program that was “uniquely transformative.” The experience, they claim, was transformative, but not in a good way. The kitchen at The Refuge Girls Academy in Lebanon, Indiana. Former residents allege in a lawsuit that meals were often limited to peanut butter and jelly sandwiches and that they were denied basic necessities. (Photo courtesy of refugeacademy.com) The lawsuit reports they suffered long-term consequences, including psychological distress, mental health issues, lost trust, lost wages and lost faith. Just entering the program was traumatic for some of the former residents, the lawsuit says. Some say they were forced to undergo pelvic exams that were not medically necessary. Others allege they were — with their parents’ permission — abducted from their homes by Teen Challenge staff in the middle of the night. Makayla Launius, for example, says men working for the Christian organization burst into her bedroom in Missouri and grabbed her, restrained her and put her in a van. She didn’t know what was happening or where she was going. When the girls got to the school, they say they were strip searched. According to the lawsuit, this “in almost all cases included squatting and an inspection … by staff members and sometimes also by a student ‘mentor.’” They were then put into isolation, according to the lawsuit. Some recount being deprived of a bed and sleeping on the floor. They say they were given nothing to read or do to occupy the time between meals, which consisted of peanut butter and jelly sandwiches with a glass of milk. Several women claim they weren’t let out of the “safe room” or “prayer room” even to use a toilet. They say they pounded on the door and waved at the security camera, desperately trying to get someone’s attention. “When their pleas were ignored, these girls would sit on and urinate into the sink,” the lawsuit says. “(The teenage girls) believed their complete obedience and compliance … was the only way for them to ever leave.” Exorcism left bruises Four of the women, now adults, also recall going through exorcisms that staff said were necessary to expel the demons making them misbehave. One said she had a panic attack as people prayed over her loudly. The one plaintiff who is still a child said her head was forcibly and repeatedly dunked into two buckets of water as part of an exorcism. She claims the attempt to cast out a demon left visible bruises on her neck. Many of the 35 former residents say they did come to believe this was God’s will for them. According to the lawsuit, they thought they were being punished for their sins, they deserved this punishment and believed questioning anything that happened to them was the same as questioning God. They shared their testimonies at churches. Some of the women recall this as embarrassing and say they were pressured to be explicit or exaggerate, if necessary. They say they also had to be careful, though. If they seemed too friendly or made eye contact with any men, they would be punished, the lawsuit claims. Punishment could include time in isolation or forced labor, on top of normal chores, the women claim. Three of the women recall being forced to cut a lawn on their hands and knees with child-safety scissors. Others say they were sent to jobs at local nonprofits, but never saw any of the money. Now they would like to be compensated for the things they say happened to them. “(Teen Challenge) received financial benefits from running a business that recruited, exploited, and manipulated children from desperate families,” the lawsuit says. The women now believe the program was really “involuntary servitude under the guise of helping the children with their purported mental health struggles.” A pretrial hearing has been scheduled for July 16. Daniel Silliman is senior reporter/editor at The Roys Report. He began his two decades in journalism covering crime in Atlanta and has since led major investigations into abuse and misconduct in Christian contexts. Daniel and his wife live in Johnson City, Tennessee. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Nigerian authorities arrest controversial polygamous pastor
item.author: K.C. Nwajei
item.dc:creator: K.C. Nwajei
item.description: Security operatives of the Government of Anambra State in Nigeria have taken a pastor into custody, alleging he has persuaded up to 75 married women to abandon their husbands and join his household. Social media posts that have gone viral show the alleged polyamorous religious leader, Pastor Harrison Charles, at his home with 11 babies […]
item.content:encoded: Pastor Harrison Charles, founder of Day Spring Assembly in Onitsha, Nigeria. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) Security operatives of the Government of Anambra State in Nigeria have taken a pastor into custody, alleging he has persuaded up to 75 married women to abandon their husbands and join his household. Social media posts that have gone viral show the alleged polyamorous religious leader, Pastor Harrison Charles, at his home with 11 babies whom he claimed were from his wives. Anambra State Commissioner for Information, Law Mefor, confirmed the arrest to media. He didn’t say if the case is an isolated scandal or symptomatic of a wider crisis involving spiritual gullibility, manipulation, congregational vulnerability, and accountability (or lack of) within religious movements He said the pastor was arrested in accordance with the Anambra Homeland Law of 2025. The controversial section 18 of the law criminalizes administering charms or substances for gaining wealth using supernatural or unlawful means or misleading people by claiming supernatural powers one doesn’t possess. Critics claim the law infringes on religious liberty, but proponents say it targets only exploitative “quacks and evil native doctors,” not legitimate cultural and religious practices. “The law is not against religion and shouldn’t be,” Mefor said. “In fact, it aims to sanitize both religious practice and traditional worship now filled with fake people who hide under religion and traditional worship to deceive our people, particularly the youths” Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Pastor Harrison Charles. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) “They have dealt with our youths making them to believe that they can make money without work (Oke Ite and Odeshi) – bullets, rituals and amulets. The bullet proof amulets they make for criminals have also given them Dutch courage and many youths have been killed confronting our security forces while relying on these fake fetish materials. We believe that this is also the foundation for ritual killings we find around, from time to time” The allegations against Charles, who founded the Day Spring Assembly based in Awada, Onitsha, has spurred mixed response within religious circles. Many Nigerians expressed disbelief over congregational vulnerability and the apparent spiritual influence of religious leaders. The pastor’s arrest has also ignited debates about regulation, oversight, and public accountability of religious organizations and self-styled clerics. Mefor emphasized that Anambra, located in the country’s southeastern region, is a secular state where people cannot be harassed for how they worship or their choice of religion. But he noted that the state government has zero tolerance for “crime and criminality such as criminal idolatry and fake miracles-merchants. They are rogues confusing and fleecing our people.” Asked by media about the danger of government infringing on the fundamental rights of the citizens, in the context of the pastor’s arrest, the commissioner responded: “The said pastor will have his day in court after investigations. So, if he believes his rights have been violated, he will soon have a judge to complain to for remedy. Wheresoever there is an injury, there is a remedy. “But I can assure you that suspects under custody are kept under best possible conditions. No suspect under us has ever complained of violations of their fundamental human rights”. Charle’s case remains under probe but public sentiment against him is generally negative. On social media, he has been referred to as a “fake pastor.” On X, the site formerly called Twitter, the government had been praised for having the courage to arrest the religious leader and “put a stop to these atrocities.” Philip Adikankwu who’s a retired public official, Roman Catholic, and an influential community leader in the state said that the alleged scandal involving Charles could further damage public confidence in genuine religious ministries. Governor Charles Soludo of Anambra State has intensified the campaign against fraudulent religious practices A message encouraging the public to report fake pastors to a hotline has been the focus of a public awareness campaign. Soludo said the campaign’s goal is to curb activities considered harmful to public safety and order. Many welcomed the initiative as necessary to tackle fraud and exploitation, but religious leaders urged the state to for transparency, noting that any action against an individual or ministry must be a result of a thorough investigation. The leaders also emphasized the need to distinguish legitimate religious activities from crimes committed with faith as a cover. This article has been reprinted with permission from The Living Church. K.C. Nwajei is a freelance journalist based in Nigeria. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Ohio pastor, nonprofit leader indicted for stealing $375K in ministry donations
item.author: Rebecca Hopkins
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Hopkins
item.description: An Ohio pastor and CEO of a Christian nonprofit ministry that mentors at-risk youth was indicted June 17 for stealing $375,000 in donor money. Jamar Fleming, 41, is accused of diverting donations from Agape Ministries in Canton, Ohio, for personal use from 2022 to 2025, according to Ohio Attorney General Andy Wilson. Fleming and his […]
item.content:encoded: Agape Ministries in Canton, Ohio, a youth-mentoring nonprofit, is at the center of a felony indictment alleging its pastor diverted $375,000 in donations for personal use. (RR Graphic) An Ohio pastor and CEO of a Christian nonprofit ministry that mentors at-risk youth was indicted June 17 for stealing $375,000 in donor money. Jamar Fleming, 41, is accused of diverting donations from Agape Ministries in Canton, Ohio, for personal use from 2022 to 2025, according to Ohio Attorney General Andy Wilson. Fleming and his ministry face felony charges of aggravated theft and telecommunications fraud, according to Wilson’s press release. In 2021, Fleming was a Democratic candidate for Canton’s city council, but lost, according to the Canton Repository. He ran on the platform of helping urban youth who need better housing, activities and educational programs. Previously, Fleming served four years in the U.S. Marine Corps and studied business at Palomar College in California before transferring to Malone University in Canton for theology, according to USA Today. It’s not clear whether he graduated from either institution, although a 2020 Facebook post from the Ohio Community Coalition says he has a bachelor’s degree in Bible and theology. Fleming was indicted on six counts by a grand jury in Stark County, about 60 miles south of Cleveland. He is charged with stealing donor contributions and failing to file income tax returns for 2022, 2024 and 2025. He also filed an incomplete, false and fraudulent return in 2023, according to the indictment. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Jamar Fleming, pastor and CEO of Agape Ministries in Canton, Ohio, was indicted June 17 on felony charges of stealing $375,000 in donor funds. (Photo courtesy of social media) The charges include second, third and fifth-degree felonies. According to the Canton Repository, Fleming is originally from South Carolina. When he ran for city council in 2021, he helped with ongoing housing projects as a board member of Crossroads United Methodist Church. He also worked as a mentor for the Stark County Community Initiative to Reduce Violence. Fleming denied the allegations in a statement in a private message to the Roys Report (RR) on his ministry’s Facebook page. “I disagree with the allegations,” Fleming wrote. “I am reviewing the matter with my attorney and will not be making any further comment at this time.” Rebecca Hopkins is a journalist based in Colorado. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Arkansas church’s insurer settles sex abuse suit, a pastor says
item.author: Mark A. Kellner
item.dc:creator: Mark A. Kellner
item.description: Immanuel Baptist Church, one of Arkansas’ largest churches, has settled a sex-abuse lawsuit involving a female volunteer accused of assaulting a 15-year-old boy attending the Little Rock congregation. “Immanuel Baptist Church announced yesterday that the church’s insurance carrier has reached a settlement in the John Doe civil case to the mutual satisfaction of all involved,” […]
item.content:encoded: Reagan Danielle Gray, a former Immanuel Baptist Church praise team member and middle school teacher, pleaded no contest to sexually assaulting a 15-year-old boy. (RR Graphic) Immanuel Baptist Church, one of Arkansas’ largest churches, has settled a sex-abuse lawsuit involving a female volunteer accused of assaulting a 15-year-old boy attending the Little Rock congregation. “Immanuel Baptist Church announced yesterday that the church’s insurance carrier has reached a settlement in the John Doe civil case to the mutual satisfaction of all involved,” executive pastor Mark Carter said in a statement carried by Baptist Press June 18. “The case will be dismissed upon completion of the settlement process.” Reporter Frank Lockwood of the Arkansas Democrat-Gazette confirmed the settlement with the victim’s attorney, Josh Gillespie, who said “the matter was resolved amicably.” Terms of the settlement were not disclosed. “John Doe,” a pseudonym, claimed that Reagan Danielle Gray, a member of the church’s praise team and a former middle school teacher, began assaulting him in 2020. Doe was 15 and Gray was 23. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Steven Smith, then-senior pastor of Immanuel Baptist Church in Little Rock, was named in the civil suit alleging the church knew or should have known the danger the volunteer posed. (Photo courtesy of Southwestern Baptist Theological Seminary) According to KARK-TV Ch. 4, the two were exchanging messages and possibly images via text and Snapchat that were sexual in nature, and some physical contact was involved. Doe sued the church, its then-senior pastor Steven Smith, and Brotherhood Mutual Insurance Company, in December 2024. He claimed the church and Smith “knew or should have known” the dangers Gray posed. Gray was arrested on sexual assault charges in April 2024. The following May, she pleaded “no contest.” She was sentenced to six years of probation, fined $1,000, and placed on a registry of sexual offenders. As of Friday morning, Roys Report (RR) saw no record of the settlement being entered at the Pulaski County, Arkansas, civil court’s online case file. The Southern Baptist congregation, once the state’s largest church, once included then-Gov. Bill Clinton in its choir. The church has faced a schism over the Gray case as well as the case of Patrick Stephen Miller, a former church worker who molested a girl in a Sunday School classroom. The Miller abuse case — which heads to a criminal trial in the fall — was allegedly hushed up for seven years by Immanuel officials. A 2023 lawsuit against the church by Miller’s victim, identified as “Jane Doe,” is still on the county court’s docket. Mark A. Kellner is a reporter based in Mesquite, Nevada. He most recently covered statewide elections for the New York Post and was for three years the Faith & Family Reporter for The Washington Times. Mark is a graduate of the University of the Cumberlands and also attended Boston University’s College of Communication. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Washington church expanding despite criticism and shrinking in ‘cultish’ Network parent org
item.author: Rebecca Hopkins
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Hopkins
item.description: An association of churches known as “Network” has a growing church in the state of Washington, despite it being founded by a leader with a history of criminal sexual activity. Four years ago, Network founder Steve Morgan’s hidden criminal history became public, as Roys Report (RR) published at the time. Ex-members of Network churches across […]
item.content:encoded: A map posted in a Network-affiliated church in Indiana shows the U.S. reach of the Network, founded and directed by Pastor Steve Morgan. (RR graphic) An association of churches known as “Network” has a growing church in the state of Washington, despite it being founded by a leader with a history of criminal sexual activity. Four years ago, Network founder Steve Morgan’s hidden criminal history became public, as Roys Report (RR) published at the time. Ex-members of Network churches across the country have spoken out about what they call Morgan’s cult-like practices, questionable recruiting practices at colleges and lack of accountability. By 2024, five of the 26 Network churches in the United States, United Kingdom and Taiwan had left, and another one had closed. By March 2025, the Network had lost nearly half of its congregations, according to Ministry Watch. But one of its churches — Hills Church in southeastern Washington’s college town of Pullman — is growing. It’s located in a 7,000-square-foot building downtown, one-half mile from Washington State University. In December, Hills Church spent $3 million on a 46,000-square-foot building previously owned by Village Centre Cinemas, according to Whitman County records. Hills Church pastor Ern Menocal recently explained the church’s purchase and upcoming move to the Daily Evergreen, WSU’s student newspaper. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Pastor Ern Menocal preaches at Hills Church in Pullman, Washington. The church has purchased a historic building in downtown Pullman that will cater to their growing congregation. (Source: Hills Church) “We’ve simply outgrown the space,” Menocal said. “Purchasing a permanent location will help keep making room for those who are believing and growing as Christians, and also be a great springboard for us to plant churches in other cities.” But Andrew Lumpe, a former friend of Morgan and former board vice president of another Washington Network church, is worried for the nearby university students. “They need to be aware of the background of this church, the background of the leader and the way they treat people in a high-control environment,” Lumpe told RR. RR emailed Menocal, seeking comment, but Menocal declined. RR also emailed Morgan’s current congregation, the Joshua Church in Austin, Texas, but Morgan didn’t immediately respond. In 2004, Steve Morgan and a team of approximately 50 people from Vineyard Community Church in Carbondale, Illinois relocated to Seattle, Washington and founded Blue Sky Church. (Source: Leaving the Network) History of Hills Church In 1987, Steve Morgan, then 22, was charged for allegedly committing aggravated criminal sodomy against a teenage boy. At the time, Morgan was a youth group leader in a Kansas church connected to what then known as the Reorganized Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints. (That denomination, an offshoot of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints now goes by the name “Community of Christ.”) Though court records show the district attorney over the case believed the evidence showed Morgan committed the crime, Morgan signed a diversionary agreement. The agreement means Morgan’s charges weren’t prosecuted in a criminal court. Instead Morgan agreed to paying penalties, participating in mandatory counseling and paying for the victim’s therapy. By 1995, Morgan had embraced evangelical Christianity and founded Vine Church in Carbondale, Illinois, the home of Southern Illinois University. Morgan describes his history as a church planter in a document he wrote in “Our Story and How We Do Church,” which he wrote in 2011. In 2004, Morgan and approximately 50 leaders and members of Vine Church moved to Bellevue, Washington. In this high-tech affluent city just east of Seattle, Morgan founded Blue Sky Church. Andrew Lumpe told RR that he and Ern Menocal were among those who joined Morgan from Vine to work at Blue Sky. Lumpe became an elder, pastor and vice president of the board. In 2007, Morgan disaffiliated Vine Church, Blue Sky and other Vineyard churches from Vineyard to found the Network. Jesus was telling him to create a church planting model with “clear, Biblical theology and our values of doing local church,” Morgan wrote in his 2011 document. Blue Sky planted its first church in 2012: Hills Church, 280 miles to the east in Pullman, sending Menocal to be its pastor. “Where did Ern learn to be a pastor?” Lumpe said to RR. “It was from Steve Morgan, a credibly accused sex offender.” Steve Morgan preaches at a Network Conference at Vine Church in Carbondale, Illinois in 2013. Network pastors and board members stand behind him. (Source: Facebook) Sexual abuse allegations made public Lumpe told RR that in 2007 he started to learn details about the allegation against Morgan. Lumpe had been concerned for Morgan’s emotions at that time. So Morgan and some elders disclosed Morgan’s criminal history to Lumpe as an explanation. When in 2017, the #MeToo and #ChurchToo movements sought to bring accountability for high profile leaders’ sexual abuse, Lumpe decided he needed to do more. In 2019, Lumpe began to seek accountability for Morgan’s past sexual abuse. He reached out to Godly Response to Abuse in the Christian Environment (GRACE), hoping the Network would hire GRACE to investigate. Bu, Lumpe told RR, Network leaders refused. In March 2020, Lumpe, frustrated at the lack of transparency, left Blue Sky and the Network. Lumpe, along with several former Network pastors and members, started a website in 2021 called Leaving the Network. It compiles documents, timelines and stories about how the Network isolates people from their families, controls personal decisions and manipulates followers. Aaron Wright is a former overseer/elder at Vine Church, the Network’s original and flagship church. Vine left the Network in 2024 in the aftermath of public allegations against Morgan. Although this encouraged Wright, he told RR at that time that Vine’s leaders needed to repent more. “They failed to address the issues of Steve Morgan (and) numerous allegations of cultish behavior in the Network,” he said. Lumpe told RR that Network leaders defend Morgan with talking points. Morgan committed the alleged crime “a long time ago,” they said. Others pointed out that Morgan wasn’t a Christian at the time of his alleged crime. Some Network leaders, Lumpe said, said that the teenage boy, described in court records as “under 16” had consented to the act. That point, he added, doesn’t recognize an important power differential between the boy and Morgan. “(Morgan) was a spiritual leader of the youth group,” Lumpe said. In 2013, Pastor Steve Morgan, leader of The Network, prays for a congregant at a conference held at Vine Church in Carbondale, Illinois. (Source: Facebook) ‘Independent?’ When talking to the Daily Evergreen, Menocal defended Morgan, echoing the talking point about the alleged crime happening years ago. “Regarding Steve Morgan’s history, it’s true that he went through the judicial system for a sexual misconduct charge nearly 40 years ago, when he was 22 years old. Steve acknowledges his conduct was appalling, and he accepted the legal consequences,” Menocal told the Daily Evergreen. “But this was nearly a decade before he became a believer in Jesus or entered Christian ministry, which radically changed him.” Menocal also spoke positively about his personal experience with Morgan over several years. “In all this time, I have never seen a hint of impropriety of any kind in his life,” Menocal told the Daily Evergreen. “Instead, I’ve seen the fruit of a man forgiven for his sin, transformed and healed by God.” Menocal also told the Daily Evergreen that though Hills Church is part of the Network, it is autonomous. “Hills has always been an independent church with its own governing body,” Menocal said. “We are affiliated with a small group of churches, sometimes called ‘the Network,’ but affiliation consists of being helped and encouraged by our leadership team when we need wisdom, prayer or vision for the mission of the local church.” Lumpe questions this claim of independence. Morgan has influence over Menocal due to their decades-long ministry relationship. In addition, Network pastors meet for training and to share resources, he said. “All those materials are developed by Steve Morgan and his associates,” Lumpe said. From the Network’s roots in Illinois, a key component of Network churches is their effort to recruit college students into a high-control church system, Lumpe said. “The language that Steve used to use was that we want to recruit the best and the brightest and that’s where you find those — in universities,” Lumpe said. His concern is that Hills Church will continue this in Washington. “People will graduate and then they’ll tell them, ‘You’ve got to stay here in town. You shouldn’t take that job in Seattle,’” Lump said, “because they’re going to keep them in the church.” *Correction: Our original article incorrectly stated that Aaron Wright helped with the Leaving the Network website. Rebecca Hopkins is a journalist based in Colorado. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Christian schools ex-board member charged with sexual assault and servitude of a minor
item.author: Peter Johnson
item.dc:creator: Peter Johnson
item.description: Michael Robert Herbert, a suburban Chicago real estate attorney and former Christian school board member, was arrested and charged on May 14 with 10 felony counts of sexual crimes involving minors, court documents show. Herbert, 55, lives in Aurora, Illinois, about 40 miles west of Chicago.  A male victim came forward in December 2025, according […]
item.content:encoded: Michael Robert Herbert was dismissed from the Aurora Christian Schools Board after the Aurora Police Department began investigating allegations he groomed and sexually assaulted minors. Now the FBI is involved. (Source: The Aurora Police Department) Michael Robert Herbert, a suburban Chicago real estate attorney and former Christian school board member, was arrested and charged on May 14 with 10 felony counts of sexual crimes involving minors, court documents show. Herbert, 55, lives in Aurora, Illinois, about 40 miles west of Chicago. A male victim came forward in December 2025, according to a press release by the Aurora police. The abuse started when the victim was a teenager, the department said. WLS-TV, Ch. 7, an ABC affiliate in Chicago, put the age at 13. Herbert was, until his arrest, a trusted and popular board member of Aurora Christian Schools, where he could often be found on campus, WLS reported. The victim is now 24, according to WBBM-TV, Ch. 2, the CBS Chicago affiliate. Herbert allegedly groomed the boy over several years, providing gifts, money and other incentives to manipulate and abuse him, police spokesman Jim Levicki said. The deals included trips to Disney World in exchange for sex acts. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Aurora detectives concluded that Herbert may have had similar interactions with other young men statewide and outside the state. The department has worked with the FBI, whose Chicago Division said Herbert primarily targeted minor boys between 2013 and 2023. Federal officials are asking anyone who may have been victimized by Herbert to fill out a form on their website. Aurora Christian Schools reacted to Herbert’s arrest by calling the allegations “deeply disturbing” in a May 15 statement. “We are committed to responding to this matter with the seriousness, care, and urgency required,” the school board wrote. Herbert faces one charge of involuntary sexual servitude of a minor under 17, punishable under Illinois law by six to 30 years in prison. He also faces one charge of involuntary sexual servitude of a minor age 17-18, four charges of criminal sexual assault and three charges of aggravated sexual assault. The court document lists a final felony account, also known as “child pornography-possess” involving “films, videotape, photographs, etc.” A status hearing is slated for July 22. Peter Johnson is a student at Boston University in Boston, Massachusetts. He is studying his Masters in Journalism. SHARE THIS:

item.title: “They have already suffered enough”: Central African clergy respond to US deportation
item.author: Tonny Onyulo
item.dc:creator: Tonny Onyulo
item.description: Religious leaders in the Central African Republic say they were stunned by the arrival Friday (June 12) of migrants deported from the United States to their country without cultural or familial ties, questioning why people who fled religious and political persecution were sent to a nation still grappling with its own history of sectarian violence […]
item.content:encoded: In this July 8, 2019, file photo, U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officers detain a man during an operation in Escondido, Calif. (AP Photo/Gregory Bull /Courtesy RNS) Religious leaders in the Central African Republic say they were stunned by the arrival Friday (June 12) of migrants deported from the United States to their country without cultural or familial ties, questioning why people who fled religious and political persecution were sent to a nation still grappling with its own history of sectarian violence and instability. The U.S. government flew at least two dozen migrants from countries including Iran, Afghanistan, Turkey, Armenia and Georgia to Bangui, the Central African Republic’s capital, as part of the Trump administration’s third-country deportation agreements with several African and Latin American countries. Human rights groups and immigration lawyers say several of those deported had established credible fears of persecution in their home countries, including torture, imprisonment and death. Among them were Christian converts at risk and at least one Iranian pro-democracy activist who could face severe punishment if returned to Iran for her political activity and religious beliefs. “I was surprised to hear that migrants who fled persecution in their own countries had been deported to ours,” Jean Ngaba, an evangelical pastor in southern Central African Republic, told Religion News Service. Some of the deportees had been granted withholding of removal, a legal protection preventing their deportation to their countries of origin because of the risk of persecution. Rather than being returned home, they were transferred to the Central African Republic under a bilateral agreement between Washington and Bangui. Advocacy groups have expressed concern that the migrants could face onward refoulement, meaning they could eventually be sent back to the countries they originally fled. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Christians in a U.S. border community advocate for immigrants. (Photo: AP Photo/Russell Contreras/File) In particular, the Iranian American Legal Defense Fund has warned that deporting Iranians to the Central African Republic is potentially fatal, pointing to close ties between the African country and Moscow, a key ally of Iran. “The Central African Republic is poor and still trying to heal after years of conflict between Christians and Muslims,” said Ngaba, who works on local grassroots peace and reconciliation initiatives. “It is inhumane for any government to do this to people who have already suffered because of their beliefs or political views.” So far, no church, mosque or faith-based charity has been formally tasked with receiving the deportees, although religious leaders interviewed by RNS said they would be willing to help if asked. “As religious leaders, we are ready to assist them if we are called upon or if we meet them,” said Ngaba. According to immigration advocates and officials familiar with the operation, the deportees are being temporarily housed in apartments in Bangui while authorities determine their next steps. Their long-term future remains uncertain, and the Central African government has not publicly clarified whether they will remain in the country or eventually seek asylum elsewhere. The International Organization for Migration is providing post-arrival humanitarian assistance at the request of the Central African government but has stressed that it is not involved in the U.S. deportation process itself. Cardinal Dieudonné Nzapalainga, the Catholic archbishop of Bangui and an internationally recognized advocate for interfaith peace, said he was aware of the arrivals but was still gathering information about the situation. The cardinal said the Catholic Church would be willing to assist the migrants if called upon, reflecting the church’s commitment to helping people in need. Central African Republic, red, in central Africa. Image courtesy of Creative Commons Muslim leaders have also voiced concern over the deportations. Cleric Moussa Ibrahim, a Bangui-based Muslim leader who has worked to promote peace and reconciliation, said many of the deportees had escaped religious persecution only to arrive in a country with its own complex history of sectarian tensions. “Most of these people escaped persecution because of their beliefs,” Ibrahim said. “But here in the Central African Republic, we have a long history of religious violence because of conflict and weak state authority. Muslims have fought Christians and Christians have fought Muslims.” For more than a decade, the country has experienced repeated cycles of violence involving the predominantly Muslim Séléka coalition and the largely Christian and animist Anti-Balaka militias. Although a ceasefire reached in late 2025 reduced large-scale fighting, insecurity remains a challenge in parts of the country where armed groups continue to operate. According to the Open Doors World Watch List 2026, the Central African Republic remains among countries where Christians face significant persecution, particularly in areas where government control is weak. Ibrahim questioned how the migrants would rebuild their lives in a country facing enormous economic and social challenges. “How are they going to survive here?” he asked. “Will they stay temporarily or eventually move somewhere else? These are the questions we are asking as religious leaders because opportunities are limited, and the environment can be difficult for both Christians and Muslims, especially for people who have converted from one faith to another.” The arrival of the deportees has also raised broader humanitarian questions in the Central African Republic, where many communities continue to struggle with poverty, displacement and the lingering effects of conflict. While the long-term future of the deportees remains uncertain, Ngaba said people of faith have a responsibility to welcome those who have lost their homes and communities. “They have already suffered enough,” Ngaba said. “If they come to us, we will welcome them because that is what our faith teaches us. Before they are migrants or deportees, they are human beings, and every human being deserves compassion and dignity.” Tonny Onyulo is a freelance print and broadcast journalist based in Nairobi, Kenya, and a contributor to Religion News Service. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Passion Bible accused of plagiarism
item.author: Daniel Silliman
item.dc:creator: Daniel Silliman
item.description: A pastor at a nondenominational church in Texas says he has uncovered extensive evidence of plagiarism in the Passion version of the Bible. David Fish, the formation minister at New River Fellowship, 25 miles west of Fort Worth, told  Roys Report (RR) he found “smoking gun after smoking gun after smoking gun.” The latest edition […]
item.content:encoded: The Passion Bible has come under fire again after allegations from a Texas pastor that the translation and its footnotes contain extensive instances of plagiarism. (RR graphic) A pastor at a nondenominational church in Texas says he has uncovered extensive evidence of plagiarism in the Passion version of the Bible. David Fish, the formation minister at New River Fellowship, 25 miles west of Fort Worth, told Roys Report (RR) he found “smoking gun after smoking gun after smoking gun.” The latest edition of the Passion New Testament, with Psalms and Proverbs, has sold more than 1 million copies and is available in Christian bookstores and major retail outlets, including Walmart and Target. The Bible has been widely endorsed by independent charismatic ministers and praised by regular readers, who say this version “stirs the heart” and contains so many outstanding, illuminating and insightful footnotes. Others have been critical, arguing the Passion Bible is a paraphrase, not a translation, and takes too many liberties with the original texts. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Pastor Brian Simmons is lead translator for The Passion Translation—a controversial Bible version, which has been pulled from Bible Gateway. (Screengrab via YouTube / BroadStreet Publishing) Fish says the problems go beyond that. He argues the Passion Bible — including those beloved footnotes — is “chock-full of plagiarism.” “It’s undeniable,” Fish told RR. “How many smoking-gun-instances of plagiarism do you need before you pull something and recognize there is a serious problem?” Fish has posted online two spreadsheets with more than 100 examples, starting with Paul’s epistles to the Galatians, Ephesians, Philippians and Colossians. He started looking into the Bible translation after receiving a lead from a British Pentecostal minister who found basic errors in its Greek translations and a suspicious amount of copying in the footnotes. Fish, suspecting that the translator didn’t know the original biblical languages, began to examine unusual word choices in the Passion Bible. He found scores of verses where the Passion uses phrases that appear in popular paraphrases such as Eugene Peterson’s “The Message” but in no other English version. He’s also found many footnotes claiming to have discovered a nuance in the Aramaic that match, word for word, the observations of an eccentric independent scholar. Mark Strauss, one of the translators of the New International Version of the Bible, told Fish that the number of examples he found raises serious concerns. “If it happened very occasionally, it might be by accident,” Strauss said. “One turn of expression might be by accident, but you wouldn’t expect that a lot. It looks suspicious. There’s no doubt that it looks suspicious.” Broadstreet Publishing, the Minnesota company behind TPT, did not respond to an email from RR. Passion Bible faces plagiarism allegations after a Texas pastor claims extensive copying from other sources in its translation and footnotes, sparking controversy over its authenticity. (Source: BroadStreet Publishing) The lead translator, Brian Simmons, has previously dismissed criticism of his work as “cancel culture” and does not appear to have addressed allegations of plagiarism publicly. One entry on the Passion Bible’s FAQ page says the work went through a rigorous review process and “every book (including the numerous footnotes) is evaluated by respected scholars and editors.” Simmons has not been trained as a translator or linguist. He previously worked as a missionary in Central America and helped Bible translators do “comprehension checks” with native speakers of Paya-Kuna. Fish first presented his evidence of plagiarism in a 4.5-hour episode of the Minor Prophets podcast on June 4 with ministers JonMark Baker and Mike Winger. The three men have launched a petition asking YouVersion, the world’s most popular Bible app, to stop offering the Passion. In 11 days, more than 16,000 people have signed. ‘People should be demanding refunds’ Winger, a pastor trained at Calvary Chapel in Southern California and has a popular YouTube channel, first raised concerns about the Passion Bible in 2018. He was one of the people who successfully pushed Bible Gateway, the first multilingual, multi-translation Bible website, to remove the Passion translation from its list of available versions. Winger said the evidence that Fish uncovered goes much further than what he found to discredit Simmons and his Bible. “People should be demanding refunds,” he said on the Minor Prophets podcast. “There needs to be a mass recall on every version and every edition.” Winger called the Passion Bible a “disaster” and Simmons a “fake.” Podcaster Mike Winger raised concerns about the Passion Bible in 2018. (Source: Youtube screenshot) “This is a guy who — through all the evidence we’re seeing — is knowingly perpetrating an intentional, deliberate copy-paste translation with a bunch of deception and lies about what it is,” he told the two other ministers. “And he’s gotten away with it for years.” Fish told RR he has spent dozens and dozens of hours investigating plagiarism in the Passion Bible. He didn’t intend to devote that much time to the project, he said, but he kept finding more examples, and “fell down a wormhole.” He said that based on the evidence he has, he does not believe that the Passion Bible went through a rigorous review process. But if respected scholars are looking at Simmon’s translation choices, they missed more than 350 instances of plagiarism in four short epistles. 34 borrowed phrases in Ephesians On his spreadsheet, Fish documented dozens of examples of apparent plagiarism in Ephesians alone. In the Passion Bible, for example, Ephesians 1:9 says, “He unveiled his secret desires to us—the hidden mystery of his long-range plan.” The phrase “long-range plan” does not appear in the New International Version, the English Standard Version, the New Revised Standard Version, the New King James or the Christian Standard Bible. David Fish, the formation minister at New River Fellowship in Hudson Oaks, Texas claims he has found hundreds of examples of plagiarism in the Passion Bible. (Source: New River Fellowship) But the phrase does appear in that verse in “The Message,” which the late Eugene Peterson described as a paraphrase that he wrote using his “pastoral imagination.” In verse 14, the same thing happens with the phrase “the first installment of what’s coming.” None of the translation teams working on contemporary versions of the Bible found words to that effect in the original Greek. None of them include a footnote offering that as an alternative translation or saying the phrase occurs in some manuscripts. Peterson came up with those six words while trying to expand on the idea of the passage. Then Simmons, a decade or so later, came up with the same six words. In the next chapter of the epistle, the Passion Bible translates Ephesians 2:7 with the word “shower.” The text speaks of “the infinite, limitless riches of His grace and kindness which was showered upon us in Jesus Christ!” No other contemporary translation makes that choice. The verb in the New International Version is “show.” In the New Revised Standard Version, it’s “show.” In the New King James Version, “show.” But in the Message, Ephesians 2:7 says God will “shower grace and kindness upon us in Christ Jesus.” Fish found 31 more apparently borrowed phrases in the Passion translation’s version of Ephesians. The nondenominational minister took the side-by-side comparisons to his uncle, Jeffrey Fish, who teaches in the classics department at Baylor University. Professor Fish said any student who turned in a translation like the Passion Bible in Greek class would receive a failing grade. “The times in my career when this has happened,” the elder Fish said on the podcast, “it’s resulted in a conversation, the student failing the course and sometimes even having to leave the university.” Footnotes traced to eccentric scholar The younger Fish said what he found in the footnotes was even stranger. Almost every reference to Aramaic, the original language spoken by Jesus and the disciples, is lifted whole cloth from other sources, he told RR. In the 2013 Passion Bible version of Colossians, for example, readers find 15 footnotes offering insights from the Aramaic translation of the text. Colossians 3:16 says, “Let the word of Christ live in you richly.” A footnote in the Passion Bible explains that “the Aramaic literally means ‘Manifestation.’” But that biblical text wasn’t written in Aramaic. The epistles, like the rest of the New Testament, were composed in Koine Greek, the dominant trade language of the day. And the standard Aramaic translation of the Greek text, called the Peshitta, does not use “manifestation,” either. It says “dwell.” The Passion Translation, which many theology scholars regard as a paraphrase, has been pulled from top biblical search website Bible Gateway. (Courtesy of BroadStreet Publishing) The next verse, Colossians 3:17, tells Christians that whatever they do, they should do it in the name of Jesus. The Passion Bible translates this as “Let every activity … be drenched with the beauty of our Lord Jesus.” A footnote adds that “activity,” in Aramaic, “literally means ‘commitment.’” And in the next chapter, a footnote adds that the word “duty,” in Aramaic, “literally means ‘sell your last crust of bread,’ which is a metaphor for making a full commitment.” But none of this appears in the standard edition of the Aramaic Bible or in multiple English translations of the Aramaic. Fish tracked each footnote — and other comments — to a text by Victor Alexander, a filmmaker who claimed to have discovered the true Scripture, which had been, he said, suppressed by scholars. Alexander, writing in a blog in the 1990s and 2000s, claimed that all Western Bibles and all known Aramaic Bibles were corrupt. He said they “strayed from the teachings of Eashoa the Messiah” and “distorted major concepts of the Scriptures, such as the Trinity.” He offered his own English translation. It is unclear which manuscripts, if any, he based his translation on. Simmons borrowed Alexander’s words and ideas liberally, according to Fish. “I’ve personally found hundreds and hundreds of examples,” Fish said on the podcast. “He copied from Victor Alexander while claiming to be translating from the Aramaic.” Fish appeared on the Minor Prophets podcast, hosted by JonMark Baker to speak about plagiarism in the Passion Bible. (Source: Screenshot) Call to repentance JonMark co-host of the Minor Prophets podcast, said it’s concerning that a Bible translation that purports to be “entirely new, fresh, fiery,” would involve so many instances of cut-and-paste. And it’s concerning, he added, that the Passion Bible appears to have plagiarized the work of a man almost no Christians would consider reliable. But what really upsets him, Baker said, is the many, many theological claims that have been made for this translation Simmons has repeatedly recounted how he was commissioned, supernaturally, to work on this translation. He’s shared the story on Christian television and in many churches, wowing audiences and moving them to thank God for the Passion translation. “I want to press this home,” Baker said on the podcast. “The guy claimed Jesus himself appeared to him and commissioned him to write this. … I don’t think Jesus himself commissions people to lie.” Fish told RR that accurately citing sources is important, but the problem with the Passion’s plagiarism goes deeper than that. Christians are being deceived, he said, and manipulated to accept a massive money-making deception. “This happens over and over and over,” Fish told RR. “People should repent.” Daniel Silliman is senior reporter/editor at The Roys Report. He began his two decades in journalism covering crime in Atlanta and has since led major investigations into abuse and misconduct in Christian contexts. Daniel and his wife live in Johnson City, Tennessee. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Historic Florida church leaves Southern Baptist Convention in support of female pastors
item.author: Rebecca Hopkins
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Hopkins
item.description: A years-long campaign to ban women’s ordination in the Southern Baptist Convention (SBC) has prompted the 155-year-old First Baptist Church in Gainesville, Florida, to leave its ranks. The church announced its decision in anticipation of a vote at the SBC’s annual national conference on an amendment to ban churches with female pastors. At 12.3 million […]
item.content:encoded: First Baptist Church of Gainesville is leaving the Southern Baptist Convention after decades-long disputes over women’s ordination, following a preliminary vote on a proposed amendment that would ban churches with female pastors. (Source: First Baptist Church) A years-long campaign to ban women’s ordination in the Southern Baptist Convention (SBC) has prompted the 155-year-old First Baptist Church in Gainesville, Florida, to leave its ranks. The church announced its decision in anticipation of a vote at the SBC’s annual national conference on an amendment to ban churches with female pastors. At 12.3 million members, the SBC is America’s largest Protestant denomination. “To treat women as other than or less than — and that includes where and how they can serve — I just don’t think that honors God,” First Baptist pastor Ray Johnson told Roys Report (RR). “I don’t believe that’s the design for the church.” Local church delegates, called “messengers,” approved the amendment June 10 in Orlando, Florida, with 74.6% voting in favor. The amendment must pass at next year’s meeting to take effect. The “Truth and Unity Amendment” is the official title of the measure, but First Baptist church administrator Greg Magruder questions whether the vote brought unity. He told RR that this northern Florida congregation, which has ordained female pastors since at least 1984, had hoped to stay in the SBC and work through the differences. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. First Baptist Church in Gainesville, Florida is leaving the SBC over the denomination’s changing views of woman in pastoral roles. (Courtesy image) “The truth is, we as a church have never wanted to be kicked out of an association or try to cause any kind of disturbance,” Magruder told RR. “It wasn’t our choice. They’ve kind of pushed us out.” At a March semiannual meeting of the local North Central Florida Baptist Association (NCFBA), officials voted to disfellowship from First Baptist because of the church’s female pastors. NCFBA, a regional association, partners with the statewide Florida Baptist Convention, which is associated with the SBC. The expulsion came despite Johnson’s message to the NCFBA meeting. “(Johnson) reminded the association that all Christians interpret Scripture imperfectly and should approach one another with grace rather than punitive action,” Magruder wrote in a press release issued May 28. Seeing the handwriting on the wall, Magruder said, First Baptist left all three associations on April 26. Ahead of this week’s SBC annual conference, Magruder wrote about First Baptist’s decision in a press release first published May 28 in Baptist News Global. Messengers vote during the Southern Baptist Convention annual meeting, Wednesday, June 10, 2026, at the Orange County Convention Center in Orlando. (RNS photo/Bob Smietana) “As 20,000 expected messengers gather in June in Orlando, the Southern Baptist Convention plans to further dismantle a great denomination once united around missions and evangelism,” Magruder wrote. “Through a proposed constitutional amendment, women will be further excluded in ministry and pastoral leadership.” Advocates of the amendment say a change is needed to keep the SBC from getting liberal. “There’s a great line that divides liberal and biblical evangelicalism, and you can see it on this very issue,” said Al Mohler, president of the Southern Baptist Theological Seminary, author of the amendment. “The trajectory of liberal denominations is clear.” On June 9, SBC members voted in a new president, Florida pastor Willy Rice, who has said he believes the denomination has become “woke” and liberal. He also supports the ban on women pastors, calling it biblical. RR emailed and called the SBC, NCFBA and the FBC for their response to First Baptist’s exit. The NCFBA and FBC were unavailable for comment. The SBC sent a statement, saying, in part, “Partnering churches agree to the convention’s statement of faith and voluntarily fund global missions, domestic churches, seminaries and disaster relief through the Cooperative Program.” Willy Rice, pastor of Calvary Church in Clearwater, Fla., in a sermon at the 2026 SBC Pastors’ Conference on June 8, 2026, in Orlando. (Photo by Luc Stringer/Baptist Press) Ongoing debate In 2000, the SBC approved an official faith statement called the Baptist Faith and Message. Its list of beliefs includes an error-free Bible, baptism by immersion and allowing only men as elders and pastors. Long before this point, some Baptist churches, including First Baptist of Gainesville, ordained pastors. Each Southern Baptist church is considered autonomous, with no hierarchy outside its own doors. A pastor in the SBC is ordained in individual churches rather than on a denominational level. Johnson told RR that First Baptist was part of the three Baptist associations since its founding. It was, he added, of the longest-standing members of the NCFBA and the FBC. First Baptist’s website describes a history of “working cooperatively with other churches and faith groups” and being an early leader in encouraging racial integration. Some of First Baptist’s female members served on NCFBA’s boards and committees. Though the SBC’s statement of faith has excluded women from pastoral roles since 2000, only in recent years has it banned member churches with female pastors. First Baptist Church Gainesville pastor Ray Johnson. (Courtesy image) In 2021, Saddleback Church in California, one of the SBC’s largest churches, ordained women as pastors. The SBC responded by ousting Saddleback and began the process of amending its constitution to ban female pastors, as RR previously reported. Only in 2025 did the NCFBA formally affirm the SBC’s 2000 Faith and Message statement that includes the ban. But since 2000, some in NCFBA have attempted to ban churches with female pastors, Magruder said. ‘We’re not going to fight this battle anymore’ Johnson first noticed changed views about women as pastors when he returned to the United States after being an SBC missionary in the Philippines in the 1980s. Some Baptist seminaries had begun restricting women’s roles and firing female professors. Johnson first noticed changed views about women as pastors when he returned to the United States after being an SBC missionary in the Philippines in the 1980s. Some Baptist seminaries had begun restricting women’s roles and firing female professors. “I literally cried tears over it,” Johnson told the RR. In recent years, Johnson told the RR, he realized he was dealing with a pastoral issue. The relationship between churches and denominational associations is a financial one, with churches contributing funds. But it’s also a discipleship one, with an association sharing resources with churches. “I don’t want a church that uses materials or whatnot that does not honor the role of women in all aspects of life and aspects of church leadership,” Johnson said. “I don’t believe that is healthy for our women or for our men, for that matter.” He added, “We also do not want to contribute to them to sustain their effort to further denigrate women and further alienate them from life in the church.” Some of First Baptist’s staff and members have grieved their departure from these long-held relationships, he said. For others, it’s a relief. “Once the association decided to kick us out, all of us knew, all right, it’s coming, we’re not going to fight this battle anymore,” Johnson told RR. “Let’s just leave and send our money elsewhere.” An earlier version of this story showed photos of First Baptist Church in Gainesville, Georgia. We apologize and regret this error. Rebecca Hopkins is a journalist based in Colorado. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Trump drops Lahmeyer endorsement, pastor ends congressional bid after texting scandal
item.author: Mark A. Kellner
item.dc:creator: Mark A. Kellner
item.description: Jackson Lahmeyer, the Tulsa pastor endorsed by President Donald Trump in the race for an open Oklahoma congressional seat, suspended his campaign Wednesday shortly after losing the president’s backing.   The move effectively ended the prospect of an Aug. 25 Republican runoff in Oklahoma’s 1st Congressional District, which would have pitted Lahmeyer against state Rep. […]
item.content:encoded: President Donald Trump withdrew his support for Jackson Lahmeyer on June 17 amid a texting scandal, leading the Oklahoma pastor to end his congressional bid and back away from a GOP runoff. (Source: Social media) Jackson Lahmeyer, the Tulsa pastor endorsed by President Donald Trump in the race for an open Oklahoma congressional seat, suspended his campaign Wednesday shortly after losing the president’s backing. The move effectively ended the prospect of an Aug. 25 Republican runoff in Oklahoma’s 1st Congressional District, which would have pitted Lahmeyer against state Rep. Mark Tedford. “After prayerful consideration with my wife, Kendra, and my team over the last 24 hours, I’ve made the difficult decision to suspend my campaign for Congress,” Lahmeyer said in a statement. “I do not want to be a distraction to my family, my church, and the great people of Oklahoma’s 1st Congressional District, who deserve a strong conservative voice representing them in Washington.” Lahmeyer is the founder and lead pastor of Sheridan Church in Tulsa, which describes itself as a non-denominational, Spirit-filled congregation. Lahmeyer is founder of Pastors for Trump and says on his Facebook page he has met several times with Trump in the Oval Office. His announcement came within minutes of a Truth Social post in which Trump withdrew his support and endorsed Tedford, who led Tuesday’s 10-candidate Republican primary. “I greatly appreciate Jackson Lahmeyer’s hard work under difficult circumstances — He has always been with me, and I will always be with him,” Trump wrote. “But, when it comes to the current Congressional race for Oklahoma’s 1st Congressional District, I will be supporting America First Patriot, Mark Tedford.” Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. MAGA-supporter and congressional candidate Jackson Lahmeyer has lost the support of President Trump. (Source: Jackson Lahmeyer on social media) According to the Associated Press, Tedford received 32.2% of the vote. Lahmeyer garnered 25.9%, forcing a runoff because no candidate won a majority. Two days before the primary, the Daily Mail published allegations involving text messages between Lahmeyer and Republican fundraiser Caitlin Simmons Key. Key, a former Miss Oklahoma USA winner who worked on Lahmeyer’s unsuccessful 2022 campaign against Sen. James Lankford, later joined his congressional effort. The newspaper reported that the exchanges became increasingly intimate. It also reported that Lahmeyer invited Key to his hotel room late at night. The newspaper said he described leaving a Trump-related event at Mar-a-Lago to visit a strip club, where he was offered — but declined — cocaine. Key told the newspaper she and Lahmeyer were never physically involved. Before the primary, Lahmeyer, who is divorced and remarried, acknowledged inappropriate communications. He rejected the newspaper’s broader portrayal. “This matter was already dealt with privately between me and my wife, Kendra, through counsel and prayer with God and spiritual advisors,” he wrote on X. “I own crossing a boundary line through text messaging. I also ended all communication. The British Tabloid tried to paint me out in a way which is not the case.” The Republican nominee will face Democrat John Croisant, a former teacher and businessman who ran unopposed in his party’s primary. The seat opened when state Rep. Kevin Hern resigned after deciding to run for the U.S. Senate seat vacated by Markwayne Mullin, whom Trump appointed in March to lead the Department of Homeland Security. Political analysts rate the district as strongly Republican. Mark A. Kellner is a reporter based in Mesquite, Nevada. He most recently covered statewide elections for the New York Post and was for three years the Faith & Family Reporter for The Washington Times. Mark is a graduate of the University of the Cumberlands and also attended Boston University’s College of Communication. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Trump-backed pastor moves to Republican primary runoff, overcoming report of inappropriate texts
item.author: Mark A. Kellner
item.dc:creator: Mark A. Kellner
item.description: A pro-MAGA pastor caught up in a sexting scandal could become Tulsa’s next member of Congress as he advances to an Aug. 25 runoff election. Jackson Lahmeyer, 34, who is endorsed by President Donald Trump and other leading Republicans, received more votes than any of the 10 candidates in the party primary. Since no candidate […]
item.content:encoded: Jackson Lahmeyer, a Trump-endorsed pastor and congressional candidate, advanced to an Oklahoma Republican runoff despite a recent sexting scandal. (RR Graphic) A pro-MAGA pastor caught up in a sexting scandal could become Tulsa’s next member of Congress as he advances to an Aug. 25 runoff election. Jackson Lahmeyer, 34, who is endorsed by President Donald Trump and other leading Republicans, received more votes than any of the 10 candidates in the party primary. Since no candidate garnered a majority of the votes, however, he faces another election before the November general election. Lahmeyer, lead pastor of Tulsa’s Sheridan Church, a self-described “non-denominational, spirit-filled church,” has been embroiled in a text-messaging scandal involving a woman who is not his wife. Two days before the primary, the Daily Mail reported that the married pastor and father of five sent steamy text messages to a political fundraiser. The fundraiser, Caitlin Simmons Key, was a former Miss Oklahoma USA winner. The paper described her now as a 40-year-old single mother and Republican Party operative, who first met Lahmeyer during his unsuccessful 2022 bid to unseat Sen. James Lankford, an Oklahoma Republican. Key worked on the campaign, and she apparently remained in touch, joining Lahmeyer’s 2026 effort. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Caitlin Simmons Key, a Republican political fundraiser and former Miss Oklahoma USA winner. (Photo courtesy of social media) In recent months, the paper said, text messages between the two heated up, despite Key affirming she and Lahmeyer were never physically involved. The Daily Mail reported that the pastor texted a woman identified as Key, inviting her to his hotel room late at night. It also said he described leaving a Trump-related event at Mar-a-Lago to visit a strip club, where he reportedly said he was offered — but declined — cocaine. At one point, Key texted Lahmeyer, “Jackson if u become congressman & if ever got caught u would be headlines.” The texts were reportedly discovered by Kendra Lahmeyer, the candidate’s wife, just before Mother’s Day. She texted Key, calling her a “home wrecking whore” and warned Key to stay away from her husband, the Daily Mail reported. The candidate, in a post on X, said the article was a political hit piece. “This matter was already dealt with privately between me and my wife, Kendra, through counsel and prayer with God and spiritual advisors,” Lahmeyer wrote. “I own crossing a boundary line through text messaging. I also ended all communication. The British Tabloid tried to paint me out in a way which is not the case.” Initial election results indicate the scandal did not hurt Lahmeyer at the polls. With 99% of the votes counted, the Associated Press reported 25.9% of ballots were cast for Lahmeyer. Rep. Mark Tedford, a Republican who represents Tulsa in the Oklahoma legislature, garnered 32.2% of the vote. If those results hold, the two will face off on Aug. 25. Jackson Lahmeyer’s campaign highlights endorsements from President Donald Trump and several prominent conservative figures. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) The Republican runoff winner will face former teacher and businessman John Croisant, who was unopposed in the Democratic primary. The Republican nominee will be the overwhelming favorite to win in November. The seat opened when Rep. Kevin Hern decided to run for the U.S. Senate, filling a vacancy created when Sen. Markwayne Mullin resigned to become Homeland Security secretary. The Cook Political Report rates the district as “Solid Republican,” according to The New York Times. “Tonight’s results showed career politicians cannot stop the MAGA movement,” Lahmeyer said in a statement emailed to supporters. “I thank God, my wife, Kendra, my family, and my supporters who came out and backed our campaign. But the job is not over. We need everyone who came out today to keep fighting until we succeed. Let’s send a Trump-endorsed Warrior to fight for Oklahoma values in Congress. Game on.” Trump has called Lahmeyer a “MAGA Warrior” who “WILL NOT LET YOU DOWN.” Mark A. Kellner is a reporter based in Mesquite, Nevada. He most recently covered statewide elections for the New York Post and was for three years the Faith & Family Reporter for The Washington Times. Mark is a graduate of the University of the Cumberlands and also attended Boston University’s College of Communication. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Maryland church sued for allegedly ignoring of sexual abuse by former youth pastor
item.author: Rebecca Hopkins
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Hopkins
item.description: Note: This story contains descriptions of child sexual abuse. Central Christian Church (CCC), a suburban Baltimore Assemblies of God (AOG) church, is accused, in a lawsuit filed yesterday, of ignoring alleged sexual abuse by its former youth pastor. Using the initials “R.W.,” the alleged victim is also suing his former youth pastor, Thomas G. Pinkerton […]
item.content:encoded: Thomas George Pinkerton Jr., also known as “Pastor Tommy,” faces charges alleging that he abused six male teenagers at Central Christian Church in Rossville, Maryland, between 2006 and 2010. (RR Graphic) Note: This story contains descriptions of child sexual abuse. Central Christian Church (CCC), a suburban Baltimore Assemblies of God (AOG) church, is accused, in a lawsuit filed yesterday, of ignoring alleged sexual abuse by its former youth pastor. Using the initials “R.W.,” the alleged victim is also suing his former youth pastor, Thomas G. Pinkerton Jr., 52. R.W. states that Pinkerton, also called “Pastor Tommy,” started grooming him in 2006 when he was 15, subjecting him to four years of “severe and repeated sexual abuse.” The lawsuit alleges that in 2008, R.W.’s grandfather brought “serious concerns” about Pinkerton to the church’s lead pastor at the time. But the church ignored the warnings, the suit states. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Pinkerton is facing 24 charges involving the sexual assault of six victims during his tenure as youth pastor at Central Christian Church in Baltimore, Maryland from 2006-2010. (Photo courtesy of Central Christian Church) “We have a situation where (children) absolutely could have been better protected, but the church failed to do so,” Kylie Tejera, R.W.’s attorney, told the Roys Report (RR). “We’re ready to hold accountable the individuals who need to be held accountable.” Tejera works for the national law firm Andreozzi + Foote which has represented survivors suing the Boy Scouts of America and the Catholic Church. The suit, which also sometimes refers to the church as Central Christian Assembly, Inc. was filed on June 15 in the Circuit Court of Maryland for Baltimore County. The AOG is the world’s largest Pentecostal denomination with almost 13,000 American churches. A 2025 investigation found allegations against 200 church leaders since the 1970s, the RR previously reported. Pinkerton pleaded guilty on June 8 to one count of criminal sexual abuse of a minor, the RR previously reported. Authorities have said Pinkerton sexually abused six male teenagers, ages 13 to 19, at CCC and at Pinkerton’s home in Perry Hall, Maryland, from 2006 to 2010. Perry Hall is located 15 miles northeast of Baltimore. CCC is located in Rossville, Maryland, nine miles northeast of Baltimore. A Maryland arrest warrant includes allegations from a seventh man saying Pinkerton abused him in Georgia, a report referred to Georgia police, NBC News previously reported. R.W. is one of the boys who reported the sexual abuse to Maryland police in 2024, Tejera told the RR. Tejera said R.W. declined an interview with the RR due to the stress of discussing what happened to him. Through his attorney, Justin Hollimon, Pinkerton has maintained his innocence despite the plea. His plea deal caps prison time at four years, requires him to serve five years of probation after prison and to register as a sex offender for life. Pinkerton accepted the deal rather than risk a trial, Hollimon told the RR. Pinkerton is in jail awaiting sentencing, which is scheduled for Dec. 14. Holliman hasn’t commented on the civil case yet, despite the RR providing him with a copy of the suit. The RR called and emailed CCC requesting comment on the allegations in the lawsuit, with no response. On June 8, CCC posted a response to Pinkerton’s guilty plea, calling for prayer and promising better child safety. The church included a quote from its lead pastor, Larry Kirk: “There is absolutely no place for abuse, ever, in the church.” “Brotherly” touch used to deceive According to the lawsuit, R.W.’s family was actively involved at CCC. At 15, R.W. regularly attended youth group, which was a “primary source of social, cultural and spiritual community” for R.W., the suit states. “Plaintiff reasonably relied on Defendant Central Christian’s presentation of Defendant Pinkerton as a safe, vetted, and trusted religious leader who was fit to care for, mentor, and supervise youth.” But Pinkerton used his position to target, isolate and groom R.W., the suit states. Pinkerton showed R.W. favoritism, and gave him “inappropriate whispers” and physical touch, such as massages. Pinkerton would call it “brotherly” affection to deceive, control, and lower R.W.’s defenses, the suit alleges. Pinkerton then began sexually abusing R.W. at the church, in Pinkerton’s house and during trips to Georgia, the suit states. Tejera told the RR her firm is still investigating the nature of those trips to Georgia. Pinkerton moved to Georgia sometime after pastoring in Maryland and worked as a traveling evangelist in recent years, NBC News previously reported. The lawsuit states that the church allowed Pinkerton one-on-one access to R.W. without adequate supervision or monitoring. What the church knew In 2008, R.W.’s grandfather told CCC’s lead pastor at the time his concerns about Pinkerton. Other parents and staff also reported concerns about Pinkerton to the lead pastor, the suit states, but the church didn’t investigate or intervene. “Despite receiving these explicit reports, Defendant Central Christian ignored the warnings, failed to investigate Defendant Pinkerton’s conduct, failed to implement operational oversight, and failed to intervene to protect Plaintiff, thereby allowing the severe abuse to continue unabated for years,” the suit states. Tejera told the RR her firm is still investigating who, specifically, that lead pastor was. CCC’s current lead pastors, according to its website, are Larry and Pearl Kirk. Larry is son of Terry and Carol Kirk, listed on the website as “pastor emeritus and founding pastors.” An archived version of CCC’s website from December 2008 describe Terry Kirk as senior pastor and Larry Kirk, one of his sons, as co-pastor. Impact Due to Pinkerton’s abuse and CCC’s failures, R.W. was physically, psychologically and emotionally traumatized, the suit states. “These injuries have severely impaired Plaintiff’s quality of life, disrupted his personal and professional development, and caused significant financial damages, including costs for medical and psychological treatment, as well as a loss of income or diminished earning capacity,” the suit states. R.W. is suing CCC on three counts of negligence: The first count is related to negligent hiring, retention and supervision practices; Count two is a general negligence claim about CCC’s lack of proper child safety policies; The third count of gross negligence and recklessness claims that CCC failed to protect Pinkerton from sexual abuse. “Specifically, Defendant Central Christian acted with conscious, willful, and wanton indifference to the safety of Plaintiff,” the suit states. The suit is asking for at least $75,000 in damages from CCC. R.W. is suing Pinkerton on two counts, one for battery and a second for intentional infliction of emotional distress. The suit is also asking for $75,000 in damages from Pinkerton. Tejera is asking Pinkerton’s other alleged victims to consider joining the suit. “It’s about whether concerns were taken seriously and what steps were seriously taken to protect those children,” she told RR. A 2023 Maryland Child Victims Act allows alleged sexual abuse victims to sue their abusers, with no statute of limitations. The act eliminated a 2017 law that prevented victims who were over 38 years old from suing institutions that hired alleged abusers. Before 2017, victims couldn’t sue after age 25. Rebecca Hopkins is a journalist based in Colorado. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Texas pastor fired after prostitution sting arrest
item.author: Mark A. Kellner
item.dc:creator: Mark A. Kellner
item.description: David Ritchie, lead pastor of Redeemer Christian Church in Amarillo, Texas, is free on bond after a June 13 solicitation of prostitution arrest, the county sheriff’s office told the Roys Report (RR) on Tuesday. Following the arrest, the congregation “immediately removed” Ritchie “from all ministry roles and responsibilities,” Andrew Merker, another lead pastor, said in […]
