item.content:encoded: Pastor and author David Ritchie was arrested in Amarillo, Texas for alleged prostitution solicitation. (Source: Redeemer Christian Church / the Randall County Jail Roster) David Ritchie, lead pastor of Redeemer Christian Church in Amarillo, Texas, is free on bond after a June 13 solicitation of prostitution arrest, the county sheriff’s office told the Roys Report (RR) on Tuesday. Following the arrest, the congregation “immediately removed” Ritchie “from all ministry roles and responsibilities,” Andrew Merker, another lead pastor, said in a statement to the RR. The church officially terminated Ritchie’s employment on Monday. Ritchie, 42, was taken into custody by the Texas Department of Public Safety in an undercover sting operation that saw eight men, including Ritchie, arrested. “The operation was conducted as a part of DPS’s ongoing efforts to combat criminal activity and protect Texas communities,” an agency statement said. According to Sgt. Hillary Garcia of the Randall County Sheriff’s Office in Amarillo, Ritchie posted a $3,500 bond on Monday and “is no longer in custody.” Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. David Ritchie preaches at Redeemer Christian Church in Amarillo, Texas. (Photo courtesy of Redeemer Christian Church) Canyon District Attorney Robert Love, whose area includes Amarillo, told the RR that his office has yet to decide whether it will charge Ritchie with a crime. Redeemer Christian’s website no longer lists Ritchie as lead pastor. Ritchie’s personal website has a biography that says in 2011 he “replanted a historic church in Amarillo that was on the verge of near death. By the grace of God, Redeemer Christian Church has now grown exponentially, celebrating multiple conversions, baptisms, and newly planted churches.” The congregation Ritchie “replanted” is now reeling, Merker told the RR. “We are shocked and heartbroken by this news, and the conduct alleged is inconsistent with the gospel and the standards the scriptures provide for anyone serving in a leadership position at our church,” the pastor’s statement said. “We are committed to exercising appropriate accountability.” The statement also said, “The legal process will determine the facts surrounding the allegations. Much of what has been reported remains outside the church’s direct knowledge, and we will not speculate on matters we cannot verify. Our focus is on caring for our congregation, maintaining the integrity of our ministry, and ensuring stable leadership moving forward.” Ritchie promoted himself on his website as an op-ed writer for the Amarillo Globe News and a “part-time Instructor of Religion at West Texas A&M University”. The newspaper does not list a phone number, and the university told the Amarillo Tribune that Ritchie is not employed there. The now-former pastor described himself as serving “on the boards of numerous non-profit organizations and ministries, including the Refugee Language Project and the Redeemer Network, and has also been an advocate for racial conciliation efforts in Amarillo.” A registrar at Reformed Theological Seminary in Orlando, Florida, confirmed that Ritchie was a 2021 graduate, earning a master of arts in religion from its North Carolina campus. Ritchie’s graduate school thesis became his book, “Why Do the Nations Rage?,” in which he claims nationalism is “as demonic as the worship of Baal.” Mark A. Kellner is a reporter based in Mesquite, Nevada. He most recently covered statewide elections for the New York Post and was for three years the Faith & Family Reporter for The Washington Times. Mark is a graduate of the University of the Cumberlands and also attended Boston University’s College of Communication. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Chinese police raid Early Rain Covenant Church, detain elders and members
item.author: ChinaAid
item.dc:creator: ChinaAid
item.description: Authorities in southwestern China launched a large-scale raid against Early Rain Covenant Church (ERCC) during its Sunday worship service on June 14, detaining dozens of believers, according to reports received by ChinaAid and a subsequent church statement. The church’s worship service, which included families and children, was being held in a hotel conference room in […]
item.content:encoded: On June 14, 2026, local authorities halted worship services of the Early Rain Covenant Church and arrested church leaders, members, and several children, during a Sunday service held in a hotel conference room in Jiangyou City, Sichuan Province, southwestern China. (Courtesy Photo / ChinaAid) Authorities in southwestern China launched a large-scale raid against Early Rain Covenant Church (ERCC) during its Sunday worship service on June 14, detaining dozens of believers, according to reports received by ChinaAid and a subsequent church statement. The church’s worship service, which included families and children, was being held in a hotel conference room in Jiangyou City, about 100 miles northeast of Chengdu, when it was raided at approximately 11 a.m. local time. According to a statement released by ERCC leaders, 60 to 70 government personnel — including police officers, SWAT personnel, Domestic Security Protection Unit officers, religious affairs officials, and local government representatives — “stormed the venue and forcibly took control of the gathering.” Church leaders said 33 believers were detained during the operation, including two elders, Yan Hong and Wu Wuqing. According to the church, detainees were transported to a centralized registration and detention facility in Jiangyou after authorities collected personal identification information from attendees. The church said believers were held for hours while authorities conducted registrations and questioning. According to the statement, some attendees were pressured to sign a “guarantee letter” before being released, though officials allegedly refused to disclose the document’s contents unless individuals first agreed to sign it. Most church members declined and remained at the site until authorities allowed them to leave after the second round of personal identification information collection that began at 6 p.m. According to the church, all detainees were released between 9 p.m. and 11 p.m. Sunday after being questioned. As of Monday morning, Elders Wu Wuqing and Yan Hong were sentenced to 15 and 14 days administrative detention, respectively, while the other believers returned to Chengdu. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. ChinaAid said the operation represents the latest escalation in a years-long campaign against one of China’s best-known Protestant house churches. “This raid is another stark reminder that the Chinese Communist Party continues to treat peaceful Christian worship as a threat to state control,” said Dr. Bob Fu, founder and president of ChinaAid. Local authorities in Jiangyou City, Sichuan Province in southwestern China halted worship services of the Early Rain Covenant Church and arrested church leaders, members, and several children, during a Sunday service on June 14, 2026. (Courtesy Photo / ChinaAid) Continuing pressure on Early Rain Covenant Church Early Rain Covenant Church is one of China’s most prominent independent Protestant house churches and has faced sustained government pressure since authorities launched a sweeping crackdown against the congregation in December 2018. The church’s founding pastor, Wang Yi, was sentenced in December 2019 to nine years in prison on charges of “inciting subversion of state power” and “illegal business operations” after criticizing restrictions on religious freedom and refusing to join state-controlled religious organizations. Since Wang’s imprisonment, church members have continued meeting despite repeated detentions, surveillance, evictions, and disruptions by authorities. The latest raid follows previous operations targeting Early Rain members and leaders, underscoring what advocates describe as an ongoing effort to dismantle the church’s ministry and discourage participation in independent Christian congregations. Texas-based nonprofit group ChinaAid condemned the operation and called on Chinese authorities to immediately release all detained believers. “The detention of church leaders, ordinary believers, and even children for participating in a Sunday worship service is a grave violation of religious freedom and fundamental human rights,” Fu said. “We call on the international community, democratic governments, and all people of conscience to speak out and demand the immediate release of those detained.” ChinaAid urged Christians around the world to pray for the safety and protection of detained believers, the well-being of the children affected by the raid, and strength and peace for families awaiting news of their loved ones. The organization also called for prayer for the continued faithfulness and unity of Early Rain Covenant Church, as well as for greater religious freedom throughout China. This article was originally published at ChinaAid News and has been reprinted with permission. Founded in 2002, ChinaAid is an international, Christian nonprofit human rights organization that inspires, informs, and invites people to transformative action on behalf of persecuted people of all faiths in China. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Sean Feucht claims donor who gave $250,000 does not have right to sue for fraud
item.author: Daniel Silliman
item.dc:creator: Daniel Silliman
item.description: Worship leader Sean Feucht says he can’t be sued for allegedly misspending $250,000 because the contribution was given through a donor-advised fund. Feucht’s attorneys asked the U.S. district court in Los Angeles on June 5 to dismiss a disgruntled donor’s allegations of fraud against the nationally recognized singer  and political activist. The donor, California businessman […]
item.content:encoded: Feucht leads worship at an event in Ellis Island, New York in May 2026. He has filed a motion to dismiss businessman Steve Bray's (inset) lawsuit that alleged Bray's donation to Feucht's Let Us Worship ministry was misused. (RR graphic. Source: Social Media) Worship leader Sean Feucht says he can’t be sued for allegedly misspending $250,000 because the contribution was given through a donor-advised fund. Feucht’s attorneys asked the U.S. district court in Los Angeles on June 5 to dismiss a disgruntled donor’s allegations of fraud against the nationally recognized singer and political activist. The donor, California businessman Steve Bray, sued Feucht in March. He said in court documents that he believed his donation would pay for 10 stops on Feucht’s 2023 Let Us Worship—Kingdom to the Capitol Tour. But then he began to wonder if Feucht took that money and used it on himself. Feucht’s attorneys told the court that worry seems disingenuous. They suggested Bray is attempting “a large-scale fishing expedition” into the worship ministry’s financial records as part of a “larger effort to harass and target” the vocal supporter of President Donald Trump. The case should be dismissed, Feucht’s attorneys argue, because Bray donated to the tour through a third party, the National Christian Foundation. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Christian musician Sean Feucht, of California, sings to the crowd during a rally at the National Mall in Washington, Sunday, Oct. 25, 2020. (AP Photo/Jose Luis Magana) The foundation has helped Christians give more $25 billion to approximately 90,000 ministries in the last 44 years. But when Bray asked the nonprofit to arrange a donation of $250,000 to Sean Feucht Ministries, Inc., Feucht’s lawyers say, he gave up his legal standing to sue. “Plaintiff did not cut a check to Defendants. Nor did Plaintiff make a restricted gift. Quite the contrary: Plaintiff donated to a third-party entity,” attorney David A. Shaneyfelt wrote. “The alleged misuse of donor funds cannot have injured Plaintiff, because (he) had no legal right in the funds at the time that Defendants allegedly received them.” Feucht’s attorneys also argue that the worship ministry operates an association of churches, which the First Amendment exempts from government interference. They are asking the federal court to dismiss the lawsuit “with prejudice.” The motion to dismiss notably does not deny accusations that Feucht used the $250,000 for his personal benefit. Nor does it offer details about how the money was spent. The federal court filing does not even concede that Feucht received a gift of $250,000. The financial contribution is characterized as an “alleged” donation. Force transparency Bray, who has also donated to the Roys Report (RR) and is represented by an attorney who also sits on the RR board, said he hopes his lawsuit will force some much-needed transparency. The businessman told the RR that he liked Feucht when he first met him, and he liked his vision of bringing Christian worship and a call to repentance to capitals across America. Bray had a personal meeting with Feucht before deciding to donate. “He’s very, very, very convincing,” Bray said. “He left and we talked about, ‘Hey, you know, we want to help this guy accomplish this tour that he’s doing.’” Bray had second thoughts, however, after he met Feucht at the Sean Feucht Ministries, Inc., headquarters in Washington D.C. Sean Feucht interviews Thomas Teepell at a house Feucht calls “Camp Elah” in Washington, D.C. (Video screen grab) Feucht calls the two-bedroom, two-and-a-half-bath townhouse that serves as the headquarters “Camp Elah,” after the valley in the biblical story of David and Goliath. He has described the building, which is located less than a mile from the U.S. Capitol and the Supreme Court, as a “fortress of prayer” and “a small place that takes down big giants.” Tax records show Sean Feucht Ministries purchased the 1,050-square-foot townhouse for $967,000. When Bray visited, he was surprised the ministry headquarters was located in the expensive Capitol Hill neighborhood. Paying for that neighborhood seemed unnecessary, he said, and raised questions about how Feucht was using funds. According to an Associated Press investigation, Sean Feucht Ministries also owns about $6 million of property in California, Montana and Texas. Feucht and his wife, Kate, personally own another $4.5 million of real estate in California, Montana and Pennsylvania, reporters found. Feucht, 42, has spent his adult life in worship ministry. Bray said that when he started to think about it, the financial situation didn’t add up. “None of this makes sense. None of it fits together,” the businessman told the RR. “When something doesn’t fit, you have to take it apart, which, you know, is the point of the lawsuit.” Bray said he grew more concerned when he learned that Sean Feucht Ministries is registered with the IRS as an association of churches and does not disclose financial information. Transparency seems like an important Christian value, he said, for ministers who are called to be above reproach. “He needs to have everything disclosed. If you want to be personal, start a company … you don’t have to tell anybody what your salary is,” Bray said. “But if you’re in ministry, you better be willing to, you know, pony up. And when they don’t want to do it, huge red flags, sirens, yellow lights, red lights.” Churches don’t have to disclose finances Feucht’s attorneys defend the ministry’s lack of transparency in the court motion filed this month. Sean Feucht (right) appears with President Donald Trump in a photo Feucht posted on Jan. 20, 2025. (Photo: Facebook) While watchdog groups like Ministry Watch place a high value on nonprofit tax filings, called 990s, Feucht’s lawyers point out that churches are not legally required to disclose financial information. Sean Feucht Ministries ought not be accused of hiding something when it is just following the law, the motion says. “The lack of 990s is not evidence of anything untoward,” the motion to dismiss says. “And needless to say, the acquisition of real estate by a ministry is neither unusual nor wrongful. Were that the rule, a church could not build a sanctuary, nor a pregnancy care center a home for expectant mothers.” Feucht’s attorneys argue also that because his organization is classified as an association of churches, the federal court does not have jurisdiction in the case. Even if Bray can prove he has standing to sue, they argue, the court is prohibited from getting involved because the First Amendment protects freedom of religion. The Supreme Court ruled in 2020 in Our Lady of Guadalupe School v. Morrissey-Berru that religious organizations are free to decide “matters of faith and doctrine.” Feucht’s lawyers argue that those matters include the way the worship minister uses donations. “Mr. Feucht is not merely a secular musician, but a pastor and worship leader,” the motion says. “The ‘Let Us Worship—Kingdom to the Capitol Tour’ … was not merely a succession of secular musical performances, but a series of worship events.” Feucht’s attorneys warn the federal court that if it takes up Bray’s suit, a decision would not only be unconstitutional, but would threaten the freedom of every congregation in the country. “Allowing this suit to proceed risks inviting disgruntled congregants, upset with the practices of their respective ecclesial bodies, to attack and undermine churches’ worship practices by alleging that their tithes or offerings were misused,” the motion says. “That will foster precisely the sort of entanglement with religious doctrine that courts are ill-equipped to adjudicate.” ‘He needs to come clean’ Bray has previously criticized Christian nonprofit that reclassify themselves as churches to avoid financial disclosures, calling the maneuver a “sham.” Many large Christian nonprofits — including Focus on the Family, Compassion International, and the Billy Graham Evangelistic Association — have sought and received an “association of churches” designation in recent years. When criticized, they often defend the move as necessary to protect donors. Bray, however, doesn’t feel protected. “It’s not on the up and up,” he told the RR. “He needs to come clean.” The federal court has not yet set a date to consider the motion to dismiss. Daniel Silliman is senior reporter/editor at The Roys Report. He began his two decades in journalism covering crime in Atlanta and has since led major investigations into abuse and misconduct in Christian contexts. Daniel and his wife live in Johnson City, Tennessee. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Dismissed Chicago Church of Christ pastor faces federal fraud charges
item.author: Mark A. Kellner
item.dc:creator: Mark A. Kellner
item.description: Winston Batino, 55, of Chicago, faces federal charges in what prosecutors allege is a Ponzi-style scheme that snagged at least $2 million from his congregants. He served as a pastor at Chicago Church of Christ’s North Ministry Center in suburban Buffalo Grove until he was dismissed when the fraud emerged. The congregation belongs to the […]
item.content:encoded: Federal prosecutors say former pastor Winston Batino used church relationships to solicit investments in a scheme that allegedly defrauded congregants of more than $2 million. (Screen grab) Winston Batino, 55, of Chicago, faces federal charges in what prosecutors allege is a Ponzi-style scheme that snagged at least $2 million from his congregants. He served as a pastor at Chicago Church of Christ’s North Ministry Center in suburban Buffalo Grove until he was dismissed when the fraud emerged. The congregation belongs to the International Churches of Christ, a network of churches that grew out of the “Boston Movement” centered on the Boston Church of Christ in the late 1970s and 1980s. Both groups have drawn criticism from former members, religious scholars, and some counter‑cult ministries for what is described as authoritarian leadership, intensive “discipling” relationships that closely monitor members’ lives, and high‑pressure recruitment and retention practices. A criminal information was filed in U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Illinois against Batino on May 26, 2026. He faces one count of wire fraud and another charge of perjury while filing a income tax return. Prosecutors allege Batino ran a fraudulent scheme from approximately February 2020 through May 2025. The alleged scheme targeted at least 40 victims, most of them fellow church members. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Chicago Church of Christ’s North Ministry Center in Buffalo Grove, Illinois, where Winston Batino served prior to his termination. (Photo courtesy of Google Maps) Batino allegedly told victims their money would be invested in luxury rehabilitation facilities. Those facilities, prosecutors say, did not exist. He inked agreements with victims promising to repay their investments within a specified time. According to the federal court filing, Batino knew he could not make those repayments. Prosecutors allege Batino instead spent the money on personal expenses, including gambling. He also used funds to pay off earlier victims — a hallmark of a Ponzi-style fraud — according to the charging document. A Ponzi scheme, named for early 20th century con artist Charles Ponzi, is one where initial investors are repaid by later investors, who become victims when no more investors appear. To conceal what he had done, prosecutors say, Batino lied to victims about how he would use their funds. He also allegedly hid the existence and purpose of the scheme itself. The wire fraud count relates to a specific transaction. On or about Jan. 8, 2025, Batino allegedly caused an electronic transfer of approximately $46,500 from the bank account of a victim in nearby Skokie to his own account. The second count involves his 2021 federal tax return, filed on or about Oct. 17, 2022. Batino reported his total income was $195,071. Prosecutors allege he knew his actual income substantially exceeded that figure. Batino was arraigned in Chicago on Monday, June 9, 2026. He was released on an unsecured $10,000 bond. A status hearing is scheduled for Aug. 14. The Chicago Church of Christ issued a statement following the arraignment, according to the Lake & McHenry County Scanner, a news outlet. Church leaders said they first learned of Batino’s alleged misconduct in May 2025. They said he was terminated and removed from fellowship immediately after that. The church commissioned an independent investigation by the law firm of Cheronis & Parente. That investigation concluded recently. Attorney Chris Parente — a former federal prosecutor — submitted his final report to the church in late April. “This independent investigation corroborated the allegations of wrongdoing by Mr. Batino, detailing intentional steps he took to conceal his schemes from key church leaders,” church leaders said in the statement. The investigation found the church was “neither engaged in nor complicit” in Batino’s actions, according to the statement. Church leaders said they have cooperated with law enforcement throughout the past year. The deposed minister’s sermons, such as one from Aug. 20, 2023, remain online at the outpost’s YouTube channel, however. Vadim A. Glozman, Batino’s attorney, told the Roys Report (RR) via email Monday he had “(n)o comment at this time” about the charges. Mark A. Kellner is a reporter based in Mesquite, Nevada. He most recently covered statewide elections for the New York Post and was for three years the Faith & Family Reporter for The Washington Times. Mark is a graduate of the University of the Cumberlands and also attended Boston University’s College of Communication. SHARE THIS:

item.title: U.N. experts warn of rights violations against Christian women and girls in Nigeria
item.author: ICC Staff
item.dc:creator: ICC Staff
item.description: The Office of the United Nations High Commissioner for Human Rights has raised alarm over credible reports of grave human rights violations affecting women and girls in Nigeria, including killings, abductions, sexual violence, forced conversion, forced marriage, and enforced disappearance — particularly targeting Christian and other religious minority communities in the northern and Middle Belt […]
item.content:encoded: Rights violations against Christian women and girls in Nigeria are worsening, U.N. experts warn, citing abductions, abuse and forced marriages. (Source: ICC) The Office of the United Nations High Commissioner for Human Rights has raised alarm over credible reports of grave human rights violations affecting women and girls in Nigeria, including killings, abductions, sexual violence, forced conversion, forced marriage, and enforced disappearance — particularly targeting Christian and other religious minority communities in the northern and Middle Belt regions. According to the statement issued by U.N. experts on June 8, armed extremist groups, including Boko Haram and the Islamic State West Africa Province (ISWAP), remain active in parts of northern Nigeria. The experts also referenced violence linked to broader farmer–herder conflict dynamics, noting widespread insecurity but without providing a detailed attribution framework for all reported incidents. The U.N. communication described disturbing patterns of abuse, including abductions from churches and schools, prolonged captivity involving sexual violence, forced religious conversion, and forced marriage — often affecting minors. In one documented case, a 13-year-old girl in Bauchi State was reportedly abducted and subjected to forced marriage and conversion attempts. In another case, a 16-year-old girl was said to have suffered injury after resisting coercion into marriage allegedly linked to armed actors in her community. The statement further highlighted heightened risks faced by displaced women and girls in internally displaced persons (IDP) camps, where some survivors reportedly face exploitation and coercion in exchange for food, shelter, and necessities. It also noted that some displaced persons conceal their religious identity to avoid targeted harm in areas controlled by armed groups. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. The case of Leah Sharibu, abducted in 2018 from Government Girls Science and Technical College, Dapchi, was again referenced as a symbol of unresolved abductions of schoolgirls in Nigeria, following reports that she remained in captivity after refusing to renounce her Christian faith. Another widely cited case, Deborah Emmanuel, a student killed in Sokoto in 2022 following blasphemy accusations, was referenced in relation to ongoing concerns over mob violence and impunity in religiously sensitive cases. U.N. experts emphasized that these patterns of abuse may constitute violations of international human rights law, including the rights to life, liberty, security, freedom of religion or belief, and protection from torture, slavery, and trafficking. They urged Nigerian authorities to strengthen civilian protection, secure the release of abducted persons, and ensure accountability through effective investigations and prosecutions. The statement also pointed to the broader insecurity across northern Nigeria and the Middle Belt, where cycles of violence have persisted for years amid farmer–herder tensions and the presence of multiple armed groups. Responding to the U.N. communication, advocacy voices referenced by International Christian Concern (ICC) noted that attacks in the Middle Belt continue to affect farming communities, with repeated reports of village raids, church attacks, and mass displacement in states including Plateau, Benue, Kaduna, and Nasarawa. They argue that the scale and pattern of violence in rural Christian communities are often underrepresented in international summaries of the conflict. ICC has previously documented repeated assaults on rural settlements, destruction of livelihoods, and prolonged displacement, raising concerns about accountability gaps and delayed prosecutions that contribute to cycles of impunity. The U.N. experts concluded by calling for urgent measures to protect women and girls, expand psychosocial and rehabilitation services for survivors, and ensure independent investigations into all allegations of serious violations. They warned that continued insecurity and impunity risk deepening civilian suffering, particularly in rural and displacement-affected communities across northern Nigeria and the Middle Belt. This article was originally published at persecution.org and has been reprinted with permission. Founded in 1995, International Christian Concern (ICC) is a Christian organization. It’s an ecumenical, non-governmental, and non-partisan group focused on protecting the human rights of Christians and religious minorities. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Investigation confirms Orange founder Reggie Joiner sexually abused six women
item.author: Daniel Silliman
item.dc:creator: Daniel Silliman
item.description: A Christian nonprofit that produced the youth group curriculum used by one million children every week has quietly released a summary of the findings of a third-party investigation into allegations of sexual abuse. The board of reThink Group, parent company of Orange, said the investigation confirmed founder Reggie Joiner is guilty of sexual misconduct. Victims […]
item.content:encoded: A third party investigation into Orange founder Reggie Joiner has confirmed he is guilty of sexual misconduct. (Courtesy photo) A Christian nonprofit that produced the youth group curriculum used by one million children every week has quietly released a summary of the findings of a third-party investigation into allegations of sexual abuse. The board of reThink Group, parent company of Orange, said the investigation confirmed founder Reggie Joiner is guilty of sexual misconduct. Victims include four women who worked for Joiner, according to the report, and two who were under his pastoral care at North Point Community Church. Joiner cofounded the metro Atlanta megachurch with Pastor Andy Stanley. One of the women at the church was 19 when the abuse began, the statement said. ReThink called the findings “heartbreaking.” Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. “The investigative findings have left us grieving for those who experienced pain, stress and confusion during their time with reThink,” the board said. “We recognize that there was a betrayal of trust placed in the organization, and most importantly, in its leaders.” North Point Community Church Lead Pastor Andy Stanley (right) and Reggie Joiner speak at a church event in September 2019. (Photo: Facebook) ReThink is not releasing the full investigation done by the law firm Castañeda + Heidelman LLP, citing the need to protect confidentiality. The summary offers few details about the sexual abuse, spending more time reporting on victims willingness to cooperate with the investigation. The summary statement was posted as an update to a statement reThink posted on its mostly defunct website the Friday before Thanksgiving Day 2025. News of the update was first reported by Ministry Watch on June 8. ‘Zero accountability for the board’ Victims and whistleblowers told the Roys Report (RR) the timing seemed designed to avoid attention and evade responsibility. “It does not apologize or center the victims or demonstrate any kind of victim awareness at all,” said whistleblower Kim Nunes, a former project manager for Orange. “There’s zero accountability for the board. This is what I expected, but there was one percent of me that was hoping the right thing would be done.” Board members did not respond to emails from the RR. A spokesperson anonymously declined to answer further questions. Reggie Joiner (Courtesy Photo) “The statement regarding the third-party investigation was published in November 2025 and has been available continuously at www.rethinkgroup.org for the past seven months,” the unsigned email to RR said. “No additional statements have been made or are planned.” According to reThink’s November 21 statement, Joiner concealed his sexual misconduct, and no one on the board knew or could have known what was happening. The board notes the nonprofit did not have written policies about sexual harassment, a professional human resources department or procedures for reporting misconduct. The statement does not apologize for these oversights. The board instead expresses hope that the “painful lessons” will be useful to others. “Our prayer is that the truth revealed here will be a step toward healing for those who have suffered, and a reminder to all of us that leadership is a sacred trust, never to be taken lightly,” the statement said. “This can serve as an opportunity for accountability, growth, and change.” A former employee who spoke to investigators said it was sad the board didn’t take the opportunity it had to be accountable. “I honestly feel the whole thing was sort of a joke,” she told the RR, speaking on condition of anonymity, citing mental health concerns. “All of the questions we had, it was like, ‘Wait for the report.’ When the report came out, it was nothing.” The youth group curriculum created by Orange is used by one million children every week, predominantly across the United States. (Source: Orange official website) Curriculum was widely endorsed Joiner founded the organization that became reThink and Orange in 2001 while still serving as executive director of family ministry at North Point. He went out on his own in 2006, producing youth group curricula that was widely and quickly adopted by children’s ministries across the country. ReThink’s annual revenue grew from $168,500 in 2001 to more than $4 million in five years. And it kept growing. In 2024, when accusations of sexual abuse were made public, the Georgia-based nonprofit brought in $24 million. The reThink curricula covered every age group from preschool to high school. For preschoolers, “Let’s Fly” taught Christ-like compassion. For older kids, there was “Table of Context,” a Bible study presented as “ancient words for modern times,” and “GOAT,” teaching teens to become “the greatest” with Jesus’ teachings about sacrifice and servant leadership. When Joiner wrote a book articulating his ideas for getting churches and families to work together to develop children’s faith, it was widely endorsed by evangelical megachurch leaders. Stanley called it a “paradigm shift” and a “brand-new approach to capturing the hearts and imaginations of this generation’s parents and kids.” Craig Groeschel, pastor of Life.Church, said “Reggie Joiner’s ideas … will empower and motivate Christian leaders around the world to rethink how the church and families can and should work together.” Sue Miller, a former director of children’s ministries at Willow Creek Community Church, praised the book as a “must read” and “just what we all need to strategically partner with parents.” Pastor Craig Groeschel delivers the closing session at the 2021 Global Leadership Summit, focusing on emotions in leadership. (Photo courtesy of open blog.life.church) ‘Compliance and silence’ While the company grew, Joiner was grooming and manipulating women into sex. One woman, in an anonymous statement, said she was “fully conditioned … to compliance and silence.” When Joiner kissed her, she said, it “felt like being shoved down a dark tunnel slide.” Another victim has previously said she was prepared to “protect Reggie at all costs,” and “labored under the belief that protecting Reggie was the only way to protect Orange.” In 2025, Joiner told investigators that he believed all the relationships were consensual — admitting only that one, with a teenage girl in his church, involved abuse. Georgia law prohibits employers from sexual harassing employees. The legal definition includes any “conduct of a sexual nature” that employees are asked to accept, “implicitly or explicitly,” as a “term or condition of work.” Joiner speaks in a promotional video for Orange Conference 2020 (Screen shot) Georgia also passed a law in May criminalizing clergy sexual abuse. The law prohibits ministers from claiming consent as a defense The behavior that many ministers have called “an affair” or an “inappropriate relationship” is now a felony, punishable by up to 25 years in prison. Joiner has not been charged with any crime. He reportedly still lives in Atlanta. ReThink remains open but is not producing any more materials. The organization’s suburban Atlanta office now appears to be occupied by a dentist. Material still for sale The existing curricula and the subsidiary company Orange were acquired in 2024 by the Amazing Life Foundation. The nonprofit, founded at that time, has revamped some of the material and rebranded Orange’s annual conference. Figures from Form 990 reports of The reThink Group, the nonprofit behind Orange, showing Orange CEO Reggie Joiner’s past four years of compensation. (Spreadsheet: TRR Analysis) Malachi Boyuls, president of Amazing Life, told the RR that the company has “no preexisting or ongoing relationship” with Joiner, reThink or anyone in leadership at reThink. Boyuls declined to answer any questions about royalty payments. According to the last 10 years of available tax records, reThink was paying between $1 million and $3 million in royalties per year. Some of that money went to Joiner, who also earned a salary from reThink. Victims and whistleblowers who spoke to the RR said everything still feels unresolved. Instead of stating what happened definitively and for the record, the board’s statement leaves much cloaked in secrecy, they said. “I wish that more would come out,” a former employee told the RR. “I want the truth to be out there. But I’ve lost hope.” Daniel Silliman is senior reporter/editor at The Roys Report. He began his two decades in journalism covering crime in Atlanta and has since led major investigations into abuse and misconduct in Christian contexts. Daniel and his wife live in Johnson City, Tennessee. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Alabama pastor arrested, elders blame disgruntled former members
item.author: Ann Marie Shambaugh
item.dc:creator: Ann Marie Shambaugh
item.description: Elders at an Alabama church are blaming the arrest of their senior pastor on disgruntled former members, saying their own investigation already cleared him.  Scott Willmore, 50, who leads The Word Community Church in the Tuscaloosa suburb of Northport, has been charged with one count of first-degree theft of property, according to local news website […]
item.content:encoded: Pastor Scott Willmore who leads The Word Community Church in the Tuscaloosa, has been charged with one count of first-degree theft of property. (RR Graphic. Source: Tuscaloosa County Jail, CBS42 screen shot) Elders at an Alabama church are blaming the arrest of their senior pastor on disgruntled former members, saying their own investigation already cleared him. Scott Willmore, 50, who leads The Word Community Church in the Tuscaloosa suburb of Northport, has been charged with one count of first-degree theft of property, according to local news website Al.com. Willmore spent approximately one hour in the Tuscaloosa County jail on June 8 and was released after posting a $30,000 bond. The Tuscaloosa County Sheriff’s Office began investigating Willmore after an unspecified number of church members reported concerns about the congregation’s finances, according to WIAT Ch. 42, a CBS affiliate in Birmingham. A sheriff’s office spokesman declined to confirm to WIAT whether the person who brought the complaint is still active in the church. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Willmore (pictured with his wife, unnamed) is supported by The Word Community Church, who claim the charges are false and stem from disgruntled former members. (Source: The Word Community Church) A grand jury indicted Willmore after reviewing evidence from the investigation. Officials did not reveal how much money Willmore is accused of stealing, but a theft of more than $2,500 is a first-degree felony. The elder board posted a statement on the church website about the charge, disclaiming responsibility. “As the Elders at The Word Community Church we would like it to be known that the church is not the one that is pursuing the matter that is being published in the news media,” the statement reads. The statement adds that the elders conducted their own inquiry. “The current Elder Board with the current Finance committee have not found any wrongdoing of our Pastor and we stand behind him,” the statement reads. “These charges are being brought by disgruntled former members of the church, and he is being falsely accused. We want the public to know our side of these allegations.” Elder Dale Lester told WIAT that there are no plans to remove Willmore from his position. “It is former members who walked out of the church who are doing this,” Lester said. “They want him out, and the crazy thing is they had the power to fire him. Why do all of this and drag his name through the mud?” Lester described Willmore as a “man of integrity.” “I’ve been a friend of his for seven years,” Lester said. “I know him, and I think I know him well enough.” An arraignment for Willmore is scheduled in August, according to Al.com. Ann Marie Shambaugh has reported as a print journalist in multiple states, including currently in Carmel, Indiana. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Former PCA pastor, now a former PCA member: presbytery deposes and excommunicates convicted rapist Christopher Willett
item.author: Mark A. Kellner
item.dc:creator: Mark A. Kellner
item.description: Mississippi pastor Christopher Willett — convicted in March of raping his teenage daughter — has been stripped of his Presbyterian Church in America post and kicked out of the denomination. At a called meeting this week, the Grace Presbytery voted unanimously to depose Willett from his ministerial office and excommunicate him from the church, byFaith, […]
item.content:encoded: Christopher Willett, former senior pastor of First Presbyterian Church (PCA) in Crystal Springs, Mississippi, was arrested and pleaded guilty to raping his teen daughter. (RR Graphic) Mississippi pastor Christopher Willett — convicted in March of raping his teenage daughter — has been stripped of his Presbyterian Church in America post and kicked out of the denomination. At a called meeting this week, the Grace Presbytery voted unanimously to depose Willett from his ministerial office and excommunicate him from the church, byFaith, the PCA’s online magazine, reported June 12. The two presbytery’s two actions — desposition and excommunication — are distinct. Deposition stripped Willett of his ordained office. Excommunication stripped him of church membership, excluding him from the sacraments and from fellowship in the church. The vote ended a church disciplinary process that ran parallel to a criminal case. Willett, 48, formerly senior pastor of First Presbyterian Church in Crystal Springs, 25 miles southwest of Jackson, pleaded guilty March 16 in Copiah County Circuit Court in Hazlehurst, the Roys Report (RR) reported. He pleaded guilty to one count each of statutory rape, sexual battery, and incest, according to District Attorney Daniella Shorter Levy’s office. The victim was his teenage daughter. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. First Presbyterian Church in Crystal Springs, Mississippi, where Christopher Willett served as senior pastor before his conviction and subsequent deposition and excommunication by the Presbyterian Church in America. (Photo courtesy of Google Maps) The court sentenced Willett to 30 years in prison, with five years suspended and 25 years to serve. Upon release, he must serve five years of supervised probation and register as a sex offender, according to the sentencing order. He is barred from contacting the victim in person, electronically, or through intermediaries. The criminal case began March 7, 2025, when Willett’s 19-year-old daughter reported her father to Crystal Springs police. Investigators determined the abuse was systematic and repeated over several years. Willett was serving as minister of the Crystal Springs church during the abuse and its reporting, officials said. The presbytery’s process tracked the criminal case. At its May 2025 meeting, Grace Presbytery found a strong presumption of guilt, appointed a prosecutor, and suspended Willett from ministry, byFaith reported. It waited for the criminal case to resolve before holding a separate trial. At its May 2026 meeting, with Willett unable to appear and having submitted no written plea, a not-guilty plea was recorded for him. Counsel was appointed, and trial was set for June 9. The presbytery conducted the trial, found Willett guilty, and inflicted the censures of deposition and excommunication. Even a suspension order the presbytery imposed in May 2025 had not ended Willett’s ministerial activity. After May 2025, the presbytery learned Willett was baptizing fellow inmates of the Copiah County Jail, byFaith reported. Charges for violating his suspension were included in a second church indictment, which the presbytery deemed moot once he was deposed and excommunicated. Willett’s legal troubles may not be over. He has been indicted on similar charges in Marion County, and byFaith reported he may face charges in an additional county. Mark A. Kellner is a reporter based in Mesquite, Nevada. He most recently covered statewide elections for the New York Post and was for three years the Faith & Family Reporter for The Washington Times. Mark is a graduate of the University of the Cumberlands and also attended Boston University’s College of Communication. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Lutheran pastor and bus driver in Ohio arrested for sexual battery, grooming
item.author: Peter Johnson
item.dc:creator: Peter Johnson
item.description: A former Lutheran pastor and bus driver in Deshler, Henry County, Ohio, was arrested last week and charged with sexual battery and grooming, according to a Deshler Police Department press release. Christopher G. Walter, 52, from Deshler, Ohio, about 45 miles southwest of Toledo, was booked into the Corrections Center of Northwest Ohio on June […]
item.content:encoded: Christopher Walter, former lead pastor of Immanuel Lutheran Church and a local school bus driver, was arrested and charged with sexual battery and grooming.(Source: Corrections Center of Northwest Ohio) A former Lutheran pastor and bus driver in Deshler, Henry County, Ohio, was arrested last week and charged with sexual battery and grooming, according to a Deshler Police Department press release. Christopher G. Walter, 52, from Deshler, Ohio, about 45 miles southwest of Toledo, was booked into the Corrections Center of Northwest Ohio on June 2. Details about the victim are not publicly available. Walter was the lead pastor of Immanuel Lutheran Church, which has deep roots in Ohio and was founded by German immigrants in 1888, according to its website. Walter has been at the church for a little over five years, according to his LinkedIn. Walter first started working in ministry in a United Methodist Church in rural Ohio. He was a youth pastor who focused on kids between 7th and 12th grade and liked to teach “growth in spiritual discipline.” He went on to work at four more United Methodist Churches in Ohio before taking his position in the mainline Lutheran congregation in 2021. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. An online church biography said “Pastor Chris is a passionate amateur cook and a dog lover, often sharing glimpses of his culinary creations and canine companions on social media.” Walter served at Immanuel Lutheran in Deshler, Ohio for five years. (Courtesy Deshler Chamber of Commerce) Daniel Beaudoin, the bishop of the Northwestern Ohio Synod of the Evangelical Lutheran Church of America, fired Walter on June 3. He described the allegations as “distressing” and “serious.” “The abuse of anyone, especially minors, has no place in our church,” Beaudoin said in a press release. Immanuel Lutheran Church declined to comment. Walter was also a bus driver for the Patrick Henry Local Schools in Deshler. In a statement on Facebook, officials from the school district said Walter has been suspended without pay. They had no further comment. According to court documents, Walter was arraigned on June 3. He was charged with sexual battery, a felony, which in Ohio law includes cases where the offender is a minister and the victim attends the congregation. He is also facing a charge of sexual grooming, which the law defines as enticing, coercing or solicit a minor to engage in sexual activity. The state criminalized grooming in 2025. He waived the preliminary hearing and was bound over to the Henry County Common Pleas Court for further proceedings, court records show. Walter posted bail according to Napoleon Municipal Court documents and was released on condition he wear a GPS tracker, check in with a probation officer and have no contact with anyone under the age of 18. Walter faces a maximum possible sentence of three years and three months. Peter Johnson is a student at Boston University in Boston, Massachusetts. He is studying his Masters in Journalism. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Indiana megachurch pastor C. J. Johnson fired after years of concerns and ‘a significant allegation’
item.author: Daniel Silliman
item.dc:creator: Daniel Silliman
item.description: An Indiana megachurch that has long defended its pastor from internal and external criticism changed course Wednesday and fired him. Northview Church, a multi-site with 14 locations from Indianapolis to Lafayette, announced the dismissal of C. J. Johnson in an email that assured members the decision wasn’t as sudden as it seemed. “We recognize that […]
item.content:encoded: Northview Church previously defended Senior Pastor C.J. Johnson against criticism but announced his termination on June 10 after elders determined dismissal was warranted. (Photo courtesy of Northview Church) An Indiana megachurch that has long defended its pastor from internal and external criticism changed course Wednesday and fired him. Northview Church, a multi-site with 14 locations from Indianapolis to Lafayette, announced the dismissal of C. J. Johnson in an email that assured members the decision wasn’t as sudden as it seemed. “We recognize that this news may bring a range of emotions,” the June 10 email said. “The elders did not arrive at this decision quickly or lightly.” The dismissal is effective immediately. The email said that the church investigated “a significant allegation” last year and that all three executive pastors serving under Johnson brought additional concerns to the elders last month. Church leaders did not explain the nature of those concerns, saying only that they included “leadership style,” “interpersonal conduct” and “behaviors.” Johnson, 41, could not be reached for comment. Ten days before he was let go, Johnson preached about temptation. Using the Old Testament story of Samson, he urged the congregation of 8,800 to be wary of the ways they might be tricked into not trusting God. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Flagship campus of multi-site megachurch Northview Church in Carmel, Indiana. (Photo via social media) “You will be tempted far greater in the area of your strengths than you will your weaknesses,” he said. “None of us want to admit that we have blind spots — areas in our life that are obvious to others — but we keep making the same mistake. And it’s areas in our strength that we assume we can manage without God’s assistance.” Hired for his preaching Johnson took the senior pastor position at Northview in 2021. He replaced retiring pastor Steve Poe, an entrepreneur-turned-pastor who grew the nondenominational church from a single congregation of roughly 500 people in a suburb of Indianapolis to a multi-site with attendance numbers above 10,000. “It was a goal to reach as many people as we possibly could,” Poe said as he approached retirement. “The motto we use here is that every number has a name, every name has a story, and every story matters to God.” At the time Poe believed Johnson was the clear choice for his successor. Johnson was one of seven finalists selected by VanderBloemen, a leading church search firm, and widely praised for his effective preaching. Poe watched a sermon that Johnson preached at Southland City Church, an Assemblies of God congregation outside Minneapolis, and really liked what he saw. Even at Johnson’s hiring, however, there were concerns about his management abilities. Poe told church members that Johnson had been accused of mishandling finances and mistreating people in the Minnesota congregation. But the outgoing pastor said he and the elders were “110% convinced” the allegations were false. Poe and Johnson worked together for a year to ensure a smooth leadership transition. Staff departures raise concerns As Johnson took control of Northview in 2022, however, similar concerns reemerged. Current, a local newspaper covering eight communities in the suburbs of Indianapolis, reported an exodus of dozens of staff members and church leaders. Eight told Current that asking questions about Johnson’s past or present was not allowed at the megachurch. “I care about Northview,” one former staff member said. “I don’t want it to sink. But I think it’s sinking, and if there’s not enough humility, grace and willingness to have this conversation, it’s going to go down.” Husband and wife CJ and Kristin Johnson partnered in ministry at Northview Church in Carmel, Indiana. (Photo courtesy Northview Church) The elders at the time rejected these concerns. They reaffirmed their “unwavering support” for Johnson in a public statement. “We did do our homework and stand resolute in our conviction that this was God’s choice for our church,” Northview leaders said. “Pastor CJ is a talented leader … His heart for God is full and genuine.” Johnson received a standing ovation when he preached the following Sunday. That affirmation didn’t end the turmoil inside Northview, however. According to former leaders who spoke to the Roys Report (RR), 10 campus pastors quit by the end of 2025, citing “toxic leadership.” The former leaders also said that four elders — Greg James, Travis Theobold, Steve Hilger and Scott Meyer — were pushed out for insufficient support of the senior pastor. Johnson reportedly told one elder to stop “sticking his nose where it doesn’t belong.” All information about the church’s elders and the elder board was subsequently removed from the Northview website. New allegation In January 2025, elders learned of a new allegation that they did consider serious enough to investigate. A woman in Scottsdale, Arizona, said that Johnson propositioned her for sex during marriage counseling. Kerri Mosher, 55, told the RR it took her months to come forward. “I didn’t want money. I didn’t want the attention. But it really was so sickening to me,” she said. “It was so creepy. And I was just shocked to learn the church didn’t have anything in place to prevent this.” Mosher said her husband, Gregg, occasionally attended Northview when he was in Indiana. He liked Johnson’s preaching and the two would talk about business opportunities and financial investments. When the Moshers had serious marriage problems, Johnson flew to Arizona to help. According to Kerri Mosher, however, it seemed like he had other things in mind. When Johnson first arrived, she said, she showed him around the house, and he kept making comments that seemed sexual. He walked into the master bathroom, she recalled, and said “You could have some fun in this shower.” Then he looked in her closet, picked up a pair of boots and commented “Oh you need to wear these.” The statements seemed suggestive, but Mosher shrugged it off. “It’s just hard to believe that a pastor would be like that,” she told the RR. “Until you’re a part of it.” She said that Johnson, however, kept pushing and insinuating they should have sex. At one point he asked her if she’d really “never ever” cheated on her husband, she said. At another point, she remembers the megachurch pastor telling her, “You need to explore your sexual side. … You should be having the best enjoyment at this stage of your life.” Mosher later told church leaders that “110% he wanted to do something physical that night.” Law firm investigated Northview elders hired an Indianapolis law firm that specializes in criminal defense, personal injury and family law to investigate Mosher’s allegations. An attorney with the firm found that Mosher’s husband and daughter confirmed relevant parts of her story. Both said that Mosher immediately told them about her interaction with Johnson, and they’d never known her to make up stories. Greg Mosher told the attorney that when his wife recounted what happened, she shook and started to cry. C. J. Johnson preaches at Northview Church in Carmel, Indiana near Indianapolis. (Video screengrab) When the attorney spoke to Johnson, however, he denied most of the allegations. He said he only brought up sex and cheating as a “pressure test,” which he described as a counseling technique. The attorney said in his final report in September 2025 that Johnson’s account of events was “self serving” and lacked any corroborating evidence. He also advised the elders to remember that where there’s smoke, there is usually fire. According to the elders’ statement this week, the “findings in the investigation were inconclusive.” In a statement emailed to RR, the elders said they “made the decision not to terminate CJ Johnson at that time based on the third-party report they received.” ‘Additional concerns’ Nine months later, executive pastors Kurt Brodbeck, Mike Polster and Jason Pongrantz met with the elders. Executive Pastor Mike Polster was one of three Northview Church leaders who met with elders to discuss concerns about C.J. Johnson. (Photo courtesy of Northview Church) The three men brought up “additional concerns” about Johnson, the church’s statement said, and “the elders determined termination of his employment was warranted.” The church declined to provide details about the additional concerns. Current and former members alike were surprised by the sudden change in leadership. “We recognize that this news may bring a range of emotions,” the elders wrote to the congregation. “Our commitment is to acknowledge the unhealthy culture that developed, learn from our past, support one another, and move forward together in a healthier direction.” Mark Crull, the discipleship pastor at Northview since 2014, has been appointed interim lead pastor. His brother, Mike Crull, is operations pastor. Correction: A previous version of this article incorrectly stated Mark Crull’s previous role at Northview. Daniel Silliman is senior reporter/editor at The Roys Report. He began his two decades in journalism covering crime in Atlanta and has since led major investigations into abuse and misconduct in Christian contexts. Daniel and his wife live in Johnson City, Tennessee. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Long Island pastor gets 17.5-year prison term for child sex exploitation
item.author: Mark A. Kellner
item.dc:creator: Mark A. Kellner
item.description: Note: This story contains graphic details about child sexual abuse. A Long Island, New York, pastor who coerced children into producing sexually explicit material and raped a minor in a public restroom was sentenced June 9 to 210 months (17.5 years) in federal prison. Jose Saez Jr., 30, of Brentwood, New York, received the sentence […]
item.content:encoded: Jose Saez Jr. served as a pastor at Iglesia Cristiana Alumbrando El Camino in Brentwood, New York, before pleading guilty to child sexual exploitation charges. (Photo courtesy of FBI.gov) Note: This story contains graphic details about child sexual abuse. A Long Island, New York, pastor who coerced children into producing sexually explicit material and raped a minor in a public restroom was sentenced June 9 to 210 months (17.5 years) in federal prison. Jose Saez Jr., 30, of Brentwood, New York, received the sentence from U.S. District Judge Joan M. Azrack in federal court in Central Islip. Saez had pleaded guilty on March 11, 2025, to one count of sexual exploitation of a child, the second count of a 12-count superseding indictment. Saez was a pastor at Iglesia Cristiana Alumbrando El Camino (“Lighting the Way Christian Church”) in Brentwood. He is also the father of three children, who were age 1 to 5 during the time of the alleged crimes. Prosecutors said that Saez exploited his trusted role and standing in the community to identify, groom and abuse child victims. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Iglesia Cristiana Alumbrando El Camino (“Lighting the Way Christian Church”) in Brentwood, New York. (Photo courtesy of Google Maps According to a U.S. Justice Department sentencing memorandum, Saez created and used social media accounts and an encrypted messaging service to contact minors and engage them in sexually explicit conversations, including on video. He took on false identities, including that of a minor, a politician and a law enforcement officer, to gain the trust of children and persuade them to comply with his sexual demands. Investigators said Saez coerced several minors into creating and sending him images and videos in which they masturbated and inserted objects into their anuses. Prosecutors described Saez’s actions as causing the victims to “participate in their own sexual abuse.” Saez then distributed some of those files to others. In August 2023, according to a separate DOJ letter, Saez told an undercover law enforcement officer, during an online conversation, that he had sexually abused an infant. The “sweet spot” for victim, he said, was between the ages of 11 and 15, and he indicated that he found many of his younger victims at “church,” according to court records. Under a court-authorized search warrant, agents seized Saez’s phone and other devices and recovered what prosecutors described as a large collection of child sexual abuse material. A subsequent letter prosecutors sent to the court changed that to hundreds of videos and thousands of images of such material. Saez admitted he knew the material was child pornography and that he had engaged in similar conduct with multiple minors. In May 2023, Saez directed a minor victim to meet him at a public restroom in Mount Sinai, New York, and raped the victim on the restroom floor, prosecutors said. Court documents state that Saez communicated with minor victims both over the internet and in person. In the sentencing memorandum, prosecutors said Saez’s conduct was “egregious and predatory” and argued that he manipulated multiple children “over an extended period,” sending them “hundreds and hundreds of messages” from different accounts as part of a calculated effort to continue the abuse. They said his actions included collecting, soliciting and distributing child sexual abuse material, as well as raping a minor in person. His “incessant conduct requires a substantial term of imprisonment,” they concluded. Saez was arrested in late September 2023, according to an FBI press release. The 210‑month sentence will be followed by five years of supervised release, with special conditions. Saez is also subject to a $100 special assessment and a criminal forfeiture order. Remaining counts in the superseding indictment were dismissed at the government’s request as part of the plea disposition. The sentence was announced by Joseph Nocella Jr., U.S. Attorney for the Eastern District of New York, and James C. Barnacle Jr., assistant director in charge of the FBI’s New York Field Office. “Today’s lengthy sentence holds the defendant accountable for his exploitation of children and his abuse of a position of trust,” Nocella said in a statement. “As a pastor, the defendant occupied a role that carries with it a profound responsibility to protect and guide others, particularly the most vulnerable. Instead, he used that position to harm children. Protecting our community from predators like the defendant will always be a priority of our Office.” Barnacle called Saez’s conduct indefensible and praised the law enforcement team that investigated the case. “Jose Saez abused his position as a (p)astor by coercing innocent children to send sexually explicit images and even forcing one minor to engage in sexual acts in a public space,” Barnacle said. “The pain he inflicted on the children who trusted him is indefensible, and the FBI with its partners brought this individual to justice.” Mark A. Kellner is a reporter based in Mesquite, Nevada. He most recently covered statewide elections for the New York Post and was for three years the Faith & Family Reporter for The Washington Times. Mark is a graduate of the University of the Cumberlands and also attended Boston University’s College of Communication. SHARE THIS:

item.title: ‘Now the victims can speak out’ — Tennessee court rules against NDA in child sexual abuse case
item.author: Rebecca Hopkins
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Hopkins
item.description: A landmark decision in a Nashville court has given victims of child sexual abuse in Tennessee the right to disclose details despite previously signing nondisclosure agreements (NDAs). “Now the victims can speak out,” Elizabeth Phillips, a leader in a nationwide effort to end NDAs that silence victims of sexual abuse, wrote on social media.  Thanks […]
item.content:encoded: Elizabeth Phillips (pictured) and other advocates are celebrating a landmark Tennessee court ruling that allows child sexual abuse victims to speak out despite previously signed NDAs. (RR Graphic) A landmark decision in a Nashville court has given victims of child sexual abuse in Tennessee the right to disclose details despite previously signing nondisclosure agreements (NDAs). “Now the victims can speak out,” Elizabeth Phillips, a leader in a nationwide effort to end NDAs that silence victims of sexual abuse, wrote on social media. Thanks to a ruling by a Tennessee appeals court, the plaintiff, a mother whose toddler son was allegedly subjected to sexual abuse in 2014, can speak freely. The ruling reflects a growing effort across several states to make NDAs unenforceable in civil case settlements that involve allegations of sexual abuse. “What we are seeing now in Texas, Missouri, Louisiana, Alabama, Georgia, and now in this Tennessee court ruling is that the tide is turning,” Phillips, founder of No More Victims, told the Roys Report (RR). “Freedom of speech is a fundamental constitutional right and is especially important in protecting victims’ voices.” Appeals Court Judge Andy Bennett ruled in favor of a mother who had previously alleged that a 14-year-old church volunteer sexually abused her then son, then 3 years old. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. In 2015, “Jane” and her husband sued Fellowship Bible Church (FBC), a nondenominational church in the Nashville suburbs, on behalf of their son. Their 2016 settlement with the church included confidentiality and non-disparagement clauses to prevent them from speaking about the abuse. But now that silence is broken. A Nashville court ruling in favor of “Jane” has given child sexual abuse victims in Tennessee the right to disclose details of their abuse despite having signed nondisclosure agreements. (Source: Stock image/ Screen grab Tennessee Court of Appeals YouTube) “If people did bad things, and it was harmful to people … people need to know,” Jane told The Tennessean. Jane is a pseudonym used in some court filings. RR typically keeps identities of sexual abuse victims and their close family members confidential. Testing a new law voiding NDAs A key factor for the ruling came from a 2018 state law. On the heels of the #MeToo movement, the Tennessee General Assembly unanimously and with bipartisan support voted into law Tennessee Code 29-34-103. The law determined that NDAs that are part of settlement agreements in child sexual abuse civil cases are “void and unenforceable and contrary to public policy of state.” Jane and her free speech attorney Daniel Horwitz filed suit in 2023, becoming the first plaintiff in the state to test out whether the new law applied to a preexisting NDA. Jane wanted the right to speak publicly about what happened to her son. Until then, The Tennessean reported, no one had mentioned the new law in a single court case. “I wonder if people even knew about it,” Horwitz told The Tennessean. “It becomes law and it just sits. Nothing happens with it. It never seems to come up or get cited anywhere.” A trial court dismissed Jane’s case, but the appeals court reversed that ruling, saying NDAs made before 2018 should be voided as well. Because the ruling came in a lower court, it applies to all earlier NDAs in the state, but it doesn’t automatically void them. “Jane” and her husband sued Fellowship Bible Church in Nashville, Tennessee, alleging that their 3 year old son was molested by a 14 year old volunteer in 2015. (Stock photo) Hopefully it sets precedent, said Robert Leland, who has worked with the movement #NDAFree since 2022. Leland also co-founded #NDAFreeCampus in 2024. The Tennessee ruling could provide a template for other states enacting similar laws. The possibility that a new law could have enough teeth to invalidate real NDAs from the past is a significant step forward, Leland said. “It’s a big win,” Leland told RR. “The biggest message I would like to get across today is that NDAs hurt people.” A history of abuse allegations Jane wasn’t the first to make allegations against this particular volunteer at FBC. In August 2014, a couple left their 3-year-old son in the children’s ministry during the worship service, during which he was sexually assaulted, the couple’s attorneys’ stated in 2015. The parents filed suit against the church, seeking $37.5 million, according to The Tennessean. The parents’ attorneys said the church didn’t stop the teen from attending services and told the family not to press charges. They pursued charges anyway. The teen later pleaded guilty to aggravated sexual battery, as reported by The Christian Post. Fellowship Bible Church in Brentwood, Tennessee settled outside of court with “Jane” in 2016, requiring her to sign an NDA saying she would not speak about the abuse of her child. (Source: Fellowship Bible Church) The church published a statement on its website, denying the parents’ accusation it didn’t want police involved, according to the Christian Post article. Church staff immediately reported the abuse to authorities, the statement said. Church leaders complied with a police request to keep the matter confidential so as to let them continue the investigation, they said. They also said the teen was removed from his position. But the church waited an entire year to tell parents — including Jane — who had kids in that same children’s ministry, The Tennessean reported in 2016. In July 2015, the church scheduled a meeting with parents, describing the goal in an email to discuss “an incident that occurred between two juveniles,” The Tennessean reported. By December 2016, the couple’s lawsuit was resolved and sealed, The Tennessean reported. Jane was concerned that her own child had spent a year under this teen’s unsupervised care, as he was experiencing medical issues and developmental delays. After medical and psychological professionals said sexual abuse could be the cause, Jane sued FBC in 2015. FBC sent a statement to RR, saying the allegations from 2015 were “thoroughly investigated” by FBC, the Brentwood Police Department, and the Tennessee Department of Children’s Services. “Given there were no findings from those investigations, all parties, including both parents, agreed that it was in the best interest of their child for the matter to remain sealed and confidential,” FBC stated. Jane told The Tennessean, however, that she does not know whether her own son’s case was investigated. The church uses a computer tagging system to match kids in its children’s ministry with their parents, according to the church website. The church also told The Tennessean that it regularly updates child protection policies and prioritizes safety and security. Elizabeth Phillips of No More Victims is the founder of Trey’s Law. In this photo, she holds a photo of Trey Carlock who died by suicide in 2019 at age 28 after he was retraumatized by a civil litigation process against Kanakuk Ministries. (Source: Social Media) “These (policies) include mandatory background checks for staff and volunteers, a two-adult rule to ensure no adult is ever alone with a child, trained security personnel who monitor the facility whenever children’s programming is taking place, and many other safeguards,” FBC stated. The fact that FBC hired lawyers to fight Jane and her ability to speak about the abuse shows how far churches still need to go to be safe, Leland told RR. Churches, with their attorneys, often hold the upper hand with greater power over victims when victims sign NDAs, he added. “Being silenced about sexual abuse; that is incredibly destructive,” Leland said. Leland argues in a post published on Medium that NDAs are not only unbiblical but keep survivors from sharing their pain with their Bible study group or their family. They also keep survivors from warning others, he said. “It really is something that goes against everything the Bible teaches … like love your neighbor as yourself,” Leland told RR. “NDAs have a really nasty effect of isolating people.” ‘Trey’s law’ effort growing Since at least 2025, advocates have pushed states to enact “Trey’s Law,” to make NDAs related to sexual abuse cases unenforceable. The law has been introduced as different bills in several states, gaining approval in Texas, Missouri, Louisiana, Alabama and Georgia. A Trey’s Law bill was also recently introduced in U.S. Congress, unanimously passing in the Senate and now awaiting action in the House of Representatives. Two states were early movers: California, with a new law in 2016 and Tennessee, with its 2018 law. The recent effort is named after Trey Carlock, a Dallas native who suffered a decade of grooming and child sexual abuse by Pete Newman, a camp director for Kanakuk Ministries in the Ozarks. Carlock sued Kanakuk and settled out of court with a restrictive NDA. At age 28, he died by suicide after telling a therapist, “They will always control me, and I’ll never be free.” Rebecca Hopkins is a journalist based in Colorado. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Southern Baptists ban women pastors again. This time, they want to put the ban in the constitution.
item.author: Bob Smietana
item.dc:creator: Bob Smietana
item.description: A proposal by the nation’s largest Protestant denomination to ban churches that have women pastors or let women preach on Sunday mornings passed overwhelmingly on Wednesday (June 10), marking the first step toward enshrining the ban in the Southern Baptist Convention’s constitution. Almost three-quarters (74.6%) of local church delegates, known as messengers, voted in favor […]
item.content:encoded: President Clint Pressley, on video screen, calls a vote during the Southern Baptist Convention annual meeting, Wednesday, June 10, 2026, at the Orange County Convention Center in Orlando. (RNS photo/Bob Smietana) A proposal by the nation’s largest Protestant denomination to ban churches that have women pastors or let women preach on Sunday mornings passed overwhelmingly on Wednesday (June 10), marking the first step toward enshrining the ban in the Southern Baptist Convention’s constitution. Almost three-quarters (74.6%) of local church delegates, known as messengers, voted in favor of the so-called Truth and Unity Amendment during a Wednesday morning session of the SBC’s annual meeting. A quarter (25.09%) voted against the measure. Under the denomination’s rules, any change to its constitution must be approved by a two-thirds majority in two consecutive years. Two previous attempts to approve the ban had failed. Al Mohler, president of the Southern Baptist Theological Seminary in Louisville, Kentucky, has said the ban will keep the SBC from getting too liberal. “There’s a great line that divides liberal and biblical evangelicalism, and you can see it on this very issue,” said Mohler, who wrote and proposed the amendment. “The trajectory of liberal denominations is clear.” Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here.
