The Rev. Al Mohler addresses the Southern Baptist Convention annual meeting, Tuesday, June 9, 2026, in Orlando. (RNS photo/Marty Jean-Louis) Ahead of the SBC meeting, Baptist Women in Ministry, which provides support and resources for women in ministry, paid for a billboard to go up in Orlando — where the SBC gathering is being held — defending women who teach and preach the Bible. “Women in ministry deserve affirmation, respect, and the opportunity to follow God’s call,” the group said in a statement after the vote’s results were made public. “We are heartbroken that they have been denied those fundamental freedoms in the process of this vote.” The SBC’s statement of faith, known as the Baptist Faith and Message, has held since 2000 that only men can be pastors. However, until 2021, the SBC took no national action to expel churches with women pastors. Then, a couple of social media posts changed everything. First, Bible teacher Beth Moore, then a Southern Baptist, tweeted about speaking at a church on Mother’s Day in 2019, which set off an online firestorm over the issue of women preachers and pastors. Then, in 2021, Saddleback Church, one of the largest churches in the SBC, ordained several women on staff as pastors. “Yesterday was a historic night for Saddleback Church in many ways!” read a post on the California megachurch’s Facebook page at the time. Author and speaker Beth Moore speaks during a panel on sexual abuse in the Southern Baptist Convention at the Birmingham-Jefferson Convention Complex in Birmingham, Alabama, on June 10, 2019. (RNS photo by Adelle M. Banks) In a press conference, Mohler said that social media has made it possible for Southern Baptists to know what is going on in local churches—especially churches that allow women to preach or have women pastors. All of a sudden, he said, churches said, “Wait a minute.” That led to a move in the SBC to expel churches like Saddleback and to change the denomination’s constitution to make clear that similar churches were no longer welcome. “Saddleback has taken actions that place itself in direct conflict with the stated doctrines of the Southern Baptist Convention,” Mohler told Religion News Service in an interview at the time. In 2023, Saddleback was expelled from the SBC. It’s one of 10 churches deemed out of “friendly cooperation” – an SBC term for removing churches — over the issue of women pastors, according to the report from the SBC’s credential committee. Jonathan Sams, chair of the credentials committee, told SBC messengers that over the past seven years, 44 SBC churches were found to have women pastors on staff. Of those, 20 left on their own, while 10 were expelled. The committee gave no details about what happened to the other churches. Willy Rice preaches at the 2026 SBC Pastors’ Conference on June 8, 2026, in Orlando, Florida. (Photo by Luc Stringer/Baptist Press) Earlier this year, a Texas church changed the titles of some women on staff from pastor to minister. Some churches have claimed that the rule limiting the pastoral office to men only applied to a church’s senior leader and have given women staff the title of pastor. Other churches have also allowed women to preach during the Sunday services, something that proponents of the amendment say should not be allowed. Willy Rice, a Florida pastor who won the SBC’s presidential election on Tuesday, blamed what he called “sloppy ecclesiology” and “cultural pragmatism” for misuse of the title “pastor.” “We started handing out titles indiscriminately, and pastor, you’re a pastor, you’re a pastor, you’re a pastor,” he told a group of SBC pastors on Tuesday. “We lost the meaning of the word ‘pastor.’” A similar amendment to ban women pastors was first passed by Southern Baptists in 2023 but failed to get a two-thirds majority in 2024. Last year in Dallas, a proposed amendment on the same issue also failed. In Orlando, the messengers were eager to vote again. They lined up at microphones around the meeting hall, waiting to have their say about the amendment. Several pushed a button to alert outgoing SBC President Clint Pressley that they wanted to speak before the amendment was even up for debate. Attendees hold up their ballots while voting on a motion during the annual meeting of the Southern Baptist Convention, Tuesday, June 9, 2026, in Orlando, Fla. (AP Photo/Phelan M. Ebenhack) Pressley finally warned messengers to let the meeting get on with the business. “Then from there, we’ll open it up for a free-for-all,” Pressley told messengers. After a brief debate, one of the messengers called for the question, a parliamentary procedure to force a vote. The other messengers agreed and the vote went on. In other business, messengers approved a $186 million budget for 2026-2027, of which more than half (51%) will go to the SBC’s international mission board. That budget requires cuts from the SBC’s seminaries and other agencies, including $600,000 from the SBC’s Executive Committee. The most spirited debate came during a discussion of future sites for the SBC meeting, with a number of messengers objecting to planned future meetings in Anaheim, California, which is far from most SBC churches. Abel Galvan, a bivocational pastor from La Palma, California, defended the proposed meetings on the West Coast, which were eventually approved. “I just want to invite you to come back to California. I know it looks like a mess, but there are many of us who love Jesus there,” he told messengers. Bob Smietana is a national reporter for Religion News Service. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Texas pastor may only lead church remotely after allegedly violating bond conditions
item.author: Ann Marie Shambaugh
item.dc:creator: Ann Marie Shambaugh
item.description: A Fort Worth pastor has been limited to preaching to his congregation by video call after allegedly violating bond conditions tied to a previous sexual assault case.  On Aug. 31 last year, Journey House Fort Worth Church lead pastor Alonzo Diego Fuller, 40, was arrested and charged with sexual assault. He was released from the […]
item.content:encoded: Journey House Fort Worth Church lead pastor Alonzo Diego Fuller allegedly violating bond conditions tied to a previous sexual assault case and can now only preach at his church remotely, (Source: Facebook/Tarrant County inmate record website) A Fort Worth pastor has been limited to preaching to his congregation by video call after allegedly violating bond conditions tied to a previous sexual assault case. On Aug. 31 last year, Journey House Fort Worth Church lead pastor Alonzo Diego Fuller, 40, was arrested and charged with sexual assault. He was released from the Tarrant County jail the next day on a $40,000 surety bond, as previously reported by the Roys Report (RR). In March, Fuller was indicted on two counts, according to CBS News. The first count alleged Fuller used his position of spiritual authority to exploit and sexually touch a female without her consent. The second count alleged Fuller lured a person younger than 18 into a sexual act. Police arrested Fuller again on May 5 after he reportedly had contact with a minor, a violation of his bond conditions, according to KERA, an NPR affiliate in the Dallas-Fort Worth area. During a June 8 hearing, a Haltom City police officer testified that he saw Fuller hug and hold two young children in April, KERA reported. The off-duty officer had been working at the church to supervise Fuller, which was required as part of the bond conditions. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. (L-R) Asia and Diego Fuller. The couple planted Journey Church in Fort Worth in 2022. (Source: Journey House website) Another officer testified that in January she saw Fuller fist bump and greet families with children. “(Fuller) was aware of (his bond conditions), and he continued to violate those rules,” said Darren De La Cruz, an attorney for the alleged victim, according to KERA. “Although he is greeting family members, fist bumping them, interacting with them, that, simply put, is a violation of his conditions.” Several people testified at the hearing that they had not seen Fuller have contact with a minor since the restrictions were put in place, KERA reported. District Court Judge Ryan Hill reinstated Fuller’s bond and set it at $75,000. Additionally, the pastor is no longer permitted to lead worship services in person. A status conference is scheduled for July 9 in the 371st District Court, according to the Tarrant County Criminal Court docket. Fuller, who founded Journey Fort Worth with his wife in 2022, denied wrongdoing after his 2025 arrest. Church elders said in a September 2025 social media post that the accusations were without merit. “Pastor Diego fully supports and welcomes a thorough investigation by the criminal justice system and looks forward to the opportunity to clear his name,” the statement reads. “He remains committed to transparency and truth as the legal process moves forward.” Fuller has continued to lead the church throughout his case. He remained in custody as of June 10. RR contacted Journey Fort Worth and Bret Martin, Fuller’s attorney, for comment but did not receive a response before publication. Ann Marie Shambaugh has reported as a print journalist in multiple states, including currently in Carmel, Indiana. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Ex-AG pastor, 81, sentenced to 10 years to life for child sexual assault
item.author: Mark A. Kellner
item.dc:creator: Mark A. Kellner
item.description: Note: This story contains details about child sexual abuse. A former California Assemblies of God pastor was sentenced June 5 to 10 years-to-life in prison for sexually assaulting a child more than 65 years younger than him.  David Lee Helbling, 81, pleaded guilty in April to kidnapping to commit rape as well as to lewd […]
item.content:encoded: After being arrested on multiple felony charges in April, David Lee Helbling pleaded guilty and was sentenced to 10 years-to-life in prison. (Photo: Riverside County Sheriff’s Department) Note: This story contains details about child sexual abuse. A former California Assemblies of God pastor was sentenced June 5 to 10 years-to-life in prison for sexually assaulting a child more than 65 years younger than him. David Lee Helbling, 81, pleaded guilty in April to kidnapping to commit rape as well as to lewd acts on a child under 14, the Inland Valley Daily Bulletin reported. The pleas were entered under a pretrial agreement with the Riverside County District Attorney’s Office. In exchange for his admissions, prosecutors dropped four related charges, media reports indicated. Riverside County Superior Court Judge Arthur Bocanegra certified the terms of the plea deal during a hearing at the Riverside Hall of Justice. The judge imposed the sentence stipulated by the prosecution and the defense, City News Service reported. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. The Daily Bulletin identified the victim as a female and court documents identified her only as “E.A.,” according to City News Service. No other details about the victim were disclosed, including her relationship to Helbling. The Blessing Center First Assembly of God in Moreno Valley, California. (Photo: Google Maps) Sheriff’s Sgt. Robert Martinez said deputies were informed in late January 2024 of an attack on the victim in the 23000 block of Hemlock Avenue in Moreno Valley. Court papers indicated Helbling committed all the offenses on the same day: Jan. 30, 2024. Helbling kidnapped the girl, “and the movement of the victim substantially increased the risk of harm,” she incurred, the criminal complaint stated, according to City News Service. “The defendant resided in the same home, and had recurring access (to her) with the intent of arousing, appealing to and gratifying (his) lust, passions and sexual desires,” the complaint stated. Sheriff’s deputies were dispatched about 2:30 p.m. that same day to reports of a past sexual assault involving a child, CBS Los Angeles reported. While investigating, detectives learned the juvenile victim had been assaulted by a local pastor, identified as Helbling, then 79. Deputies arrested Helbling on Feb. 14, 2024, while serving a search warrant at the Moreno Valley property, according to CBS. “During the service of the search warrant, evidence related to the crime was collected, and Helbling was taken into custody without incident,” the sheriff’s department said in a statement, which is no longer posted online. Helbling served as a pastor at The Blessing Center First Assembly of God in Moreno Valley, RR reported in February 2024. Helbling initially faced charges including a forcible lewd act with a child under 14 and oral copulation or sexual penetration with a child under 10. Court records also listed counts of committing three or more lewd acts with a child under 14 and kidnapping a child under 14. RR’s initial article noted videos posted on the church’s Facebook page showing Helbling preaching or leading prayer in 2020 and 2022. Helbling had no documented prior felony convictions in Riverside County, City News Service reported. The sentencing came more than two years after his arrest. Mark A. Kellner is a reporter based in Mesquite, Nevada. He most recently covered statewide elections for the New York Post and was for three years the Faith & Family Reporter for The Washington Times. Mark is a graduate of the University of the Cumberlands and also attended Boston University’s College of Communication. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Ex-pastor pleads guilty to stealing $250,000 from his Tennessee congregation
item.author: Peter Johnson
item.dc:creator: Peter Johnson
item.description: A former Tennessee pastor who used funds to pay for family vacations and ‘female companionship’ has pleaded guilty to stealing $250,000, under an agreement reached in late May. Nick Rains, 48, once the pastor of Canvas Church, since renamed Vineyard Church of Lenoir City, pleaded guilty May 29. Under the plea agreement, he will receive […]
item.content:encoded: Former pastor Nick Rains has pleaded guilty to stealing $250,000 from Canvas Church, now Vineyard Church of Lenoir City in Tennessee, and will receive an eight-year sentence. (RR Graphic. Source: Facebook / Photo of Rains courtesy District Attorney Russell Johnson) A former Tennessee pastor who used funds to pay for family vacations and ‘female companionship’ has pleaded guilty to stealing $250,000, under an agreement reached in late May. Nick Rains, 48, once the pastor of Canvas Church, since renamed Vineyard Church of Lenoir City, pleaded guilty May 29. Under the plea agreement, he will receive an eight-year sentence, starting with 120 days in the Loudon County jail, according to District Attorney General Russell Johnson. Rains will spend the rest of his sentence on probation, according to a press release Johnson posted on Facebook. Rains will also pay the church $100,000, as well as court costs and fees. The investigation began when members of Vineyard Church became aware that Rains had misused a large amount of funds. He was removed from the pastorate and after an internal audit, church elders contacted the district attorney in 2023. Vineyard Church in Lenoir City Tennessee meets for worship in a local school hall. (Source: Facebook) An investigative audit by the Tennessee comptroller’s office found that Rains used a church credit card and church funds to pay for family vacations, a trip to Alaska and to make house payments. He also used church resources on what the district attorney’s press release called “expenditures for female companionship.” Though the press release did not report a total for Rains’ spending, it noted that he pleaded guilty to “Theft Over $250,000, a class B felony.” Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. The case “reached its conclusion with a plea deal,” Vineyard church said in a statement. “We are grateful for the work of those who pursued accountability on our behalf.” The church said it forgives Rains, adding, “We are committed to being a place of hope, integrity, and grace, and we believe the best days of this church are still ahead.” Rains’ story should serve as a “cautionary tale to similar churches,” the district attorney’s press release said. It concluded with a famous line from President Ronald Reagan: “Trust but verify.” Peter Johnson is a student at Boston University in Boston, Massachusetts. He is studying his Masters in Journalism. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Willy Rice, Florida pastor and abuse crisis skeptic, elected SBC president
item.author: Bob Smietana
item.dc:creator: Bob Smietana
item.description: A Florida pastor who has argued that the nation’s largest Protestant denomination has become too woke and liberal was elected president of the Southern Baptist Convention on Tuesday, June 9. Willy Rice, senior pastor of Calvary Church in Clearwater, Florida, received 5,217 votes — 57% of the votes cast. His opponent, Josh Powell, lead pastor […]
item.content:encoded: Pastor Willy Rice from Calvary Church in Clearwater, Florida has been elected as the new president of the Southern Baptist Convention (SBC). Photo courtesy of Calvary Church on Facebook. A Florida pastor who has argued that the nation’s largest Protestant denomination has become too woke and liberal was elected president of the Southern Baptist Convention on Tuesday, June 9. Willy Rice, senior pastor of Calvary Church in Clearwater, Florida, received 5,217 votes — 57% of the votes cast. His opponent, Josh Powell, lead pastor of Taylors First Baptist Church in South Carolina, received 3,821 votes, or 42%. Rice’s election is a triumph for critics who claim that the denomination has lost its way in recent years. He has alleged that the SBC’s sexual abuse crisis was more hoax than reality and said that the denomination’s leaders had followed the culture more than the Bible. The two candidates were similar. Both are conservative. Both are in favor of a ban on churches with women pastors. Both are fans of missionary work and are lifelong Southern Baptists. Both claimed that concerns that the SBC had a sexual abuse crisis were overblown. But they offered disparate views of the state of the convention during a lunchtime forum, held a few hours before the election, during the SBC’s gathering at Orange County Convention Center in Orlando this week. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Willy Rice preaches at the 2026 SBC Pastors’ Conference on June 8, 2026, in Orlando, Florida. (Photo by Luc Stringer/Baptist Press) “I’m afraid, you know, if we are not careful, we’re going to hug ourselves to death,” Rice told the audience at the forum, hosted by Baptist21, a group of younger pastors. “All we are going to do is talk about how great we are. We are going to wake up one day and be Kodak or Blockbuster.” Rice told attendees that Southern Baptist leaders were led astray by what he called a “cultural riptide” on issues of race, social justice and politics. That led to what he said were bad decisions that have undermined trust in the SBC’s leadership. He defended those who say the SBC has lost its way. “Look, if you look at the last 10 years and say, ‘I think it’s been great, we haven’t had any drift. … We weren’t caught in any kind of riptide,’ well, then I don’t know that I could say anything here today that would make you change your mind,” he said. “To me, it was obvious.” Rice also faced questions about some of his supporters. He has had close ties to the Center for Baptist Leadership, a group that claims that the SBC has become liberal. That group is led by former Trump administration staffer William Wolfe, known for his controversial social media posts. Texas pastor Juan Sanchez, one of the panel speakers, said that some of Rice’s supporters “say pretty intense stuff online” and asked what role those supporters might have if he became president. “We can play that game all day long,” Rice said, adding that people with all kinds of viewpoints say controversial things online. Rice said he wants to listen to everyone and that sometimes people who say harsh things online have a point. “Unity is not telling people to hush,” Rice said. “It’s listening, it’s sitting down and saying, ‘Tell me why you’re bothered.’” At the forum, Powell cited a Bible verse about avoiding those who stir up divisions. And he said that despite the SBC controversies, the convention is making a difference in the world. More missionaries are going out, and more churches are being planted, he said. “These are all things that we can absolutely celebrate, and if I am a cheerleader, I will cheer for that all day long,” Powell told attendees. Powell’s more optimistic view failed to sway the messengers, or church delegates to the conference who elect the president. Former SBC presidents pray for 2026 candidates Willy Rice and Josh Powell (seated) during a forum at Calvary Church in Clearwater, Florida on June 8. Rice was announced as the new SBC president the following day. (Source: Social Media) SBC Critics Since COVID-19, a group of critics, known in the past as the “SBC pirates,” has been concerned that the denomination has become too woke, mainly because they felt leaders had been too concerned about social justice and racism. Two previous presidential candidates aligned with that group — Florida pastor Tom Ascol and Georgia pastor Mike Stone — lost. Wolfe, the executive director of the Center for Baptist Leadership, said Rice’s election was a vindication for critics. “Willy Rice’s election proves that there is an appetite in the SBC for real conservative reform and renewal,” Wolfe told RNS in an interview. “We believe his victory is a major vindication of the issues we have addressed and fought for over the last few years in the SBC.” Wolfe, who endorsed Rice and posted a number of interviews with the Florida pastor online, hopes the president-elect will help the SBC get past recent controversies. “We pray that his leadership will successfully help Southern Baptists forge a brighter and better path out of a time of disagreement and decline, one built on the foundation of sound doctrine and increased accountability and empowering a courageous, conservative and biblical witness to the world,” he said. Rice’s election raises questions about the future of the SBC’s abuse reforms. The Florida pastor, who dropped out of the SBC presidential race in 2022 over concerns about past misconduct by a leader at his church, has said that any abuse should be reported to the police. But he also has said the SBC’s response to abuse went wrong. A 2021 report from Guidepost Solutions found that convention leaders had long sought to downplay the issue of sexual abuse and had stonewalled efforts to address the matter on a national basis. That led to a series of reforms, which have largely stalled. “Those who place institution and platform above the vulnerable rallied to be sure that no abuse reform would move forward,” said Tiffany Thigpen, an SBC abuse survivor and activist. “They call us hoaxes and liars, few respond to call it evil and denounce their callousness, yet they still become your top respected leaders.” In 2022, the SBC passed a resolution apologizing to Thigpen and other survivors for ignoring their concerns and for not taking action to prevent abuse. Jeff Iorg, CEO of the SBC Executive Committee, speaks at the Southern Baptist Convention annual meeting on June 9, 2026, in Orlando, Florida. (RNS photo/Bob Smietana) “That is a sign of these times where the church forgets what matters,” she said. SBC delegates also suspended one of the denomination’s standing rules, paving the way for a vote on a proposed constitutional amendment that would bar churches that have women pastors or that allow women to preach on Sunday mornings. Usually, a proposed amendment faces a one-year delay before a vote, but the messengers voted to suspend that requirement. Two similar amendments failed in the past. A vote on the proposed amendment is set for Wednesday morning. The amendment will need to receive a two-thirds majority in two consecutive years to be approved. After the election, Rice praised his opponent, saying the SBC needed “1000 pastors like him” in a social media post. And he said he hopes the SBC will enter “a season of reconciliation and renewal” after years of controversy. “It is my hope to listen and to serve and I ask for your prayers, your patience, and your continued cooperation as we continue in the good work God has given us to do,” Rice said in a post on X. Bob Smietana is a national reporter for Religion News Service. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Prominent Acts 29 church asks former members for help with investigation of founding pastor and ministry culture
item.author: Daniel Silliman
item.dc:creator: Daniel Silliman
item.description: Vintage Church, a successful multi-site congregation in North Carolina’s Research Triangle, is asking whether its culture and organization led to the scandal that ended the tenure of its long-time senior pastor. At a meeting last week, church leaders ask current and former members of its campus in downtown Raleigh to participate in a survey as […]
item.content:encoded: Staff at Vintage Church in North Carolina have announced an independent investigation after founding pastor Tyler Jones (inset) was fired for infidelity in February 2026. (RR Graphic) Vintage Church, a successful multi-site congregation in North Carolina’s Research Triangle, is asking whether its culture and organization led to the scandal that ended the tenure of its long-time senior pastor. At a meeting last week, church leaders ask current and former members of its campus in downtown Raleigh to participate in a survey as part of an independent investigation. They described the survey as critical to the congregation’s path forward. The church fired senior pastor Tyler Jones in February. Jones, who co-founded Vintage in 2002 and spent years training church planters for the global Acts 29 network, admitted to improper behavior. According to the church, he did not confess, but acknowledged, when pushed, an “extramarital relationship” lasting several years. The church hired Guidepost Solutions to do an outside investigation into what happened and look at potential cultural and structural problems. “The biggest piece of this whole thing is the survey,” Darrell Martin, director of people and organizational development, said in the June 3 meeting. “Please take 10, 15 minutes as you feel led to respond. … Be honest.” Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Acts 29 Vice President Tyler Jones was removed from his role after confessing to ‘long-term extramarital relationship’. (TRR Graphic) One of the 17 questions on the survey asks about prior “knowledge or concerns” about about Jones’ “leadership, conduct, or relationships.” Several other questions ask about how women have been treated at the church. Vintage, along with other Acts 29 congregations, teaches that men and women have different God-given roles and women are barred from some leadership positions. “Are female staff, female members, and female volunteers supported, valued, heard, and safe at Vintage?” one question says. Another says, “During your time at Vintage, have you observed or experienced any problematic opinions, attitudes, barriers or issues related to gender?” Jones is not the first minister caught in misconduct at Vintage. In 2012, executive pastor Nate Williams resigned and confessed to immoral sexual behavior and mistreating female employees. Vintage was, at that time, occasionally called “Mars Hills East.” The North Carolina church was closely connected with the Seattle congregation that was then widely praised for its vibrant — and virile — new evangelical style. The Seattle church imploded in scandal a few years later. Evangelicals across the country have reckoned with the fallout of the failure of Mars Hill. Many have looked at the way the theology seemed to authorize men to behave badly and often gave leaders, especially megachurch pastors, unchecked authority. It is unclear what reckoning happened at Vintage and how much of the Mars Hill model Jones kept in place. Changes being considered Last week, leaders at Vintage Church in downtown Raleigh held a meeting and asked current and former members to fill out the survey as part of an independent investigation into church culture. (Screenshot) Interim pastor Eric Spivey said on June 3 that Vintage leaders are now prepared to look at potential cultural and structural changes, which could include theology. “We want to be humble enough to say, ‘Okay, let’s take a look at that,’” Spivey told members. “Even if that flies in the face of, like, how things have always operated.” At the same time, Spivey assured the congregation that any changes to Vintage’s agreed-upon statement of beliefs would follow an agreed-upon process. Individuals in the congregation will have plenty of time to give input. “It’s in our bylaws, how our church would go about a change (in doctrine),” Spivey said. “If we were to road map that, if we were to change anything, it would be a slow, deliberate process. There’s nothing that we can ramrod (through).” The church is already planning some changes to the leadership structure. Elder Tom Johnson told the congregation that when Jones was “extricated” from leadership, some problems immediately became clear. Jones structured church decision-making so everything ran through him, Johnson said. He was the only one communicating between the elders, the church board and the leadership team. “Hindsight, it’s like, man, we should have split that up,” Kayla Pleasants, the downtown campus director, said in the meeting. “But that’s one (thing) that’s actively changing and for the better. We’re all in the same room together, figuring it out and fumbling through it …. because we weren’t operating the way that we should have been.” Interim pastor Shaun Cross told church members that leaders know already that they have had some significant shortcomings, including encouraging staff to work at a frenetic pace. (Source: Vintage Church website) Didn’t listen to women on staff Another interim pastor, Shaun Cross, said he expects other cultural changes based on the report that Guidepost will submit after its investigation. He said leaders know already that they have had some significant shortcomings. Vintage has put too much emphasis on activity, he said, and often encouraged staff to work at a frenetic pace. Cross also believes the leadership has sidelined some members of the church who had important things to say. “We failed to hear and heed the voices of women on our staff,” he said. “We’re willing to do what it takes to be healthy and safe and to demonstrate the gospel to our community to you and to the city.” The church has been criticized for its views on women. In a February 2014 profile in the Durham-based publication Indy, the church was described as “hip fundamentalism” and accused of shaming women who are not wives or mothers. In addition to the survey, church leaders have invited people to fill out a form on the website and set up a meeting with them directly. Cross said church leaders say they know they have a responsibility to the people who left. “There are a lot of different sets of hurting people,” he said. “Some in this room. Some represented by this room. Some who used to be in this room but can’t be anymore. We have a debt and a duty of love — to them and to y’all. I just want to make that clear.” Not saying ‘sexual abuse’ Church leaders still seem uncertain, however, how to characterize Jones’ misconduct. When the news of his firing first broke, they spoke of an “extramarital relationship with a woman.” This implied the relationship was inappropriate because Jones was not married to the unidentified woman, but it said nothing about sex, consent, power or abuse. Then the language grew more cryptic. One church leader spoke of “the situation.” A church-wide email mentioned “recent events.” In last week’s meeting with members, interim pastor Eric Spivey talked of “what we’ve been through” while elder Tom Johnson referred to “what has happened, moral failure-wise.” Former church members and staff, piecing details together, told RR they believe the victim worked for Vintage and was a part of the congregation. She considered Jones her boss and pastor, they said. Eric Spivey, the interim location pastor Vintage’s downtown Raleigh Campus, said Vintage Church is prepared to consider cultural, structural and theological changes. (Screenshot) Experts do not call secret sexual relationships between bosses and employees or pastors and congregants “situations.” They call them sexual abuse or sexual misconduct. North Carolina law prohibits sexual relationships in the workplace if “consent” is an explicit or implicit condition of employment. To comply with the law, most workplaces do not allow any type of romantic relationship between employees and supervisors. Sexual contact between a boss and employee can also be criminal. In North Carolina, unwanted touching is defined as sexual battery, even if everyone involved is fully clothed. It is punishable by up to 150 days in jail. One-third of the 50 states similarly criminalize clergy sexual abuse because spiritual authority can be used to manipulate and coerce consent. In Tennessee, for example, the law says that “a victim is incapable of consent” if the perpetrator is acting in his or her capacity as a minister. An “inappropriate relationship” is considered sexual battery. Vintage leaders seem to believe that Jones’ actions merited immediate dismissal. They have said he will not return to ministry at Vintage. But they are also avoiding the phrase “clergy sexual abuse.” Asking people to fill out the Guidepost survey, church director of people and organizational development Darrell Martin used the circumlocution “the things they are investigating.” He also noted the church needed “somebody come alongside who is objective,” give leaders more perspective and help Vintage move forward. Guidepost will “make their recommendations and then we start putting stuff into place,” Martin said. “We are excited about what they’re doing. We’re excited about the future. We are excited about … moving forward.” Guidepost’s survey is open until June 21. Investigators plan to submit their final report in the fall. Before then, at the start of the school year, the church is planning a sermon series on repentance. Daniel Silliman is senior reporter/editor at The Roys Report. He began his two decades in journalism covering crime in Atlanta and has since led major investigations into abuse and misconduct in Christian contexts. Daniel and his wife live in Johnson City, Tennessee. SHARE THIS:

item.title: December sentencing set for former Maryland youth pastor who pleaded guilty to child sexual abuse
item.author: Mark A. Kellner
item.dc:creator: Mark A. Kellner
item.description: Note: This story contains details about child sexual abuse. A former Baltimore County youth pastor who, authorities say, sexually abused teenage boys at his church and home pleaded guilty Monday to one count of sexual abuse of a minor. Thomas George Pinkerton Jr., 52, entered the plea in Baltimore County Circuit Court under a deal […]
item.content:encoded: Thomas George Pinkerton Jr., also known as “Pastor Tommy,” faces charges alleging that he abused six male teenagers at Central Christian Church in Rossville, Maryland, between 2006 and 2010. (RR Graphic) Note: This story contains details about child sexual abuse. A former Baltimore County youth pastor who, authorities say, sexually abused teenage boys at his church and home pleaded guilty Monday to one count of sexual abuse of a minor. Thomas George Pinkerton Jr., 52, entered the plea in Baltimore County Circuit Court under a deal capping a possible prison sentence at four years, according to The Baltimore Banner. Sentencing is scheduled for Dec. 14. Under the agreement, Pinkerton will serve five years of probation and must register as a sex offender. Pinkerton, also known as “Pastor Tommy,” served as youth pastor at Central Christian Church, an Assemblies of God congregation in Rossville, Maryland. An arrest warrant seen by NBC News and other media outlets allege that he abused six male teenagers at the church and at his Perry Hall, Maryland, home between 2006 and 2010. Victims ranged in age from 13 to 19. Roys Report (RR) was unable to obtain an online version of the warrant. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Although Pinkerton pleaded guilty to a single count, prosecutors plan to present accounts from at least all six Maryland victims at sentencing. A seventh man separately reported abuse in Georgia, media reports indicated. That allegation was referred to Georgia authorities. Pinkerton is facing 24 charges involving the sexual assault of six victims during his tenure as youth pastor at Central Christian Church in Baltimore, Maryland from 2006-2010. (Source: Central Christian Church) Pinkerton denies allegations Pinkerton’s attorney, Justin Hollimon, told RR in a telephone interview his client still denies the allegations but made a strategic decision to accept the deal rather than risk a trial. “Mr. Pinkerton still maintains his innocence, but he understood that if he took a chance at trial, any significant sentence would result in almost a life sentence for him,” Hollimon said. “He wanted to control the outcome, as opposed to having a jury control the outcome,” Hollimon added. Hollimon noted the stakes for his client, who turns 53 in July and faces multiple felony counts. “There are a lot of innocent people sitting in jail today, because they decided to go to trial and roll the dice, and it didn’t go their way,” he said. Asked about an appeal, Hollimon suggested it would not happen. “In Maryland, when you take a plea, it’s very hard to appeal it,” he said. “There’s very limited grounds” for any further court action. The attorney offered a further assessment. “The victims have their story, Mr. Pinkerton has his story, and at the end of the day, there are no winners, and that’s the tragic part about a case like this,” Hollimon said. The gap between the plea and sentencing, roughly seven months, is standard for sex offense cases in Maryland. Hollimon said the court requires a psychosexual evaluation of individuals convicted of such offenses before a sentence can be imposed. The investigation began in October 2024, when a West Virginia man called Baltimore County police to report that Pinkerton had abused him as a minor. That victim provided detectives with the names of others. Six additional men came forward with similar accounts. Police obtained an arrest warrant in late June 2025. Forsyth County, Georgia, deputies arrested Pinkerton at a traffic stop on July 16, 2025. He was extradited to Maryland on Aug. 7, 2025, and held without bond. A pattern of grooming Charging documents described a pattern of grooming. Pinkerton allegedly cultivated a circle of male “favorites” in his youth group, targeting athletic, popular teenage boys. He referred to himself as a “spiritual father.” He reportedly justified kissing boys on the lips as a “heavenly kiss,” likening it to how Jesus greeted his disciples. Victims also described massages, hand-holding, and genital touching. Some said Pinkerton shared a bed with male youth during out-of-state mission trips. He was originally charged with 24 counts: 12 felonies and 12 misdemeanors. These included child abuse by a custodian, sexual abuse of a minor and third- and fourth-degree sex offenses. Central Christian Church in Baltimore County, Maryland. (Photo courtesy of Google Maps) Central Christian said in a 2025 statement that Pinkerton left the congregation more than 15 years earlier to start his own ministry. Lead Pastor Larry Kirk called the allegations “deeply heartbreaking” and said, “There is absolutely no place for abuse — ever — in the church.” Central Christian did not respond to an RR request for comment on the guilty plea. After leaving the church, Pinkerton worked as a traveling evangelist and preached in at least six states, media reports said. His ministry website listed mission work in Ukraine, Mexico, Peru and the Philippines, according to media accounts. At the time of his arrest, he was living in Winder, Georgia, with his wife and three children. In 2025, the Potomac District of the Assemblies of God told NBC News that Pinkerton was never formally credentialed as a minister with the denomination, despite working at an affiliated church. District Superintendent Mark Lehmann did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Mark A. Kellner is a reporter based in Mesquite, Nevada. He most recently covered statewide elections for the New York Post and was for three years the Faith & Family Reporter for The Washington Times. Mark is a graduate of the University of the Cumberlands and also attended Boston University’s College of Communication. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Right-wing voices in PCA rally to support pastor suspended for online comments
item.author: Peter Johnson
item.dc:creator: Peter Johnson
item.description: A pastor was recently suspended for comments he posted on X three years ago, and the action by denominational officials has riled up right-wing voices in the Presbyterian Church in America (PCA). “What happened to my friend Zachary Garris was a travesty of justice,” pastor Sean McGowan wrote on X May 29. He characterized his […]
item.content:encoded: Presbyterian pastor and author Zachary Garris has been suspended by the Presbyterian Church of America for unwholesome speech. (RR Graphic) A pastor was recently suspended for comments he posted on X three years ago, and the action by denominational officials has riled up right-wing voices in the Presbyterian Church in America (PCA). “What happened to my friend Zachary Garris was a travesty of justice,” pastor Sean McGowan wrote on X May 29. He characterized his friend’s comments as “‘mildly harsh tweets’ at best.” That same day, Joseph Spurgeon, a Christian nationalist pastor and host of the Patriarchy Podcast, decried the suspension. “I believe in the Presbyterian form of church government, but this makes no one want to be Presbyterian,” he posted. “It seems like such a gross miscarriage of justice that I can’t even fathom it’s real.” The Rio Grande Presbytery (RGP), however, thought a suspension was warranted. On March 28, it found Zachary Garris, pastor of Bryce Avenue Presbyterian Church in White Rock, New Mexico, guilty of “unwholesome speech” and suspended him indefinitely, according to McGowan. Roys Report (RR) reached out to the RGP for comment about its decision but did not receive a reply. The RGP deemed Garris’ comments in a 2023 exchange with Anthony Bradley “unwholesome speech,” prompting an indefinite suspension. Bradley is an African American author, scholar and professor of interdisciplinary and theological studies at Kuyper College in Grand Rapids, Michigan. Garris, who holds a master’s in divinity degree from Reformed Theological Seminary, is expected to appeal the ruling to the PCA’s Standing Judicial Commission. Zachary Garris has been suspended indefinitely by the Rio Grande Presbytery for his online communication with theologian and religion professor Anthony Bradley on X in 2023. (Source: X) Report finds ‘a quarrelsome spirit’ The initial complaint against Garris followed his online dispute with Lamont English, a director with the PCA’s missionary arm Mission to the World. It dealt with the Bible’s treatment of chattel slavery. According to a Substack post by podcaster Jon Harris, a defender of the Confederacy, numerous PCA ministers found fault with the exchange and complained to Garris’ presbytery. This led to an investigation by the RGP Judicial Committee, which produced a report highly critical of Garris. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. The report noted that Garris argued on X that chattel slavery was not “absolutely sinful,” which he claimed was the position of “the majority of American Presbyterians.” When pressed about what he meant, Garris responded, “I’m saying the Bible does not condemn chattel slavery per se, and therefore neither can we.” Garris later apologized publicly for being “insufficiently clear” in his posts and for “not being more sensitive.” While the RGP committee commended Garris for his apologies, it said that Garris’ posts betrayed a “conviction” that “chattel slavery is biblically defensible.” Controversial pastor Zachary Garris leads at Bryce Avenue Presbyterian Church in White Rock, New Mexico. (Source: Bryce Avenue official website) The report also pointed to Garris’ 2023 X interactions with Bradley. Though Bradley has since deleted his posts in the exchange, the report contains them. In an exchange with Bradley, Garris defended slavery, noting a passage in Leviticus that permitted Israel to buy slaves from other nations. Bradley pushed back, calling Garris “biblically and historically illiterate” and “pathetic.” Garris replied that “manstealing is wrong” but mocked Bradley for condemning all slave buying. “Sometimes things are more complex than a Ph.D. can handle,” he wrote. Bradley earned a doctorate from Westminster Theological Seminary. The RGP report concluded that Garris’ posts “present a pattern that betrays an entrenched misunderstanding of Scripture, a quarrelsome spirit, and a posture of mockery and dishonor towards other Christians with whom he differs.” It added that this pattern “indicates a conviction at odds with our standards.” Supporters react on X According to McGowan, the RGP exonerated Garris of charges connected to his exchange with English on slavery. But it found his treatment of Bradley worthy of indefinite suspension. In a previous incident on social media, Garris posted a lengthy apology to X on June 7, 2024, after he defended slavery. He was later exonerated by the RGP. (Source: X) McGowan posted a screenshot excerpt from the report, showing select comments from the exchange on X. This prompted many X users to respond, asking with confusion and anger why Garris was suspended indefinitely. Some commentators suggested that Garris is being persecuted. David D. Edgington, pastor and author of “White Knights and Reviling Wives: How Feminism Destroys Families, wrote, “This is not a PCA problem. This is a church-wide problem with White Knights just acting according to their cowardly, anti-biblical natures.” He called the suspension part of efforts by “effeminate church ladies of both genders to destroy good men every chance they get.” Harris writing in a Substack post, said that Bradley received more favorable treatment. “Many observers pointed out that Bradley’s own online behavior has often been harsher than Garris’s while remaining unpunished within the PCA. This has led many to conclude that the Rio Grande Presbytery weaponized judicial proceedings against Pastor Garris because they disliked him personally and ideologically, not because they were faithfully applying their governing documents.” Garris has not directly addressed the suspension. In a June 2 post on X, he wrote, “The Ninth Commandment requires ‘the preserving and promoting of … the good name of our neighbor … a charitable esteem of our neighbors; loving, desiring, and rejoicing in their good name’ (Westminster Larger Catechism 144). Sadly, some Christians disregard this for brothers they consider to be ‘enemies.’” Garris reposted McGowan’s tweet. Bradley has not said anything publicly about the X posts or Garris’ suspension. Peter Johnson is a student at Boston University in Boston, Massachusetts. He is studying his Masters in Journalism. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Rhema Bible Church fires chief financial officer for unspecified misconduct
item.author: Ann Marie Shambaugh
item.dc:creator: Ann Marie Shambaugh
item.description: Rhema Bible Church of Broken Arrow, Oklahoma, has cut ties with Craig Hagin, its chief financial officer and one of its former pastors, after receiving reports of alleged unspecified misconduct.  Hagin is the grandson of Kenneth E. Hagin, the leader of the Word of Faith movement, who died in 2003. His father is Kenneth W. […]
item.content:encoded: Rhema Bible Church in Tulsa, Oklahoma released a statement about the removal of former pastor Craig Hagin on June 7 through social media. (RR graphic) Rhema Bible Church of Broken Arrow, Oklahoma, has cut ties with Craig Hagin, its chief financial officer and one of its former pastors, after receiving reports of alleged unspecified misconduct. Hagin is the grandson of Kenneth E. Hagin, the leader of the Word of Faith movement, who died in 2003. His father is Kenneth W. Hagin, Rhema’s founding pastor. In March, Craig Hagin and his wife, Mia Hagin, were charged in a Tulsa County court with animal cruelty. Police had discovered more than two dozen cats and dogs living in unsanitary conditions in a home they owned. His firing, however, is unrelated to that charge, according to a June 3 statement by Kenneth W. Hagin posted on the church website. “Within the past week, our leadership team became aware of separate and credible information regarding conduct inconsistent with the standards expected of those serving in ministry leadership,” the statement reads. The elder Hagin also stated that his son will no longer serve “in any ministerial, leadership, or administrative capacity and has no role in the governance, direction, operations, or decision-making of this ministry.” The church also removed his access to organizational systems, accounts and ministry platforms. Craig W. Hagin preaches at Rhema Bible Church in Tulsa, Oklahoma on April 14, 2024. (Screen grab) Rhema Bible Church placed the younger Hagin on sabbatical after his arrest for animal cruelty. In the statement, his father declined to comment on the criminal case. “That matter remains before the courts, and we believe it is important to allow the legal process to proceed without speculation,” the statement reads. Craig Hagin’s preliminary hearing in the case is July 7. Mia Hagin pleaded no contest in April, and her charges were reduced to two misdemeanor counts of disturbing the peace, according to KOTV-TV Ch. 6, a CBS affiliate in Tulsa. Craig and Mia Hagin made news we they announced they were divorcing after 20 years of marriage in May 2026. (Photo courtesy of social media) Mia Hagin announced May 21 in a social media post, no longer public, that she and her husband were divorcing. She published the post, which accused her husband of betrayal but did not provide details, on the couple’s 20th wedding anniversary. “What was once one of the happiest days of my life is now the saddest,” the post read. “Divorce feels like failure and feels like a death, and I have to grieve their loss. You can’t spend 20 years living with someone and not miss them once they are gone. It’s a painful process. Please Pray for both Craig and I.” Hagin, the father of three sons, had been married before. Mia Hagin, also a divorcee, had four children. Their blended family consisted of seven children when they married, he recounted on the Timmy Gibson Show in 2022. In his statement, the elder Hagin asked those with ties to the Rhema community to pray for his son and others harmed by the unspecified misconduct. “This is a deeply painful matter for our family, our church, and our Rhema worldwide family,” the statement reads. “Craig remains a member of our family, and we continue to pray for repentance, healing, and restoration in his personal life. At the same time, we have a responsibility to act clearly and decisively to protect the integrity of this ministry, the trust placed in us, and the people we serve.” Ann Marie Shambaugh has reported as a print journalist in multiple states, including currently in Carmel, Indiana. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Former Colorado youth pastor convicted of child sexual assault, faces 8 years to life
item.author: Mark A. Kellner
item.dc:creator: Mark A. Kellner
item.description: A former Colorado youth pastor who was convicted May 29 of sexually assaulting a child faces a sentence that could run from eight years to life, authorities said. Joshua Lucero, a former young adult pastor at Denver-area Word Alive Church, was convicted of a single count of sexual assault on a child by a person […]
item.content:encoded: Joshua Lucero, a former pastor at Thornton Word Alive Church in Denver, was convicted of a single count of sexual assault on a child on May 29. (RR Graphic, Inset photo courtesy of Thornton Police Department) A former Colorado youth pastor who was convicted May 29 of sexually assaulting a child faces a sentence that could run from eight years to life, authorities said. Joshua Lucero, a former young adult pastor at Denver-area Word Alive Church, was convicted of a single count of sexual assault on a child by a person in a position of trust. The trial started on May 18, and the jury returned its verdict on May 29 after two days of deliberations. Details of the trial and arguments presented were not immediately available. The trial was not livestreamed. Sentencing is scheduled for Sept. 11, though Lucero’s attorney, Chloe Gleichman of the Colorado Legal Defense Group, has requested a new date, which the court has not yet set. She told The Denver Post the outcome was disappointing. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. “While Mr. Lucero accepts the jury’s verdict, he intends to pursue all avenues on appeal,” the newspaper quoted her as saying. District Attorney Brian Mason will make a specific sentencing request at that hearing, a spokesman said in an email to Roys Report (RR). Word Alive is in Thornton, a suburb north of Denver. It describes itself as “Jesus-centered. Holy Spirit-empowered” and “a family shaped by God’s presence.” The church did not respond to a request for comment on the conviction. Thornton police arrested Lucero, then 28, on March 25 of last year, as reported shortly thereafter by RR. Lucero — who goes by “Josh” on social media — is one of two adult children of the church’s senior pastors, Randy and Veronica Lucero. Police said the assaults occurred between July 2017 and July 2020 while Lucero oversaw juveniles ages 13 to 18. Jan Martin, who said she was a church elder, wrote on Facebook at the time that she “was asked to protect the pastor’s son who admitted to sexually assaulting a minor.” She added, “As a Christian, I could not condone these actions. I resigned from my position and filed a police report with the Thornton Police Department in 2021.” Kylynn McTague, a police spokesperson, said the victim initially declined to proceed in 2021 but came forward in 2025. Mark A. Kellner is a reporter based in Mesquite, Nevada. He most recently covered statewide elections for the New York Post and was for three years the Faith & Family Reporter for The Washington Times. Mark is a graduate of the University of the Cumberlands and also attended Boston University’s College of Communication. SHARE THIS:

item.title: SBC tries to move on from abuse crisis, will debate women pastors and immigration
item.author: Bob Smietana
item.dc:creator: Bob Smietana
item.description: More than 11,000 Southern Baptist church representatives will gather in Orlando this week, where they will debate women pastors and send out missionaries. Over the past decade, members of the Southern Baptist Convention have touted the denomination’s diversity, sought to keep sexual abusers away from churches and passed statements advocating for immigration reform and a […]
item.content:encoded: People line up for the Southern Baptist Convention at Orange County Convention Center on Monday, June 8, 2026, in Orlando, Florida. (RNS photo/Bob Smietana) More than 11,000 Southern Baptist church representatives will gather in Orlando this week, where they will debate women pastors and send out missionaries. Over the past decade, members of the Southern Baptist Convention have touted the denomination’s diversity, sought to keep sexual abusers away from churches and passed statements advocating for immigration reform and a path to legal status for those in the country without authorization. Now, for a vocal group of Southern Baptists, diversity is seen as too woke for the Bible. The two candidates running for SBC president say the SBC does not have an abuse crisis, with one claiming the sexual abuse crisis was a “snipe hunt” that led Baptists astray. And the more than 11,000 local church delegates, known as messengers, gathering at the Orange County Convention Center in Orlando, Florida, this week will debate a resolution that claims compassion should not get in the way of deportation. They will also consider a call to ban churches with women pastors and churches that allow women to preach in Sunday services. Al Mohler, president of Southern Baptist Theological Seminary in Louisville, Kentucky, plans to introduce what he calls a “Truth and Unity Amendment” during the SBC annual meeting, set for June 9-10. That amendment to the SBC Constitution would bar churches that affirm, appoint or endorse women pastors — and specifically mentions women who preach. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. The SBC’s statement of faith already says that only men can be pastors. But some churches believe that only applies to the senior pastor of a church and give women on staff titles like associate pastor or children’s pastor. And some allow women to preach. The Rev. Al Mohler addresses the Southern Baptist Convention annual meeting at the Kay Bailey Hutchison Convention Center in Dallas, June 11, 2025. (RNS Photo/Tim Heitman) Two previous attempts to bar those churches have failed, falling short of the two-thirds majority needed for approval. Leaders of the SBC also appear to want to shift the narrative — away from internal disputes, news of declining members and headlines about sexual abuse and toward a focus on missions instead. “I’ve said it before, I keep saying it over and over again — Southern Baptists are a force for good,” Jeff Iorg, CEO of the SBC Executive Committee, told trustees in a meeting Monday (June 8). “We have much to celebrate, and that will be our focus these next two days.” Rev. Willy Rice, pastor of Calvary Church in Clearwater, Florida, told RNS in an interview that he believes a decade of controversies and bad decisions has hurt the SBC. Local churches, he said, no longer trust the denomination’s leaders. “The seams of our fellowship seem to have been stretched and torn over the last decade,” Rice, one of two pastors running for SBC president, said. “I’m concerned about our trajectory if something doesn’t change.” Rice has said that concerns about an abuse crisis in the SBC were overblown and that the convention’s response to concerns about abuse was overblown, too. SBC worship and group attendance climbs as membership declines” (Graphic courtesy of Lifeway Research) A 2021 report from Guidepost Solutions, which was commissioned by the SBC messengers, found that convention leaders had long sought to downplay the issue of sexual abuse and had stonewalled efforts to address the issue on a national basis. That led to a series of reforms, including more training for churches on abuse prevention and a database of abusive pastors. While progress has been made on church training on how to prevent and respond to abuse, plans for that database have been largely abandoned. Rice, who dropped out of the SBC presidential race in 2022 over concerns about past misconduct by a leader at his church, has said that any abuse should be reported to the police. But he also has said the SBC’s response to abuse went badly wrong. “The Southern Baptist Convention got the Brett Kavanaugh treatment — and probably for the same reasons,” he wrote in a 2024 essay for the Center for Baptist Leadership, a group that claims the SBC has become too liberal. Rice has also said the SBC needs more financial transparency. If elected, he also wants the SBC to get back to a focus on missions and evangelism. A fourth-generation Southern Baptist, he said he loves the SBC despite its issues. “I love Baptist people, and I care about the Southern Baptist Convention,” he told RNS. “There is so much that I celebrate.” Rev. Josh Powell, pastor of Taylors First Baptist Church (CQ) in South Carolina, agrees. The Southern Baptist Convention annual meeting at the Indiana Convention Center in Indianapolis, June 11, 2024. (RNS Photo/AJ Mast) Powell, who is also a candidate for SBC president, said that for the most part, the denomination is doing well. The number of missionaries has grown, he said, and the SBC continues to start new churches. Baptisms and church attendance have also started to rebound post-COVID. Still, he said, convincing more than 40,000 churches to get along is not easy. “Cooperation is hard,” he said. “It’s always been hard.” He worries that, like the country in general, the SBC has become too polarized. Focusing on missions, he said, can unite the denomination. During their meeting in Orlando, the SBC’s International Mission Board will recognize 63 new missionaries being sent out. The convention will also vote on a plan that would boost the IMB’s budget by more than $2 million a year. If approved, 51% of the denomination’s total budget would go to missions. During the meeting, Southern Baptists will debate a number of proposed resolutions, including one on immigration. A resolution entitled “On Immigration, Human Dignity, and the Rule of Law” expresses support for “lawful immigration enforcement” and says that “Christian compassion and hospitality do not negate lawful order or excuse indifference to public justice and social peace.” The immigration resolution also rejects nativism and “ethnic supremacy,” as well as amnesty for those in the country illegally. Unlike past SBC resolutions, it makes no mention of a path to legal status for those in the country without approval. Last fall, the SBC’s public policy arm dropped out of an evangelical immigration advocacy group. With 12.3 million members, the SBC remains the largest Protestant denomination in the country. At its peak in 2006, the SBC claimed 16.3 million members. A report released earlier this year found that attendance and baptisms in the SBC have continued to rebound from declines during COVID. In 2025, about 4.5 million worshippers attended SBC churches, while 263,075 people were baptized. By contrast, in 2015, more than 5.6 million people attended SBC churches, a loss of more than a million attenders. Bob Smietana is a national reporter for Religion News Service. SHARE THIS:

item.title: ‘Kanye’s pastor’ Adam Tyson accused of distributing revenge porn
item.author: Daniel Silliman
item.dc:creator: Daniel Silliman
item.description: The California minister called “Kanye’s pastor” is facing a lawsuit accusing him of showing revenge porn to a woman’s father.   Alison Wilder, 36, says she went to Placerita Bible Church, north of Los Angeles, for marriage counseling. According to the lawsuit she filed in Los Angeles County Superior Court on May 18, pastor Adam […]
item.content:encoded: New lawsuit accuses ‘Kanye’s pastor’ Adam Tyson of involvement in alleged revenge porn scandal. The California minister called “Kanye’s pastor” is facing a lawsuit accusing him of showing revenge porn to a woman’s father. Alison Wilder, 36, says she went to Placerita Bible Church, north of Los Angeles, for marriage counseling. According to the lawsuit she filed in Los Angeles County Superior Court on May 18, pastor Adam Tyson dismissed concerns about her husband’s addiction, anger, violence and manipulation. Instead, she says, Tyson and and elders Patrick Hamblin and Mark Madrid, who are both in law enforcement, believed her husband’s allegations that she was having an affair. And when Andrew Wilder, 51, sent them nude photos of her as evidence, the lawsuit claims, they showed the images to her father, who was an elder at the church. Alison Wilder told the Roys Report (RR) that her family has been part of this church community, which is closely associated with the ministry of the late John MacArthur, for more than 20 years. She believed the pastors and elders might help her as she and her husband went through a divorce. She said she was “stunned” and “grieved” by the way she was treated. Alison Wilder says she went to Placerita Bible Church in Los Angeles for marriage counseling after experiencing abuse at the hands of her husband, Andrew Wilder. (Source: Provided) “I never imagined such a profound betrayal,” Alison Wilder told RR. “These were men, some even in law enforcement, who I expected to protect me and my children.” Andrew Wilder, a TV writer whose credits include “Criminal Minds” and “FBI,” has two restraining orders against him, court records show. The state of California is also prosecuting him under the first-in-the-nation law banning revenge porn. He has been charged with criminal distribution of a private image to cause emotional distress. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. He did not return text messages from RR. Tyson, who is also an adjunct professor of biblical counseling at The Master’s University, said the church could not comment on pending litigation. “What we can say is this: Domestic violence is a grave harm and a serious sin,” Tyson told RR in an email. “Our pastoral counsel — without exception — is that anyone in such a dangerous situation should seek safety. We tell people to leave immediately, contact law enforcement, and make use of available legal protections. We affirm that authorities exist to enact justice (Romans 13:1-5).” Church said no grounds for divorce Alison Wilder told the Los Angeles County court, however, that church leaders said they wouldn’t help her unless she dropped the divorce case. They said she didn’t have biblical grounds to leave her husband and could not continue with marriage counseling unless she agreed, according to the lawsuit. Adam Tyson (pictured) is the senior pastor and an elder at Placerita Bible Church in Los Angeles. (Courtesy image) She claims that several months later church leaders joined her husband’s “systematic campaign of domestic violence,” helping him as he tried to use private images to control and punish her. According to the lawsuit, the church’s actions demonstrated “willful and conscious disregard” of Alison Wilder’s “rights and safety.” Her lawyer argues this disregard legally constitutes a breach of obligation with malice, which caused “severe emotional distress, humiliation, trauma, and psychological harm.” The lawsuit asks a jury to order the church to pay financial compensation and punitive damages. The church’s lawyers have not yet responded to the suit. Alison Wilder filed for a dissolution of marriage in April 2024, citing “irreconcilable differences.” The two married in March 2014. He was 39 and she was 24, a 15-year age difference. They met through work. Andrew Wilder was honest from the beginning about his struggles with addiction, Alison Wilder said in a statement submitted in the divorce proceedings. He described himself as an addict, but said he’d gotten help through regular attendance at Alcoholics Anonymous meetings. “It is a large part of his life,” Alison Wilder told the court. “He has said … that if I ever was concerned about his sobriety, that he would be willing to take a drug test.” Alison Wilder quit her job to plan the wedding and then got pregnant two weeks after they were married. The couple agreed that she would stay home, raise their children and eventually homeschool, while he would pursue his career and provide for them financially. Andrew Wilder was, at the time, writing on “Those Who Kill,” a network television show about an obsessed detective who hunts a serial killer. The following year, 2015, he started working on “Secrets and Lies,” another network crime show, which starred Ryan Phillippe and Juliette Lewis. The Wilders bought a four-bedroom home near Alison’s parents in Santa Clarita. They had a second child a year after the first. According to Alison Wilder, they liked their respective roles and felt their lives were going according to plan. “Andrew has always had the freedom to travel and work late and on weekends and to grow his career to its greatest potential because he knew our home was held down by me and (that) our kids were well taken care of,” she told the court. “There was never a discussion between us about a new TV show job. … I supported whatever show he took, and if possible, would take our kids to travel to visit him on set.” In 2019, however, Alison Wilder claims her husband relapsed. He went to rehab and got sober, but relapsed again in 2023, according to court documents. A drawing by the Wilder Children depicting the divorce of their parents was submitted in the lawsuit. (Source: Los Angeles County Superior Court) Addiction problems She told the court her husband has been “drinking on and off” ever since. And when he drinks and does drugs, Alison Wilder said, her husband gets mean and aggressive. He would threaten her, push her, block her path, throw things and slam things out of her hand, according to the lawsuit. Andrew Wilder also became convinced that his wife was cheating on him with a Mexican man. Although she repeatedly denied it, he didn’t believe her. “As he was leaving in his truck with our children on April 4, 2024, Andrew rolled down all the windows in his truck and shouted at me, ‘Have fun with your Mexican boyfriend! Have fun eating tacos!!” Alison Wilder told the court. “Andrew called me a ‘whore’ and assorted other derogatory names, again in front of our children.” She also accused her husband of coaching their children to say she confessed to an extramarital affair. She told the court that Andrew Wilder has destroyed things in anger, including a vase of flowers and a flagpole. He played rap music by Eminem with the volume turned way up, singing along. Lyrics included the phrases “my life would be so much better if you just dropped dead” and “you think it’s a joke ’til you’re bullet-riddled.” Alison Wilder was still willing to try marriage counseling. “I want our children to have their dad,” she told the court, “but a healthy and present one.” In 2019, Kanye West (then known as Yeezy) stirred excitement for Evangelicals with the release of his album Jesus Is King. (Source: Graham MacDonald on Medium) Turn to biblical counseling Placerita Bible Church initially seemed like a promising place to get counseling. Tyson is a certified biblical counselor and a fellow with the Association of Certified Biblical Counselors (ACBC). He has two advanced degrees from The Master’s Seminary, the Christian school long associated with John MacArthur. In 2019, he briefly grabbed global attention as the pastor preaching 12-minute sermons at rapper Kanye West’s Christian events. He vouched for the sincerity of West’s turn to Christ and his spiritual transformation. Tyson told Christianity Today he was “fired up” about this “opportunity to reach pop culture and America for Christ.” West quickly moved on to other things, however, including a presidential campaign and the controversy with repeated antisemitic remarks and praise Adolf Hitler. Tyson turned his attention back to his congregation and biblical counseling. “I don’t know about you, but I love God’s Word,” he said in 2023, “and my goal for myself as a biblical counselor and for you as a counselor and the people that you counsel is for you to engage with God through His Word. You can’t know God truly without knowing Him through the Scriptures.” Jim Sojka, Alison Wilder’s father, encouraged her to ask Tyson and the other “Master’s Men” on staff for help. Sojka had been a member of Placerita Bible Church, which had regular attendance of a few hundred people, including many students from The Masters’ University, for more than 20 years. He was also an elder. Sojka was disappointed, he told RR, when church leaders didn’t seem to believe his daughter or take her concerns seriously. Still, Sojka said, when Tyson and elders Hamblin and Madrid asked to meet with him in August, he didn’t think it had anything to do with the counseling — which stopped after two sessions — or the ongoing divorce. He certainly wasn’t prepared to see explicit photos of his adult child. “I thought they were going to ask me to head up some ministry or something,” Sojka told RR. “I was completely blindsided. I could never have imagined what happened that night. I was dumbfounded.” According to court records, Alison Wilder is alone in the images that church leaders shared, and they do not show her having sex with another man. Impact of revenge porn Placerita elders Mark Madrid (pictured) and Patrick Hamblin believed Wilder’s claim that his wife was having an affair. (Source: Placerita Bible Church) Research on revenge porn finds that sexual images shared without consent have “devastating impacts … on survivors’ mental health.” Victims report symptoms of post-traumatic stress disorder, anxiety, depression and suicidal thoughts, along with intense feeling of betrayal, loss of control and loss of trust. The trauma is similar to that caused by sexual assault. Experts say revenge porn should be considered a form of sexual violence. It is illegal in 48 states, including California, where prosecutors have prosecuted hundreds of cases. A Los Angeles police officer going through a divorce pleaded no contest to a revenge porn charge in 2024, a few months before Andrew Wilder allegedly sent pictures of his wife to Placerita Bible Church leaders. The victim in that case said she would have preferred that her husband punch her in the face. Alison Wilder said that learning that her husband and church leaders shared nude images of her caused “severe emotional distress and humiliation.” Her attorney will argue in court that the church leaders “acted with intent to cause injury … and with willful and conscious disregard of her rights and safety.” Daniel Silliman is senior reporter/editor at The Roys Report. He began his two decades in journalism covering crime in Atlanta and has since led major investigations into abuse and misconduct in Christian contexts. Daniel and his wife live in Johnson City, Tennessee. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Former Kentucky pastor gets 13 years after pleading guilty in child sex abuse case
item.author: Mark A. Kellner
item.dc:creator: Mark A. Kellner
item.description: Note: This story contains details about child sexual abuse. A former Kentucky megachurch pastor was sentenced June 4 to 13 years in prison after pleading guilty March 23 to multiple sex crimes involving a girl who attended the Lexington church. Former LexCity Church executive pastor Zachary King, 48, was sentenced in Fayette Circuit Court, according […]
item.content:encoded: Zachary King, former executive pastor of LexCity Church in Lexington, Kentucky, has been sentenced after pleading guilty to multiple felony charges involving the abuse of a minor. (RR Graphic) Note: This story contains details about child sexual abuse. A former Kentucky megachurch pastor was sentenced June 4 to 13 years in prison after pleading guilty March 23 to multiple sex crimes involving a girl who attended the Lexington church. Former LexCity Church executive pastor Zachary King, 48, was sentenced in Fayette Circuit Court, according to WKYT-TV 21, a CBS affiliate. Under the plea agreement, King also must register as a lifetime sex offender and must not contact the victim. The case centered on abuse that authorities said began when the victim was 15 and continued for about 18 months. King pleaded guilty to seven felonies: first-degree rape, first-degree sodomy, procuring or promoting the use of a minor by electronic means, first-degree unlawful transaction with a minor, third-degree rape, third-degree sodomy and first-degree sexual abuse. WKYT reported court documents said King restrained the victim on one occasion and raped and sodomized her. He later contacted the victim for additional meetings where other assaults took place. LexCity Church closed in July 2024 after executive pastor Zachary King was arrested for having a sexual relationship with a minor. (Source: Youtube/Fox58 Screen grab) The Roys Report (RR), citing state officials, reported that King told investigators in 2024 he had sexual intercourse with the girl at her home, at his home and at LexCity Church, where he worked. RR also reported that King confessed to using Snapchat and WhatsApp to exchange sexually explicit images with the girl. Kentucky Attorney General Russell Coleman said after the guilty plea, “This so-called pastor’s abuse of his position of spiritual trust to prey on a young victim was heartbreaking. We are sending this predator to prison to protect more Kentuckians from horrific abuse.” King had resigned from his pastoral role after church staff confronted him in 2024 and reported the suspected abuse to Lexington police, leading to his arrest, media reports said. The criminal case helped trigger the collapse of LexCity Church, a once-large Lexington congregation that announced in July 2024 it would permanently cease ministry. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church.
