Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. According to ABC affiliate WTVQ-TV 36, church leaders issued a statement at the time, saying “Through thoughtful prayer and heartfelt tears, the leadership team of Lexington City Church has voted to permanently cease our ministry.” The statement added: “Due to the continued effects of the financial situation our church inherited several years ago and the impact of the ongoing investigation of a former staff member, we have lost the ability to remain financially viable and fulfill our God-given mission.” LexCity also said then that its “hearts are broken for those who have been hurt and are hurting,” and that it would continue to cooperate fully with the investigation. Before his arrest, King had worked in evangelical churches for nearly two decades. His employers included Life.Church and The MET Church in Houston, as RR previously reported. At the time of the guilty plea, RR quoted Coleman calling the crimes “heartbreaking.” “We are sending this predator to prison,” Coleman said, “to protect more Kentuckians from horrific abuse.” WKYT reported in March that King’s recommended sentence under the plea deal was 13 years. Mark A. Kellner is a reporter based in Mesquite, Nevada. He most recently covered statewide elections for the New York Post and was for three years the Faith & Family Reporter for The Washington Times. Mark is a graduate of the University of the Cumberlands and also attended Boston University’s College of Communication. SHARE THIS:

item.title: No miracle for charismatic leader Ché Ahn in California election results
item.author: Daniel Silliman
item.dc:creator: Daniel Silliman
item.description: Ché Ahn will not be moving to Sacramento next year to take up residence in the governor’s mansion. The charismatic apostle, who oversees an international network of churches thought that God might intervene in California’s gubernatorial election. But when the results of the all-party primary came in on Tuesday, the miracle failed to materialize. Ahn, […]
item.content:encoded: Ché Ahn, a charismatic pastor and gubernatorial candidate, continues campaigning after courts ruled he will not appear on California’s primary ballot due to missing tax disclosures. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) Ché Ahn will not be moving to Sacramento next year to take up residence in the governor’s mansion. The charismatic apostle, who oversees an international network of churches thought that God might intervene in California’s gubernatorial election. But when the results of the all-party primary came in on Tuesday, the miracle failed to materialize. Ahn, 70, said on the campaign trail that he believes that God woke him up one night, a little over a year ago, at 2:22 a.m. Looking at the numbers on the clock, he felt divine direction to read Isaiah 22:22. “I will place on his shoulder the key to the house of David,” the verse says. “What he opens no one can shut, and what he shuts no one can open.” Ahn understood that to mean that he should run for governor. He believed God wanted to use him, a Korean-born minister in the New Apostolic Reformation (NAR) movement, to conquer what some charismatics call the “mountain” of government, and bring transformation to the Golden State. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Ché Ahn, founding and senior pastor of Harvest Rock Church in Pasadena, California. (Photo courtesy of cheahn.org) He could provide new leadership, solve the problem of undocumented immigration, restore law and order, eliminate excessive regulation, restore economic freedom and make the state affordable again, putting families first. The race to replace the term-limited Democrat Gavin Newsom was wide open, after all. And Ahn believes in divine intervention. “I’m not limiting the Holy One of Israel,” he told supporters. “I’m going in it with an intent to win. I’m going in it with the intent that God’s going to do something supernatural.” When California officials started counting the ballots on Tuesday, however, Ahn didn’t come in first. Nor did he finish second. Nor third. Nor fourth. On Friday morning, the California Secretary of State reported that county elections officials had counted 60% of the vote. The final, exact vote count will be certified in July, but Ahn’s name does not appear anywhere near the top of the list. Ahn is not ahead in Los Angeles County, where his church is located, in nearby Orange County, or in Lassen, which is by some measure’s the most conservative county in the state. In Kings County, where Trump won by 10 points in 2024, Ahn got just 23 votes. In Kern County, another Republican stronghold in the San Joaquin Valley, Ahn got 28 votes, about .04% of the total. There were more than a million ballots cast in San Diego County. Voters wrote the charismatic apostle’s name down 17 times. Failed to get on the ballot Part of the problem, of course, was that Ahn was not on the ballot. He was disqualified in March after his campaign failed to file five years of his tax returns, as required by state law. Ahn’s campaign manager, who is also an elder at the multisite church he founded in Pasadena in 1994, made a mistake. Ahn went to court to fight for his spot on the ballot but lost. He appealed. And lost again. Ahn decided not to drop out, however. He got certified as a write-in candidate, alongside six other marginal contenders, and keep pushing his message. Campaign materials for Ché Ahn, who said God called him to run for governor of California. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) “My mission has never changed since the day I announced my campaign for Governor,” Ahn said in one campaign post on social media. “I will not stop fighting until California is transformed. I believe the Gospel is the answer to the deepest challenges we face—not just politically, but morally, spiritually, and culturally.” Ahn’s campaign material was changed to say: “Write-in CHE AHN for Governor.” And his team put out explanations of the write-in process on the campaign website and in short videos on social media. “There’s no accent over the e,” one Facebook reel said. “Just write CHE AHN!” Floundered from the start Close observers, however, noticed the campaign had problems from the very beginning. The announcement video, for example, started with a staffer noting there were too many empty seats. The video was filmed in front of the Pasadena campus of Ahn’s church, which attracts several thousand people every Sunday. But the crowd gathered for the campaign launch looked thin. Organizers asked supporters to bunch together to improve the optics. The request was not edited out of the video before it was posted online, so the first minute is staffers telling people to move. “Ahn’s campaign is not serious,” historian John Fea, author of “Believe Me: The Evangelical Road to Donald Trump,” told the Roys Report (RR). “Conservative and charismatic evangelicals want something akin to a Christian nationalist in office, but they are also savvy enough to back a candidate who has a chance of winning.” Fea noted that Ahn couldn’t even manage to secure key endorsements from obvious ideological allies in California. Christian musician Sean Feucht, of California, sings to the crowd during a rally at the National Mall in Washington, Sunday, Oct. 25, 2020. (AP Photo/Jose Luis Magana) For example Jack Hibbs, pastor of Calvary Chapel-Chino Hills, supported Republican frontrunner Steve Hilton. So did worship leader Sean Feucht. Despite his important, ongoing partnership with Ahn’s church, Feucht made an appeal for Christians to unite behind the Fox News commentator and former advisor to British politicians. “The church is too divided on who to support,” Feucht wrote on X. “There’s only 1 candidate who could win.” Ahn did, however win the endorsement of a few prominent charismatics who share his belief that the church has entered a new age of modern-day apostles and prophets. He had the public support of apostles Bill Johnson, Lou Engle and Cindy Jacobs, as well as megachurch pastor Landon Schott and Charisma magazine founder Stephen Strang. Fundraising shows strength Ahn also managed to raise a good deal of money. According to financial reports his campaign filed with the state, he received more than 880 donations, raising $967,000. The campaign accepted contributions from more than 60 ministers in California and across the country. Donations — large and small — also came from a nursery worker, a janitor, a FedEx driver and an executive assistant at Warner Bros., campaign finance records show. The assistant manager of a Chick-fil-A sent in $100. A valet gave another $100. The secretary at a San Diego printing company donated $52. Ahn’s name didn’t register in election polls, but his fundraising numbers put him in the middle of the pack. Having a large number of contributors doesn’t necessarily translate into a win, but experts say it does indicate political strength. “Donor breadth doesn’t guarantee votes,” political analyst Mason Herron said. “But it often signals something harder to fake: sustained political presence and a base that predates the campaign itself.” Modern American politics is wildly expensive, though, and Ahn’s donors could not keep pace with the top fundraisers in California’s gubernatorial race. Democrat Xavier Baccer, who went into election day with a polling average of around 25%, raised $12.9 million. Hilton, the leading Republican with about 23%, raised more than $14 million. After Ahn failed to get on the ballot, he saw his support collapse. During the first month, the number of donors dropped more than 20%, according to campaign financial reports. The next month — in the home stretch of the campaign — the number fell another 40%. One prediction market analyst put Ahn’s chances of winning the election at 0.01%, “a virtual impossibility.” Some supporters were undaunted, taking to social media to post comments proclaiming a miracle in Jesus’ name. Ahn, however, said that while God told him to run, God didn’t tell him he would win. The candidate started to talk about other ways of measuring success. Perhaps his campaign would “raise a new generation of Republicans,” in a state where the party can only claim one out of every four voters. Perhaps he would mobilize and organize Christians committed to pray for the state. Perhaps his campaign would lead to a spiritual awakening or even a revival. Matthew Taylor, a scholar of charismatic movements. (Courtesy photo) Religious scholar Matthew Taylor, an expert on the NAR, said that Ahn’s campaign should be viewed as an experiment. This group of Christians has, just in the last few decades, come to believe that they have to get involved in politics. But they are still figuring out how. “It’s become a theological imperative,” Taylor told RR. “But they’re still trying to find tactics and strategies. It is a hard translation problem. They really don’t know anything about winning people who aren’t attached to their theology.” Ahn lost in California, Taylor said, but that doesn’t mean an apostle won’t win in another race in another state. And it doesn’t mean that Ahn himself won’t learn from his campaign’s failure and run again. “Ché is a strategic thinker. He thinks about advancing his version of the kingdom of God, and the size and scope of his network is testament to his doggedness and capacity to gather other people,” Taylor said. “What if he finds more competent campaign staff? It’s not crazy to think, amid all the political upheavals, that he’d have a path to victory.” Ahn, for his part, appears to think the same thing. On a podcast in March, he admitted he might lose the upcoming election but hurried to assure supporters that God would keep the promise of Romans 8:22 and use the loss for good. “Because I’m going to run again,” Ahn said. “And now I know how to build for a campaign. … I will have four more years to build correctly, raise the funds that are necessary. We’re getting the grassroots movement started now and we’re going to continue until I win.” Daniel Silliman is senior reporter/editor at The Roys Report. He began his two decades in journalism covering crime in Atlanta and has since led major investigations into abuse and misconduct in Christian contexts. Daniel and his wife live in Johnson City, Tennessee. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Christians in India denied clean water, jobs, food in ongoing religious persecution
item.author: Diana Chandler
item.dc:creator: Diana Chandler
item.description: Hundreds of Christians in central India have been denied clean water, fresh produce and employment for nearly a month in unsuccessful attempts to force them to denounce Christianity, International Christian Concern (ICC) reported May 29. More than 180 Christian tribal families in Chhattisgarh state in central India are blocked from community rivers and other water […]
item.content:encoded: Religious freedom advocates say persecution of Christians and other religious minorities has increased in India in recent years. (Photo courtesy of Unsplash/Austin Curtis) Hundreds of Christians in central India have been denied clean water, fresh produce and employment for nearly a month in unsuccessful attempts to force them to denounce Christianity, International Christian Concern (ICC) reported May 29. More than 180 Christian tribal families in Chhattisgarh state in central India are blocked from community rivers and other water sources; have been denied employment in a scheme orchestrated by the government, and are barred from collecting and selling non-timber forest products, a main tribal source of nourishment and income. The families, termed Vivasis because of their tribal heritage, are spread among 32 villages in the Kanker district of Chhattisgarh, where many Christians reside among the majority Hindi nation of nearly 1.5 billion people. “Christian community leaders have stated that these severe boycotts are aimed at pressuring believers into abandoning their faith and participating in Ghar Wapsi or ‘homecoming’ programs, meaning re-converting to Hinduism,” ICC said in reporting the persecution. “These areas of the Kanker districts have also previously seen broader tensions in the past, with various local village authorities issuing resolutions to restrict Christian burials and the entry of pastors.” Violence often follows such boycotts and religious exclusions, ICC said, “with police and district administrations unable to take decisive action to prevent the atrocities.” Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. A Christian church in a village in Manipur, India, burns on May 5, 2023. (Photo: MangteC/X) Chhattisgarh civil rights groups including the Progressive Christian Alliance and Jila Masih Astha Samaj have complained in writing of the abuse, but the targeted Christian communities continue to face severe hardships. The persecution follows the May 13 murder of three Christian church leaders among the indigenous Kuki-Thadou people in northeastern India’s Manipur State, and tracks with increased persecution of Christians, Muslims and other minorities the U.S. Commission on International Religious Freedom (USCIRF) has noted. Pastor Paogoulen Sitlhou and two preachers, Vumthang Sitlhou and Kaigoulun Lhouvum, were killed when unidentified gunmen opened fire in mountainous terrain on a larger group leaving the United Baptist Convention Assembly in Churachandpur District, Christian persecution watchdog CSW reported May 22. At least five others suffered life-threatening gunshot injuries and were taken to Shija Hospital in Imphal, CSW said. Graffiti reading “Go Back” and “Kuki” marks a wall in Manipur, India, where escalating ethnic tensions and militant violence recently left three Kuki-Zo Christian pastors dead in an ambush. (Screen grab) The Baptist convention focused on reconciliation and peacebuilding among tribal communities in Manipur. But the killings spurred days of retaliatory violence, CSW said, with reports of armed Kuki groups abducting Naga civilians, and Naga village members and armed groups abducting Kuki. “These targeted attacks on revered church leaders who were returning from a dedicated mission of peace and tribal reconciliation constitute a horrific assault on human life and the freedom of religion or belief in Manipur,” CSW Founder and President Mervyn Thomas said. “The subsequent retaliatory abductions and hostage crisis underscore how rapidly instability can spiral when faith leaders and peacebuilders are targeted. … The international community must not look away as tribal Christian communities and peace advocates in Manipur face escalating vulnerabilities.” The U.S. State Department should designate India a Country of Particular Concern (CPC) for egregious religious freedom violations, USCIRF recommended in its 2026 annual report, noting persecution of Christians, Muslims and other minorities despite religious freedom guarantees in India’s constitution. In October 2025, Indian police arrested U.S. citizen James Watson, accusing him of converting Hindus to Christianity in Maharashtra. Watson was released on bail after two weeks, but USCIRF did not disclose in its report any resolution of the case. Watson and two Indian nationals were charged with “hurting religious sentiment.” India accused them of “violating the state’s anti-black magic law, luring individuals to convert by promising ‘miracle cures’ and prosperity,” USCIRF said in its annual report. Religious freedom continued to deteriorate in India in 2025, USCIRF said in its report, with the government enforcing harsh laws against Christians and other minority communities and states attaching longer prison sentences to anti-conversion laws. Detention of members of minority religious communities, illegal expulsion of citizens and religious refugees, and unchecked vigilante attacks on minority religious communities increased. Among India’s numerous laws targeting minority religious communities are the Foreign Contribution Regulation Act, the 1967 Unlawful Activities Prevention Act, the 2019 Citizenship (Amendment) Act, the National Register of Citizens, the 2025 Waqf Bill, and the 2025 Immigration and Foreigners Bill. Article 295A of the Penal Code functions as a blasphemy law by criminalizing actions deemed to “outrage religious feelings.” This article was originally published by Baptist Press and has been reprinted with permission. Diana Chandler is senior writer for Baptist Press. SHARE THIS:

item.title: NorthWoods Deaf Church launches with baptisms, fellowship, music loud enough to feel
item.author: Diana Chandler
item.dc:creator: Diana Chandler
item.description: When Corey Harrison struggled with deep anger in college, wondering why God created him Deaf, a friend invited him to a men’s retreat. There, Harrison recalled the love he experienced when his childhood church hired Deaf pastor Paul Cannon. “I remember the first time he signed to me,” Harrison, now himself a pastor, told Baptist […]
item.content:encoded: Worshipers praise God in American Sign Language at the launch of NorthWoods Deaf Church, a ministry of NorthWoods Church in Evansville, Ind. (Courtesy photo) When Corey Harrison struggled with deep anger in college, wondering why God created him Deaf, a friend invited him to a men’s retreat. There, Harrison recalled the love he experienced when his childhood church hired Deaf pastor Paul Cannon. “I remember the first time he signed to me,” Harrison, now himself a pastor, told Baptist Press. “I was speechless.” Harrison is pastor of NorthWoods Deaf Church, which NorthWoods Church of Evansville launched May 17. Harrison made the difficult decision to leave his first pastorate, New Life Deaf Church in Clarksville, Tenn., to lead the Evansville church plant. “Over the past two years, God placed a growing burden on my heart to reach more Deaf people with the Gospel,” Harrison said. “During that time, several people connected with NorthWoods Church in Evansville, Ind., reached out to me.” Senior Pastor Bobby Pell and Missions Pastor Ryan Moore met Harrison through a lead from the North American Mission Board, Pell and Moore told Baptist Press. Through the two of them, Harrison saw an open door to respond to his godly burden and visited a Deaf community bonfire at NorthWoods last October. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Members and friends of the local Deaf community gathered at NorthWoods Deaf Church in Evansville, Ind., for a recent fellowship event. (Courtesy photo) “Listening to Deaf individuals share their struggles and desire for Bible study and church ministry deeply moved me,” Harrison said. “I began teaching an eight-week class called ‘The Church,’ and through that process God clearly confirmed His calling for us to plant NorthWoods Deaf Church.” Pell and Moore learned the need for a Deaf church after area resident Julia Aregood visited NorthWoods around Easter 2025, they told Baptist Press. She visited with a Deaf friend, and in the following weeks began to sign the sermon and bring additional Deaf friends. Aregood’s friends began to ask her such questions as whether the guy on the stage was telling the truth. “He keeps talking about sin. What is sin? You keep talking about heaven and hell and our need to know Jesus in order to go to heaven,” Aregood said her friends wanted to know. “We didn’t go look for this. This came to us,” Pell said. “I think the best things that have happened in Northwoods did not come from our strategies. They came from God at work. And then we saw it. We joined it. … What’s interesting is that I learned there’s a need for Deaf churches.” While there are about 1,400 Deaf or hearing-impaired people in Clarksville, the community increases to between 4,000 and 4,500 in Evansville, the pastors said. The mother church baptized two Deaf believers in the weeks leading up to the launch, the pastors said, and will baptize three more on May 31. “We’ve learned a lot about this segment of the population,” Moore said. “It’s not uncommon for them to feel prejudice and like they are the outcast and they are not included as a part of normal society. And we have really tried to make them feel like they matter and they’re made in God’s image just as we’re made in God’s image.” NorthWoods Deaf Church meets on the church campus, large enough to host the two congregations simultaneously. Families including Deaf and hearing individuals attend the new church, as do Deaf individuals and hearing friends. Harrison preaches in American Sign Language (ASL). Worshipers sign praises. Pastor Corey Harrison (left) uses American Sign Language to greet worshipers at the launch of Northwoods Deaf Church in Evansville, Ind. (Courtesy photo) The new church has already held several fellowship events with more planned. NorthWoods Church members have requested an ASL class to learn to communicate with the new church, and Moore and Pell also plan to learn ASL. And then there’s the music, or more succinctly, the music volume, which translates as a good thing. “Oh it’s loud,” Pell said. “Very, very loud,” Moore added. Loud music helps the community feel the music they can’t audibly hear. “You wouldn’t think there’d be music, but they like music,” Pell said. “It has to be loud because they need to feel the music, right? And so the subwoofers are turned up as loud as they’ll go. When you’re walking in from the parking lot, you can feel it outside. And so they just can’t hear it, obviously, but they can feel the bass. They can feel it.” Harrison wondered how God would use him as a Deaf pastor soon after he felt God’s call at the men’s retreat during college, he told media. “During that retreat, I surrendered my anger and fully sensed God calling me into ministry. At first, I struggled with the idea because I wondered how a Deaf person could become a pastor,” Harrison said. “I reached out to Pastor Paul Cannon, and he encouraged me, prayed with me, and helped me realize there was a great need for Deaf ministry and Deaf churches.” In college, Harrison attended a Deaf Day of Prayer event in Louisville, Ky., he said. There, he learned that less than 2 percent of Deaf people were followers of Christ. “That was my first experience attending a Deaf church and meeting Deaf pastors. I heard about the overwhelming spiritual need among Deaf people,” Harrison said. “That burden stayed with me.” Pell and Moore encourage other churches to follow God’s guidance in planting congregations, whether to reach Deaf communities or other largely unreached people groups. “All of our communities have people groups that are not currently being reached with the Gospel,” Moore said. “Just look at your community. Are there opportunities there for church planting to take place to reach this segment of the population? And then just step out in faith and start taking steps towards reaching them with the Gospel and just see what doors of opportunity God opens up.” “And I think the only thing I’d add to that,” Pell said, “is in all of our churches, I think God’s saying yes to us for something. And I just think we have to be ready and willing to look to see what are the opportunities that are in front of us that we haven’t seen.” This article was originally published by Baptist Press and has been reprinted with permission. Diana Chandler is senior writer for Baptist Press. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Threatened split at Mark Driscoll’s Arizona church prompts warning of lawsuit, attack on former men’s minister
item.author: Daniel Silliman
item.dc:creator: Daniel Silliman
item.description: Pastor Mark Driscoll warned his followers Wednesday of “fierce wolves … among you,” following the sudden departure of an associate minister and former security guard known as Driscoll’s chief henchman. A mass email Driscoll sent at 7:44 a.m. accused Caleb Glennie of attempting to split Trinity Church, a Scottsdale, Arizona, congregation with reported weekly average […]
item.content:encoded: Mark Driscoll, founder and pastor of Trinity Church in Scottsdale, Arizona, accused a former top leader of attempting to divide the megachurch and lead members away. (RR Graphic) Pastor Mark Driscoll warned his followers Wednesday of “fierce wolves … among you,” following the sudden departure of an associate minister and former security guard known as Driscoll’s chief henchman. A mass email Driscoll sent at 7:44 a.m. accused Caleb Glennie of attempting to split Trinity Church, a Scottsdale, Arizona, congregation with reported weekly average attendance of 5,000 people. “(He) took church members’ private information to solicit, urged staff members to resign and follow him … and announced a Men’s gathering on Tuesdays,” the email said. “Caleb has been lying for months. We rebuke Caleb and urge him to repent of sin patterns that have disqualified him as a pastor.” Driscoll started Trinity Church in 2016 after a series of scandals led to the implosion of his previous church, Mars Hill Seattle. His former elders have said that he’s not qualified for ministry. His current church has no elders. With the new congregation, Driscoll has said that one of the core values is going on offense. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Caleb Glennie, a former Army veteran and Trinity Church pastor. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) He has also emphasized the need for loyalty and built a large security team that runs extensive surveillance operations on people who leave the church. Glennie, a 43-year-old Army veteran, played a key role on the team, according to former members. He helped develop tactics and procured equipment, including semiautomatic, AR-style rifles. Caleb Glennie. Courtesy photo. Glennie was later promoted to head of the men’s ministry, “Real Men.” Now Driscoll is threatening legal action and calling Glennie a false teacher, quoting the apostle Paul’s warning in Acts 20. “Pay careful attention,” the Bible verse says. “Fierce wolves will come in among you … and from among your own selves will arise men speaking twisted things,” “to draw away the disciples after them.” Trinity Church did not immediately respond to an email from the Roys Report (RR) with questions about disqualifications and false teaching. Glennie did not respond to multiple texts. Internal divisions becoming public Vince Manuele, author of “Kiss and Tell,” an insider’s account of what he calls Driscoll’s “corruption, control, and abuse,” said the church’s aggressive response to Glennie is telling. Trinity’s typical approach is to ignore critics and remain silent when people leave. “This is definitely a crack up,” Manuele told RR. “The fact that they sent this thing out shows that there’s a rift, and it’s starting to show.” Glennie was baptized at Driscoll’s church in 2020. A year later, his wife, Jill, and two of their three children were also baptized at Trinity. “This is the best day of my life,” Glennie said at the time. “I can’t even explain the feeling.” Glennie worked as a real estate agent in 2021 but stopped selling houses to join the church staff. He was ordained in the church in 2023 and became the men’s minister, overseeing an area of major emphasis for Driscoll. Glennie did not go to seminary or receive any kind of theological education, according to Manuele. He was mentored by Driscoll and became Driscoll’s right-hand man. Another former member of Trinity who once called Glennie her “spiritual father” told RR that Glennie was devoted to Driscoll. “He was at Mark’s beck and call,” she said, speaking on condition of anonymity due to fear of retribution. “Caleb was about Mark, Mark, Mark. He saw himself as Mark Driscoll’s protector, absolutely.” Didn’t care about past scandals Glennie and others who joined Trinity knew of Driscoll’s controversial past, of course. They knew he once boasted about the “pile of dead bodies” he’d left behind him in his determination not to let anything get in the way of ministry. They knew the story about how Driscoll screamed “How dare you!” in a church service. They knew he’d said crude things about women and pushed through a change of the bylaws at the church in Seattle that gave him more control and less accountability. Mark Driscoll’s ministry app offers sermons, Bible teaching, and culture-war commentary. (Screen grab) They knew he’d been accused of abusive leadership. Some of the accusations they dismissed as fabrications. But others they believed, Manuele said, and kind of liked. “The whole thing is about men and masculinity,” he told RR. “They have an obsession with Mark and like being close to him. Mark is always going to have people he can attract. He could kill someone and still have people follow him.” Driscoll’s ministry and message have resonated with enough people that his Arizona church is apparently growing. Trinity is constructing a new building that will quadruple the size of its current footprint and have five times as much parking. The project is expected to cost $50 million. Driscoll has also launched his own app, with his sermons, Bible exposition, culture war commentary and “practical tools to fix your marriage.” According to the ministry, the app has been downloaded more than 150,000 times. Ministers leaving But there still appears to be a lot of ongoing, internal turmoil at Trinity. Multiple staff members have left, calling the church culture disgusting and cult-like. One former member of the security team told RR that church leaders rank members, 1 to 10, on a loyalty scale, and maintain a list of former members banned from the property. Julie Krombien. Courtesy photo. An archived version of the church website shows that four of Trinity’s 11 ministers have left in the last year, including Glennie and “Real Women” minister Julie Krombien. According to the church email, Krombien and her husband, Todd, are joining Glennie and his wife to start a new church. The cause of the split remains unclear. According to the church email, Glennie informed leadership last week that he and Jill were leaving “effective immediately.” He asked for six month’s severance pay but also, the church claims, used a Trinity credit card for personal expenses. The email claims that Glennie refused to meet with anyone and wiped all the information from his church-provided laptop before disappearing. If he thought that it was going to be a quiet departure, the email dispelled that misconception. “No one is safe from Mark’s wrath,” Manuele said. “This is obviously a pattern with Driscoll. Anyone that leaves the church and anyone that even talks to people that leave the church, they are outcast.” Daniel Silliman is senior reporter/editor at The Roys Report. He began his two decades in journalism covering crime in Atlanta and has since led major investigations into abuse and misconduct in Christian contexts. Daniel and his wife live in Johnson City, Tennessee. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Bethel Church promises reforms, deplatforms four men accused of misconduct
item.author: Ann Marie Shambaugh
item.dc:creator: Ann Marie Shambaugh
item.description: Four months after suspending a top leader accused of sexual assault, Bethel Church announced several governance and cultural reforms, saying it will no longer platform four men accused of misconduct. The charismatic megachurch in northern California provided the updates in a May 28 letter posted on its website. The 11,000-member church, known worldwide for its […]
item.content:encoded: Bethel Church announced it will no longer platform Todd Bentley, Mike Bickle, Shawn Bolz, and Bob Hartley as part of a governance and culture review launched after multiple scandals and allegations of misconduct. (RR Graphic) Four months after suspending a top leader accused of sexual assault, Bethel Church announced several governance and cultural reforms, saying it will no longer platform four men accused of misconduct. The charismatic megachurch in northern California provided the updates in a May 28 letter posted on its website. The 11,000-member church, known worldwide for its Bethel Music worship ministry, initiated a review of its governance and culture this year after several scandals prompted concerns about its operations and leadership. In February the church suspended Ben Armstrong, overseer of its prophetic ministry, after a former student at the Bethel School of Supernatural Ministry (BSSM) claimed he forced her into bed with him twice. Previously, Armstrong had admitted to a “moral failure” with an unnamed intern. He was reinstated to ministry and praised for his honesty. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. Ben Armstrong, former overseer of Bethel Church’s prophetic ministry, was suspended after a former student accused him of sexual assault. (Photo courtesy of Facebook) The letter, which is signed by the Bethel senior leadership team and board of elders, does not name Armstrong. It states that in February, Bethel began working with an “independent organization with expertise in complex investigations” that is “leading the inquiry into allegations involving a specific leader.” Bethel stated in the letter that it would not provide further comment on the matter while the investigation is ongoing. Over the past several months, Bethel has sought feedback on its leadership and culture from its congregation, staff, elders, BSSM alumni, various Christian leaders and others. Bethel leaders acknowledged in the letter that they could have been more consistent in handling “specific challenges,” without specifying which ones. “The engagement of outside professionals earlier may have been helpful, and we could have provided more open communication and offered clearer, timelier responses,” the letter states. “At times, our emphasis on internal discussions and confidential processes may have appeared to limit accountability, resulting in unintentional harm to individuals both within and beyond our church community.” The letter acknowledges that the Bethel “platform carries real weight and responsibility to be used consistently with our biblical principles.” It states that while restoration should always be pursued, not every leader accused of misconduct should be reinstated to a ministry position. Bethel senior pastor Bill Johnson speaking at the Bethel School of Supernatural Ministry. (Photo bssm.net) “As we review platforming, we want to follow clear biblical standards for public ministry leadership,” the letter states. “Scripture calls leaders to live above reproach and to show self-control, faithfulness, humility, integrity, and care for those they lead. Public ministry influence is a responsibility, not a right.” Those who will no longer be platformed by Bethel are: Todd Bentley, former leader of Fresh Fire USA. He was was disqualified from public ministry in 2020 by a panel of six charismatic Christian leaders after he faced credible accusations of adultery, sexting, vulgar language and substance abuse Mike Bickle, founder of the International House of Prayer of Kansas City, who is accused of sexually abusing at least 17 women Shawn Bolz, a minister accused of fabricating prophecies by using online resources Bob Hartley, a self-proclaimed prophet who has close ties to Bickle and has been accused of sexual misconduct The letter did not provide details about why Bethel specifically chose not to platform three of the men but did provide a clarification about Hartley. “Bethel took action after concerns were raised in previous years,” the letter states. “We restricted his ministry involvement, informed our church community, and later limited his access to Bethel’s campus. As part of our current review, we want to clearly state that Bob Hartley is not endorsed, platformed, or permitted to serve in ministry influence at Bethel.” Bethel leaders stated that they will not make decisions about platforming based “solely on rumors, pressure, or unverified claims.” Credible concerns may be further reviewed, they said. “If there are proven patterns of behavior that do not align with biblical standards, we cannot continue to platform, endorse or support that person for ministry leadership here,” the letter states. Since February, Bethel has made several “intentional changes” to put the “local church and staff first,” the letter states. Changes include: reshaping its men’s and women’s conferences to focus on its own church community; developing a focus group to strengthen accountability and connection; and cancelling a planned healing conference. The church is working with external leadership consultants Martin West and XGap to review its governance, organizational structure, leadership culture and operational health. Bethel Church in Redding, California An executive team led by Steve Moore, lead pastor of Bethel Online, and Bethel Chief Technology Officer Richard Gordon now lead Bethel’s daily operations, the letter says. Pastors Bill Johnson, Kris and Kathy Vallotton and Dann Farrelly will remain founders and “senior spiritual leaders,” it adds. The elders have increased their involvement and oversight in church matters. In addition, Bethel is reviewing its oversight into prophetic ministry and training. Other areas under scrutiny include pastoral standards and conduct, reporting and escalation processes and the church’s biblical theology and cultural frameworks. Holly Pivec, an author whose work has focused on the New Apostolic Reformation, said Bethel’s reforms don’t go far enough. “As long as Bethel continues to promote NAR teachings about apostles and prophets – and as long as its leaders, including Bill and Kris, function as authoritative apostles and prophets – the spiritual abuse will continue because NAR teachings about apostles and prophets are unbiblical and inherently abusive,” Pivec said in an email to the Roys Report (RR). Pivec’s co-auther, Doug Geivett, questioned the timing of the letter. “And why are they coming to their senses just now when they are supposed to be known for prophetic discernment?” Geivett asked in the email to RR. Ann Marie Shambaugh has reported as a print journalist in multiple states, including currently in Carmel, Indiana. SHARE THIS:

item.title: Wheaton College: Repent!
item.author: Julie Roys
item.dc:creator: Julie Roys
item.description: (Opinion) — Wheaton College is my alma mater and at one time, I was proud of that. The college was founded by abolitionist Jonathan Blanchard. And over the years, it has shown great concern for causes ranging from the indigenous of Ecuador to Americans of color to the point of removing or changing offensive on-campus […]
item.content:encoded: Current Wheaton College President Phil Ryken, former Vice President for Student Development Sam Shellhamer, and former President Duane Litfin are central figures in ongoing questions about the college’s handling of sexual abuse allegations of Dennis Massaro. (RR Graphic) (Opinion) — Wheaton College is my alma mater and at one time, I was proud of that. The college was founded by abolitionist Jonathan Blanchard. And over the years, it has shown great concern for causes ranging from the indigenous of Ecuador to Americans of color to the point of removing or changing offensive on-campus memorials. But stunningly, Wheaton now refuses to show similar care for its own students who were sexually assaulted by a former administrator. In fact, the college has a divinity school and an on-campus café named after those responsible for covering up the alleged sexual crimes of that administrator, Dennis Massaro. Wheaton also has done nothing to censure its current president, Phil Ryken, who learned a decade ago about Massaro’s assaults but did nothing. That reportedly enabled Massaro to abuse again. And again. Your tax-deductible gift supports our mission of reporting the truth and restoring the church. Donate $50 or more to The Roys Report, and you can elect to receive our featured offer, “Kiss and Tell: The Innocent Moment That Shattered Mark Driscoll’s Cult” by Vince Manuele – click here. James Oliver Buswell Jr., who led Wheaton College from 1926 to 1940, had his name removed from the college library over racist admissions practices. (Photo courtesy of PCAhistory.org) The hypocrisy and incongruity are stunning and unconscionable. Three years ago, Wheaton stripped the name of its third president, James Oliver Buswell Jr., from its on-campus library, after learning that Buswell had a “de facto policy of denying qualified Black applicants admission.” Similarly, Wheaton removed a plaque honoring slain missionaries Jim Elliot and Ed McCully, which referred to their killers as “savages” and the Waorani people as “Auca,” a derogatory term meaning “savage Indians.” It then replaced the plaque with one describing the Waorani as “indigenous peoples.” But when it comes to sexual assault, committed and covered up by Wheaton’s own staff, such noble sentiments evaporate. Antithesis of Wheaton’s motto As detailed in our series, “Wheaton Cover-Up,” the college knew in 1998 — almost 30 years ago — of three separate allegations that former administrator Dennis Massaro molested male students. The assaults all followed the same pattern. Massaro, who was once director of Wheaton’s Office of Christian Outreach (OCO), isolated the victims in a room with him while on Wheaton-sponsored retreats. Then, when the students were asleep, Massaro fondled them. When confronted, Massaro claimed he was sleepwalking and unaware of what he was doing. Dennis Massaro with a male student. (Photo used with permission.) After hearing of a third “sleepwalking” narrative in 1998, the higher-ups smelled a rat. Rather than reporting such activities to police — which would be classified as felony assaults — college officials covered them up. As we have documented in our series and accompanying podcasts, at least three current and former administrators were party to the cover-up: former President Duane Litfin; former Vice President for Student Development Sam Shellhamer; and more recently, President Phil Ryken. Not only has Wheaton failed to publicly acknowledge and apologize for its deplorable actions,Wheaton’s divinity school is named after Litfin. A café at the college is named “Sam’s,” after Sam Shellhamer. Former Wheaton Dean of Students Sam Shellhamer. (Photo courtesy of social media) These men were not involved in the school’s cover-up in a cursory or accidental way. Their acts were clearly intentional. Shellhamer was the administrator who heard the accounts of the three victims in 1998 and fired Massaro. Yet, Shellhamer remained silent when the college, under President Litfin’s leadership, had Massaro sign a confidentiality agreement, promising to keep his crimes secret or pay the college $5,000. Shellhamer also reportedly called an alleged victim, after dismissing Massaro, and pressured him not to talk about what Massaro had done. Shellhamer and Litfin then allowed The Wheaton Record, the college student newspaper, to report that Massaro had resigned for “personal reasons.” Then they allowed Massaro to continue frequenting Wheaton’s campus and endangering students, even after three alumni with knowledge of Massaro’s alleged abuse urged them to ban the former administrator. The college, under Litfin, also resisted complying with a subpoena requiring Shellhamer to testify in the custody proceedings involving the children of Massaro and his ex-wife Kathy Gallagher. Shellhamer complied only after Wheaton’s attorney, Christian Poland, secured a confidentiality agreement with Gallagher’s attorney. This ensured the truth would remain buried, so even Massaro’s own children would later doubt the veracity of the allegations against him. That Wheaton would prioritize its own reputation above the well-being of Gallagher and Massaro’s young daughters is unconscionable. It is the antithesis of standing for “Christ and His Kingdom,” the college motto. Instead, it is the preservation of Wheaton’s kingdom at the expense of everyone else. Wheaton College in Wheaton, Illinois. (Courtesy image) As documented in our fourth article, Wheaton’s silence and Shellhamer’s testimony, praising Massaro as an “outstanding alumnus,” likely helped Massaro get unsupervised visits with his children. This led to their repeated traumatization, leaving scars that endure to this day. Wheaton’s callous disregard for anyone but itself continued for decades. In 2008, Wheaton magazine reported that Massaro was a notable guest at the school’s on-campus celebration of the 50th anniversary of its Student Missionary Project. Imagine being one of Massaro’s alleged victims and seeing his presence celebrated in the college’s official alumni magazine! Given that Wheaton claims to represent Christ, imagine how that impacted their faith. This happened under President Litfin, whose name is emblazoned on one of Wheaton’s schools — and not just any school — but the school that studies our holy God who defends the vulnerable! Wheaton College posted this photo of former administrator Dennis Massaro attending a choir event in 2012, years after college officials learned of multiple allegations that he sexually abused students. (Photo courtesy of social media) But the cover-up continued under Ryken, who assumed the presidency in 2010. Two years later, Wheaton posted a picture on its social media of Massaro and an alumnus posing together at a college concert. Some may think Ryken had no knowledge of the prior scandal. And in 2012, that may have been possible, though unlikely. As Kathy Gallagher noted in a podcast, Ryken’s former executive assistant, Marilee Melvin, knew all about Massaro in 2014 and referred to him as a “pervert.” But after December 2014, there’s no doubt Ryken knew all about the allegations against Massaro. That’s when Kathy Gallagher, and then her husband Doug Gallagher, pleaded with Ryken to tell the world the truth about Massaro, as RR reported in part three of our series. But like his predecessors, Ryken refused. What’s worse, it appears that he lied about it. According to Kathy Gallagher, Ryken consulted with Wheaton’s general counsel and then told her the abuse and cover-up “never happened.” Ryken followed up with a deceptive letter, claiming that Wheaton was “under no contractual confidentiality to (Massaro).” Technically, that’s true. The separation agreement with Massaro was one-way, meaning Massaro was under contractual confidentiality with Wheaton but not vice-versa. Yet, Ryken conveniently failed to mention that. He further wrote that he was unaware of “any ‘cover up,’” despite certain knowledge of Wheaton’s agreement with Massaro. He also stated that the “personnel to whom Dennis reported during his employment are no longer employed by the College, and I have no personal knowledge of any of the events surrounding Dennis’s departure.” Ryken could have easily walked to Shellhamer’s house, which is less than a half mile from Wheaton’s campus, and obtained that “personal knowledge.” I did that myself before publishing any of these pieces. But Ryken didn’t trouble himself to do even that. The bottom line: Ryken didn’t want to know the truth. He wanted to maintain plausible deniability. Why? His letter suggests the answer. “Knowing personally the leadership in place at the College in 1998, I feel confident they did their best to honor God in making wise decisions with the facts known at the time,” he wrote. Former Wheaton College presidents Duane Litfin and Philip Ryken, under whose administrations the school is accused of keeping allegations against longtime ministry leader Dennis Massaro confidential. (Photo courtesy of social media) Litfin and Shellhamer were friends and important men, and Ryken wanted to protect them. The Gallaghers and other victims meant nothing to him. This is disgusting. Wheaton’s leadership in 1998 acted reprehensibly, and Ryken — 17 years later — approved their misconduct. Equally disgusting is Ryken’s conclusion that “(a)fter prayerful consideration, and after discussion with the College’s legal counsel,” he is “unable” to share information about Massaro’s misconduct. As noted in Ryken’s letter, the Gallaghers had told him that a former student believed Massaro had more victims, which seems likely. Once Ryken was made aware of Massaro’s alleged crimes, he had a responsibility to search for those victims. The Gallaghers also noted that one of their daughters did not know or believe what Massaro had done. Certainly, if he had sexually assaulted students as her mother said, Wheaton would not still allow him on campus and post pictures of him on X! Ryken noted their concern that their daughter was not “appropriately guided” in her relationship with her father. But even this did not move him. Instead, he protected the institution. Who cares about the one when there are 99 to consider? Ryken’s behavior contradicts all that Jesus taught and is the epitome of worldliness. Abusing Iranians As we previously reported, a few months after Ryken’s interaction with the Gallaghers, a Wheaton alumnus told Ryken that a church in Turkey had hired Massaro as its pastor. The alumnus told RR that Ryken responded with silence, not saying a word to warn the man — who was the former pastor of the Turkish church — about Massaro’s predatory ways. A few years later, the pastor learned that Massaro had engaged in sexual misconduct in Turkey, undermining the church’s Christian witness in an already skeptical and hostile Muslim community. According to a Turkish newspaper, Massaro’s misconduct included abusing Iranian refugees by extorting sex from them in exchange for help in obtaining asylum in the U.S. Dennis Massaro gives the benediction at a baptism service in Turkey in 2015. (Courtesy Photo) All of this is on Ryken’s head. The Gallaghers pleaded with him to sound an alarm. Ryken refused to act as a watchman, resulting in continued devastation to people formed in the image of God and harm to the cause of Christ. Is this a man really qualified to lead an elite Christian school? Can he be trusted to care for current students when he failed to care for past students and the children of a predator? Apparently, the Wheaton trustees think he is. They have done nothing — despite our extensive reports and podcasts detailing all of this. So now, it’s up to Wheaton alumni, students, faculty, staff and the broader Christian community to act. Sadly, I have learned that the only thing that motivates Christian institutions in crisis to do the right thing is external pressure. A petition launched today is urging Wheaton to place Ryken on leave and commission a truly independent investigation, unlike the one in 2024, which failed to even produce a report, let alone publish one. The petition also calls for the college to remove Litfin’s name from Wheaton’s divinity school and to rename “Sam’s.” And it urges Wheaton to show its repentance by “releasing all parties from existing non-disclosure agreements” and “providing restitution for the survivors (some of whom may not yet be known) of Dennis Massaro’s abuse.” Scripture calls us to confess sin, not hide it, and the very fact that this needs to be said is an indictment of the institution. Living for Christ and His Kingdom means identifying with the marginalized. And Jesus said that whatever we do for the “least of these,” we do for Him. At its founding, Wheaton understood this. But what Wheaton has done to the vulnerable over the past three decades — and by extension, to Jesus —boggles the mind. Someday, its leaders will have to give an account to Christ for all of this. And all their lawyers’ arguments and carefully crafted letters will be demolished in the face of the one who is the Truth. In the meantime, the rest of us can do our part to call Wheaton to repentance by signing the online petition praying for God to change hearts. Wheaton College, repent now, while those you have harmed are still alive! Then, bear fruit in keeping with that repentance and let the healing, which for many has been stunted due to your reprehensible actions, begin. Julie Roys is a veteran investigative reporter and founder of The Roys Report. She is a graduate of Wheaton College who previously hosted a national talk show on the Moody Radio Network, called Up for Debate, and has worked as a TV reporter for a CBS affiliate. Her articles have appeared in numerous periodicals. SHARE THIS:
